### [1] [SANA-Streaming: Real-time Streaming Video Editing with Hybrid Diffusion Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30409)
*Yuyang Zhao,Yicheng Pan,Qiyuan He,Jincheng Yu,Junsong Chen,Tian Ye,Haozhe Liu,Enze Xie,Song Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A real-time, high-res video-to-video editing system (SANA-Streaming) using a hybrid diffusion transformer, cycle-reverse regularization, and system co-design to achieve 1280x704 at 24 FPS on RTX 5090, with the DiT core at 58 FPS, outperforming SOTA in temporal coherence and throughput.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time V2V editing demands stringent temporal consistency and high inference throughput on consumer GPUs. Existing methods struggle to maintain coherence under streaming constraints and may not fully exploit hardware capabilities.

Method: (1) Hybrid Diffusion Transformer: integrates softmax attention in select blocks to enhance local modeling while keeping the efficiency of linear layers. (2) Cycle-Reverse Regularization: training strategy that enforces semantic consistency by predicting source frames from generated content via flow matching, without needing paired long edited videos. (3) Efficient System Co-design: fused GDN kernels and Mixed-Precision Quantization (MPQ) optimized for NVIDIA Blackwell (RTX 5090); profiling-based MPQ to maximize Tensor Core utilization without degrading quality.

Result: System achieves real-time 1280×704 resolution editing at 24 end-to-end FPS on a single RTX 5090, with the DiT core running at 58 FPS. Experimental results show the co-design approach significantly outperforms existing SOTA methods in temporal coherence and system throughput.

Conclusion: The proposed co-design yields substantial gains in temporal coherence and throughput for real-time V2V streaming editing on consumer GPUs, demonstrating the practicality of end-to-end streaming editing with a jointly optimized algorithm-system stack.

Abstract: Real-time streaming video-to-video editing (V2V) is critical for interactive applications such as live broadcasting and gaming, yet it remains a formidable challenge due to the stringent requirements for temporal consistency and inference throughput. In this paper, we present SANA-Streaming, a system-algorithm co-designed framework for high-resolution, real-time streaming video editing on consumer GPUs, with the following three core designs: (1) Hybrid Diffusion Transformer architecture introduces softmax attention in part of the blocks to improve local modeling capabilities while preserving the efficiency of linear layers. (2) Cycle-Reverse Regularization is a novel training strategy that enforces semantic consistency by predicting source frames from generated content via flow matching, improving temporal consistency without requiring paired long edited videos. (3) Efficient System Co-design combines fused GDN kernels and Mixed-Precision Quantization (MPQ) optimized for the NVIDIA Blackwell (RTX 5090) architecture. By profiling real-world throughput, our MPQ maximizes Tensor Core utilization while maintaining generation quality. The resulting system achieves real-time 1280 x 704 resolution editing at 24 end-to-end FPS on a single RTX 5090 GPU, with the DiT core running at 58 FPS. Experimental results demonstrate that our co-design approach significantly outperforms existing SOTA methods in both temporal coherence and system throughput.

</details>


### [2] [Mitigating Content Shift and Hallucination in GenAI Image Editing via Structural Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30437)
*Luxi Zhao,Michael S. Brown*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A post-processing framework for structure-preserving GenAI image retouching that fuses GenAI-enhanced outputs with the input to retain perceptual improvements while maintaining pixel-level fidelity, addressing spatial misalignment, texture distortion, and content hallucination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GenAI image editors provide appealing retouching via prompts but often introduce spatial misalignment, texture distortion, and hallucinated content, which harms downstream tasks requiring pixel-level structural fidelity.

Method: Establish coarse spatial and photometric correspondences between the original input and the GenAI-enhanced image, then perform a fusion stage that transfers desired enhancements while suppressing hallucinations to preserve structure and resolution.

Result: Experimental evaluation against representative methods from photorealistic style transfer and image fusion indicates improved preservation of aesthetic quality along with better pixel-level structural consistency at the original input resolution.

Conclusion: The proposed post-processing fusion framework effectively combines GenAI refinements with the input’s structure, enabling high-quality retouched outputs that maintain fidelity for downstream workflows.

Abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) image editors, such as Nano Banana, produce visually compelling results for retouching tasks, enabling non-experts to edit images through text prompts alone. However, the generative nature of these models often introduces spatial misalignment, texture distortion, and content hallucination, all of which are detrimental to downstream workflows that require pixel-level fidelity. We identify a problem setting we call "structure-preserving GenAI fusion" for black-box GenAI image retouching: retain the perceptual enhancements of a GenAI output while enforcing structural faithfulness to the original input image. To address this problem, we propose a post-processing framework that fuses an input image with its GenAI-enhanced counterpart by first establishing coarse spatial and photometric correspondences, then performing a fusion stage that transfers desired enhancements while suppressing hallucinated content. In the absence of direct prior work in this setting, we evaluate our framework against representative methods from photorealistic style transfer and image fusion. Our experiments demonstrate that our method better preserves aesthetic quality while maintaining pixel-level structural consistency and the input resolution.

</details>


### [3] [Seeing Isn't Knowing: Do VLMs Know When Not to Answer Spatial Questions (and Why)?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30557)
*Yue Zhang,Zun Wang,Han Lin,Yonatan Bitton,Idan Szpektor,Mohit Bansal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SpatialUncertain to test VLM spatial reasoning under occlusion and perspective ambiguity; reveals overconfidence and poor viewpoint-identification, advocating abstention-aware evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual observations are incomplete in the real world; existing benchmarks assume reliable observations. The work seeks to assess whether models know when to abstain and how to obtain additional evidence for spatial reasoning.

Method: Construct SpatialUncertain with two observation challenges (occlusion and perspective ambiguity). Design spatial questions that are answerable under clean observations but require abstention under challenges. Evaluate whether models can identify which additional viewpoints would resolve perspective ambiguity. Test across frontier open- and closed-source VLMs.

Result: Models show low accuracy under occlusion (~30%) and perspective ambiguity (<10%). Some models still perform near random in identifying which extra views would provide reliable evidence, even when such views are available.

Conclusion: There is a need to move beyond answer correctness to evaluating whether models know when to abstain and how to seek reliable evidence; SpatialUncertain provides a framework to assess abstention and evidence-seeking in VLMs.

Abstract: Spatial reasoning is a fundamental capability for vision-language models (VLMs) deployed in real-world environments. However, visual observations are inherently limited representations of a 3D world: occlusion can render objects invisible, and perspective can make geometric properties misleading. Despite this, existing spatial reasoning benchmarks typically assume that observations are sufficient and reliable, focusing on whether models produce correct answers rather than whether they recognize when a question cannot be answered and what additional observations would be needed. In this work, we challenge this assumption by constructing a controlled evaluation framework, SpatialUncertain, and introducing two types of observation challenges: (1) occlusion, which hides target information, and (2) perspective ambiguity, which produces misleading visual cues. For each configuration, we design spatial questions that are answerable under clean observations but require abstention under the introduced challenges. We further evaluate whether models can identify which additional viewpoints would resolve perspective ambiguity. Our results across a diverse set of frontier open- and closed-source VLMs reveal two consistent failure modes. First, models are prone to overconfident answering, attempting to solve spatial reasoning tasks even when visual evidence is incomplete or misleading, with average accuracy around 30\% under occlusion and below 10\% under perspective ambiguity. Second, even when additional views are available, some models perform near random chance in identifying which would provide reliable evidence. Together, our findings call for moving beyond answer correctness toward evaluating whether models know when to abstain and how to seek reliable evidence.

</details>


### [4] [VLM3: Vision Language Models Are Native 3D Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30561)
*Zhipeng Cai,Zhuang Liu,Yunyang Xiong,Zechun Liu,Vikas Chandra,Yangyang Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A simple, scalable VLM-based approach (VLM3) enables standard vision-language models to master 3D tasks with three key enablers—focal length unification, text-based pixel references, and data mixture/scaling—without architecture changes or heavy losses; it achieves competitive depth estimation (0.84 to 0.9) and supports pixel correspondence, pose estimation, and 3D object understanding, matching expert models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D understanding relies on specialized vision models with task-specific designs; aim to democratize 3D learning by leveraging native 3D capabilities of VLMs via simple, scalable strategies.

Method: Identify three enabling factors: focal length unification, text-based pixel reference, and data mixture and scaling; implement on standard VLMs without architectural changes, heavy augmentations, or complex loss functions; train with text-based supervision; produce VLM3.

Result: Significant improvement in depth estimation (0.84 → 0.9) and capability across 3D tasks (pixel correspondence, camera pose estimation, object-level understanding), matching expert-model accuracy while preserving standard architectures and textual training.

Conclusion: Proposes a new paradigm: simple, scalable 3D learning via VLMs, reducing dependence on bespoke architectures and complex losses, enabling broad 3D capabilities with standard VLMs.

Abstract: Vision Language Models (VLMs) enable a unified model to solve various vision tasks through prompting. They have shown promising performance in semantic understanding. However, 3D understanding still largely relies on expert vision models with complex task-specific designs. The key argument this work wants to make is that VLMs are native 3D learners. Our in-depth large scale study shows that 1) focal length unification, 2) text-based pixel reference and 3) data mixture and scaling, are all you need for effective 3D learning. Model architecture changes, large models, heavy data augmentations, and complex losses including the regression formulation, many of which form the foundation of expert vision models, are actually not necessary conditions. As a result, we propose VLM3, a scalable method with the simplest design that enables standard VLMs to master diverse 3D tasks. VLM3 not only advances the VLM depth estimation accuracy by a large margin (0.84 -> 0.9), but also enables diverse 3D tasks such as pixel correspondence, camera pose estimation and object-level 3D understanding, matching expert vision model accuracy while maintaining standard architectures and text-based training. We believe VLM3 opens up a new paradigm for simple and scalable 3D learning.

</details>


### [5] [Prior Availability in Industrial Visual Sim-to-Real: A Review of CAD-Guided and CAD-Unavailable Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30581)
*Chenxi Tao,Seung-Kyum Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A taxonomy reframes industrial visual sim-to-real as a domain-gap problem based on prior availability (CAD-available, CAD-unavailable, boundary-prior); links CAD-based detection/pose with industrial anomaly and surface-inspection literatures; empirical anchors show that CAD render count alone does not ensure transfer, with source distribution, detector capacity, and calibration often more influential; CAD at test-time adds verification channels (mask/pose/depth), while CAD-unavailable approaches rely on calibrated normality and feature deviations; argues against a single cross-task leaderboard and for deployment-grounded priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial deployment involves mismatches beyond synthetic-to-real (sensors, lighting, materials, calibration, production variation, rare defects). A taxonomy structured by prior availability clarifies transfer challenges and unifies CAD-based and anomaly-detection literatures.

Method: Proposes a taxonomy of domain gaps conditioned on prior availability; anchors the taxonomy with empirical references to T-LESS/BOP, MVTec AD, and VisA; analyzes how different priors influence transfer, verification channels, and cross-task connections.

Result: The framework shows that CAD render count is not the sole determinant of transfer; design choices like source distribution, detector capacity, and small real calibration can dominate; CAD at test-time yields additional verification channels (mask, pose, depth); CAD-unavailable inspection relies on calibrated normality and feature deviation; rejects a single cross-task leaderboard in favor of deployment-grounded priors.

Conclusion: Advocates focusing on deployment priors rather than universal cross-task performance; emphasizes bridging CAD-based detection/pose estimation with industrial anomaly/surface-inspection literature under a unified domain-gap taxonomy.

Abstract: Industrial visual sim-to-real is often described as transferring from synthetic images to real images, but industrial deployment usually involves a broader mismatch between available evidence and required decisions. A system may be built from CAD renderings, simulated RGB-D observations, normal reference images, synthetic defects, pretrained feature spaces, or language prompts, yet deployed under different sensors, lighting, materials, fixtures, calibration, production variation, and rare defect modes. This review reframes industrial visual sim-to-real as a domain-gap problem organized by prior availability. We distinguish CAD-available settings, where explicit object geometry can support rendering, calibration, pose estimation, segmentation, and test-time geometric verification; CAD-unavailable settings, where geometry is replaced by normal-reference appearance, feature distributions, teacher-student residuals, synthetic anomaly assumptions, foundation features, or vision-language priors; and boundary-prior settings, where approximate models, templates, reference views, or semantic correspondences preserve only part of the CAD role. This framing connects CAD-based detection and 6D pose-estimation literature with industrial anomaly and surface-inspection literature that is usually reviewed separately. To make the taxonomy concrete, we use empirical anchors on T-LESS/BOP, MVTec AD, and VisA. The anchors show that CAD render count alone does not close transfer; source-distribution design, detector capacity, and small real calibration can matter more. They also show that CAD at test time creates a distinct verification channel through mask, pose, and depth consistency, whereas CAD-unavailable inspection relies on calibrated normality and feature deviation. The review therefore argues against a single cross-task leaderboard and instead asks what prior grounds the deployment decision.

</details>


### [6] [ReGuLaR: Relation-Grounded Latent Reasoning for Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30587)
*Zihu Wang,Karthik Somayaji N. S,Peng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A relation-grounded latent reasoning framework (ReGuLaR) for LVLMs that grounds latent states in object-level evidence via a training-time ReGFormer and a new dataset RGROUNDING-351K, achieving SOTA performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete textual chain-of-thought lacks alignment with continuous visual evidence; there is a need to ground latent reasoning in the compositional and relational structure of visual data.

Method: Introduces ReGuLaR with a training-time ReGFormer that focuses latent reasoning on question-relevant objects and inter-object relations; inference-time reasoning occurs without ReGFormer; builds RGROUNDING-351K dataset annotated with object bounding boxes and inter-object relations.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks show that ReGuLaR consistently outperforms existing approaches and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: Code will be released upon acceptance; the approach demonstrates effective grounding of latent reasoning in visual evidence and the dataset supports training; results suggest robustness across benchmarks.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has significantly improved the reasoning ability of large vision-language models (LVLMs) by verbalizing intermediate reasoning steps in natural language. However, such discrete textual rationales are often insufficient for encoding continuous visual evidence. Recent work addresses this limitation by moving reasoning into continuous latent space. Despite promising progress, existing methods leave latent reasoning insufficiently connected to the compositional and relational structure of visual evidence. To address this gap, we introduce ReGuLaR, a relation grounded latent reasoning framework that explicitly grounds latent states in these critical yet overlooked visual evidence. ReGuLaR uses a training-time ReGFormer to focus latent reasoning on question-relevant objects and inter-object relations, while at inference time the model reasons and generates answers without invoking the ReGFormer. To support training ReGuLaR, we construct RGROUNDING-351K, a real-world vision-language dataset annotated with key object bounding boxes and inter-object relations. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks show that ReGuLaR consistently outperforms existing approaches and achieves state-of-the-art performance. We include our code in the submission and will release the code and training data publicly upon acceptance.

</details>


### [7] [Crafter: A Multi-Agent Harness for Editable Scientific Figure Generation from Diverse Inputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30611)
*Haozhe Zhao,Shuzheng Si,Zhenhailong Wang,Zheng Wang,Liang Chen,Xiaotong Li,Zhixiang Liang,Maosong Sun,Minjia Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Crafter, a general-purpose, multi-agent harness for figure generation across types and input conditions, and CraftEditor for turning raster outputs into editable SVGs; accompanied by CraftBench, a benchmark for three figure types and four input conditions. Empirical results show Crafter outperforms single-type generators and baselines, while CraftEditor yields editable SVGs surpassing baselines; code and benchmark released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific figures are central to communication but difficult to produce; existing automation is narrow (single figure type, text-only input) and cannot produce editable results, creating a need for a generalizable, editable figure-generation pipeline.

Method: Propose Crafter as a multi-agent harness that generalizes across figure types and input conditions without changing architecture; propose CraftEditor that converts raster outputs into editable SVGs using the same pattern; introduce CraftBench benchmark with three figure types and four input conditions with human quality annotations; provide implementation and evaluation on PaperBanana-Bench and CraftBench.

Result: Crafter substantially outperforms standalone generators and an agentic baseline on PaperBanana-Bench and CraftBench; ablations show each component contributes; CraftEditor converts outputs into editable SVGs that surpass all baselines.

Conclusion: The approach delivers a generalizable, editable figure-generation pipeline with a publicly released codebase and benchmark, enabling broader, higher-quality automated figure production across diverse figure types.

Abstract: Scientific figures are among the most effective means of communicating complex research ideas, yet producing publication-quality illustrations remains one of the most labor-intensive parts of paper preparation. Existing automated systems each target a single figure type under text-only input, leaving the diversity of types and conditions researchers actually use unaddressed; their raster outputs further cannot be locally revised. Because scientific figures are structured compositions of discrete semantic components, the localized errors generators produce on such layouts demand not a stronger backbone but a harness. We instantiate this harness in two complementary systems: Crafter, a multi-agent harness for figure generation that generalizes across figure types and input conditions without architectural changes, and CraftEditor, which applies the same pattern to convert raster outputs into editable SVGs. Moreover, we introduce CraftBench, a benchmark spanning three figure types and four input conditions with human quality annotation. Experiments show that Crafter substantially outperforms both standalone generators and the agentic baseline on PaperBanana-Bench and CraftBench, with ablations confirming each component's independent contribution; CraftEditor faithfully converts outputs into editable SVGs that surpass all baselines. Our code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/HaozheZhao/Crafter.

</details>


### [8] [Controllable Lung Nodule Synthesis via Histogram-Regularized Latent Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30631)
*Arunkumar Kannan,Yanbo Zhang,Han Liu,Michael Baumgartner,Jianing Wang,Alexander Hertel,Bogdan Georgescu,Sasa Grbic*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Protoype of a controllable latent diffusion model for 3D CT pulmonary nodules that uses HU histogram-based regularization and conditioning on nodule subtype, mask, and histogram to better capture lesion intensity distributions and improve data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity and limited diversity in annotated pulmonary nodules; existing conditional diffusion methods rely on voxel-wise/spatial losses that yield over-smoothed textures and poor attenuation distribution modeling for nodule subtypes (solid, part-solid, ground-glass).

Method: A controllable latent diffusion model that synthesizes nodules within full 3D CT volumes. It uses subtype, spatial mask, and HU histogram conditioning, coupled with a differentiable feature-space histogram regularization term to constrain voxel intensity distributions during generation.

Result: Demonstrates strong visual realism via quantitative metrics and a visual Turing test. When used for data augmentation, the generated nodules improve downstream clinical task performance, especially for underrepresented nodule subtypes, and may benefit subtype-informed malignancy classification.

Conclusion: Histogram-based regularization plus multi-conditional control in a 3D diffusion framework yields more realistic, subtype-consistent nodules and offers tangible gains for data augmentation and potentially malignancy-related tasks.

Abstract: While automated diagnosis systems have achieved remarkable success in computed tomography (CT)-based lung cancer screening, their development remains limited by the scarcity of diverse, annotated pulmonary nodule datasets. Diffusion-based generative models offer a promising strategy for data synthesis; however, many existing conditional approaches primarily optimize spatial reconstruction losses, which encourage voxel-wise similarity but may inadequately constrain lesion-level intensity distributions. As a result, these methods may produce over-smoothed texture profiles and underrepresent the distinct attenuation characteristics of different nodule subtypes, including solid, part-solid, and ground-glass nodules. To address this challenge, we propose a controllable latent diffusion model that synthesizes pulmonary nodules within full 3D CT volumes while accurately modeling nodule-specific intensity distributions. Specifically, rather than relying solely on spatial losses, we introduce a histogram-based regularization term that constrains voxel intensity distributions during the generative process. The model combines subtype, spatial mask, and Hounsfield unit (HU) histogram conditioning with the differentiable feature-space histogram regularization term to better align lesion-level intensity distributions, improving the visual plausibility and subtype consistency of synthesized nodules. Extensive experiments on lung CT data demonstrate that our framework achieves strong visual realism, validated through both quantitative metrics and a visual Turing test. Furthermore, when used for data augmentation, the generated nodules improve performance in downstream clinical tasks, particularly for underrepresented nodule subtypes, and show a potential benefit for subtype-informed malignancy classification.

</details>


### [9] [PInVerify: An Offline Embodied Benchmark for Active Instance Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30639)
*Yuhang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Active Instance Verification (AIV) and the PInVerify benchmark for embodied agents to verify fine-grained object descriptions via active multi-view inspection; reports that open-source multimodal LLMs with LoRA on-device outperform embedding baselines but NBV strategies offer limited gains; provides baseline pipelines and code release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging the gap between locating a target vicinity and confirming the exact object instance, especially for subtle attributes requiring close inspection in embodied environments.

Method: Formalize AIV as a finite-horizon decision process. Build PInVerify with 3,000 evaluation episodes across 18 object categories, using a 6-sector navigation topology that includes trap and unreachable views. Develop training-free and LoRA-finetuned end-to-end baselines around open-source multimodal LLMs (≤8B params) using attribute decomposition, a visibility-weighted multi-view tracker, and three NBV strategies.

Result: MLLM-based baseline outperforms the best embedding baseline by 4.9 percentage points; GT-box ablations show a +3.1 pp detection gap; NBV strategies did not yield reliable gains in this setting. A LoRA-finetuned agent (SFT+GSPO) achieves 85.6% performance. The work suggests potential but also limitations for active viewpoint planning in AIV.

Conclusion: PInVerify provides a benchmark to advance active, fine-grained semantic verification in embodied AI and will support future research into better integration of multimodal LLMs and efficient NBV strategies; code is released.

Abstract: Embodied agents have made strong progress in navigating to target objects, but reaching the goal vicinity does not guarantee that the agent has found the correct instance: subtle attribute differences (e.g., "white floral" vs. "white striped") often require close-range, multi-view inspection. We address this gap with Active Instance Verification (AIV), a task in which an agent actively selects viewpoints around a candidate object to decide whether it matches a fine-grained natural-language description. We formalize AIV as a finite-horizon decision process and introduce PInVerify, an offline embodied benchmark for AIV: 3,000 evaluation episodes across 18 object categories, delivered as multi-view captures with a 6-sector navigation topology that exposes trap views (navigable but uninformative) and unreachable sectors. As reference baselines we build a training-free pipeline and a LoRA-fine-tuned end-to-end agent around open-source multimodal large language models (MLLMs) at on-device scale ($\leq$8B parameters), with attribute decomposition, a visibility-weighted multi-view tracker, and three next-best-view (NBV) strategies. In our evaluation across Qwen3-VL (4B/8B), SenseNova-SI-1.2-InternVL3-8B, CLIP, and SigLIP2, the best MLLM-based baseline exceeds the best embedding baseline by 4.9 pp; GT-box ablations show a +3.1 pp detection gap; and we do not observe reliable gains from active viewpoint selection within the tested NBV strategies. A LoRA-fine-tuned agent (SFT+GSPO) reaches 85.6%. PInVerify aims to support further work on active, fine-grained semantic verification in embodied AI. Code: https://github.com/Avalon-S/PInVerify.

</details>


### [10] [WristCompass: Kinematic Coupling as a Learnable Visual Concept for Ego-Camera Orientation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30671)
*Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj,Jiahang He,Anya Singh,Jai Relan,Cabrel Happi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Wrist-based kinematic coupling dynamics enable ego-camera orientation estimation under occlusions; a compact 4D feature with GRU achieves strong zero-shot transfer to Epic Kitchens, rivaling a large 1B-parameter model trained on full scene geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In egocentric manipulation, disentangling hand and camera motion is crucial for imitation learning. Geometry-based orientation inference fails under occlusion; need anatomy-rooted, temporally-aware cues that generalize across datasets.

Method: Define 4D inter-wrist features capturing kinematic relations along the arm-shoulder-head chain; train a GRU over short temporal windows to predict camera orientation. Use tabletop manipulation data for training; evaluate zero-shot on Epic Kitchens; compare to VGGT.

Result: On Epic Kitchens, median geodesic error 14.3 degrees; zero-shot transfer; achieves performance approaching 1B-parameter scene model with only ~200k GRU parameters; outperforms constant predictor on TACO.

Conclusion: Kinematic coupling dynamics provide a compact, temporal, and anatomy-grounded cue for ego-motion estimation that is robust to occlusions and transfers across datasets without scene-appearance cues.

Abstract: Recovering ego-camera orientation from manipulation video is a prerequisite for disentangling hand motion from camera motion, a key step in imitation learning from egocentric demonstrations. The obvious approach, inferring orientation from scene geometry, fails when hands occlude the frame: VGGT, a 1B-parameter scene reconstruction model, scores worse than a constant predictor on the TACO benchmark. We identify an alternative visual concept that is present precisely when scene geometry is absent: kinematic coupling dynamics, the structured physical relationship between wrist motion and camera orientation imposed by the arm-shoulder-head chain. We find that this concept is compact (4D inter-wrist features outperform 126D full hand keypoints), temporal (requiring a GRU over short windows rather than per-frame retrieval), and physically grounded (transferring zero-shot across datasets because it is rooted in anatomy rather than scene appearance). Trained only on tabletop manipulation, WristCompass transfers zero-shot to Epic Kitchens cooking video, achieving 14.3$^\circ$ median geodesic error and approaching the performance of a 1B-parameter scene model at 200K GRU parameters.

</details>


### [11] [ConTrans: Learning Text-enhanced Local-global Temporal Representations for Zero-shot Temporal Action Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30689)
*Kanchan Keisham,Thenukan Pathmanathan,Thangarajah Akilan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces ConTrans, a local-global multi-scale encoder for zero-shot Temporal Action Localization; fuses convolutional inductive biases with Transformer self-attention to capture local and global dependencies, achieving state-of-the-art on ActivityNet-1.3 and THUMOS14.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two limitations in ZS-TAL: (1) neglect of relative-offset-based local frame correlations, and (2) limited feature representation power due to shallow architectures; aim to enhance representations via integrated local-global modeling.

Method: Proposes a multi-scale encoder architecture called ConTrans with a local-global multi-scale feature representation module that blends convolutional inductive biases with Transformer self-attention to jointly model fine-grained local dependencies and long-range context.

Result: Empirical evaluations on ActivityNet-1.3 and THUMOS14 show that ConTrans significantly outperforms existing methods, setting a new benchmark for ZS-TAL.

Conclusion: ConTrans demonstrates the effectiveness of integrating convolutional inductive biases with self-attention to enrich feature representations for zero-shot TAL, addressing both local and global information capture.

Abstract: Zero-shot Temporal Action Localization (ZS-TAL) aims to detect and locate previously unseen actions in untrimmed videos. However, existing approaches primarily focus on modeling long-range contextual information, often neglecting the critical relative-offset-based local correlations between video frames. Furthermore, their performance is hindered by limited feature representation capabilities due to the shallow nature of their network architectures. In this paper, we address these limitations by introducing a novel local-global multi-scale feature representation module. We propose a novel multi-scale encoder architecture, termed ConTrans, that integrates convolutional (Conv) inductive biases with transformer Self-attention to jointly capture fine-grained local dependencies and long-range global context, leading to more comprehensive feature representations than existing methods. Experimental evaluations on the ActivityNet-1.3 and THUMOS14 datasets demonstrate that ConTrans significantly outperforms existing methods, establishing a new benchmark for ZS-TAL.

</details>


### [12] [Seeing Before Agreeing: Aligning Multi-Agent Consensus with Visual Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30698)
*Yuhan Wang,Shuochen Chang,Yalin Feng,Dongsheng Ma,Yuanzi Li,Zhengren Wang,Yinglong Yang,Yufei Chen,Yikang Wang,Shaoxu Sun,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Align visual evidence among multiple VLM agents for robust, training-free VQA via evidence-aligned grounding (EAGLE).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on text-centric protocols and the inadequacy of answer-level agreement by introducing aligned visual evidence to reduce hallucinations and improve trustworthy consensus in multimodal VQA.

Method: A training-free framework that exposes each agent's grounding regions as visual evidence, enables mutual verification over the evidence, and uses evidence consistency to guide final decisions.

Result: Demonstrates best average performance across six VQA benchmarks, while remaining lightweight and interpretable, enabling practical deployment.

Conclusion: Evidence-aligned grounded multi-agent reasoning is essential for trustworthy VQA; EAGLE provides a practical, interpretable framework that improves reliability and cross-domain performance.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved strong performance on visual question answering (VQA). To mitigate individual hallucinations and blind spots, aggregating diverse perspectives via multi-agent collaboration has emerged as a promising paradigm. While this approach has shown great success in textual QA, its potential in the multimodal domain remains under-explored. Existing multi-agent VQA methods predominantly adapt text-centric protocols, focusing on textual discussions while ignoring the alignment of visual information. In this work, we reveal a key insight: answer-level agreement is insufficient for reliable multi-agent VQA; \textit{aligned visual evidence} -- shared support from the image regions agents rely on -- is essential for trustworthy consensus. To leverage this insight, we propose EAGLE (\textbf{E}vidence-\textbf{A}ligned \textbf{G}rounded mu\textbf{L}ti-agent r\textbf{E}asoning), a training-free evidence-centered framework for coordinating multiple VLM agents. EAGLE explicitly exposes each agent's grounding regions as visual evidence, enables mutual verification over the evidence, and uses evidence consistency to guide final decision-making. Experiments on six VQA benchmarks show that EAGLE achieves best average performance across domains while remaining lightweight, interpretable, and practical for deployment.

</details>


### [13] [Mathematical Morphology in Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30700)
*Erick Oliveira Rodrigues,Aura Conci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Morphology-inspired clustering and a Minkowski-Chebyshev hybrid distance are proposed, enabling shape/density-aware ML with fast dilation; results show competitive k-NN performance on 33 UCI datasets and introduction of morphological classifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage mathematical morphology to capture structural properties (shape, density, fractality) that conventional ML often overlooks, aiming to improve clustering quality and distance-based learning.

Method: Morphological reconstruction-based clustering preserving cluster shapes and density with intrinsic maximal clusters and noise removal; a novel Minkowski-Chebyshev distance for efficient dilations; implementation in Z^2 for fast neighborhood iterations; evaluation using k-NN across 33 UCI datasets; introduction of morphological classifiers modeling shape, density, and fractal information.

Result: Clustering preserves shapes/density; dilation is fast (≈1.3× Manhattan; ≈329.5× Euclidean). The proposed distance yields above-average accuracy in 26/33 datasets and best overall accuracy in 9; 14 baseline distances compared.

Conclusion: Morphology-based methods offer a unique framework to model shape, density, and fractal information, potentially improving clustering and distance-based classification over standard ML approaches.

Abstract: This work introduces mathematical morphology-an established visual computing theory-into machine learning to exploit shape and density aspects often overlooked by standard techniques. We propose a fast clustering algorithm based on morphological reconstruction that accurately preserves cluster shapes and density. This scheme offers unique features: an intrinsic sense of maximal clusters, cost-free noise removal, and diverse growth patterns controlled by structuring elements.Additionally, we propose a novel distance metric combining Minkowski and Chebyshev distances, highly efficient for morphological dilations. In $Z^2$ discrete neighbourhood iterations, it is roughly 1.3 times faster than Manhattan and 329.5 times faster than Euclidean distances. When evaluated using a k-Nearest Neighbours (k-NN) classifier across 33 UCI datasets against 14 other distances, our metric achieved above-average accuracies most frequently (26 of 33 cases) and the best overall accuracy in 9 cases.Finally, we introduce novel morphological classifiers. Unlike current literature, this proposal uniquely models shape, density, and fractal information in datasets.

</details>


### [14] [Equivariant Latent Alignment via Flow Matching under Group Symmetries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30705)
*Sunghyun Kim,Jaehoon Hahm,Jeongwoo Shin,Joonseok Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We introduce Residual Latent Flow to fix latent misalignment in equivariant latent spaces for geometry-aware vision tasks, improving novel view synthesis under SO(n).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent misalignment undermines equivariant relations in latent spaces; existing methods struggle to enforce exact group actions, harming geometric consistency and generalization.

Method: A flow-based Residual Latent Flow that corrects latent codes to better satisfy the group equivariance, aligning latent transformations with SO(n) symmetries.

Result: Experiments show reduced latent misalignment and improved novel view synthesis quality, demonstrating the method's effectiveness.

Conclusion: Residual Latent Flow offers a practical remedy for latent misalignment in equivariant representations, enhancing fidelity and consistency in geometry-aware generation and view synthesis for rotation groups.

Abstract: Geometry-aware generative models and novel view synthesis approaches have shown strong potential in visual fidelity and consistency. In parallel, equivariant representation learning has emerged as a powerful framework for constructing latent spaces where analytically known group transformations could act directly, capturing geometric structure in data and enhancing both interpretability and generalization in novel view synthesis. However, we identify that existing approaches often suffer from latent misalignment, a discrepancy between the intended group action and the actually required transformations in the latent space. Consequently, the learned latents often fail to consistently preserve the equivariant relations imposed by the underlying group symmetry. To address this, we propose Residual Latent Flow, a flow-based framework that corrects the misaligned latents, thereby improving compliance with the underlying equivariance relation. Our comprehensive experiments show that our method significantly reduces latent misalignment and improves novel view synthesis quality, under rotation groups SO(n).

</details>


### [15] [Vision-Based Localization in Dense Urban Environments: A Case Study of an Urban Village in China](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30714)
*Menglin Wu,Rui Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A vision-based geo-localization framework for dense urban villages using a low-cost dual-camera data pipeline to create a specialized 360° panoramas dataset (Shipai Village, Guangzhou) and evaluate existing models, highlighting potential and limitations for navigation, delivery, and emergency management in GPS-poor areas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense urban villages exhibit unreliable GPS and incomplete maps, hindering mobility and emergency response. A robust, low-cost localization solution tailored to these environments can support vulnerable migrant populations.

Method: Develop a practical data collection pipeline with a panoramic camera and smartphone camera to capture synchronized 360° panoramas and query images. Build a specialized image geo-localization dataset from Shipai Village. Compare existing localization models across scene types to identify strengths and weaknesses.

Result: Demonstrates the feasibility of visual-based localization in dense urban-village environments, revealing both its potential and current limitations across different scenes. Provides empirical guidance on model selection and dataset needs for such settings.

Conclusion: The proposed framework can enhance pedestrian navigation, last-mile delivery, and emergency management in GPS-denied areas, contributing to safer and more accessible mobility for residents of informal settlements.

Abstract: Urban villages, the widespread informal settlements which have emerged as a result of rapid urbanization, are now major residential hubs for migrant workers in large cities in China. The dense arrangement of buildings in these areas often leads to unreliable GPS signals, while incomplete mapping data further impairs accurate route planning and navigation. These issues not only hinder everyday mobility but also pose significant challenges for emergency response, as confusing road layouts and GPS inaccuracies can complicate evacuation efforts. To address these challenges, we propose a practical vision-based geo-localization solution tailored for dense urban environments. Our approach features a low-cost data collection pipeline utilizing a dual-camera system, comprising a panoramic camera and a smartphone camera, to capture synchronized 360-degree panoramas and query images. Using Shipai Village, a well-known densely populated urban village in Guangzhou, as a case study, we develop a specialized image geo-localization dataset. We then assess and compare the performance of existing models across various scene types to identify their strengths and weaknesses. The findings demonstrate both the potential and limitations of visual-based localization in dense urban-village environments. Our framework aims to enhance pedestrian navigation, last-mile delivery, and emergency management in areas with poor GPS coverage, ultimately supporting the vulnerable populations living within these informal settlements.

</details>


### [16] [Simple Token-Efficient Vision-Language Model for Case-level Pathology Synoptic Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30716)
*Zhiyuan Yang,Jiahao Cheng,Vincent Quoc-Huy Trinh,Mahdi S. Hosseini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Token-efficient vision–language model for case-level synoptic pathology report generation from WSIs using a frozen patch encoder, a lightweight 2-layer MLP aligner, and an LLM decoder. Two-stage training (aligner-only WSI captioning; case-level fine-tuning) with 512×512 patches at 5× magnification and a WSI separator token to handle multi-WSI cases. Demonstrates strong ROUGE-L/METEOR/BLEU-4 scores with memory-efficient training (half an H100) and ablations showing robustness and efficiency as a practical baseline for multi-WSI VLM in pathology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pathology reports from gigapixel WSIs are challenging due to massive input size, long visual-token sequences, and the need for case-level reasoning across multiple slides with heterogeneous tissues. A memory-efficient, scalable approach is needed to enable practical generation of structured pathology reports.

Method: Three-component architecture: (1) frozen pathology patch encoder; (2) a lightweight two-layer MLP vision-language aligner; (3) a large language model (LLM) decoder. A WSI marker token separates slides within a case. Training in two stages: (A) aligner-only WSI captioning using heterogeneous WSI-text pairs; (B) case-level supervised fine-tuning on case-report pairs for structured report generation. Patch-level representation uses 512×512 patches at 5× magnification to reduce sequence length. Efficient training techniques enable full training on roughly half a NVIDIA H100 GPU.

Result: Achieves high ROUGE-L, METEOR, and BLEU-4 scores across both training stages. AI-based evaluations show the model is consistently preferred over strong baselines. Extensive ablations quantify performance-efficiency trade-offs and identify simple choices that boost robustness in multi-WSI settings. Claims a strong, reproducible baseline for efficient pathology report generation suitable for low-compute environments.

Conclusion: Proposes a practical baseline for efficient, multi-WSI pathology report generation using a token-efficient VLM with a WSI separator token and two-stage training, enabling robust case-level report generation under constrained compute and facilitating further research in multi-WSI vision-language modeling for pathology.

Abstract: Generating clinically useful pathology reports for pathology cases from whole-slide images (WSIs) is challenging due to gigapixel resolution, long visual-token sequences, and the complexity of case-level reasoning, where a single case may contain multiple WSIs with heterogeneous tissues and ambiguous findings. We present a simple token-efficient vision--language model for case-level synoptic report generation that remains practical under constrained GPU memory. Our architecture follows a minimal three-component design: a frozen pathology patch encoder, a lightweight two-layer MLP vision-language aligner, and a large language model decoder, with an explicit WSI marker token to separate slides within a case. Training proceeds in two supervised stages: (1) aligner-only WSI captioning using heterogeneous WSI-text pairs, and (2) case-level supervised fine-tuning on case-report pairs for structured report generation. To reduce sequence length, we represent each slide using $512 \times 512$ patches at $5\times$ magnification, which reduces the average sequence length by up to $64\times$ times compared to the commonly used $20\times$ patches. Combined with efficient training techniques, we enable practical training with only half a NVIDIA H100 GPU. Across both training stages, our approach achieves high ROUGE-L/METEOR/BLEU-4 scores while being substantially more efficient in memory and runtime. In AI-based evaluations, our model is consistently preferred over strong baselines. Extensive ablations characterize performance-efficiency trade-offs and identify simple choices that improve robustness in multi-WSI settings. Overall, this work provides a strong, reproducible baseline for efficient pathology report generation, lowering the barrier to multi-WSI VLM research under limited compute.

</details>


### [17] [Annotations Are Not All You Need: A Cross-modal Knowledge Transfer Network for Unsupervised Temporal Sentence Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30742)
*Xiang Fang,Daizong Liu,Wanlong Fang,Pan Zhou,Yu Cheng,Keke Tang,Kai Zou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised temporal sentence grounding (TSG) is approached by transferring cross-modal knowledge from cheap labels (Image-Noun, Video-Verb) into TSG, enabling effective video-query grounding without segment annotations during training; achieved via appearance and action knowledge transfer with a copy-paste refinement, yielding strong results on ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on costly video-query paired annotations for TSG by leveraging easily obtainable cross-modal labels to supply appearance and action cues, enabling practical unsupervised grounding.

Method: 1) Extract entity-aware appearance knowledge from Image-Noun tasks and adapt it to each video frame. 2) Extract event-aware action representations from Video-Verb tasks and refine via a copy-paste approach to emulate real-world variability. 3) Modulate and transfer both appearance and action knowledge to the unsupervised TSG setting to correlate videos and queries without explicit training on video-query pairs.

Result: On two challenging datasets (ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA), the proposed method outperforms existing unsupervised methods and competitively matches supervised approaches.

Conclusion: Cross-modal knowledge transfer from cheap labels enables effective unsupervised TSG, offering a practical path to reduce annotation costs while maintaining competitive performance.

Abstract: This paper addresses the task of temporal sentence grounding (TSG). Although many respectable works have made decent achievements in this important topic, they severely rely on massive expensive video-query paired annotations, which require a tremendous amount of human effort to collect in real-world applications. To this end, in this paper, we target a more practical but challenging TSG setting: unsupervised temporal sentence grounding, where both paired video-query and segment boundary annotations are unavailable during the network training. Considering that some other cross-modal tasks provide many easily available yet cheap labels, we tend to collect and transfer their simple cross-modal alignment knowledge into our complex scenarios: 1) We first explore the entity-aware object-guided appearance knowledge from the paired Image-Noun task, and adapt them into each independent video frame; 2) Then, we extract the event-aware action representation from the paired Video-Verb task, and further refine the action representation into more practical but complicated real-world cases by a newly proposed copy-paste approach; 3) By modulating and transferring both appearance and action knowledge into our challenging unsupervised task, our model can directly utilize this general knowledge to correlate videos and queries, and accurately retrieve the relevant segment without training. Extensive experiments on two challenging datasets (ActivityNet Captions and Charades-STA) show our effectiveness, outperforming existing unsupervised methods and even competitively beating supervised works.

</details>


### [18] [Immuno-VLM: Immunizing Large Vision-Language Models via Generative Semantic Antibodies for Open-World Trustworthiness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30745)
*Xiang Fang,Wanlong Fang,Wei Ji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Immuno-VLM introduces an immunology-inspired negative selection mechanism in high-dimensional latent spaces, where LLM-generated Semantic Antibodies describe near-distribution outliers to bound known-class decision regions, achieving state-of-the-art open-world robustness on ImageNet-1K and OOD benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the vulnerability of large vision-language models in open-world settings, termed the "Hubris of Semantics", where unknown anomalies are forced into known categories due to lack of explicit negative knowledge.

Method: Propose Immuno-VLM, a bio-inspired framework that adapts Immunological Negative Selection to latent spaces. Unlike passive density-based open-set methods or pixel-space outlier generation, it uses generative reasoning of Large Language Models to hallucinate textual descriptions of near-distribution outliers (Semantic Antibodies) to bound the decision space of known classes.

Result: Extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K and four challenging OOD benchmarks show that Immuno-VLM achieves new state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: Immuno-VLM provides a robust open-world recognition framework by integrating biologically inspired negative selection with LLM-generated semantic outliers, enhancing trustworthiness of vision-language models in open-world deployment.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models have achieved unprecedented success in zero-shot recognition by aligning visual features with broad semantic concepts. However, this semantic abstraction creates a critical vulnerability in open-world deployment: the ``Hubris of Semantics'', where models force-fit unknown anomalies into known categories with high confidence due to the lack of explicit negative knowledge. To address this \textit{Open-World Trustworthiness Paradox}, we propose \textbf{Immuno-VLM}, a bio-inspired framework that adapts the biological principle of \textbf{Immunological Negative Selection} to high-dimensional latent spaces. Departing from traditional Open-Set Recognition methods that rely on passive density estimation or inefficient pixel-space outlier generation, Immuno-VLM leverages the generative reasoning of Large Language Models to actively hallucinate ``Semantic Antibodies'', textual descriptions of near-distribution outliers (e.g., look-alikes, contextual anomalies) that effectively bound the decision space of known classes.Extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K and four challenging OOD benchmarks reveal that Immuno-VLM establishes a new state-of-the-art.

</details>


### [19] [SLAP: The Semantic Least Action Principle for Variational Video-Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30750)
*Xiang Fang,Wanlong Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Semantic Least Action Principle (SLAP) to interpolate long-horizon video trajectories using variational mechanics on a Riemannian manifold, solving a boundary value problem with discrete Euler-Lagrange equations to enforce object persistence without pixel-level rendering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address temporal gaps and semantic drift in LVLMs caused by sparse frame sampling and unreliable generative extrapolation; seek principled, semantically consistent long-horizon interpolation.

Method: Model latent video trajectory as a path on a Riemannian manifold governed by a Semantic Lagrangian; formulate interpolation as a Boundary Value Problem; solve with discrete Euler-Lagrange equations; emphasizes semantic persistence over pixel-level rendering.

Result: Extensive experiments indicate SLAP improves temporal coherence and object persistence over baseline generation methods; demonstrates effectiveness in maintaining semantic consistency.

Conclusion: Variational mechanics with a semantic least-action framework provides a principled, non-rendering approach to long-horizon video interpolation, linking classical mechanics to semantic dynamics and offering robustness for LVLM-guided tasks.

Abstract: In the era of Large Video-Language Models (LVLMs), the computational necessity of sparse frame sampling creates a fundamental ``temporal gap'', rendering models blind to critical causal transitions. Existing solutions relying on generative hallucination (e.g., latent diffusion) or autoregressive extrapolation often fail to maintain semantic consistency over long horizons, suffering from object vanishing and energetic instability. We propose a paradigm shift from probabilistic generation to variational mechanics with the \textbf{Semantic Least Action Principle (SLAP)}. Drawing a rigorous isomorphism between classical mechanics and semantic dynamics, we model the latent video trajectory as a path on a Riemannian manifold governed by a Semantic Lagrangian. By formulating the interpolation task as a Boundary Value Problem (BVP) solved via the discrete Euler-Lagrange equations, SLAP naturally enforces object persistence without pixel-level rendering. Extensive experiments show the effectiveness of our proposed SLAP.

</details>


### [20] [DisPlace: Discriminative Place Projections for Multi-Reference Visual Place Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30769)
*Dhyey Manish Rajani,Michael Milford,Tobias Fischer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DisPlace introduces a multi-reference VPR fusion method by solving a generalized eigenvalue problem that maximizes between-place separability while suppressing within-place variation across reference traversals, yielding a compact, discriminative descriptor and improved robustness with lower storage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VPR faces severe appearance and viewpoint changes; conventional multi-reference fusion either aggregates references uniformly or selects heuristically, failing to separate place-identity information from condition/viewpoint-induced variations. A fusion method that exploits cross-reference variation can identify stable descriptor subspaces.

Method: Formulate descriptor fusion as a generalized eigenvalue problem that maximizes between-place separability and suppresses within-place variation across multiple reference traversals. This identifies linear combinations of descriptor dimensions that preserve place identity while discarding condition/viewpoint-specific variation, producing a single compact descriptor per place.

Result: Evaluated on Oxford RobotCar, Nordland, Pittsburgh30k, and Google Landmarks v2 across six state-of-the-art VPR descriptors. DisPlace outperforms seven multi-reference baselines in 49 of 54 appearance-varying conditions; consistently improves descriptor-level fusion under viewpoint and unstructured settings; requires less storage during inference than all compared fusion methods.

Conclusion: DisPlace effectively leverages cross-reference variation to learn robust, compact place representations, offering improved robustness to appearance changes with reduced storage and broad applicability across multiple VPR descriptors.

Abstract: A key challenge in Visual Place Recognition (VPR) is matching query images against reference maps captured under diverse environmental conditions and viewpoints. While multiple reference traversals improve robustness, existing fusion strategies either aggregate references uniformly or rely on heuristic selection, without distinguishing descriptor variations that preserve stable place identity from those caused by changing conditions or viewpoints. In this paper, we propose DisPlace, a multi-reference VPR framework that fuses multiple reference descriptors into a single compact and discriminative place representation. DisPlace formulates descriptor fusion as a generalized eigenvalue problem that maximizes between-place separability while suppressing within-place variation across references, rather than preserving overall descriptor variance. Unlike existing multi-reference fusion methods, DisPlace exploits variation across reference traversals to identify which linear combinations of descriptor dimensions preserve place identity and which capture condition- or viewpoint-specific variation. We evaluate DisPlace on Oxford RobotCar, Nordland, Pittsburgh30k, and Google Landmarks v2 across six state-of-the-art VPR descriptors. DisPlace outperforms seven multi-reference baselines in 49 out of 54 appearance-varying conditions, consistently improves descriptor-level fusion performance under viewpoint and unstructured settings, and requires less storage during inference than all compared fusion methods.

</details>


### [21] [CameraNoise: Enabling Faithful Camera Control in Video Diffusion through Geometry-Flow-Guided Noise Warping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30774)
*Haoyu Zhao,Jiaxi Gu,Haoran Chen,Qingping Zheng,Yeying Jin,Hongyi Yang,Junqi Cheng,Yuang Zhang,Zenghui Lu,Huan Yu,Jie Jiang,Peng Shu,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A flow-to-noise warping approach (CameraNoise) embeds camera motion into diffusion noise space to preserve geometric consistency and trajectory fidelity in video diffusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directly injecting numerical camera parameters into diffusion backbones causes a gap between abstract coordinates and visual content, leading to structural distortions; a temporally coherent representation that decouples motion from appearance is needed.

Method: Introduce Geometry-guided Reprojection Flow and a noise warping algorithm that encode camera motion into the noise space, preserve the Gaussian prior of diffusion, and ensure consistent noise propagation under camera transformations; integrate CameraNoise into the diffusion process to decouple motion from appearance while maintaining trajectory dynamics.

Result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements in visual quality and trajectory faithfulness over prior methods; project page and code are provided for reproducibility.

Conclusion: CameraNoise enables stable, high-fidelity video generation by embedding camera motion into the diffusion noise space, preserving geometry and trajectory dynamics while maintaining diffusion priors.

Abstract: Precise camera pose control is critical for video diffusion, yet maintaining geometric consistency remains a challenge. Existing methods that directly inject numerical camera parameters into the diffusion backbone often fail to bridge the gap between abstract coordinates and visual content, leading to structural distortions. To address this issue, we propose CameraNoise, a flow-to-noise warping method that encodes camera motion into a temporally coherent stochastic representation. Unlike conventional conditioning, CameraNoise embeds camera poses directly into the noise space. This decouples motion from scene appearance while faithfully preserving trajectory dynamics. Specifically, we introduce a novel Geometry-guided Reprojection Flow and a noise warping algorithm, which jointly preserve the Gaussian prior of diffusion and ensure consistent noise propagation under camera transformations. By integrating CameraNoise into the diffusion process, our framework delivers stable, high-fidelity videos. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms prior methods in both visual quality and trajectory faithfulness. The project page and code are available at: https://gulucaptain.github.io/CameraNoise/.

</details>


### [22] [Text-guided Feature Disentanglement for Cross-modal Gait Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30784)
*Zhiyang Lu,Ming Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cross-modal gait recognition using text-guided disentanglement (TCFDNet) to bridge LiDAR and RGB modalities via GMTD, CLIP-based encoding, and feature stability modules, achieving state-of-the-art on SUSTech1K and FreeGait.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world gait analysis requires cross-modal robustness across 2D videos and 3D point clouds; substantial modality gap and data scarcity hamper performance; leveraging semantic priors from language models can guide disentanglement of modality-shared vs modality-specific features.

Method: Construct GMTD from LLMs; CLIP-based Multi-grained Feature Encoder aligns visual and textual features; TFDisentanglement selects top-k textual descriptors to reconstruct modality-specific and derive modality-shared representations via residual decomposition and orthogonality constraints; FSE module models spatial and channel correlations to enhance stability; cross-modal patch exchange to aid generalization.

Result: Demonstrates new state-of-the-art performance on SUSTech1K and FreeGait datasets, validating proposed modules.

Conclusion: Text-guided, disentangled cross-modal representation learning effectively closes the LiDAR-RGB gap for gait recognition, with improved robustness and generalization.

Abstract: Gait recognition is a biometric technique that identifies individuals based on their walking patterns, offering advantages in long-range, non-intrusive scenarios. However, real-world scenarios often involve heterogeneous sensing modalities such as LiDAR and RGB cameras, making LiDAR-Camera Cross-modal Gait recognition (LCCGR) a critical yet challenging task due to the substantial modality gap between 2D videos and 3D point cloud sequences. To address this challenge, we propose TCFDNet, a Text-guided Cross-modal Feature Disentanglement Network, which leverages modality-aware textual priors as semantic anchors to guide the learning of disentangled modality-shared representations. Specifically, we construct a Gait Modality Text Dictionary (GMTD) using large language models to generate rich semantic descriptions of gait across modalities and viewpoints. A CLIP-based Multi-grained Feature Encoder then aligns visual and textual features within a unified vision-language space. Furthermore, the Text-guided Feature Disentanglement (TFD) module selects the topk matched textual descriptions to reconstruct modality-specific representations and derive modality-shared features via residual decomposition and orthogonality constraints. To mitigate the fragility of the disentangled shared features, we propose a Feature Stability Enhancement (FSE) module, which models spatial and channel-wise correlations to improve feature robustness. In addition, a cross-modal patch exchange strategy is introduced to further improve generalization. Extensive experiments on SUSTech1K and FreeGait datasets demonstrate that TCFDNet achieves new state-of-the-art results and validate the effectiveness of the proposed modules.

</details>


### [23] [MechVQA: Benchmarking and Enhancing Multimodal LLMs on Comprehensive Mechanical Drawing Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30794)
*Qian Kou,Xiaofeng Shi,Yulin Li,Xiaosong Qiu,Xinyang Wang,Hua Zhou,Cao Dongxing*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces MechVQA dataset and MechVL model for mechanical drawing understanding; achieves improved performance on VQA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanical drawings pose unique challenges for multimodal LLMs due to high-density annotations, limited domain knowledge, and stringent spatial constraints; a domain-specific benchmark and model are needed.

Method: Semi-automated construction of a high-density mechanical drawings QA dataset (MechVQA: 3.3k images, 21k QA across 10 tasks and three capability levels) and a multi-stage training paradigm to develop MechVL, a domain-specialized baseline.

Result: MechVL outperforms the strongest closed-source baseline by 7.57 percentage points on MechVQA total score, demonstrating improved mechanical drawing understanding.

Conclusion: The dataset and model provide a reusable foundation for deploying MLLMs in mechanical design and inspection, enabling better performance on real-world mechanical drawings.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated significant achievements in general visual question answering (VQA) tasks. However, they remain brittle on mechanical engineering drawings, where high annotation density and weak domain knowledge, compounded by unreliable spatial relation reasoning under strict projection rules and geometric constraints, make decisive cues easy to miss and frequently lead to wrong answers. To bridge this gap, we introduce the first comprehensive mechanical drawing understanding dataset, MechVQA, created through a semi-automated construction and quality-control pipeline. MechVQA contains 3.3k high-density pictures with 21K question-answer pairs, spanning 10 different fine-grained tasks across three capability levels: Recognition, Reasoning, and Judging, providing a testbed to evaluate and improve MLLM understanding on real-world mechanical drawings. On top of MechVQA, we then develop the MechVL model through a multi-stage training paradigm, building a strong domain-specialized baseline. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that MechVL outperforms the strongest closed-source baseline by 7.57 percentage points on the MechVQA total score, significantly enhancing mechanical drawing understanding ability and providing a reusable foundation for deploying MLLMs in mechanical design and inspection scenarios.

</details>


### [24] [Function2Scene: 3D Indoor Scene Layout from Functional Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30819)
*Ruiqi Wang,Qimin Chen,Daniel Ritchie,Angel X. Chang,Manolis Savva,Kai Wang,Hao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Function2Scene: generate 3D indoor layouts from functional design briefs using a check-and-repair loop across geometry, LLM reasoning, and visual assessment; outperforms baselines in 30 cases with 94.3% preference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses misalignment between object-centric prompts and real design goals; emphasizes occupant use, activities, and needs.

Method: Parse occupant personas and activities from natural-language briefs, derive 17 functional criteria across spatial, ergonomic, activity, environmental dimensions; iteratively generate and refine layouts with a tool-augmented check-and-repair loop using geometric measurements, LLM reasoning, and VLM visual assessment.

Result: Outperforms recent LLM-based baselines in satisfying functional requirements; preferred in 94.3% of pairwise comparisons across 30 cases.

Conclusion: Shifts text-driven scene synthesis from object placement to designing spaces that actively support human use.

Abstract: Most text-driven 3D indoor scene synthesis methods generate rooms from object-centric prompts, asking what furniture should be placed rather than how the space is used. Yet in real interior design, a layout is judged by how well it supports its occupants, e.g., their activities and physical needs. We introduce Function2Scene, a framework for generating 3D indoor layouts from functional specifications, i.e., natural-language design briefs describing who will use a room and what they need to do there. Given such a specification, our system parses occupant personas and activities, derives a customized set of functional design constraints from a taxonomy of 17 criteria spanning spatial, ergonomic, activity, and environmental considerations, and uses these constraints to guide layout generation. Rather than relying on an LLM to directly produce a final scene, Function2Scene performs iterative evaluation and refinement through a tool-augmented check-and-repair loop, combining geometric measurements, LLM-based contextual reasoning, and VLM-based visual assessment. Experiments on 30 professionally written interior-design cases show that Function2Scene produces layouts that better satisfy functional requirements than recent LLM-based scene synthesis baselines, with our results preferred in 94.3% of pairwise comparisons. Our work reframes text-driven indoor scene synthesis from placing plausible objects to designing spaces that support human use.

</details>


### [25] [LegSegNet: A Public Deep Learning System for Lower Extremity CT Tissue Segmentation and Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30829)
*Yuwen Chen,Yaqian Chen,Roy Colglazier,Haoyu Dong,Hanxue Gu,Maciej A. Mazurowski,Kevin W. Southerland*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end LegSegNet for lower-extremity CT tissue segmentation and quantification achieving state-of-the-art Dice and enabling scalable body composition analysis; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for comprehensive, automated analysis of lower-extremity CT tissue for sarcopenia, body composition, and musculoskeletal monitoring; existing tools mostly provide masks and lack end-to-end quantification.

Method: Train LegSegNet on 1,302 annotated CT slices; segment bone, skeletal muscle, subcutaneous adipose tissue, inter/intramuscular adipose tissue; compute quantitative tissue measurements; evaluate on 900 held-out slices with radiologist review; compare against CNN-based, transformer-based, and finetuned foundation models; test on an external public dataset.

Result: Average Dice score of 89.31 on held-out test; best overall segmentation performance; first publicly available end-to-end system for lower-extremity CT tissue segmentation and quantification.

Conclusion: LegSegNet provides a practical evaluation tool and a foundation for future medical image analysis in this domain; code and weights released at GitHub.

Abstract: Lower extremity computed tomography (CT) contains clinically relevant information for body composition analysis, sarcopenia assessment, and musculoskeletal disease monitoring, but extracting these measurements at scale requires accurate tissue segmentation and an automated quantification workflow. Existing public segmentation tools are not designed for comprehensive lower extremity CT analysis, particularly for clinically important inter/intramuscular adipose tissue, and most public methods only provide mask prediction rather than an end-to-end quantification system. To address this problem, we present LegSegNet, a deep learning system for lower extremity CT tissue segmentation and body composition quantification. Given an input CT scan, LegSegNet segments bone, skeletal muscle, subcutaneous adipose tissue, and inter/intramuscular adipose tissue. It then computes quantitative tissue measurements for downstream analysis. We developed the segmentation model using 1,302 manually annotated CT slices and evaluated it on 900 held-out test slices, with all annotations reviewed by radiologists. We benchmark LegSegNet against a broad set of 2D segmentation methods, including CNN-based models, transformer-based models, and finetuned foundation models, and further evaluate its generalization on an external public CT dataset. LegSegNet achieves the best overall segmentation performance, with an average Dice score of 89.31 on the held-out test set. To our knowledge, LegSegNet is the first publicly available end-to-end system for lower extremity CT tissue segmentation and quantification, providing a practical evaluation tool for future computer vision research in medical image analysis. The code and model weights are available at: https://github.com/mazurowski-lab/LegSegNet

</details>


### [26] [Count Anything](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30846)
*Mengqi Lei,Shuokun Cheng,Wei Bao,Shaoyi Du,Jun-Hai Yong,Siqi Li,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A generalist, text-guided counting framework that operates across diverse domains using discrete instance points, built on the CLOC dataset, with dual counters (region-level sparse and pixel-level dense) and a parameter-free fusion mechanism to count targets aligned with natural-language queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no universal counting model; existing approaches are domain-specific and struggle to generalize across categories, domains, scales, and density distributions. A unified, interpretable counting framework guided by language can improve cross-domain generalization and localization.

Method: Introduce CLOC, a large-scale, cross-domain counting dataset spanning six visual domains (General Scene, Remote Sensing, Histopathology, Cellular Microscopy, Agriculture, Microbiology) with ~220k images, 619 categories, and 15M object instances. Propose Count Anything, a text-guided counting model that uses discrete instance points for counting rather than density maps. It features a Region-level Sparse Counter for large, sparse targets and a Pixel-level Dense Counter for small, crowded targets, both trained with a point-centric supervision strategy. Complementary Count Fusion combines the two counters in a parameter-free manner to yield the final count.

Result: Empirical results show strong accuracy and robust cross-domain generalization, outperforming existing open-world counting methods on the CLOC benchmark and potentially other datasets; code is released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: The work delivers a unified, generalizable approach to counting across diverse domains by integrating interpretable spatial localization with language guidance, enabled by a new large-scale dataset and a dual-counter counting mechanism that is fused without extra parameters.

Abstract: Object counting remains fragmented across domain-specific datasets and task formulations, despite rapid progress in generalist vision models. Existing counting models are often tailored to scenarios such as crowds, vehicles, cells, crops, or remote-sensing objects, and thus struggle to generalize across categories, visual domains, object scales, and density distributions. In this paper, we study text-guided object counting across domains, where a model takes an image and a natural-language query as input and returns an instance-grounded set of target points whose cardinality gives the count. This formulation unifies category-conditioned counting with interpretable spatial localization. To support this setting, we construct CLOC, a Cross-domain Large-scale Object Counting dataset that reorganizes diverse public data sources into a unified benchmark. CLOC covers six visual domains: General Scene, Remote Sensing, Histopathology, Cellular Microscopy, Agriculture, and Microbiology, with about 220K images, 619 categories, and 15M object instances. Based on CLOC, we propose Count Anything, a generalist model for text-guided object counting.
