Motivation: To provide explicit, editable geometry in SLAM suitable for traditional rendering pipelines, simulation, and collision/ editing tasks, addressing the lack of online, differentiable, triangle-based maps and limitations of offline or voxel/point-based approaches.

Method: Introduce Triangle Splatting as the map representation in a dense RGB-D SLAM system. Perform online differentiable rendering of a triangle soup for tracking and mapping. Convert the resulting triangle soup to a connected mesh on the fly using restricted Delaunay triangulation to enable online mesh deformation and collision checking.

Result: On Replica and TUM-RGBD benchmarks, the system improves 3D geometry quality compared to baselines, matches camera-tracking accuracy, and enables online mesh-based scene editing.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of an online triangle-based differentiable SLAM approach, bridging traditional mesh manipulation workflows with modern differentiable rendering for SLAM.

Abstract: We present a dense RGB-D SLAM system using differentiable triangles as the 3D map representation. While 3D Gaussian Splatting has emerged as the leading method for novel-view synthesis, triangles remain the standard primitive for traditional rendering hardware, game engines, and downstream tasks requiring explicit geometry such as simulation, collision, and editing. Recent offline methods have demonstrated that an unstructured 'triangle soup' can be optimised into a photorealistic mesh via Delaunay triangulation across a set of posed images. Building upon this insight, we present the first dense SLAM system to employ Triangle Splatting to perform both tracking and mapping through online differentiable rendering of a triangle soup. The map can be converted into a connected mesh on-the-fly via restricted Delaunay triangulation, enabling new online capabilities such as mesh deformation and collision checking. On Replica and TUM-RGBD, our system outperforms baselines on 3D geometry, matches the camera-tracking accuracy, and enables online mesh-based scene editing.

</details>


### [53] [Does Visual Information Play a Decisive Role in Vision-Language-Action Model Driving Behavior?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31041)
*Jingtao He,Hongliang Lu,Xiaoyun Qiu,Yixuan Wang,Xinhu Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A structured visual perturbation framework is proposed to quantify visual-behavior dependency in VLA-based driving, revealing evaluation-dependent and uneven visual grounding across abstraction levels, and advocating for principled design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA driving evaluations focus on aggregate metrics and lack diagnostics to quantify how visual input drives behavior; a structured, multi-level perturbation framework is needed to understand visual-behavior coupling and safety/robustness.

Method: Introduce a three-dimensional perturbation scheme: (1) channel-level degradation, (2) information-level disruption, and (3) structure-level modification. Apply these perturbations to VLA-based driving systems and assess responses in both open-loop trajectory prediction and closed-loop interactive safety evaluation.

Result: Experiments show that visual-behavior dependency is evaluation-dependent and that visual grounding is uneven across abstraction levels.

Conclusion: Structured analyses are necessary to understand how visual information shapes behavior; this will inform principled design of safer, more robust VLA driving models.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated promising capability in autonomous driving, highlighting the potential of unified multimodal architectures for jointly modeling perception and planning. However, how current VLA-based driving behavior is grounded in visual information remains poorly understood. Existing evaluation protocols mainly focus on aggregate performance metrics, lacking structured and practical diagnostics to quantify visual-behavior dependency. In this work, we introduce a structured multi-level visual perturbation framework to analyze visual-behavior dependency in VLA-based driving models systematically. The framework organizes controlled visual perturbations along three complementary dimensions: channellevel degradation, information-level disruption, and structurelevel modification. We apply it to VLA-based driving systems and evaluate behavioral responses under both open-loop trajectory prediction and interactive closed-loop safety evaluation. Experimental results reveal evaluation-dependent dependency patterns and uneven visual grounding across abstraction levels. These findings call for more structured analyses and principled design of VLA driving models to better understand how visual information shapes behavior and develop safer, more robust systems.

</details>


### [54] [Rethinking Efficient Crack Segmentation with Task-Aligned Structural-Directional Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31048)
*Shipeng Liu,Liang Zhao,Dengfeng Chen,Weihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RIFT introduces a morphology-aligned, lightweight crack segmentation family that treats crack segmentation as sparse structural recovery, outperforming complex generic architectures on four benchmarks with two variants: RIFT-B (top accuracy) and RIFT-T (high efficiency).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cracks exhibit thin, sparse, and morphologically regular structures with weak semantic cues; existing methods rely on heavier, generic backbones that may compromise structural preservation and directional continuity.

Method: Propose a compact, task-aligned model family (RIFT) that preserves local evidence, aggregates directional continuity, and reconstructs crack structure through lightweight multi-scale fusion, avoiding heavy generic architectures.

Result: On four public benchmarks, RIFT achieves best or tied-best performance across 16 metrics relative to reproduced baselines; RIFT-B yields the strongest overall accuracy; RIFT-T delivers high deployment efficiency with 0.47M parameters and fast inference; topology-aware evaluation, ablations, transfer experiments, and visualizations support the inductive bias and effectiveness.

Conclusion: Task-aligned simplicity can match or surpass complex hybrid architectures for crack segmentation when the inductive bias fits crack morphology, as demonstrated by RIFT across multiple metrics and efficient variants.

Abstract: Recent crack segmentation methods often follow generic semantic segmentation designs, using stronger backbones, hybrid CNN-Transformer-Mamba encoders, and auxiliary enhancement branches. Although effective, this raises whether stronger generic feature mixing is the most suitable direction for crack segmentation. We instead formulate crack segmentation as sparse structural recovery. Cracks have limited category-level semantics but strong morphological regularities, being thin, sparse, anisotropic, locally fragmented, and easily confused with textures or shadows. Thus, the key bottleneck lies in preserving weak structural evidence, recovering directional continuity, and suppressing background coupling. We propose RIFT, a compact family of morphology-aligned crack segmentation models. Rather than compressing a complex generic architecture, RIFT is simple by design, preserving local evidence, aggregating cooperative directional continuity, and restoring crack structures through lightweight multi-scale fusion. Experiments on four public benchmarks show that RIFT achieves the best or tied-best results across the 16 main metrics against reproduced representative baselines. RIFT-B gives the strongest overall accuracy, while RIFT-T provides the best deployment efficiency with only 0.47M parameters and high inference speed. Topology-aware evaluation, ablations, transfer experiments, and visualizations further verify that task-aligned simplicity can match or surpass complex hybrid architectures when its inductive bias fits crack morphology. Code: https://github.com/xauat-liushipeng/RIFT

</details>


### [55] [LVSA: Training-Free Sparse Attention for Long Video Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31057)
*Gael Glorian,Ioannis Lamprou,Zhen Zhang,Yujie Yuan,Hongsheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Long Video Sparse Attention (LVSA) is a training-free, model-agnostic block-sparse attention for video diffusion transformers that reduces compute by up to ~3x and mitigates long-horizon artifacts by combining a structured window with rotating global anchors, achieving faster generation with preserved or improved quality, validated across GPUs and NPUs using VQeval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense self-attention in long-video diffusion has quadratic compute with sequence length and tends to produce near-static or repetitive outputs beyond training horizon; existing scalable solutions are either retraining heavy or fail to balance performance and quality; a training-free, scalable, hardware-agnostic method is needed.

Method: Introduce LVSA, a training-free, block-sparse attention combining a structured window pattern with rotating global anchors to remove fixed-grid bias; implemented with a FlashInfer kernel; evaluated across Wan and HunyuanVideo GPUs/NPUs; includes VQeval to measure loopy-video failures.

Result: Compute reductions: up to 3.17x (Wan 2.1 1.3B, 6x horizon), 2.98x (Wan 2.1 14B, 6x horizon), 3.33x (HunyuanVideo 1.5, 1.5x horizon); enables 2x-horizon generation on HunyuanVideo 1.5 on a single GPU; speedups vs RIFLEx (2.41x) and UltraViCo (3.27x) on Wan 2.1 1.3B; NPUs: 2.71x (Wan 2.2 A14B) and 3.24x (Wan 2.1 1.3B) vs dense attention; quality: VQeval shows LVSA is quality-neutral at training horizon and quality-positive at extended lengths.

Conclusion: LVSA offers training-free, cross-platform acceleration for long-video diffusion transformers, enabling longer horizons with maintained or improved quality; introduces VQeval for loopy-video assessment and demonstrates broad hardware applicability.

Abstract: Dense self-attention is the compute and quality bottleneck of long-video diffusion inference: cost grows quadratically with the sequence length, and beyond the training horizon the model converges to near-static output, that is, "frozen" repetitive video. State of the art approaches are either too costly, e.g., they require retraining, or fail to satisfy both performance and quality objectives in a scalable manner. To this end, we introduce Long Video Sparse Attention (LVSA), a training-free model-agnostic block-sparse attention for video diffusion transformers that combines a structured window pattern with rotating global anchors, thus removing the fixed-grid bias which causes long-range temporal artifacts. LVSA, combined with a FlashInfer kernel, reduces compute up to 3.17x on Wan 2.1 1.3B at a 6x horizon, 2.98x on Wan 2.1 14B at a 6x horizon, and 3.33x on HunyuanVideo 1.5 at a 1.5x horizon, compared to dense attention. Beyond reducing compute, LVSA enables HunyuanVideo 1.5 generation at a 2x horizon, which is otherwise out-of-memory on a single GPU. Moreover, LVSA provides speedups up to 2.41x compared to RIFLEx and 3.27x compared to UltraViCo on Wan 2.1 1.3B. To demonstrate applicability across diverse platforms, we apply LVSA on NPUs and achieve speedups up to 2.71x on Wan 2.2 A14B and 3.24x on Wan 2.1 1.3B compared to dense attention. To evaluate quality in a fair way, we introduce VQeval, a tool properly scoring loopy video failures, which instead are rewarded in state of the art evaluators like VBench-Long. LVSA is quality-neutral for generation at training horizon length and quality-positive at extended lengths.

</details>


### [56] [HQ-JEPA: Hybrid Quantum Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Cross-Modal Remote Sensing Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31068)
*Md Aminur Hossain,Ayush V. Patel,Sanjay K. Singh,Biplab Banerjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid quantum-classical joint-embedding predictive architecture (HQ-JEPA) for cross-modal remote sensing learning, extending JEPA to paired Sentinel-1/2 data with latent-space predictive objectives and quantum fidelity regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve remote-sensing representation learning across heterogeneous modalities (SAR and optical) by learning semantic latent representations via joint embedding, predictive self-supervision, and regularization, instead of pixel reconstruction.

Method: HQ-JEPA employs JEPA-style masked latent prediction with four objectives: latent token prediction, cross-modal token alignment, SIGReg-based Gaussian regularization in the fused latent space, and a differentiable SWAP-test-based Fidelity Quantum Similarity (FQS) loss. Learns representations in latent space and uses quantum state-overlap-based similarity as regularization.

Result: Pretrained encoder evaluated on GeoBench for classification and segmentation under linear probing and fine-tuning; HQ-JEPA achieves competitive or superior performance compared to strong self-supervised and remote-sensing foundation-model baselines, highlighting benefits of predictive self-supervision, cross-modal geometric regularization, and quantum fidelity-based representation learning.

Conclusion: Integrating predictive latent-space self-supervision, cross-modal geometric regularization, and quantum fidelity-based learning yields robust latent representations for cross-modal remote sensing tasks, with demonstrated gains on GeoBench benchmarks.

Abstract: We introduce HQ-JEPA, a hybrid quantum-classical joint-embedding predictive architecture for cross-modal remote sensing representation learning. The proposed framework extends JEPA-style masked latent prediction to paired Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 imagery by predicting masked target representations from visible context regions while aligning heterogeneous modality features in a shared embedding space. To improve representation quality, HQ-JEPA combines four complementary objectives: latent token prediction, cross-modal token alignment, SIGReg-based Gaussian regularization in the fused latent space, and a differentiable SWAP-test-based Fidelity Quantum Similarity (FQS) loss. Unlike pixel reconstruction methods, HQ-JEPA learns semantic representations directly in latent space and uses quantum state-overlap-based similarity as an additional regularization signal. We evaluate the pretrained encoder on GeoBench classification and segmentation tasks under linear probing and fine-tuning settings. Results show that HQ-JEPA achieves competitive and often superior performance over strong self-supervised and remote sensing foundation-model baselines, demonstrating the benefit of integrating predictive self-supervision, cross-modal geometric regularization, and quantum fidelity-based representation learning for remote sensing applications.

</details>


### [57] [Towards Effective Long-Video Event Prediction via Multi-Level Event Semantics Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31069)
*Bo Peng,YuanJie Lyu,PengGang Qin,Tong Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISTA introduces a three-stage, multi-level semantic mining framework for long-video event prediction: 1) character-centric visual prompts for detailed event-relevant visuals; 2) knowledge-enhanced iterative retrieval to guide LLMs in constructing coherent event chains; 3) a propose-then-retrieve strategy to generate diverse future-oriented predictions. Validated on real-world datasets with strong results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long videos encompass vast multimodal context and complex narratives. Existing LVLMs struggle with event prediction due to insufficient extraction of event-specific details and limited fine-grained narrative analysis. A framework that integrates multi-level semantics is needed to improve prediction accuracy in long-form video contexts.

Method: Stage 1: apply character-centric visual prompts to extract event-related details and enhance detail-level semantics. Stage 2: employ a knowledge-enhanced iterative retrieval strategy to guide the LLM in progressively constructing logically coherent event chains. Stage 3: adopt a human-like propose-then-retrieve approach to generate diverse future-oriented proposals and integrate multi-level clues for robust predictions.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets validate VISTA’s effectiveness for long-video event prediction, showing improved performance over baselines and demonstrating robustness across scenarios.

Conclusion: By integrating precise visual detail extraction, knowledge-guided iterative reasoning, and diverse proposal generation, VISTA provides a robust framework for long-video event prediction and offers a pathway for broader multi-level multimodal event understanding.

Abstract: Accurately predicting future events is fundamental to content understanding and decision-making across various domains. While prior research has primarily focused on text or short-video scenarios, long-video event prediction, characterized by vast multimodal context and more complex narratives, remains underexplored. Meanwhile, although recent Long-Video Language Models (LVLMs), built on Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), have shown promise in long-video question answering and summarization, they struggle to generalize to event prediction, as they can neither precisely extract event-related details nor perform fine-grained analysis of event development. To address this gap, we propose VISTA, a multi-level event semantics mining framework for long-video event prediction. Initially, VISTA applies a character-centric visual prompt to precisely extract event-related visual details, enhancing detail-level semantics; subsequently, it employs a knowledge-enhanced iterative retrieval strategy, guiding the LLM to progressively construct logically coherent event chains, thereby improving event-level narratives; ultimately, VISTA adopts a human-like propose-then-retrieve strategy to generate diverse future-oriented proposals and integrate multi-level clues, producing robust and accurate predictions. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets validate the effectiveness of VISTA for long-video event prediction.

</details>


### [58] [Task-Focused Memorization for Multimodal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31075)
*Tao Zou,Yichen He,Tian Qiu,Yuan Lin,Hang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TaskMem treats memorization as a learnable policy that focuses on task-relevant content in streaming multimodal environments, using a two-phase training (offline memo quality and online adaptation) to improve memory-driven VQA performance across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of selective memory in continuous, multimodal perception. Agents receive unbounded streams of observations, but only a subset should be retained for future tasks; existing memory modules lack a principled memorization policy.

Method: Two-phase framework: Phase 1 offline memorization optimization under fidelity constraints to ensure memory quality; Phase 2 online deployment where an adapter on the base MLLM is tuned using a reward model derived from recent tasks to guide the memorization policy toward task-relevant content. Evaluation reformulates VideoMME, EgoLife, and EgoTempo as streaming benchmarks; backbone is Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B.

Result: Memory-only VQA accuracy improved by 6.3%, 7.0%, and 5.3% on VideoMME, EgoLife, and EgoTempo benchmarks respectively, indicating that task-focused memorization yields consistent gains across diverse streaming tasks.

Conclusion: TaskMem demonstrates that learning what to memorize via RL-based policy learning and an online adapter-tuning stage can significantly enhance memory usefulness for downstream tasks in streaming multimodal agents, with robust gains across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Long-term memory is essential for multimodal agents to build coherent experience, accumulate world knowledge, and achieve continual learning. However, constructing effective memory goes beyond memory module design and basic requirements such as accuracy and fidelity; the key challenge lies in determining what to memorize. Multimodal agents, such as embodied agents, continuously perceive, reason, and act in real or virtual environments, receiving an unbounded stream of multimodal observations. From this combinatorial explosion of information, an agent must selectively retain content that is relevant to its role in the environment and valuable for future tasks. To bridge this gap, we frame memory generation as a learnable memorization policy and introduce TaskMem (Task-focused Memorization Policy Learning), a reinforcement-learning-based framework that enables the policy to dynamically adjust its focus to the demands of real tasks encountered in the environment. TaskMem adopts a two-phase training paradigm: Phase One learns how to memorize by optimizing memory quality under fundamental fidelity requirements; Phase Two occurs after deployment, where the agent learns what to memorize by tuning an adapter on its base MLLM, using recent environment tasks to define a reward model that guides the memorization policy toward task-relevant content. To evaluate our approach, we reformulate VideoMME, EgoLife, and EgoTempo into streaming benchmarks that simulate a realistic setting in which an agent processes streaming observations and handles tasks arriving online. To isolate memory assessment, the questions must be answered using only the agent's memory, without access to raw video. Built on Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B, TaskMem improves VQA accuracy by 6.3%, 7.0%, and 5.3% on these benchmarks, respectively.

</details>


### [59] [On Revisiting Entropy for Identifying Mislabeled Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31090)
*Chunlei Li,Zixuan Zheng,Yilei Shi,Guanglu Dong,Pengfei Li,Jingliang Hu,Xiao Xiang Zhu,Lichao Mou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SEI (signed entropy integral) detects mislabeled data by tracking per-sample prediction entropy across training; it captures both magnitude and trend, yielding state-of-the-art mislabeled data detection on four medical imaging datasets and integrates well with CLIP; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mislabeled examples seriously harm learning in deep networks, especially with overparameterization. A robust, scalable detector leveraging training dynamics is needed to identify and filter or relabel noisy samples.

Method: Track entropy of each sample’s predictions over training epochs, compute a signed entropy integral that combines current entropy magnitude with its temporal change, producing SEI scores. Apply broadly to classifiers; show notable effectiveness with CLIP architectures.

Result: SEI achieves state-of-the-art performance for mislabeled data identification on four medical imaging datasets, outperforming existing methods while remaining computationally efficient and easy to implement.

Conclusion: SEI is a general, effective approach for mislabeled data detection across architectures (including CLIP), with practical benefits for medical imaging; code is publicly available."

Abstract: Mislabeled samples in training datasets severely degrade the performance of deep networks, as overparameterized models tend to memorize erroneous labels. We address this challenge by proposing a novel approach for mislabeled data detection that leverages training dynamics. Our method is grounded in the key observation that correctly labeled samples exhibit consistent entropy decrease during training, while mislabeled samples maintain relatively high entropy throughout the training process. Building on this insight, we introduce a signed entropy integral (SEI) statistic that captures both the magnitude and temporal trend of prediction entropy across training epochs. SEI is broadly applicable to classification networks and demonstrates particular effectiveness when integrated with contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP) architectures. Through extensive experiments on four medical imaging datasets -- a domain particularly susceptible to labeling errors due to diagnostic complexity -- spanning diverse modalities and pathologies, we demonstrate that SEI achieves state-of-the-art performance in mislabeled data identification, outperforming existing methods while maintaining computational efficiency and implementation simplicity. Our code is available at https://github.com/MedAITech/SEI.

</details>


### [60] [Cross-Modal Clinical Knowledge Integration for Mammography Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31093)
*Jiayi Zhu,Fuxiang Huang,Yu Xie,Xi Wang,Zhixuan Chen,Yuan Guo,Qingcong Kong,Zhenhui Li,Qiong Luo,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MammoRG introduces a BI-RADS-guided, two-stage mammography report generation framework that leverages prior clinical knowledge and atomic clinical terms, plus a dedicated parsing tool (MammoRGTool) to evaluate clinical efficacy; it achieves state-of-the-art BI-RADS F1 gains across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in automated mammography report generation that relies on direct visual-to-text mappings without simulating real radiologists' structured clinical reasoning and BI-RADS workflow; aims to improve clinical consistency and usefulness of generated reports.

Method: Two-stage training: (1) stage-1 classification-based supervision incorporating prior knowledge from four-view mammograms; (2) stage-2 terminology-aware supervised fine-tuning treating mammography-specific terms as atomic semantic units. Additionally, MammoRGTool parses generated reports to extract structured clinical information for evaluation.

Result: MammoRG consistently outperforms existing methods on clinical efficacy metrics, notably BI-RADS F1 gains of 2.73%, 2.04%, 1.90%, and 3.27% on internal, external1, external2, and VinDr-Mammo datasets respectively.

Conclusion: Aligning report generation with BI-RADS-guided clinical reasoning and using terminology-aware decoding improves clinical consistency and efficacy; the accompanying MammoRGTool enables robust evaluation of structured clinical content.

Abstract: Breast cancer is a major global health concern, and mammography screening plays a central role in early detection. The large volume of screening examinations creates a substantial workload for radiologists, making accurate and consistent report generation a critical clinical challenge. Existing automated mammography report generation methods primarily focus on direct visual-to-text mapping, while overlooking the structured clinical reasoning process followed by radiologists in real-world practice. To address this limitation, we propose MammoRG, a mammography report generation framework that explicitly simulates the clinical reporting workflow by following the BI-RADS guideline and incorporating prior clinical knowledge to produce diagnostic reports. Specifically, MammoRG adopts a two-stage training framework. In the first stage, the model learns to integrate clinically relevant prior knowledge from a patient's four-view mammograms through classification-based supervision. In the second stage, a terminology-aware supervised fine-tuning strategy is introduced to model mammography-specific clinical terms as atomic semantic units, enabling the generation of high-quality reports with improved clinical consistency. To facilitate clinical efficacy evaluation of generated reports, we further develop MammoRGTool, a dedicated mammography report parsing tool that extracts structured clinical information from free-text reports. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MammoRG consistently outperforms existing methods across multiple clinical efficacy metrics, particularly in diagnosis-related BI-RADS F1, where it surpasses the second-best model by 2.73%, 2.04%, 1.90%, and 3.27% on the internal, external 1, external 2, and VinDr-Mammo datasets, respectively.

</details>


### [61] [Redefining Instance Matching: A Unified Framework for Part-Aware Matching in Panoptic Segmentation Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31094)
*Erik Großkopf,Soumya Snigdha Kundu,Hendrik Möller,Nicolas Münster,Mehdi Astaraki,Paula Tamara Buzduga,Kerstin Ritter,Benedikt Wiestler,Jan Kirschke,Jonathan Shapey,Tom Vercauteren,Florian Kofler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Generalizes Panoptic Quality evaluation by exploring four segment-matching strategies (One-to-One, Many-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-Many) via constrained bipartite assignment; shows PQ is compatible with the first three, while Many-to-Many lies outside PQ; introduces vertex-based TP/FN/FP accounting; extends to part-aware segmentation and biomedical data; provides open-source Panoptica-based tooling for analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PQ relies on a One-to-One matching at IoU>0.5, which becomes ambiguous and ill-defined at lower IoU thresholds. This work systematically maps the matching space, addresses fragmentation/adjacency/noise, and seeks a coherent framework that supports part-aware evaluation and reproducibility.

Method: Recast segment matching as a constrained bipartite assignment problem. Independently bound prediction- and ground-truth degrees yield four strategies: One-to-One, Many-to-One, One-to-Many, and Many-to-Many. Establish that Only the first three are well-defined within PQ; articulate a vertex-based accounting of TP, FN, FP anchored to segments rather than edges; extend framework to part-aware segmentation; apply to biomedical data; implement analysis and evaluation in an open-source Panoptica-based package with Voronoi-based region-wise analysis and AUC-Threshold computations.

Result: Demonstrates that PQ is compatible with One-to-One, Many-to-One, and One-to-Many matchings, but not with Many-to-Many. The framework clarifies how different thresholds and matching choices impact PQ-based evaluation. Provides a unified, configurable, open-source toolset (Panoptica) for part-aware evaluation and threshold-based analyses across configurable case studies.

Conclusion: The proposed framework clarifies the scope and flexibility of PQ, enabling robust, configurable evaluation across fragmentation, adjacency, and noise. It supports part-aware segmentation and reproducible benchmarking via an open-source Panoptica package.

Abstract: The Panoptic Quality (PQ) metric is the standard for jointly evaluating instance and semantic segmentation. However, its original definition relies on a One-to-One matching between predicted and ground truth segments, which is only straightforward when the IoU threshold exceeds 0.5. Below 0.5, multiple matching strategies emerge in a poorly explored problem space. We systematically elucidate this space by recasting segment matching as a constrained bipartite assignment problem. Independently bounding the prediction- and ground-truth-side degrees yields four matching strategies: One-to-One, Many-to-One, One-to-Many, and Many-to-Many. We show that the first three are well-defined within the PQ framework, while Many-to-Many falls outside it. These strategies become relevant when instances are fragmented, adjacent objects are difficult to delineate, or annotations are noisy. Central to our framework is a vertex-based accounting of TP, FN, and FP, anchored to ground truth and predicted segments rather than to matching edges. We further show that the framework extends naturally to part-aware panoptic segmentation, and we explore part-aware evaluation on biomedical data. Across configurable case studies we report how different combinations of thresholds and matching strategies behave in practice. We release a unified open-source package built on Panoptica. It exposes Voronoi-based region-wise analysis, part-aware evaluation, and Area Under Threshold Curve computations as configurable options.

</details>


### [62] [iVGR: Internalizing Visually Grounded Reasoning for MLLMs with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31096)
*Chang-Bin Zhang,Yujie Zhong,Qiang Zhang,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes iVGR, a reinforcement learning framework that internalizes visually grounded reasoning into textual CoT via dual-stream training with a consistency reward, removing the need for explicit object grounding during inference; achieves superior performance on fine-grained benchmarks and supports tool-assisted inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visually grounded CoT can degrade inference when explicit grounding (e.g., object boxes) is required; the model may benefit from internalizing localization into textual reasoning to improve efficiency and maintain performance.

Method: Introduce iVGR with dual-stream training: a textual reasoning stream learns from a high-quality visually grounded stream and is aligned via a proposed consistency reward, enabling accurate localization without explicit grounding during inference; reinforcement learning guides the textual stream to inherit visualization capabilities.

Result: Extensive experiments show significant performance gains over baselines on fine-grained benchmarks and preserve flexibility for tool-assisted inference workflows.

Conclusion: Internalizing visual localization into textual reasoning via RL (iVGR) is effective, reducing interference from explicit grounding during inference and enabling robust, flexible multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: While visually grounded Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has emerged as a promising paradigm to enhance fine-grained perception in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), its efficacy during the inference phase remains underexplored. In this work, we empirically find that mandating explicit object boxes in visually grounded CoT during inference often degrades performance compared to standard textual CoT, which reasons without explicit visual grounding. We hypothesize that the visual localization capability can be internalized into the textual CoT and that the mandatory explicit grounding introduces unnecessary interference with the model's primary objective of answer prediction. To address this problem, we propose Internalizing Visually Grounded Reasoning (\textbf{iVGR}), a novel reinforcement learning framework that transfers localization capabilities into the textual reasoning process. We employ a dual-stream training strategy, where a textual stream is aligned with a high-quality visually grounded stream via a proposed consistency reward, enabling the model to localize accurately without explicit grounding during inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baselines on fine-grained benchmarks, while maintaining the flexibility to support tool-assisted inference workflows.

</details>


### [63] [Remembering by Reconstructing: Domain Incremental Learning With Test-Time Training on Video Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31108)
*Jonathan Swinnen,Tinne Tuytelaars*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a domain incremental learning framework that embraces forgetting by using domain-specific LoRA adapters coupled with a self-supervised MAE head; online test-time training selects adapters to recall the current domain, suited for streaming data with gradual domain shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address non-stationary, streaming data where domain shifts occur gradually, the authors argue that forgetting between domains can be exploited rather than avoided, enabling rapid adaptation and recall of the active domain.

Method: Train a main task head and a self-supervised masked autoencoder (MAE) head. Learn domain-specific LoRA adapters during incremental updates; adapters specialize to particular domains, causing forgetting of other domains. At inference, perform online test-time training on the MAE head to identify which LoRAs best match the current input and thus recall the appropriate domain representation.

Result: Demonstrates the approach on domain-incremental action recognition and semantic segmentation, showing that online MAE-based adapter selection can adapt to domain shifts and enable recall of domain-specific performance in streaming data.

Conclusion: Forgetting across domains, when guided by domain-specific adapters and augmented with online test-time learning on a self-supervised head, can be leveraged to improve adaptation in domain-incremental, streaming settings, particularly where domain shifts are gradual.

Abstract: In this work we introduce a novel approach to domain incremental learning, adapting models over time to evolving, non-stationary data. In contrast to other works, we do not attempt to avoid catastrophic forgetting, but rather allow it and exploit it. Our model combines a main task head with a self-supervised masked autoencoder (MAE) head. We then learn domain-specific LoRA adapters during incremental training. Each adapter specializes to its domain, naturally inducing forgetting on other domains in both heads. At inference, we perform online test-time training on the self-supervised MAE head to identify which LoRAs best matches the current input, so the model can `remember' the domain again. Our scheme is especially well-suited to real-world streaming data, such as video, where consecutive samples are highly correlated and domain shifts are gradual. We demonstrate our method on domain-incremental action recognition and semantic segmentation tasks.

</details>


### [64] [Polyphony: Diffusion-based Dual-Hand Action Segmentation with Alternating Vision Transformer and Semantic Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31115)
*Hao Zheng,Hu Wang,Tiantian Zheng,Prajjwal Bhattarai,Tuka Alhanai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Polyphony is a three-stage framework for dual-hand action segmentation that uses an Alternating Dual-Hand Vision Transformer, Semantic Feature Conditioning, and Diffusion-Based Segmentation with cross-hand fusion and adaptive loss. It achieves state-of-the-art results on dual-hand datasets (HA-ViD, ATTACH) and on Breakfast with a unified backbone, claiming up to 16.8-point gains and strong efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unique challenges of dual-hand action segmentation—complex inter-hand dependencies, visual asymmetry, representation conflicts where the dominant hand dominates gradients, and semantic ambiguity in fine-grained actions—by designing a unified, stage-wise method that ensures balanced learning and semantically aware discrimination.

Method: Three-stage approach: (1) Alternating Dual-Hand Vision Transformer (ADH-ViT) that alternates training on left- and right-hand mini-batches while sharing a spatio-temporal encoder to balance gradients; (2) Semantic Feature Conditioning (SFC) that aligns visual features with structured, compositional action descriptions to differentiate semantically similar actions; (3) Diffusion-Based Segmentation with cross-hand feature fusion to capture inter-hand coordination and adaptive loss weighting to balance performance.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art on dual-hand benchmarks HA-ViD and ATTACH (up to 16.8-point gains). On Breakfast, achieves 82.5% with a single unified model and backbone, surpassing prior best that used a 12x larger backbone. The unified backbone also beats baselines that use separate per-hand models.

Conclusion: Polyphony demonstrates that a unified, stage-wise framework with alternating optimization, semantic conditioning, and diffusion-based segmentation can effectively address dual-hand action segmentation, offering strong performance and efficiency advantages over per-hand or larger backbones.

Abstract: Dual-hand action segmentation, densely predicting actions for both hands from untrimmed videos, is essential for understanding complex bimanual activities. However, it poses several unique challenges: complex inter-hand dependencies, visual asymmetry between hands, representation conflicts where the dominant hand monopolizes gradients, and semantic ambiguity in fine-grained actions. We propose Polyphony, a three-stage method to address these challenges through: (1) an Alternating Dual-Hand Vision Transformer that alternates training between left- and right-hand mini-batches to ensure balanced gradient contributions from both hands while sharing a spatio-temporal encoder; (2) Semantic Feature Conditioning that aligns visual features with structured, compositional action descriptions to enhance discrimination of semantically similar actions; and (3) Diffusion-Based Segmentation with cross-hand feature fusion for inter-hand coordination and adaptive loss weighting for balancing performance. Polyphony achieves state-of-the-art on both dual-hand datasets (HA-ViD, ATTACH) with improvements up to 16.8 points, and on the single-stream Breakfast dataset (82.5%), outperforming the prior best method that uses a 12x larger backbone. Notably, our unified model with a single shared backbone surpasses baselines requiring separate per-hand models. Code is at https://github.com/x-labs-xyz/Polyphony-Dual-hand-Action-Segmentation.

</details>


### [65] [QVGGT: Post-Training Quantized Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31124)
*Zhizhen Pan,Hesong Wang,Huan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QVGGT quantizes VGGT with per-block mixed precision, token filtering with camera compensation, and task-aware scale search, achieving near-lossless W4A16 with 3–4.9× memory reduction and up to 2.8× hardware speedup, enabling edge deployment of 3D perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome VGGT’s large 1.2B parameter footprint hindering deployment on resource-constrained platforms; design a quantization framework that preserves 3D prediction accuracy.

Method: Analyze per-block quantization sensitivity and apply selective mixed-precision where fragile transformer blocks get higher precision; implement token filtering to remove outliers (high-variance camera/register tokens) and compensate with a PCA-derived global token; perform task-aware scale search evaluating not only layer reconstruction but also multi-head supervision and cross-head geometric consistency across camera poses, depth maps, and point maps.

Result: Near-lossless quantization (W4A16) preserving all 3D heads; 3–4.9× memory reduction; up to 2.8× real-hardware speedup over FP32; validated on multiple geometry perception benchmarks.

Conclusion: Enables high-fidelity 3D perception on edge devices, enabling practical deployment of feed-forward 3D reconstruction models in constrained environments.

Abstract: Estimating 3D attributes directly from images has advanced rapidly with the Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT), which predicts camera parameters, depth maps, and point clouds in a single forward pass. However, its 1.2B-parameter scale severely limits deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as UAVs and mobile AR devices. To address this limitation, we introduce QVGGT, a tailored quantization framework designed to compress VGGT. Our approach starts from the observation that transformer blocks within VGGT exhibit heterogeneous sensitivity to quantization. We thus analyze per-block quantization sensitivity and propose a selective mixed-precision strategy that allocates higher precision to the most fragile transformer blocks. To address the amplification of quantization error caused by high-variance camera and register tokens, we further introduce token filtering with camera information compensation, which removes these outliers from activation calibration and restores their geometric cues using a PCA-derived global compensation token. Finally, we develop a task-aware scale search mechanism that evaluates candidate quantization scales not only through layer reconstruction but also through multi-head supervision and cross-head geometric consistency among camera poses, depth maps, and point maps. Extensive experiments on multiple geometry perception benchmarks demonstrate that QVGGT achieves near-lossless W4A16 quantization, preserving the accuracy of all 3D prediction heads while delivering 3$\sim$4.9$\times$ memory reduction and up to 2.8$\times$ real hardware speedup over FP32. Our approach makes high-fidelity 3D perception feasible on edge devices, enabling practical deployment of feed-forward 3D reconstruction models in real-world constrained environments.

</details>


### [66] [PolSAR Image Classification using a Hybrid Complex-Valued Network (HybridCVNet)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31137)
*Mohammed Q. Alkhatib*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HybridCVNet is a hybrid complex-valued CNN and CV Vision Transformer model for PolSAR classification that leverages 3D and 2D CV CNNs alongside transformer components to extract complementary features and dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the loss of phase information in real-valued networks for complex-valued PolSAR data and to exploit interdependencies through a hybrid CV architecture.

Method: A hybrid architecture that blends CV-CNNs with CV-ViT, efficiently combining 3D and 2D complex-valued CNN feature extractors with transformer-based components to capture both local and long-range dependencies in PolSAR imagery.

Result: On common PolSAR datasets, HybridCVNet achieves 97.39% overall accuracy on the Flevoland dataset and a Kappa of 0.972 on the San Francisco dataset with only 1% sampling; source code is available at the provided GitHub repo.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the viability and effectiveness of hybrid complex-valued architectures that integrate CV-CNNs and CV-ViTs for PolSAR classification, achieving strong performance and offering reproducibility through open-source code.

Abstract: Recently, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have become popular for image classification due to their effectiveness in computer vision tasks. Now, researchers are exploring the potential of vision transformers (ViTs) in remote sensing and Earth observation. However, traditional Real-Valued networks often overlook important phase information in Complex-Valued (CV) data like polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) data. To address this, new CV deep architectures have emerged. HybridCVNet, a novel hybrid network, blends CV-CNN and CV vision transformer (CV-ViT) techniques. It efficiently combines CV 3D and 2D CNNs as feature extractors, enhancing PolSAR image classification by extracting complementary information and effectively leveraging interdependencies within the data. Experimental results from widely-used PolSAR datasets show HybridCVNet outperforms other methods, achieving an overall accuracy of 97.39% on the Flevoland dataset and showing promise even with just a 1% sampling ratio, with a Kappa value of 0.972 on the San Francisco dataset. Source code is accessible through https://github.com/mqalkhatib/HybridCVNet

</details>


### [67] [FOCUS: Forcing In-Context Object Localization through Visual Support Constraints and Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31145)
*Mohammed Asad Karim,Vinay Kumar Verma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage, category-agnostic in-context localization framework that optimizes support–query attention and uses GRPO reinforcement learning to minimize localization error, achieving strong results with a 7B model that surpasses larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for category-agnostic, visually grounded localization from few support examples, avoiding reliance on semantic priors and explicit category supervision.

Method: Two-stage training to maximize in-context attention between support bounding boxes and query images without category labels; reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to directly minimize localization error; emphasizes visual correspondence over semantic priors; 7B parameter model.

Result: A 7B-parameter model trained with the proposed objectives outperforms models up to 72B parameters; comprehensive ablations validate each component.

Conclusion: Context-aware localization objectives can outperform scaling alone; the framework yields robust instance-level localization without category supervision.

Abstract: In-context localization (ICL) seeks to localize a target object specified by a small set of support examples in a query image, operating on the fly without training or parameter updates. Despite rapid advances in vision-language models (VLMs), achieving category-agnostic and visually grounded ICL remains an open problem, even though it is essential for applications such as image editing, personalized visual search, and retrieval. Existing methods are fragile and rely on explicit category supervision, which not only limits applicability in realistic settings with unnamed or instance-specific objects but also introduces category bias that steers predictions toward semantic priors rather than visual evidence. We introduce a two-stage training framework that explicitly optimizes in-context attention between support bounding boxes and query images without category supervision. We further refine localization via reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to directly minimize localization error. This formulation enforces visual correspondence over semantic priors, yielding robust instance-level localization. Empirically, a 7B-parameter model trained with our objectives outperforms models up to 72B parameters, demonstrating that context-aware localization objectives can surpass scaling alone. Comprehensive ablations validate the contribution of each component.

</details>


### [68] [SpatialAct: Probing Spatial Reasoning-to-Action Capabilities of VLM Agents in 3D Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31148)
*Tianhui Liu,Jie Feng,Zhiheng Zheng,Shengyuan Wang,Yiming Guo,Yanxin Xi,Hangyu Fan,Yong Li,Pan Hui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpatialAct introduces a simulator-grounded benchmark to study action-conditioned spatial reasoning in 3D, revealing a gap between spatial reasoning and action in VLMs, and showing humans outperform models in multi-turn feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to diagnose and quantify where current vision-language models fail to maintain coherent spatial beliefs and actionable plans in dynamic 3D environments, addressing the reasoning-to-action gap.

Method: propose SpatialAct benchmark with Multi-turn Interactive Refinement (starting point: challenging) and its decomposed Single-step Error Detection and Fix. Five fundamental spatial ability tasks. 3D scenes simulator-grounded evaluation; compare VLMs and humans; analyze failure modes.

Result: models perform well on isolated spatial reasoning tasks but struggle with maintaining coherent spatial state and producing reliable actions under multi-turn feedback; substantial gap to human performance; evidence that robust spatial state tracking under action-induced changes is lacking.

Conclusion: current VLM agents lack robust spatial state tracking in dynamic environments; need improved persistent world models and better integration of feedback for actioned reasoning; SpatialAct provides diagnostic capabilities and tasks to guide future research.

Abstract: Humans can effortlessly perceive spatial layouts, form cognitive representations, reason about spatial relations, and translate such reasoning into actions in everyday 3D environments. Although recent vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promising performance on observation-conditioned spatial perception and reasoning tasks, it remains unclear whether they can build coherent spatial understanding, act upon it, and refine their actions through multi-turn feedback. To study this problem, we introduce \textbf{SpatialAct}, a simulator-grounded benchmark for probing \textit{action-conditioned spatial reasoning} in 3D scenes. Starting from the most challenging setting, Multi-turn Interactive Refinement, we further design its decomposed counterpart, Single-step Error Detection and Fix, together with five fundamental spatial ability tasks to diagnose the underlying causes of model failures. Experiments reveal a clear reasoning-to-action gap: current VLMs can perform well on isolated spatial reasoning tasks, but struggle to maintain coherent spatial beliefs and produce reliable actions during multi-turn feedback, substantially underperforming humans. These results suggest that current VLM agents still lack robust spatial state tracking under action-induced environment changes, even when low-level control is abstracted away.

</details>


### [69] [BIAS-ID: A Framework for Analyzing Transformation Biases in AI-Generated Image Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31153)
*Jonas Ricker,Asja Fischer,Erwin Quiring*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces BIAS-ID, a transparent framework to analyze and quantify transformation biases in AI-generated image detectors; evaluation on six detectors across two datasets reveals strong biases, underscoring the need for bias-aware evaluation for reliable detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current detectors often rely on spurious correlations due to biased training data, conflating bias with lack of robustness. There is no standard protocol to measure detector bias.

Method: Define and formalize detector bias vs robustness, propose BIAS-ID framework, and empirically evaluate six detectors on two datasets to quantify transformation biases.

Result: BIAS-ID reveals that several state-of-the-art detectors are significantly affected by biases, demonstrating the framework’s ability to expose bias-driven failures.

Conclusion: Bias-aware evaluation is essential for trustworthy AI-generated image detectors; BIAS-ID provides a transparent, replicable way to measure bias and guide the development of more robust detectors.

Abstract: Given the surge of harmful AI-generated imagery online, reliably distinguishing authentic images from generated ones has become an urgent research topic. While many proposed detection methods perform well under controlled settings, they often collapse when tested on real-world data. A potential root cause are subtle biases in the detectors' training data. As a result, detectors may rely on spurious correlations instead of learning true forensic artifacts. While a recent line of work has identified the problem, there is not yet an established protocol to evaluate how biased a detector actually is. In this work, we therefore take a step back: First, we discuss what it means for a detector to be biased, and how this differs from a lack of robustness. Second, we propose BIAS-ID, a transparent framework for analyzing and quantifying the presence of transformation biases in AI-generated image detectors. We validate our framework by performing an evaluation of six detectors across two datasets, revealing that several state-of-the-art detection methods are strongly affected by biases. Our results highlight the importance of bias-aware evaluation for developing reliable AI-generated image detectors.

</details>


### [70] [Light Interaction: Training-Free Inference Acceleration for Interactive Video World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31158)
*Jiacheng Lu,Haoyi Zhu,Sipei Yi,Enze Xie,Yu Li,Cheng Zhuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free framework Light Interaction speeds up interactive video world models by adaptive context management, denoising cache, and sparse 3D attention, enabling up to 2.59x speedups in long trajectories without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling interactive video world models to long trajectories is costly due to growing context memory, quadratic attention complexity, and repeated denoising steps; a training-free method is desirable to accelerate inference.

Method: Adaptive context management (discard spatial memory during novel exploration, adjust temporal context to local dynamics, reuse outputs when revisiting regions), denoising cache acceleration, and hardware-software co-designed 3D block sparse attention with fused Triton kernels.

Result: Achieves up to 2.59x speedup without model retraining while maintaining competitive visual quality on HY-WorldPlay and Matrix-Game-3.0.

Conclusion: Light Interaction demonstrates a practical, training-free approach to accelerate interactive video world models at scale by combining adaptive computation, caching, and sparse attention.

Abstract: Interactive video world models generate video chunk by chunk in response to user-controlled camera movements, enabling applications such as real-time game simulation, virtual scene navigation, and embodied AI training. However, scaling to long interactive trajectories is prohibitively expensive due to growing context memory, quadratic attention complexity, and repeated denoising steps. We present Light Interaction, a training-free inference acceleration framework for interactive video world models. Our key insight is that interaction naturally enables trajectory-dependent adaptive computation: retrieved spatial memory can be discarded during novel exploration, temporal context can be adjusted according to local latent dynamics, and early-step model outputs can be reused when the camera revisits familiar regions. Based on this insight, Light Interaction combines adaptive context management, denoising cache acceleration, and hardware-software co-designed 3D block sparse attention with fused Triton kernels. Evaluated on HY-WorldPlay and Matrix-Game-3.0, Light Interaction achieves up to 2.59x speedup without model retraining while maintaining competitive visual quality.

</details>


### [71] [Guidance for Low-Level Perceptual Editing in Unconditional Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31162)
*Shreyansh Modi,Akshat Tomar,Aarush Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, inference-time image-editing framework for unconditional diffusion models that uses degradation concept vectors and bottleneck patching with classifier-free guidance to steer samples away from degraded manifolds, yielding consistent aesthetic improvements without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unconditional diffusion models offer strong generative priors, but editing them aesthetically is underexplored; h-space patching fails for global, low-level transformations needed for perceptual refinement; a general, training-free editing framework is desirable.

Method: Inference-time mechanism operating on low-level features; extract degradation concept vectors; combine bottleneck patching with classifier-free guidance; guide sampling away from the degraded manifold; no model retraining.

Result: Produces consistently improved images without model retraining.

Conclusion: Proposes a generalized, training-free image-editing framework for unconditional diffusion models at inference time that leverages low-level feature vectors and hybrid guidance to achieve aesthetic refinement without retraining.

Abstract: Unconditional diffusion models offer powerful generative priors, yet steering them toward aesthetically enhanced outputs remains largely unexplored. We show that h-space patching, the dominant paradigm for training-free diffusion editing, systematically fails for global, low-level transformations required for aesthetic and perceptual refinement. We introduce a novel, generalized framework for image-editing in unconditional diffusion models without explicit training. This inference-time mechanism operates on low-level features by extracting degradation concept vectors and combining bottleneck patching with classifier-free guidance to guide sampling away from the degraded manifold, producing consistently improved images without any model retraining.

</details>


### [72] [Detect in Any Scene: An Agentic Framework for Object Detection with Experience-Aware Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31174)
*Wenlun Zhang,Jun Yin,Kentaro Yoshioka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DetAS is an agentic object-detection framework that uses a Multimodal LLM as a central agent to dynamically assemble restoration modules and specialized detectors, with two main components: Self-Adaptive Image Restoration and Multi-Expertise Detection. It extends to DetAS-X with Self-Evolving Experience Harvesting, enabling experience-aware reasoning from limited annotated data; on six benchmarks it achieves substantial gains (average +28.36% F1, up to +37.01% on DarkFace).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world object detection suffers from degradations and heterogeneous object distributions, challenging generalization. Traditional scene-specific representation learning and end-to-end pipelines struggle to adapt to dynamic environments, motivating a flexible, policy-driven approach.

Method: The framework treats detection as a dynamic decision process with a central Multimodal LLM that selects from a toolbox of restoration modules and domain-specific detectors. It comprises (1) Self-Adaptive Image Restoration, which decides when and how to enhance images, and (2) Multi-Expertise Detection, which fuses predictions from multiple detectors via instance-level reasoning. DetAS-X introduces Self-Evolving Experience Harvesting to accumulate node-level decision experience from limited annotated data, enabling experience-aware reasoning during inference.

Result: Extensive experiments on six challenging benchmarks show that DetAS-X significantly outperforms prior MLLM-based detectors, with an average F1 improvement of 28.36% and up to 37.01% on the DarkFace dataset.

Conclusion: Agentic detection is a promising paradigm for robust object detection in complex, dynamic environments, and DetAS(-X) lays a foundation for adaptive, experience-driven decision policies in real-world settings.

Abstract: Object detection in real-world scenarios remains challenging due to diverse image degradations and heterogeneous object distributions, which significantly hinder the generalization of existing detectors. Conventional approaches, including scene-specific representation learning and end-to-end pipeline design, are inherently limited by their reliance on predefined conditions and lack adaptability to dynamic environments. In this paper, we propose DetAS, an agentic detection framework that formulates object detection as a dynamic decision process. Instead of relying on static pipelines, DetAS leverages a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) as a central agent to adaptively compose detection workflows by selecting from a toolbox of restoration modules and specialized detectors. Specifically, DetAS consists of two key components: Self-Adaptive Image Restoration, which dynamically determines whether and how to enhance images for downstream detection, and Multi-Expertise Detection, which integrates multiple domain-specialized detectors and resolves their predictions through instance-level reasoning. To further improve decision quality under fine-grained conditions, we introduce Self-Evolving Experience Harvesting and extend the framework to DetAS-X, which accumulates node-level decision experience from a small set of annotated data and enables experience-aware reasoning during inference. This mechanism allows the system to progressively refine its decision policy and adapt to diverse real-world scenarios. Extensive experiments on six challenging benchmarks demonstrate that DetAS-X significantly outperforms existing MLLM-based detectors, achieving an average improvement of 28.36% in F1 score, with up to 37.01% gain on DarkFace. These results demonstrate the promise of agentic detection and establish a solid foundation for its application in complex and dynamic environments.

</details>


### [73] [Vanilla ViT for Automotive Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31177)
*Gilles Puy,Nermin Samet,Alexandre Boulch,Spyros Gidaris,Tuan-Hung VU,Renaud Marlet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vanilla ViT-based approach (VaViT) for large-scale LiDAR semantic segmentation that uses a careful tokenizer, a lightweight decoder, and targeted augmentations to close the gap with SOTA U-Net–like methods while keeping the simple ViT backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the performance gap between non-hierarchical ViTs and specialized LiDAR segmentation architectures by exploiting the global modeling of ViTs for large-scale point clouds without resorting to hierarchical or windowed attention.

Method: Use a vanilla, non-hierarchical ViT architecture augmented with a LiDAR-specific tokenizer to convert point cloud data into token sequences, a lightweight decoder segmentation head for per-pixel/voxel labeling, and tailored data augmentations to improve generalization on automotive LiDAR datasets.

Result: VaViT matches or surpasses state-of-the-art methods on major LiDAR segmentation benchmarks (nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, Waymo Open Dataset) while maintaining the simplicity and efficiency of the ViT backbone.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that plain ViTs can effectively handle large-scale 3D segmentation tasks with proper tokenization, lightweight decoding, and data augmentation, offering a simpler alternative to complex hierarchical architectures.

Abstract: Plain Transformers have become the de-facto architecture for processing text, audio, image, and video, offering a unified backbone for multimodal learning. However, state-of-the-art architectures for point cloud semantic segmentation remain dominated by U-Nets architectures where convolutions are interleaved with local or windowed attentions. In this work, we show how to effectively leverage vanilla, non-hierarchical ViTs for segmentation of large-scale automotive lidar scenes. We bridge the performance gap thanks to a carefully designed tokenizer, a lightweight decoder segmentation head, and tailored data augmentations. Our approach, VaViT for Vanilla ViT, matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art methods while maintaining the simplicity of ViT architecture. We provide extensive evaluations on nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, and Waymo Open Dataset to validate the efficiency of our method. Code and models are available at https://github.com/valeoai/VaViT.

</details>


### [74] [From Local Geometry to Global Pseudo Labeling for Robust Positive Unlabeled Learning under Covariate Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31187)
*Firas Gabetni,Alexandre Rocchi Henry,Nacim Belkhir,Ziyi Liu,Gianni Franchi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A PU-learning framework, SPUNA, detects covariate shift in vision with weak supervision by progressively annotating shifted data using local manifold structure; achieves state-of-the-art PU performance and matches fully supervised methods, with strong generalization across shift types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Covariate shift detection is essential for reliable vision systems, yet supervised methods require labeled data from both original and shifted distributions, which is often impractical. PU learning offers weaker supervision.

Method: Spectral PU Neighborhood Annotation (SPUNA) leverages the local manifold structure of visual features to progressively discover shifted data. It is a geometry-aware PU framework that uses spectral/neighborhood information to annotate unlabeled examples likely from the shifted distribution.

Result: SPUNA achieves state-of-the-art performance in PU settings and matches fully supervised methods in covariate shift detection. It generalizes robustly across different shift types.

Conclusion: A PU-based, geometry-aware approach can effectively detect covariate shift with weak supervision, offering strong performance and generalization comparable to fully supervised approaches.

Abstract: Detecting covariate shift is critical for building reliable vision systems. While most prior work focuses on improving robustness to shift, explicitly detecting covariate shift remains underexplored. Existing approaches typically rely on fully supervised training, requiring labeled examples from both original and shifted distributions, which is often impractical. In this paper, we show that covariate shift detection can be effectively addressed with weaker supervision using Positive Unlabeled (PU) learning. However, under covariate shift, in distribution and shifted data overlap significantly, making classical PU methods unstable and sensitive to noise. To overcome this challenge, we introduce Spectral PU Neighborhood Annotation (SPUNA), a geometry aware framework that progressively discovers shifted data by leveraging the local manifold structure of visual features. Extensive experiments show that SPUNA achieves state of the art performance in PU settings and remarkably matches the performances of fully supervised methods. Moreover, our approach transfers robustly across different types of shifts, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [75] [The Regularizing Power of Language-Training Deepfake Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31192)
*Benedikt Hopf,Zongwei Wu,Radu Timofte*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-encoder deepfake detector using a frozen specialist detector and a LoRA-tuned MLLM encoder, with a two-stage training curriculum (binary alignment and RL-based explain-then-classify) to improve cross-dataset generalization and interpretability; achieves state-of-the-art results on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overfitting to low-level, dataset-specific artifacts in deepfake detection and the need for interpretable models; leveraging language-model-based high-level descriptors as regularization to enhance generalization.

Method: Dual-encoder architecture: frozen specialist detector + LoRA-tuned MLLM encoder. Two-stage training: (1) binary alignment to fuse features and mitigate overfitting; (2) reinforcement learning that encourages the model to generate descriptive reasoning before classification, using only binary labels, promoting explain-then-classify behavior.

Result: Improved cross-dataset generalization and interpretability, with performance surpassing state-of-the-art methods by a large margin on benchmark datasets; reasoning chains can be omitted at inference without loss.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively couples generalization and interpretability in deepfake detection by prioritizing high-level, describable features via language-informed regularization, yielding robust performance and explainability.

Abstract: Recently, thanks to the advent of Multimodal-LLMs, deepfake detectors are striving not only to be generalizable but also interpretable. We propose that these two challenges can effectively be tackled jointly, since describable artifacts typically generalize better, opening the possibility to use language as a regularization mechanism. Since deepfake detection generally suffers from overfitting to low-level domain-specific artifacts, our intuition is that an LLM that has been pretrained on language would prefer high-level artifacts that can be described better. This way, we can use high-level features where possible, while training the model to use low-level features where necessary. We utilize a dual-encoder architecture, pairing a frozen specialist detector with a LoRA-tuned MLLM encoder, and a two-stage training curriculum: first, a binary alignment phase demonstrates that the intrinsic capability of MLLMs can effectively combine features to mitigate overfitting to dataset-specific artifacts. To further bolster generalization and achieve interpretability, we employ a reinforcement learning stage that encourages the model to generate descriptive reasoning before classifying, using only binary labels. By rewarding this "explain-then-classify" behavior, we explicitly incentivize the model to prioritize high-level, robust features. Crucially, this process yields both interpretable descriptions and a further boost in cross-dataset performance, even when reasoning chains are omitted at inference. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets validate our approach, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by a large margin.

</details>


### [76] [Probabilistic Precipitation Nowcasting with Rectified Flow Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31204)
*Johannes Schusterbauer,Jannik Wiese,Nick Stracke,Timy Phan,Björn Ommer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FREUD is a frame-wise encoder and unified decoder using rectified flow transformers for efficient, uncertainty-preserving weather nowcasting; achieves SOTA on SEVIR with continuous updates and temporal coherence, enabled by ensembling of a probabilistic first stage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the deterministic compression in existing data-driven nowcasting methods that limits uncertainty capture and scalability for high-dimensional spatio-temporal weather data, especially under extreme conditions.

Method: Introduce FREUD: a frame-wise encoder with a unified video decoder (rectified flow transformer) operating in latent space for efficient compression and temporally coherent forecasts; a first stage that preserves uncertainty and enables ensembling to capture aleatoric uncertainty; performance benefits from model and test-time scaling.

Result: State-of-the-art precipitation nowcasting on the SEVIR benchmark; improvements with model and test-time scaling; code released at the provided GitHub repository.

Conclusion: FREUD enables efficient, uncertainty-aware nowcasting with continuous updates and temporal consistency, suitable for extreme-weather scenarios and high-dimensional data; scalable through model/test-time scaling and ensembling.

Abstract: Accurate weather forecasts are essential across various domains and are safety-critical in extreme weather conditions. Compared to simulation-based forecasting, data-driven approaches show greater efficiency, enabling short-term, high-resolution nowcasting. In particular, diffusion models proved effective in weather nowcasting due to their strong probabilistic foundation. However, existing methods rely on deterministic compression to reduce the complexity of high-dimensional weather data, limiting their ability to capture uncertainty in the decoding process. In this work, we introduce $\textbf{FREUD}$, a $\textbf{Fr}$ame-wise $\textbf{E}$ncoder and $\textbf{U}$nited $\textbf{D}$ecoder model based on rectified flow transformers for efficient compression of spatio-temporal weather data. Frame-wise encoding enables continuous forecast updates, while the unified video decoder ensures temporal consistency. Our uncertainty-preserving first stage allows us to capture aleatoric uncertainty via ensembling, which is particularly beneficial for extreme weather events with high decoding variability. We achieve state-of-the-art performance in precipitation nowcasting with a compact latent-space rectified flow transformer on the SEVIR benchmark and show further performance gains by model and test-time scaling. Code available here: https://github.com/CompVis/weather-rf

</details>


### [77] [Benchmarking and Enhancing Text-to-Image Models for Generating Visual Representations in Early Arithmetic Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31212)
*Junling Wang,Boqi Chen,Heejin Do,Mubashara Akhtar,April Yi Wang,Mrinmaya Sachan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes equation-to-visual generation to produce pedagogy-aligned visuals from arithmetic equations, introducing E2V-Bench with four pedagogically grounded visual types and automatic correctness metrics; evaluates current T2I models and shows they struggle with counts and relational structure; proposes benchmark-guided strategies that improve models but reveal a need for stronger numerical and relational grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure AI-generated educational visuals faithfully encode numerical relations and pedagogical concepts, addressing a gap where traditional T2I outputs misrepresent content and structure.

Method: Develop E2V-Bench from teacher interviews and educational materials, define four pedagogically grounded visual types, create automatic metrics for visual correctness, benchmark current text-to-image models, and propose strategies guided by the benchmark to improve fidelity.

Result: Current T2I models frequently fail on equation-to-visual tasks, with errors chiefly in object counts and broken relational structure. Benchmark-guided strategies yield improvements for representative models, yet a remaining gap indicates limited numerical/relational grounding.

Conclusion: The work provides a dedicated benchmark and baseline analyses for equation-to-visual generation in education, showing promise for targeted enhancements but highlighting the need for stronger grounding in numerical and relational reasoning in T2I systems.

Abstract: AI systems are increasingly used to support educational content creation, yet it remains unclear whether they can generate outputs that faithfully represent the pedagogical concepts they are intended to teach. Thus, we introduce equation-to-visual generation, a task that, in contrast to conventional image generation, requires producing pedagogically meaningful visuals from arithmetic equations while precisely preserving their numerical and relational structure. Informed by interviews with teachers and an analysis of educational materials, we construct E2V-Bench, a benchmark spanning four pedagogically grounded visual types, along with automatic metrics for evaluating visual correctness. Our evaluation reveals that recent text-to-image (T2I) models frequently fail on this task, with errors dominated by incorrect object counts and broken relational structure. Building on this, we explore benchmark-guided enhancement strategies. These strategies improve representative models, while the remaining gap calls for stronger numerical and relational grounding in future T2I models.

</details>


### [78] [TALON: Token-Aligned Lightweight Adapters for 6-DoF Spacecraft Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31217)
*Abid Ali,Arunkumar Rathinam,Djamila Aouada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TALON: lightweight spatiotemporal adapters for frozen ViT enabling temporal reasoning for monocular spacecraft pose estimation; achieves large gains with minimal parameter overhead and strong cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing monocular pose estimation methods largely ignore temporal information or require heavy fine-tuning/flow nets, risking forgetting or high cost; need efficient temporal modeling in a frozen backbone.

Method: Injects spatiotemporal 3D adapters before self-attention layers of a frozen ViT; uses patch-token alignment loss with a prototype-conditioned KL-divergence to align keypoint structures; shared adapter per block; negligible parameter increase (<5%). Pre-attention placement allows temporal tokens to influence attention; single adapter per block outperforms post-attention variants.

Result: On SPADES: pose error reduced by 50% over SOTA; on SwissCube: ADD-0.1d improved by 21.8%; zero-shot sim-to-real on SPARK reduces pose error by 4.7x; ablations highlight adapter depth importance across in-domain and cross-domain cases.

Conclusion: Temporal reasoning can be effectively integrated into a frozen backbone via lightweight pre-attention adapters with an alignment loss, yielding strong in-domain and cross-domain performance with minimal overhead.
