Abstract: Monocular 6-DoF spacecraft pose estimation methods predominantly process individual frames, discarding the temporal information present in an image sequence acquired during spacecraft manoeuvres. Few temporal approaches require full backbone fine-tuning or auxiliary optical flow networks, risking catastrophic forgetting or increasing computational cost, respectively. We propose TALON (Token-Aligned Lightweight adapters for Orbital Navigation): spatiotemporal 3D adapters injected before the self-attention layers of a frozen ViT vision transformer, combined with a patch-token alignment loss that geometrically grounds the adapted features to keypoint structure through a prototype-conditioned KL-divergence objective. Pre-attention placement allows the frozen attention to reason over temporally enriched tokens, achieving stronger performance with a single adapter per block than post-attention alternatives. The alignment loss shapes the intermediate representations so that each keypoint induces a spatially precise activation in the token field, while the framework adds less than 5% parameters to the frozen backbone. On SPADES dataset, TALON reduces the pose error by 50% over the prior state-of-the-art, and on SwissCube dataset it surpasses the prior best by 21.8% in ADD-0.1d accuracy. Zero-shot cross-domain evaluation from sim-to-real on SPARK real data reduces pose error by 4.7x, and ablations characterise the role of adapter depth across in-domain and cross-domain settings.

</details>


### [79] [Latent Geometric Chords for Query-Efficient Decision-Based Adversarial Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31219)
*Ei Hmue Khine,Yao Li,Jiebao Sun,Shengzhu Shi,Zhichang Guo,Boying Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Latent Geometric Chords (LGC) with a Residual-based Adversarial Generation (RAG) mechanism to enable query-efficient, decision-based adversarial attacks in a compressed semantic manifold, achieving high perceptual fidelity and strong transferability while expanding the effective search space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current decision-based black-box attacks suffer from either pixel-level artifacts (unwanted high-frequency distortions) or limited search capacity due to low-dimensional latent manifolds and reconstruction flaws; there is a need for methods that combine high visual fidelity with a flexible, large search space while maintaining query efficiency.

Method: Introduce Latent Geometric Chords (LGC) with a curvature-aware geometric search in a compressed semantic manifold. A variant LGC-H is described. Residual-based Adversarial Generation (RAG) isolates semantic perturbations as geometric chords and superimposes them onto the original image, addressing reconstruction flaws and effectively doubling the search-space dimensions.

Result: Demonstrates robust cross-dataset transferability and substantial performance gains over state-of-the-art baselines. LGC achieves high perceptual fidelity (SSIM > 0.99, LPIPS < 0.01) at 5000 queries, while maintaining strong attack success rates against robustly trained models.

Conclusion: LGC and RAG collectively mitigate limitations of pixel-wise and latent-space attacks, enabling a high-fidelity, scalable, query-efficient decision-based attack framework with broad transferability; the approach expands the usable search space and maintains perceptual quality, though empirical validation across more datasets and models would further establish generality.

Abstract: While decision-based black-box adversarial attacks present a severe security threat, current methodologies suffer from fundamental limitations. Pixel-wise attacks frequently introduce unnatural, high-frequency visual artifacts, while latent-space frameworks are confined by the limited search space of low-dimensional manifolds and inherent reconstruction flaws. To resolve these limitations, we propose Latent Geometric Chords (LGC) for Query-Efficient Decision-Based Adversarial Attacks alongside a variant, LGC-H. At its core, LGC navigates decision boundaries by executing a curvature-aware geometric search within a compressed semantic manifold. To guarantee high visual fidelity and circumvent dimensionality bottlenecks, we introduce a Residual-based Adversarial Generation (RAG) mechanism. RAG isolates semantic perturbations as geometric chords and superimposes them directly onto the original source image. RAG substantially resolves baseline reconstruction flaws and effectively doubles the permissible search space dimensions. Experimental results demonstrate that LGC achieves robust cross-dataset transferability and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Notably, our method, LGC, minimizes perturbation magnitudes while achieving state-of-the-art visual fidelity--with a Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) exceeding 0.99 and a Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) below 0.01 at 5000 queries--and sustaining high attack success rates under stringent perceptual constraints, successfully compromising adversarially trained robust models. The source code is available at: https://github.com/eihmuekhine/Latent-Geometric-Chords.

</details>


### [80] [HiERO-StepG @ Ego4D Step Grounding Challenge: hierarchical activity understanding enables zero-shot step grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31227)
*Andrea Zenotto,Simone Alberto Peirone,Francesca Pistilli,Giuseppe Averta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiERO-StepG: zero-shot, weakly supervised procedural step grounding by learning a hierarchical structure from uncurated videos; uses HiERO to map related actions, clusters steps, and enforces temporal monotonicity and post-processing; achieves 56.27% R@1 (IoU 0.3) on Ego4D, second place, no task-specific annotations; project page provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Procedural tasks are hierarchically structured but traditional grounding needs extensive annotations; propose that structure emerges from co-occurring actions in uncurated videos using weak supervision.

Method: Builds on HiERO to learn a representation where functionally related actions are close; cluster in feature space to detect steps; for Ego4D Step Grounding, enforce agreement across fine and coarse levels, temporal monotonicity, and post-process to reduce noise.

Result: On Ego4D Step Grounding: 56.27% R@1 (IoU=0.3), global leaderboard second place, zero-shot, no procedure-specific annotations.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a robust, annotation-efficient approach to procedural step grounding that leverages weak supervision and simple clustering, with potential for broader applicability and further improvements in robustness and generalization.

Abstract: Procedural activities follow well-defined structures: whether we consider a cooking recipe or a mechanic repairing a car, these activities naturally decompose in a hierarchy of steps and sub-steps. Traditional approaches for step grounding require extensive annotations and scale poorly. Instead, we argue that such hierarchical structure can emerge naturally from uncurated videos of human activities through recurring patterns of co-occurring actions and activities. Our approach builds on HiERO, a weakly-supervised representation learning approach that maps close in the feature space actions that are functionally related to each other, leveraging only fine-grained action-level narrations. In this feature space, procedure steps can be detected by a simple clustering, with no additional task-specific fine-tuning. For the Ego4D Step Grounding challenge, we augment this approach by ensuring fine and coarse level agreement in step assignments, enforcing strict temporal monotonicity of the grounded steps and post-processing the detected steps to reduce the impact of noisy predictions. We call this approach HiERO-StepG and it achieves 56.27 % on the R@1 (IoU = 0.3) metric on the global leaderboard at submission time, ranking second while being completely zero-shot and not requiring procedure-specific annotations. Project page: https://github.com/andreazenotto/HiERO-StepG.

</details>


### [81] [Beyond Classification: Dynamic Adapter Routing for Continual Multimodal Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31229)
*Alicja Dobrzeniecka,Filip Szatkowski,Sebastian Cygert,Szymon Lukasik,Bartlomiej Twardowski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces continual multimodal retrieval (CMR) evaluation framework; finds standard CIL methods offer limited gains in CMR; proposes Dynamic Adapter Routing (DAR) with prototype-based routing and model merging; DAR outperforms baselines and generalizes OOD; highlights unique CMR challenges and invites further research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval is central to vision-language models, but continual updating for retrieval tasks is underexplored. Existing class-incremental learning (CIL) approaches may not capture retrieval-specific dynamics; a principled CMR framework and effective methods are needed.

Method: Develops a principled evaluation framework for continual multimodal retrieval across diverse visual domains; systematically evaluates common CIL approaches in this CMR setting; proposes Dynamic Adapter Routing (DAR) where adapters are selected by prototype-based routing and combined via model merging.

Result: DAR achieves superior performance over previous baselines and demonstrates strong generalization under out-of-distribution evaluation; standard CIL methods fail to yield meaningful gains in this challenging CMR scenario.

Conclusion: CMR presents unique retrieval challenges; the proposed DAR approach shows promise and suggests directions for future research in continual multimodal retrieval.

Abstract: While retrieval is a core function of vision-language models, continually updating these models for retrieval tasks remains critically underexplored. Existing work often approaches continual retrieval through the lens of class-incremental learning (CIL), evaluating both standard CIL methods and retrieval-oriented adaptations in settings that may not fully capture the retrieval-specific dynamics. To address this, we introduce a new, principled evaluation framework for continual multimodal retrieval (CMR) spanning diverse visual domains, and systematically evaluate common approaches within this setting. Our empirical analysis shows that standard CIL methods fail to yield meaningful gains in our more challenging scenario. Therefore, we propose Dynamic Adapter Routing (DAR), a novel approach based on adapters selected through prototype-based routing and combined via model merging.DAR achieves superior performance over the previous baselines and demonstrates strong generalization under out-of-distribution evaluation. Our results highlights the unique challenges of CMR and encourages further research in this direction.

</details>


### [82] [ERGeoBench:A Comprehensive Benchmark for Embodied Reasoning and Geo-localization in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31251)
*Kaiwen Xue,Tao Wei,Guoxin Zhang,Zhonghong Ou,Kaoyan Lu,Yu Feng,Yifan Zhu,Haoran Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ERGeoBench is a diagnostic benchmark for vision-driven embodied geo-localization across single-view, panorama-view, and embodied-view settings, with 2,207 panoramas, evaluating perception, spatial awareness, commonsense reasoning, and geo-localization reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of fine-grained evaluation for embodied geo-localization in multimodal LLMs and to diagnose core capabilities and failure modes in geographically grounded embodied tasks.

Method: Constructed a benchmark consisting of 2,207 globally distributed street-view panoramas. It supports three progressive observation settings—single-view, panorama-view, and embodied-view—allowing sequential changes in yaw, pitch, and zoom as agents actively acquire observations. Evaluates four capabilities: foundational perception, spatial awareness, common sense reasoning, and geo-localization reasoning. Benchmarks evaluated leading proprietary and open-source MLLMs.

Result: Models can infer high-level geographic semantics but struggle with fine-grained perceptual operations, metric localization, and maintaining spatial consistency across views. Geo-localization correlates strongly with other capabilities, indicating that accurate localization requires integrated perception, spatial reasoning, and commonsense inference rather than isolated recognition.

Conclusion: ERGeoBench provides a unified framework for diagnosing and advancing human-like embodied geo-localization, enabling targeted improvements and fair comparisons across models.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential as embodied agents, yet embodied geo-localization remains underexplored due to the lack of fine-grained evaluation. We introduce ERGeoBench, a diagnostic benchmark for vision-driven embodied geo-localization. ERGeoBench evaluates models under three progressive settings -- single-view, panorama-view, and embodied-view -- where agents may actively acquire observations through sequential changes in yaw, pitch, and zoom. The benchmark contains 2,207 globally distributed street-view panoramas and measures four complementary capabilities: foundational perception, spatial awareness, common sense reasoning, and geo-localization reasoning. Evaluations of leading proprietary and open-source MLLMs show that current models can infer high-level geographic semantics, but still struggle with fine-grained perceptual operations, metric localization, and spatial consistency across views. We further observe that geo-localization is strongly correlated with the other capability dimensions, suggesting that accurate localization depends on integrated perception, spatial reasoning, and commonsense inference rather than isolated visual recognition. Overall, ERGeoBench provides a unified framework for diagnosing and advancing human-like embodied geo-localization. Project Page: https://kaixuewen.github.io/ERGeoBench/

</details>


### [83] [DriveMA: Driving Vision-Language-Action Models with verifiable Meta-Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31271)
*Weicheng Zheng,Yixin Huang,Qiao Sun,Derun Li,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveMA introduces verifiable meta-actions for Driving VLA, enabling language-action alignment via trajectory-grounded verification; achieves SOTA on Waymo Vision-based E2E driving; demonstrates data-efficient RL and dense rewards; code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the language-action gap in driving-language models by introducing verifiable, trajectory-grounded meta-actions that can be contracted into language intentions and verified against motion trajectories.

Method: Propose DriveMA with verifiable meta-actions summarizing future ego motion into language-domain intentions. Build a trajectory-grounded annotation pipeline from expert trajectories. Verify meta-actions against generated trajectories using rule-based projection. Train via action-centric supervised learning and a data-efficient turn-level credit-assignment reinforcement learning framework with dense rewards to align high-level decisions with low-level planning.

Result: State-of-the-art on Waymo Open Dataset Vision-based E2E Driving: Rater Feedback Score 8.060 with a 2B model, 8.079 with a 4B model; competitive closed-loop planning on NAVSIM.

Conclusion: Verifiable, language-aligned meta-actions can yield state-of-the-art planning in driving VLAs; simple interfaces suffice when they are verifiable and well-aligned with trajectory planning; forthcoming code/data/models.

Abstract: Driving Vision-Language-Action Models (Driving VLAs) aim to use language to improve end-to-end planning, but the language-action gap limits this promise. We propose DriveMA, a Driving VLA framework built on verifiable meta-actions, which summarize future ego motion into compact language-domain intentions and can be constructed from expert trajectories with a trajectory-grounded annotation pipeline and can be verified against generated trajectories through rule-based projection. DriveMA exploits this verifiability with action-centric supervised training and a data-efficient turn-level credit assignment reinforcement learning framework, explicitly aligning high-level decisions with low-level trajectory planning through dense rewards and precise credit assignment. DriveMA sets a new state of the art on the Waymo Open Dataset Vision-based E2E Driving, achieving a Rater Feedback Score of 8.060 with a 2B model and further improving it to 8.079 with a 4B model; it also obtains competitive closed-loop planning performance on NAVSIM. These results show that even a simple meta-action interface can achieve state-of-the-art planning when made verifiable and optimized for language-action alignment. Code, data, and models will be released to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [84] [Topologically Consistent Multi-view 3D Head Reconstruction via Coarse-Guided Layered Surface Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31283)
*Timo Bolkart,Daoye Wang,Prashanth Chandran*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SHELLS proposes a memory-efficient, scalable 3D head reconstruction framework using layered sampling to decouple feature extraction from mesh resolution, achieving faster inference, lower memory, and better accuracy on dense meshes while training on synthetic data yet generalizing to real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address memory bottlenecks and surface noise in vertex-wise refinement for dense head meshes by decoupling feature extraction from mesh resolution and enabling scalable, dense topologies (>10k vertices).

Method: Hierarchical sampling: extract multi-view features with a DINOv2 backbone (LoRA adapted), projectively sample a sparse global feature cloud, predict a coarse mesh, then construct layered, surface-aware sampling shells as a discrete search space for the final reconstruction; output is significantly memory-efficient compared to volumetric baselines.

Result: On 18k-vertex meshes: reduces median registration error by 21–29%, achieves 3.5× faster inference (0.08s vs 0.29s) and uses 2.4GB versus 20GB of GPU memory, while generalizing from synthetic training to real-world captures; avoids need for costly pre-registered multi-view datasets.

Conclusion: SHELLS delivers a scalable, memory-efficient pipeline for dense 3D head reconstruction that preserves surface consistency, enabling fast, accurate reconstructions with synthetic-to-real generalization and broad applicability to dense semantic correspondence tasks.

Abstract: We present SHELLS (Semantic Head Estimation via Layered Local Sampling), an efficient feed-forward framework for 3D head reconstruction in dense semantic correspondence from multi-view images. Existing methods typically refine vertices independently via localized feature volumes. This approach couples memory-intensive feature sampling to mesh resolution, which limits scalability for dense topologies (> 10k vertices) and introduces surface noise. In contrast, SHELLS decouples feature extraction from mesh resolution via a hierarchical sampling strategy. We extract multi-view features using a DINOv2 backbone with LoRA adaptation, projectively sample a sparse global feature cloud, and predict an intermediate coarse mesh. This coarse prior guides the construction of layered, surface-aware sampling shells that serve as a discrete search space for the final reconstruction. SHELLS maintains surface consistency while using 88% less inference GPU memory (2.4GB vs. 20GB) than volumetric baselines. It reduces median registration error by 21% to 29% with a 3.5x inference speedup (0.08s vs. 0.29s) for 18k-vertex meshes. Notably, our model is trained exclusively on synthetic data yet generalizes effectively to real-world captures, eliminating the need for the costly, pre-registered multi-view datasets common in prior work.

</details>


### [85] [SAM for Robust Mitochondria Instance Segmentation in Fluorescence Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31284)
*Suyog Jadhav,Dilip K. Prasad,Krishna Agarwal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Finetune SAM on synthetically generated fluorescence microscopy data to overcome domain shift and data scarcity for mitochondrial instance segmentation, achieving higher precision and Dice on real FM images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the domain gap between natural-image foundation models and fluorescence microscopy, and mitigate the scarcity of annotated mitochondria segmentation datasets by scalable synthetic data pretraining.

Method: Generate large-scale synthetic FM mitochondria datasets that simulate diffraction-limited resolution, low contrast, and overlapping networks; finetune the Segment Anything Model on this synthetic data; evaluate on a real, manually annotated FM mitochondria dataset; perform qualitative and quantitative analyses.

Result: The finetuned model shows improved precision and average Dice score over strong baselines on real FM data.

Conclusion: Simulation-assisted training can unlock foundation-model-based FM instance segmentation, addressing data scarcity and domain shift with potential applicability to other organelles and imaging modalities.

Abstract: The morphological analysis of mitochondria in fluorescence microscopy (FM) is crucial for understanding cellular health, energy production, and metabolic regulation. While foundation models like the Segment Anything Model (SAM) have revolutionized natural image segmentation, their direct application to FM is hindered by a significant domain shift characterized by diffraction-limited resolution, low contrast, and complex overlapping organelle networks. Furthermore, the development of robust models is bottlenecked by a severe lack of high-quality, manually annotated instance segmentation datasets for mitochondria. In this paper, we propose a scalable solution to this data scarcity by finetuning SAM exclusively on synthetically generated FM data. We simulate realistic mitochondria data and emulate the optical properties of fluorescence microscopes to create a large-scale annotated dataset. We evaluate our fine-tuned model on a curated dataset of real, manually annotated FM images. Qualitative and quantitative analyses demonstrate that our synthetically fine-tuned model improves precision and average dice score over strong baselines. This work establishes the potential of simulation-assisted training for FM instance segmentation.

</details>


### [86] [Authentication of Copy Detection Patterns via Cross-Camera Dual-Synthetic Referencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31292)
*Ivan Oleksiyuk,Roman Chaban,Slava Voloshynovskiy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Enrolment-based cross-camera dual-synthetic referencing for copy-detection patterns enhances cross-camera verification by generating dual references that fuse digital templates and enrolled captures, improving robustness to printer and camera variability and ML-based forgeries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CDP verification is hindered by printer stochasticity and camera distortions. Prior camera-domain synthesis ignored printing variability, limiting cross-printer robustness. A cross-camera, enrolment-based approach with dual references aims to maximize information content and robustness across devices.

Method: Propose an enrolment-based cross-camera dual-synthetic referencing framework. Capture each printed CDP with a controlled enrolment camera, and use a deep-learning translator that jointly leverages the digital template and the enrolled capture to generate a high-quality reference for the verification image. Provide information-theoretic justification that the dual reference is more informative than template-based references.

Result: Experiments on heterogeneous mobile cameras show improved authentication performance, better robustness against ML-based copy attacks, and reliable verification from small CDP regions and on low-end devices.

Conclusion: The dual-reference strategy enhances cross-camera CDP verification by compensating for printing variability and camera distortions, enabling robust authentication even with small CDP regions and low-end hardware.

Abstract: Copy Detection Patterns (CDPs) are structures printed on physical objects to enable cost-effective authentication. Verification is achieved by comparing a captured image with the digital template from which the CDP was printed. In practice, printer stochasticity and camera distortions hinder this comparison, limiting robustness against counterfeiting. Prior work addressed camera effects by synthesising reference images in the verification camera domain, but it ignored printing variability. We introduce an enrolment-based cross-camera dual-synthetic referencing framework. Each printed CDP is first captured by a controlled enrolment camera, and a deep-learning-based translator jointly exploits the digital template and the enrolled capture to generate a high-quality reference for the verification image. We provide an information-theoretic justification showing that the dual reference is more informative than template-based references. Experiments on heterogeneous mobile cameras demonstrate improved authentication performance, robustness to machine-learning-based copy attacks, and reliable verification from small CDP regions and on low-end devices.

</details>


### [87] [TokTalk: Expressive Real-time Facial Animation from Audio-LLM Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31294)
*Qingcheng Zhao,Yifang Pan,Karan Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TokTalk translates streaming audio-tokens from Audio-LLMs into real-time, expressive 3D facial animation. It introduces a novel audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset, uses a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model, and offers a lightweight adaptation path to connect with any token-based Audio-LLM. It achieves latency-competitive performance with superior perceptual quality and expressivity across multiple avatar applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the bottleneck in conversational avatars caused by multi-stage pipelines (speech recognition, text generation, speech synthesis, and animation). By leveraging the information preserved in audio-tokens from Audio-LLMs, TokTalk aims to produce end-to-end, real-time, expressive facial animation directly from tokens.

Method: Construct a novel audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset. Train a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching (C-Flow M) model to map streaming audio-tokens to 3D facial motion. Implement a lightweight adaptation strategy to connect TokTalk to any token-based Audio-LLM with minimal computational overhead. Use chunk-based processing to enable a controllable latency–facial quality trade-off and support real-time streaming. 

Result: Ablation studies demonstrate controllable latency–quality trade-offs. TokTalk achieves real-time performance with latency comparable to prior art, and perceptual studies show improved quality, expressivity, and control of 3D facial animation. Demonstrations on diverse avatars (chatbot avatar, voice-driven user avatar, and animation director interface) validate flexibility and applicability.

Conclusion: TokTalk enables end-to-end, real-time, expressive facial animation directly from audio-tokens, with a lightweight integration path to existing Audio-LLMs and a tunable latency–quality frontier. It broadens practical AV facial applications across avatars and animation workflows.

Abstract: Recent advances in Audio-LLMs like GPT-4o have ushered in an era of conversational interaction with language models. Conversational avatars however, still seem robotic in facial expression and conversational flow, in part due to sequential stages of speech recognition, text generation, turn-based text response, speech synthesis, and audio driven facial animation. Based on our insight that audio-tokens produced by current Audio-LLMs carry sufficient information to reconstruct a plausible facial performance, we present TokTalk, a system that directly outputs expressive facial animation in real-time from streaming audio-tokens. We construct a novel audio-token to 3D facial motion dataset, on which TokTalk is trained using a Chunk-based Conditional Flow Matching model. A lightweight adaptation strategy allows our trained model to seamlessly connect to any token-based Audio-LLM at minimal computational overhead. Our chunk-based processing further enables parametric trade-off between latency and facial quality, shown through ablation studies. We further show that the real-time performance of TokTalk is comparable in latency to prior art solutions, and significantly favorable (via a perceptual study) in terms of quality, expressivity and control of the 3D facial performance. We showcase TokTalk's flexibility using a chatbot Avatar, a voice-driven user Avatar, and an animation Director's interface, as diverse audio-visual face applications.

</details>


### [88] [Learning from Fine-Grained Visual Discrepancies: Mitigating Multimodal Hallucinations via In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31312)
*Haolin Deng,Xin Zou,Zhiwei Jin,Chen Chen,Haonan Lu,Xuming Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IC-VCO introduces in-context visual contrastive optimization to align vision-language models by using a shared multi-image context for contrastive objectives, plus Visual Contrast Distillation regularizer and a contrastive sample editing strategy for hard negatives; demonstrated on five benchmarks with state-of-the-art results; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard textual Direct Preference Optimization lacks explicit visual supervision and existing visual preference DPO faces theoretical inconsistencies (partition function mismatch) and relies on coarse negatives, enabling shortcut learning; a principled objective with better negatives and regularization is needed.

Method: IC-VCO places multiple images within a shared context to formulate a mathematically rigorous contrastive objective for visual preferences. Visual Contrast Distillation (VCDist) acts as a reliability-gated regularizer to align multi-image contrastive training with single-image inference. A contrastive sample editing strategy generates hard negatives through precise semantic perturbations.

Result: Evaluation on five benchmarks shows IC-VCO achieves best overall performance; the sample editing strategy is effective; code and data released.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled, robust approach to mitigating multimodal hallucination in VLMs by combining in-context contrastive learning, a regularizer to bridge training/inference, and hard-negative editing, culminating in improved multimodal alignment.

Abstract: Multimodal hallucination remains a persistent challenge for Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Standard textual Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) often fails to mitigate it due to a lack of explicit visual supervision. While existing works introduce visual preference DPO by contrasting original images against negative ones, they suffer from a theoretically inconsistent objective caused by partition function mismatches and rely on coarse-grained negatives that could enable shortcut learning. In this work, we propose In-Context Visual Contrastive Optimization (IC-VCO). By placing contrastive images within a shared multi-image context, IC-VCO ensures a mathematically rigorous objective. We further introduce Visual Contrast Distillation (VCDist), an auxiliary reliability-gated regularizer that encourages consistency between multi-image contrastive training and single-image inference. Finally, we propose a contrastive sample editing strategy that generates hard negatives via precise semantic perturbations. Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate IC-VCO's best overall performance and the effectiveness of our sample editing strategy. Code and data are available at https://github.com/OPPO-Mente-Lab/IC-VCO.

</details>


### [89] [DecMem: Towards Minute-Long Consistent World Generation with Decoupled Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31336)
*Zhenhao Yang,Xiaoshi Wu,Zhengyao Lv,Xiaoyu Shi,Xintao Wang,Pengfei Wan,Kun Gai,Kwan-Yee K. Wong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces DecMem, a decoupled memory architecture for long-horizon video generation that overcomes computational inefficiency and attention dispersion via Sparse Global Memory and Anchored Local Memory, enabling minute-level controllable long videos with high fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maintain fine-grained spatio-temporal consistency in long-horizon generation, existing learnable memory is inefficient and suffers from attention dispersion; there is a need for scalable, stable memory that supports both global history access and reliable extrapolation.

Method: Systematic analysis of attention dispersion; propose DecMem with two memory modules: Sparse Global Memory for efficient access to global history, and Anchored Local Memory for stable extrapolation; a decoupled architecture separating long-range memory from local generation.

Result: Extensive experiments show DecMem outperforms state-of-the-art methods in fidelity, consistency, and controllability for long video generation, enabling minute-level controllable generation with high fidelity.

Conclusion: DecMem provides precise and efficient long-term memory, enabling reliable long video generation; demonstrates the benefits of a decoupled, global-local memory design for controllable video synthesis.

Abstract: Recent advances in video generative models have promoted rapid progress in controllable world models. However, maintaining fine-grained spatio-temporal consistency under long-horizon reasoning remains a key challenge. In this work, we move beyond explicit 3D memory and coarse frame-level implicit modeling, and propose a fine-grained, learnable, and scalable memory for consistent world generation. We first identify two fundamental limitations of naïve learnable memory architectures in long-horizon extrapolation, namely computational inefficiency and attention dispersion. Through a systematic analysis of attention dispersion, we propose DecMem, a decoupled memory architecture that employs Sparse Global Memory for efficient fine-grained access to global history and Anchored Local Memory for stable and high-quality extrapolation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DecMem significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods. By ensuring precise and efficient long-term memory and achieving superior extrapolation capabilities, DecMem enables minute-level controllable long video generation with high fidelity and consistency.

</details>


### [90] [FSM-Net: An Efficient Frequency-Spatial Network for Real-World Deblurring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31400)
*Vinh-Thuan Ly*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FSM-Net is a dual-domain, efficient deblurring network that achieves high PSNR with low latency by combining a Frequency Attention module (FFT-based high-frequency recovery) and a Cross-Gated Vision E-Branchformer at the bottleneck, trained with a progressive curriculum and a composite loss. It placed 2nd in NTIRE 2026 Efficient Real-World Deblurring and reports 33.144 dB PSNR on RSBlur with 4.94M parameters and 159.35 GMACs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The problem of real-world image deblurring demands both high restoration fidelity and computational efficiency. Existing methods often sacrifice speed for quality or vice versa, hindering deployment on resource-constrained devices. FSM-Net aims to balance these requirements by design.

Method: Introduce a dual-domain architecture: (1) a Frequency Attention module that uses FFT to explicitly recover high-frequency structural details; (2) a Cross-Gated Vision E-Branchformer operating at the network bottleneck to capture global dependencies with linear complexity. Training uses a progressive curriculum with a composite loss: Multi-Scale Charbonnier, Structural Edge, and Frequency losses.

Result: On the RSBlur benchmark, FSM-Net achieves 33.144 dB PSNR with 4.94M parameters and 159.35 GMACs at 1920×1200 resolution, ranking 2nd in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Real-World Deblurring. The model effectively expands the Pareto frontier for efficiency-accuracy trade-offs.

Conclusion: FSM-Net demonstrates that a careful combination of frequency-domain processing and global dependency modeling at a low parameter/compute budget can deliver strong restoration performance while remaining suitable for resource-constrained settings, establishing a robust baseline for efficient real-world image restoration.

Abstract: Real-world image deblurring demands both high-fidelity restoration and computational efficiency, a balance existing methods often struggle to achieve. In this paper, we propose FSM-Net (Frequency-Spatial Multi-branch Network), a highly efficient solution that secured 2nd place in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Real-World Deblurring. FSM-Net pioneers a dual-domain approach: a novel Frequency Attention module explicitly recovers high-frequency structural details via FFT, while a Cross-Gated Vision E-Branchformer at the bottleneck captures global dependencies with linear complexity. To ensure robust convergence, we employ a progressive curriculum training strategy guided by a composite loss function (Multi-Scale Charbonnier, Structural Edge, and Frequency). Evaluated on the RSBlur benchmark, FSM-Net achieves an outstanding 33.144 dB PSNR with only 4.94M parameters and 159.35 GMACs (at 1920x1200 resolution). By effectively pushing the Pareto frontier of efficiency and quality, FSM-Net establishes a strong baseline for resource-constrained image restoration.

</details>


### [91] [YARD: Y-Architecture Register Decoding for Efficient Hallucination Mitigation in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31429)
*Ting Chen,Geng Li,Guohao Chen,Yu Hu,Guan Huang,Mai Chen,Langsheng Lei,Jun Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: YARD is a training-free contrastive decoding framework for LVLMs that internally constructs a degraded branch by sharing shallow layers and branching at the middle decoder layer; it replaces patch-level visual tokens with register tokens to retain global semantics while reducing local evidence, enabling a faithful and efficient contrastive signal without an extra forward pass, achieving state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation with lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training-free contrastive decoding methods either degrade visual input too aggressively (dropping tokens) causing language hallucinations or require two full forward passes for two models, incurring high latency and coarse control over visual evidence. A better degradation strategy is needed that preserves semantic grounding while enabling effective contrast.

Method: Introduce Y-Architecture Register Decoding (YARD). Build the degraded branch by sharing computations up to the middle decoder layer and branching there. In the degraded branch, replace patch-level visual tokens with register tokens to preserve global semantics but lack fine-grained local evidence. This yields a faithful contrastive signal without extra forward passes (training-free and architecture-level).

Result: Extensive experiments on both generative and discriminative hallucination benchmarks show that YARD achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation across multiple LVLMs and significantly reduces inference latency.

Conclusion: YARD provides an effective, efficient training-free solution for reducing hallucinations in LVLMs by architecting the degraded branch around the middle decoder layers and using register tokens, balancing robust grounding with computational efficiency.

Abstract: Contrastive decoding (CD) seeks to mitigate hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) by contrasting the output distributions of a standard model and a visually degraded model. However, existing training-free CD methods suffer from sub-optimal degraded branches: completely dropping visual tokens is too extreme and induces language hallucinations, while corrupting input images offers coarse control over visual evidence and suffers from high inference latency due to requiring two full forward passes. To address these dilemmas, we propose YARD, a training-free Y-Architecture Register Decoding framework. Motivated by the observation that reliable text-to-vision grounding predominantly emerges in the middle decoder layers, YARD constructs the degraded branch internally by sharing shallow-layer computations and branching exactly at this critical stage. For the degraded branch, YARD replaces patch-level visual tokens with register tokens, which preserve global image semantics but lack fine-grained local evidence. This image-aware yet locally under-grounded design provides a faithful contrastive signal without extreme modality mismatch, while the Y-architecture strictly avoids a costly second forward pass. Extensive experiments on generative and discriminative hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that YARD consistently achieves state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation across multiple LVLMs, alongside a significant reduction in inference latency.

</details>


### [92] [Astra: a generalizable report generation foundation model for 3D computed tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31437)
*Zhuhao Wang,Fang Chen,Chaohui Yu,Zihan Li,Yuchao Zheng,Jing Wang,Xuan Yang,Jia Guo,Zhenlu Yang,Xingju Zheng,Yihua Sun,Haojie Han,Xiaoxiao Qin,Zhan Feng,Wenbo Xiao,Chao Zhu,Yuehua Li,Shipeng Zhang,Hao Luo,Yunsong Peng,Fan Wang,Hongen Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Astra is a generalizable CT report generation foundation model trained on a large CTRgDB, achieving style-consistent and diagnostically accurate multi-region reporting across cohorts, with real-world workflow gains and potential as AI infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address variability in radiology reporting and lack of a cross-cohort CT report generation foundation capable of multi-region reporting and strong generalization.

Method: Train on 90,678 thoracoabdominal CT–report pairs (CTRgDB) covering 353,671 abnormalities across eight organ systems; harmonize report style and apply reinforcement learning to improve diagnostic consistency; evaluate on CTRgDB and six external cohorts; assess fine-grained diagnostic metrics and real-world workflow impact.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 44.1% average improvement in fine-grained diagnostic metrics (P<0.001); real-world workflow gains: chest drafting +29.6%, abdominal report completeness +11.3% (P<0.001).

Conclusion: Astra provides a broadly accessible CT report generation foundation model and serves as a scalable platform for future CT AI development across institutions and organ regions.

Abstract: CT interpretation requires radiologists to review hundreds of volumetric slices per examination, making reporting time-consuming and highly expertise-dependent. Automated CT report generation offers a promising route to improving clinical efficiency, yet the field still lacks a generalizable CT report generation foundation model that supports multi-region reporting and remains robust across external real-world cohorts. Intrinsic inconsistencies in reporting style and diagnostic terminology across cohorts make naive joint training prone to noisy textual supervision, thereby limiting model generalizability. Here we present Astra, a generalizable CT report generation foundation model trained on 90,678 thoracoabdominal CT-report pairs (CTRgDB) with 353,671 abnormalities spanning eight organ systems. By harmonizing report style and further refining diagnostic consistency via reinforcement learning, Astra achieves style-consistent and diagnostically accurate report generation across diverse anatomical regions and institutions. Evaluating on CTRgDB and six external cohorts, Astra achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 44.1% average improvement in fine-grained diagnostic metrics (P<0.001). In real-world clinical workflows, Astra assistance accelerates chest report drafting by 29.6% and improves abdominal report completeness by 11.3% (P<0.001). Furthermore, Astra also demonstrates broad utility as a foundation for CT AI development, improving downstream diagnostic performance and scaling vision-language pretrain through high-quality report synthesis. Overall, Astra serves as a broadly accessible clinical assistant and a pivotal infrastructure for the next generation of AI-powered healthcare.

</details>


### [93] [Envisioning Beyond the Few: Disentangled Semantics and Primitives for Few-Shot Atypical Layout-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31266)
*Nan Bao,Yifan Zhao,Wenzhuang Wang,Jia Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Represents fragmentation in layout-to-image generation under few-shot atypical settings due to a granularity mismatch; introduces a representation-driven framework with Semantic Anchoring, Primitive Imbuing, and Conceptual Steering to improve stability and fidelity in 5-shot L2I.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle representation fragmentation that conflates semantic identity with visual details in L2I, especially in few-shot atypical domains.

Method: Propose a two-fold representation-driven framework: Semantic Anchoring aggregates category semantics into stable anchors; Primitive Imbuing recomposes primitives for robust local detail modeling; Conceptual Steering adds a saliency-aware objective to preserve foreground semantic consistency during optimization.

Result: Experiments show consistent improvements in 5-shot L2I over state-of-the-art methods in visual fidelity and semantic alignment across diverse atypical domains; source code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Disentangling semantics from primitives with saliency-guided optimization yields robust few-shot L2I performance and better domain adaptability.

Abstract: The layout-to-image (L2I) task enables fine-grained control over image generation via object categories and spatial layouts. However, existing L2I methods yield fragmented and distorted generations under few-shot atypical settings. We term this failure as representation fragmentation, arising from a granularity mismatch that entangles semantic identity with visual details. To address this issue, we propose a representation-driven framework that disentangles semantics from primitives for robust few-shot adaptation. Specifically, Semantic Anchoring aggregates categorical semantics into anchors for stable identity, while Primitive Imbuing models recomposable primitives for robust local detail modeling. Conceptual Steering further regulates optimization with a saliency-aware objective to preserve foreground semantic consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in the 5-shot regime over state-of-the-art L2I methods in both visual fidelity and alignment across diverse atypical domains. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/iCVTEAM/DSP.

</details>


### [94] [VisionPulse: Dynamic Visual Sparsity for Efficient Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31457)
*Hengbo Xu,Shengjie Jin,Yanbiao Ma,Zhiwu Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VisionPulse introduces step-wise pruning of visual tokens during reasoning in large multimodal models, achieving strong sparsity and shorter reasoning traces with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time overhead in large multimodal models is a bottleneck; prefill pruning assumes static visual evidence, but evidence is dynamic across decoding steps, leading to inefficiency and possible misguidance from redundant context.

Method: Compute a lightweight visual attention mass to estimate a per-step retention budget; retain the most critical visual tokens under this budget; enforce sparsity during reasoning to filter redundant context while preserving relevant evidence.

Result: Retains about 5% of visual tokens per step and reduces reasoning traces by 11.2% with accuracy remaining largely unchanged.

Conclusion: Step-wise visual token pruning is effective for reducing inference and trace length in LMMs without substantial loss in accuracy, highlighting the importance of dynamic, per-step visual context management.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large multimodal models (LMMs), inference-time overhead has become a key bottleneck for real-world deployment. Existing methods typically prune visual tokens at prefill, assuming the required visual evidence remains static during reasoning. However, we empirically show that visual evidence is strongly step-dependent: only a sparse subset of visual tokens is critical at each decoding step, and the critical set evolves across reasoning. Furthermore, we identify a coupled bottleneck where redundant visual context can steer the model toward query-irrelevant regions, lengthening the reasoning trace. Guided by these insights, we propose VisionPulse, a step-wise visual token pruning framework during reasoning. VisionPulse computes a lightweight visual attention mass to estimate the step-wise retention budget by exploiting its strong positive correlation with LMMs' effective visual token usage and retain only the most critical tokens under this budget. By enforcing visual sparsity during reasoning, VisionPulse filters redundant visual context while preserving relevant visual evidence, shortening reasoning traces naturally. Extensive experiments show that VisionPulse only retains 5% of visual tokens per step with reasoning traces shortened by 11.2%, while keeping accuracy almost unchanged.

</details>


### [95] [VolFill: Single-View Amodal 3D Scene Reconstruction with Volumetric Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31466)
*Tuan Duc Ngo,Chuang Gan,Evangelos Kalogerakis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VolFill proposes a generative 3D reconstruction framework that predicts complete scene geometry from a single RGB image by compressing sparse truncated unsigned distance function grids with a 3D VAE and denoising via a latent Diffusion Transformer, conditioned on geometry foundation models to yield a structured representation for surface extraction and occupancy queries, outperforming baselines on SCRREAM and NRGB-D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of recovering hidden scene structures from limited visual evidence and move beyond per-ray regression and unstructured point clouds to a holistic, queryable 3D representation that leverages strong spatial priors.

Method: A hybrid 3D VAE compresses sparse truncated unsigned distance function (T-UDS) grids into a latent space; a latent Diffusion Transformer denoises the latent codes to reconstruct the complete scene. Generation is conditioned on geometry foundation models to incorporate rich spatial priors. The output is a structured 3D representation enabling direct surface extraction and occupancy queries at scale.

Result: Extensive experiments on SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets show that VolFill significantly outperforms current baselines, demonstrating robust holistic spatial understanding and improved reconstruction quality over competing methods.

Conclusion: VolFill offers a scalable, structured 3D scene representation that supports surface extraction and occupancy queries, driven by priors from geometry foundation models, and establishes a strong baseline for holistic scene understanding from a single image.

Abstract: Reconstructing the complete geometry of a scene from a single RGB image remains challenging - especially when inferring hidden structures where visual evidence is incomplete. We introduce VolFill, a generative framework that predicts the 3D structure of the complete scene rather than relying on traditional pixel-aligned regression. Our method utilizes a hybrid 3D VAE to compress sparse truncated unsigned distance function grids into a compact latent space, paired with a latent Diffusion Transformer that denoises this representation to recover the complete scene. We condition the generation on geometry foundation models, leveraging rich spatial priors for robust reasoning. Unlike existing methods limited by per-ray constraints or unstructured point-cloud queries, VolFill provides a structured representation that supports direct surface extraction and occupancy queries at scale. Extensive experiments on the SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms current baselines, providing a robust foundation for holistic spatial understanding.

</details>


### [96] [Enhancing Computer Vision Model Generalization in Warehouse Facilities: A Case Study on Anomaly Detection in Vertical Material Handling Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31487)
*Ruiliang Liu,Tina Dongxu Li,Joshua Migdal,Ken Meszaros,Trevor Dardik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lab-only development for warehouse CV: optimize camera placement, triggering, model selection, and ensembles to generalize from lab to diverse facilities for vertical material handling fork anomaly detection; experimental and not production deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying computer vision in warehouse facilities is resource-intensive due to camera mounting constraints, image collection, annotation, training, and per-environment variability; a streamlined lab-based approach could reduce these burdens.

Method: Conduct the standard development pipeline entirely in a laboratory setting, focusing on vertical material handling systems and fork anomaly detection; systematically optimize camera placement, image triggering, model selection, and model ensembles; evaluate generalization to diverse, real-world warehouse environments.

Result: The integrated optimization approach enabled effective generalization from laboratory conditions to varied facility environments, suggesting substantial reductions in image annotation and retraining needs while enabling deployment with only camera mounting, image collection, and model deployment in new facilities.

Conclusion: If validated, this lab-centric approach could transform warehouse automation deployment by drastically reducing data collection and retraining requirements; it is an experimental study, not a production deployment.

Abstract: Deploying computer vision models in Warehouse Facilities traditionally requires extensive resources for camera mounting, image collection, annotation, training, and deployment - a process often needing repetition in each new environment due to camera mounting constraints and environmental variability. This paper explores an innovative approach to streamline this process by conducting the standard procedure solely in a laboratory setting, focusing on vertical material handling systems and anomaly detection in forks of the systems. Through extensive experimentation, we have found that combining optimal camera placement, strategic image triggering, careful model selection and model ensemble enables effective generalization from laboratory conditions to diverse warehouse facilities environments, potentially transforming warehouse automation implementation by simplifying warehouse facilities deployment to just camera mounting, image collection, and model deployment, thereby saving significant resources and time typically spent on image annotation and model retraining. This is an experimental research study and not a production deployment.

</details>


### [97] [How can embedding models bind concepts?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31503)
*Arnas Uselis,Darina Koishigarina,Seong Joon Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLIP-like vision–language models struggle with concept binding; scene embeddings decompose additively, but binding is high-complexity; with sufficient data coverage in controlled transformers, low-complexity, multiplicative binding generalizes to unseen concept combinations; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether binding failures in multimodal models are due to fundamental limitations or data-driven factors; quantify binding function complexity and its impact on generalization.

Method: 1) Analyze CLIP-style embeddings to characterize binding function; 2) Define and measure additive decomposition of scene embeddings; 3) Train controlled transformer models from scratch with varying data coverage to examine binding generalization; 4) Compare uni- and cross-modal retrieval and inspect recoverability of object information; 5) Characterize binding function as multiplicative interactions in low-complexity regime.

Result: CLIP's binding function exhibits high complexity, hindering generalization; object information can be recovered from unimodal embeddings; in data-rich controlled transformers, binding generalizes via low-complexity multiplicative interactions, enabling systematic generalization.

Conclusion: Binding generalization is not fundamental; with sufficient coverage and appropriate inductive bias (multiplicative binding), models can generalize binding to unseen concept combinations; this supports constructing more robust multimodal models.

Abstract: Humans easily determine which color belongs to which shape in multi-object scenes, an ability known as concept binding. Vision-language embedding models such as CLIP struggle with binding: they recognize individual concepts but fail to represent which concepts form which objects. Although CLIP behaves like a bag-of-concepts model in cross-modal retrieval, object information is recoverable from its image and text embeddings separately. We study this tension through the binding function, which maps concepts to scene embeddings. We find that scene embeddings decompose additively into object representations, explaining why uni-modal probes can recover object information. However, CLIP's binding function is high-complexity, which likely prevents the image and text encoders from learning a shared binding mechanism that generalizes to unseen concept combinations. We then ask whether this limitation is fundamental. We show that it is not. In controlled transformer models trained from scratch, binding generalization emerges with sufficient data coverage. These models learn low-complexity binding functions characterized by multiplicative interactions between concepts, enabling systematic generalization. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/oshapio/binding-concepts-complexity.

</details>


### [98] [Internalizing Temporal Consistency in Video Object-Centric Learning without Explicit Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31508)
*Rongzhen Zhao,Zhiyuan Li,Juho Kannala,Joni Pajarinen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Propose xSSC (no SSC) for video OCL by two quasi-zero-overhead mechanisms: (1) Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) splits slot representations into static and dynamic subspaces; (2) Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) reconstructs target features by fusing current static channels and target dynamic channels, using a standard OCL decoder. This implicit design enforces temporal consistency without explicit cross-frame correspondence losses. Results show improved training efficiency and SOTA on video object discovery/recognition; PCA/gradient analyses indicate time-invariant semantics and time-variant kinematics encoded in the subspaces; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To avoid reliance on a one-to-one object correspondence and the SSC loss, and to enforce temporal consistency as an intrinsic model property rather than an external objective, thereby reducing overhead and improving efficiency.

Method: Introduce Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) to disentangle slot representations along channel dimension into static (time-invariant) and dynamic (time-variant) sub-spaces. Introduce Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) that stochastically reconstructs target features of either the current or previous time step by fusing current slots' static channels with target slots' dynamic channels, using a standard OCL decoder with minor training adaptations.

Result: Empirically, xSSC yields improved training efficiency and establishes new SOTA on video object discovery and recognition. PCA and gradient analyses confirm that time-invariant semantics and time-variant kinematics are captured in the proposed sub-spaces.

Conclusion: Temporal consistency in video OCL can be achieved via implicit architectural design rather than explicit SSC loss. The CCD/CTR subspaces effectively separate semantics/kinematics, enabling robust OCL without SSC overhead; code and models are released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Video Object-Centric Learning (OCL) aims to represent objects as \textit{slot} vectors and maintain their consistency across frames. Slot-Slot Contrastive (SSC) loss has become the cornerstone for state-of-the-art (SOTA) video OCL methods. While highly effective, SSC relies on one-to-one object correspondence across frames and introduces an extra loss. Following Occam's Razor, we propose a paradigm shift: temporal consistency is better enforced as an implicit model design rather than an explicit loss. To elegantly exclude SSC (\textbf{xSSC}), we introduce two quasi-zero-overhead synergistic mechanisms: (\textit{i}) Chrono-Channel Decomposition (CCD) structurally disentangles slot representations along the channel dimension into \textit{static} and \textit{dynamic} sub-spaces, serving as an empirically unified information bottleneck; (\textit{ii}) Cross-Temporal Reconstruction (CTR) stochastically reconstructs target features of either the current or previous time step by fusing current slots' static channels and target slots' dynamic channels, using a single standard OCL decoder with minor training adaptation. Thereby, the slot sets inherently learn temporal consistency by minimizing the standard reconstruction error alone. Extensive experiments show that integrating xSSC into leading baselines not only improves training efficiency but also establishes new SOTAs on video object discovery and recognition tasks. Furthermore, our PCA and gradient analyses confirm that objects' time-invariant semantics and time-variant kinematics are encoded into the proposed sub-spaces. Our source code, model checkpoints and training logs are provided on https://github.com/Genera1Z/xSSC.

</details>


### [99] [Personalize Your Large Vision-language Models With In-context Prompt Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31513)
*Yanshu Li,Jiaqian Li,Kuai Yu,Xi Xiao,Dongfang Liu,Tianyang Wang,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ICPT is an efficient LVLM personalization method that uses a lightweight projection module to extract fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images and turn them, with identity-label mappings, into continuous prompts. It adaptively sets prompt length based on concept complexity and employs two geometric regularizations to reduce environmental bias and cross-concept interference. It achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for fast, out-of-distribution multimodal concept adaptation in LVLMs. Existing methods rely on inference-time training, which is inefficient and struggles in complex multi-image/multi-concept scenarios, with environmental bias and cross-concept interference hindering deployment.

Method: Propose in-context prompt tuning (ICPT) featuring a lightweight projection module that operates in complex scenarios to extract fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images and transforms these features alongside identity-label mappings into continuous prompts. The module adaptively determines prompt length based on intrinsic visual complexity of each concept. Two geometric regularizations decouple identities from transient environmental states and separate concepts to prevent semantic confusion.

Result: Extensive experiments show ICPT achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones, validating both effectiveness and efficiency.

Conclusion: ICPT offers a scalable, robust personalization mechanism for LVLMs, addressing environmental biases and interference between concepts, thereby enabling broader deployment of personalized LVLM systems.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated strong general multimodal capability and are increasingly deployed in downstream systems. This trend has driven growing interest in LVLM personalization, which aims to enable models to quickly and effectively learn out-of-distribution multimodal concepts to meet user-specific needs. However, many existing methods rely on inference-time training, which reduces efficiency. They also struggle to maintain accuracy in complex multi-image, multi-concept settings. These limitations restrict the broader deployment of LVLM-based systems. Therefore, this paper proposes in-context prompt tuning (ICPT). Specifically, ICPT employs a lightweight projection module capable of operating in complex scenarios to extract fine-grained visual semantics from multiple reference images, seamlessly transforming these features alongside identity-label mappings into continuous prompts. To maximize computational efficiency, this module adaptively determines the prompt length based on the intrinsic visual complexity of each concept. Crucially, to overcome the environmental biases and cross-concept interference prevalent in real-world applications, we introduce two novel geometric regularizations. These constraints refine prompt representations by decoupling key identities from transient environmental states and separating concepts to avoid semantic confusion. Extensive experiments show that ICPT achieves state-of-the-art personalization accuracy across diverse tasks and LVLM backbones.

</details>


### [100] [SVI-Bench: A Dynamic Microworld for Strategic Video Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31529)
*Yulu Pan,Han Yi,Seongsu Ha,Md Mohaiminul Islam,Benjamin Zhang,Lorenzo Torresani,Gedas Bertasius*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SVI-Bench is a large-scale, sports-based, multi-task benchmark for Strategic Video Intelligence, spanning perception to agentic synthesis, revealing a large gap between perceptual and cognitive/agentive capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a verifiable, complex, multi-agent microworld benchmark with ground-truth rules/outcomes for evaluating causal reasoning, simulation, and planning in video intelligence, addressing gaps in existing benchmarks.

Method: Create an engineered data pipeline transforming raw broadcast data into a dense cross-referenced corpus; assemble ~35k hours of video, 15M annotated actions, 15k hours of expert commentary, 23k game reports, 103k statistics across basketball, soccer, hockey; define 9 tasks across four pillars (Dynamic Scene Understanding, Causal Reasoning, Strategic Simulation, Agentic Synthesis); evaluate strong baselines.

Result: Models excel at perceptual tasks (~73% on fine-grained action QA) but sharply drop across cognitive levels; agentic tasks are hardest with strongest model at 5% accuracy on autonomous evidence gathering across 1.8M clips.

Conclusion: There is a capability cliff from perception to strategic cognition; SVI-Bench provides a scalable, verifiable platform to drive progress in causal reasoning, simulation, and agentic planning in video intelligence; suggests need for advanced multimodal and multi-agent methods.

Abstract: True video intelligence demands more than recognizing what is visible: it requires reasoning about why events unfold, predicting what would change under different conditions, and deciding what to do next. We refer to this progression, from perception through causal reasoning and simulation to strategic planning, as Strategic Video Intelligence (SVI). No existing benchmark evaluates this capability stack: in-the-wild videos lack verifiable ground truth for causal and strategic questions, while synthetic environments sacrifice the complexity of real multi-agent systems. To bridge this gap, we introduce SVI-Bench, a large-scale benchmark that leverages team sports as a dynamic microworld, combining the complexity of real-world multi-agent interaction (10-22 agents making coordinated decisions under adversarial pressure) with the verifiability of explicit rules and definitive outcomes. SVI-Bench comprises approximately 35K hours of broadcast video, 15M annotated actions, 15K hours of expert commentary, 23K game reports, and 103K structured statistical records across basketball, soccer, and hockey, all constructed via a data engine that transforms raw game data into a dense, cross-referenced corpus. We organize evaluation into 9 tasks spanning a progressive four-pillar hierarchy: Dynamic Scene Understanding, Causal Reasoning, Strategic Simulation, and Agentic Synthesis. Evaluating strong multimodal and agentic baselines, we find a capability cliff: models perform competently on perceptual tasks, achieving approximately 73% on fine-grained action QA, but degrade sharply at each successive cognitive level. Agentic tasks prove hardest: the strongest model achieves only 5% accuracy when required to autonomously gather and integrate evidence across a corpus of 1.8M clips.

</details>


### [101] [Feature-Optimized Vision for Adaptive 3D Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31534)
*Eric Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adaptive feature selection front-end for 3D reconstruction that allocates per-view feature budgets based on multi-criterion feature scoring, improving quality-aware completeness and reducing RMSE compared with baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed thresholds and uniform feature budgets waste computation on redundant or unstable image evidence; an adaptive, quality-aware front end can focus resources on discriminative, well-conditioned features to improve 3D reconstruction.

Method: Score candidate features by texture, repeatability, distinctiveness, expected triangulation angle, and spatial coverage; allocate a per-view feature budget to maximize useful tracks within a fixed reconstruction pipeline; evaluate four selection policies (adaptive, random, texture-only, uniform-grid) on synthetic multi-view scenes (corridor, facade, object-table, cluttered).

Result: Adaptive policy achieves best quality-aware completeness and lowest aggregate RMSE among tested policies while maintaining broad image coverage, outperforming random, texture-only, and uniform-grid baselines.

Conclusion: The adaptive front end is a modular improvement, not a replacement for modern learned matching or neural pipelines; it helps both classical and learned 3D reconstruction systems allocate computational resources more deliberately by prioritizing informative evidence.

Abstract: Three-dimensional scene reconstruction depends on local image evidence that is both visually discriminative and geometrically useful. Fixed feature thresholds and uniform feature budgets are easy to deploy, but they can waste computation on repeated texture, low-parallax regions, or unstable points. This paper proposes an adaptive feature-optimized vision front end for 3D reconstruction. The method scores candidate features by texture, repeatability, distinctiveness, expected triangulation angle, and spatial coverage, then allocates a per-view feature budget to maximize useful tracks under a fixed reconstruction pipeline. A small synthetic multi-view prototype evaluates four selection policies across corridor, facade, object-table, and cluttered scenes. Compared with random, texture-only, and uniform-grid baselines, the adaptive policy obtains the best quality-aware completeness and the lowest aggregate reconstruction RMSE while preserving broad image coverage. The result is not a replacement for modern learned matching or neural reconstruction systems; it is a modular front-end policy that can make classical and learned 3D pipelines more deliberate about which visual evidence they spend compute on.

</details>


### [102] [RayDer: Scalable Self-Supervised Novel View Synthesis from Real-World Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31535)
*Ulrich Prestel,Stefan Andreas Baumann,Nick Stracke,Björn Ommer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RayDer is a unified feed-forward transformer for self-supervised novel view synthesis that bundles camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering into a single model, enabling data-and-compute scaling with a minimal dynamic state to handle time-varying content. It targets static-scene NVS and uses dynamic content as scalable supervision, achieving power-law scaling and competitive zero-shot performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised NVS on realistic videos is brittle and scales poorly when using multi-network designs. A single-model, scalable approach that can absorb time-varying content without compromising static-scene NVS is highly desirable.

Method: Introduce RayDer, a unified transformer backbone that performs camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering in one feed-forward network. Use a minimal dynamic state as a nuisance factor to absorb time-varying content, enabling stable training on unconstrained real-world video. Dynamic content guides supervision but is not reconstructed as in dynamic-scene NVS. Emphasize pure static-scene NVS as the target task and study scaling across model sizes and data, reporting power-law scaling.

Result: RayDer exhibits clean power-law scaling with data and compute across multiple model sizes, outperforms mixtures of static-scene data, and achieves strong zero-shot open-set performance on a large suite of benchmarks, competitive with state-of-the-art supervised methods.

Conclusion: A single-model, unified transformer can scale NVS effectively by treating dynamic content as scalable supervision rather than a reconstruction target, preserving static-scene NVS performance while enabling robust zero-shot generalization and data-driven scaling behavior.

Abstract: Self-supervised novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging to scale, despite the abundance of video data, largely due to the brittleness of training on realistic videos and the hard-to-predict scaling behavior of multi-network system designs. We introduce RayDer, a unified, feed-forward transformer that consolidates camera estimation, scene reconstruction, and rendering into a single backbone, turning self-supervised NVS into a well-posed single-model scaling problem. A minimal dynamic state, treated as a nuisance factor, absorbs time-varying content and enables stable training on unconstrained real-world video. Importantly, RayDer keeps static-scene NVS as its target task: dynamic content is leveraged purely as scalable supervision, not reconstructed as in dynamic-scene (4D) NVS. Across multiple model sizes and orders of magnitude in data, RayDer exhibits clean power-law scaling with data and compute, and outperforms static-scene data mixtures. On a large number of benchmarks, RayDer achieves strong zero-shot open-set performance competitive with state-of-the-art supervised approaches. Project Page: https://compvis.github.io/rayder

</details>


### [103] [Automated Prediction of Postoperative Pancreatic Fistula Using Preoperative Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31539)
*Ashok Choudhary,Chris Varghese,Leo Y. Li-Han,Frank G. Lee,Ellen L. Larson,Elizabeth B. Habermann,Cornelius A. Thiels,Hojjat Salehinejad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end deep learning pipeline estimates preoperative POPF risk from preoperative CT using auto-segmented pancreas, evaluating multiple 3D CNNs with promising performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: POPF after pancreatic resection increases morbidity and cost; a preoperative, imaging-based risk stratification tool using automated pancreas segmentation could inform surgical decisions.

Method: Developed an automatic segmentation-to-classification pipeline using auto-segmented pancreas volumes from preoperative CT; evaluated multiple 3D architectures (CNN3D baseline, R(2+1)D ResNet-18, ResNet-MC3-18) on a dataset with surgical outcomes.

Result: Models across several 3D architectures demonstrated promising predictive performance for POPF risk.

Conclusion: Proposes a clinically valuable tool and methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification to support improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

Abstract: Postoperative pancreatic fistula (POPF) is a serious complication after pancreatic resection, increasing morbidity, hospital stay, and healthcare costs. We present an automatic, end-to-end deep learning pipeline-from pancreatic segmentation to classification-for preoperative POPF risk estimation and stratification using preoperative CT scans. A data set with auto-segmented pancreas volumes and surgical outcomes was used to evaluate multiple architectures, including a custom lightweight 3D CNN baseline (CNN3D), R(2+1)D ResNet-18, and ResNet-MC3-18 models. Evaluation across multiple 3D architectures demonstrated promising predictive performance. This approach offers a clinically valuable tool and a methodological benchmark for pancreas-specific CT classification, supporting improved preoperative decision-making in pancreatic surgery.

</details>


### [104] [SMART: SMPLest-X Mesh Adaptation and RAFT Tracking for Soccer Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31551)
*Parthsarthi Rawat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fine-tunes SMPLest-X with stratified clip splits, depth supervision, and broadcast augmentation, plus RAFT tracking and temporal smoothing; yields strong FIFA 2026 results: 0.647 validation score (baseline 1.053; 38.6% improvement) and 0.593 on held-out test (Global MPJPE 0.324 m, Local MPJPE 0.054 m).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need to estimate 3D world-space poses of soccer players from broadcast video for sports analytics and competition benchmarks, despite challenging broadcast cues and occlusions.

Method: Fine-tune SMPLest-X (ViT-H, 687M params) via a stratified clip split, multi-task depth supervision, and broadcast augmentation. Integrate with a RAFT dense optical flow camera tracker, foot-plane anchoring, and two-pass temporal smoothing to improve temporal consistency and camera motion handling.

Result: Validation score against FIFA baseline 1.053: SMART = 0.647 (38.6% improvement). Held-out test score: 0.593. Global MPJPE = 0.324 m; Local MPJPE = 0.054 m.

Conclusion: The approach yields substantial performance gains over the baseline, validating the effectiveness of combining high-capacity SMPL-based 3D pose models with broadcast-aware augmentation, optical-flow tracking, and temporal smoothing for soccer pose estimation from broadcast video.

Abstract: We present our approach to the FIFA Skeletal Tracking Challenge 2026, which requires estimating 3D world-space poses of soccer players from broadcast video. Our method finetunes SMPLest-X (ViT-H, 687 M parameters) via a stratified clip split, multi-task depth supervision, and broadcast augmentation, paired with a RAFT dense optical flow camera tracker, foot-plane anchoring, and two-pass temporal smoothing.
