Against the FIFA baseline score of 1.053 on the validation set, SMART achieves 0.647, a 38.6% improvement; on the held-out test set, SMART scores 0.593 (Global MPJPE: 0.324 m, Local MPJPE: 0.054 m).

</details>


### [105] [Vision-Language Models Suppress Female Representations Under Ambiguous Input](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31556)
*Arnau Marin-Llobet,Simon Henniger,Mahzarin R. Banaji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ambiguity in inputs reveals a misalignment: internal female associations exist while outputs skew male; a new zero-shot metric (LALS) shows layer-specific amplification of male signals and color cues modulating bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand whether bias mitigation in vision-language model alignment extends to gender-ambiguous inputs and quantify internal vs. output biases.

Method: Introduce Latent Association Leaning Score (LALS), a zero-shot metric that projects visual-token activations into the model's text-embedding space to measure concept associations per token and per layer. Evaluate across 15 occupations, >800 gender-ambiguous images, and four VLMs. Perform layer-wise analysis and color ablation to study internal representations versus outputs.

Result: Internally, models encode female associations for gender-ambiguous prompts, but outputs skew male. Across layers, a male signal is amplified end-to-end, while the female signal peaks mid-network and is suppressed before generation. Clothing color modulates internal associations, indicating culturally loaded visual cues influence biases.

Conclusion: There is a decoupling between internal concept representations and generated outputs in the presence of ambiguity. Alignment methods may reduce bias in clear cases but do not fully align latent representations with outputs; broader analysis of internal token-embedding spaces and sensory cues is needed to address latent biases.

Abstract: Alignment teaches vision-language models (VLMs) to avoid expressing demographic biases, and when gender is clearly visible they largely succeed. Far less is known about ambiguous inputs (a worker in full gear, a figure seen from behind) cases common in practice yet rarely studied. We find that minimal prompting pressure exposes occupation-gender defaults when prompting ambiguous input images, with models collapsing to male even for strongly female-stereotyped occupations. But do these outputs reflect what models actually encode internally? We introduce LALS (Latent Association Leaning Score), a zero-shot metric that projects visual-token activations into the model's text-embedding space to measure concept associations per token and layer. Across 15 occupations, over 800 gender-ambiguous images, and four VLMs, internal representations and outputs are systematically decoupled: models often encode a female association internally yet output male. Layer-wise analysis reveals an asymmetric filter -- male signal amplifies end-to-end while female signal peaks mid-network and is suppressed before generation -- and a color ablation shows that culturally loaded visual cues such as clothing color further modulate these internal associations.

</details>


### [106] [EGOSTREAM: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Streaming Episodic Memory in Egocentric Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31557)
*Rosario Forte,Giuseppe Lando,Antonino Furnari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diagnostic benchmark (egostream) for streaming episodic memory in egocentric vision, with AVW-based recall evaluations (8,528 tasks across 7 cognitive dimensions) evaluated under a unified Qwen3-VL backbone; reveals divergent memory profiles among memory-management strategies and suboptimal real-time performance, highlighting gaps in current architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current streaming video benchmarks have limited diagnostics for what models remember and for how long in dynamic real-world scenes; a targeted benchmark is needed to dissect memory capabilities and forgetting vs. world-state changes.

Method: Construct egostream with 2,250 questions spanning seven cognitive dimensions; introduce Answer Validity Window (AVW) to create recall-conditioned evaluations (8,528 total) and test memory across instant to ultra-long-term recall; implement a unified MLLM framework (Qwen3-VL) with multiple memory-management mechanisms (sliding windows, attention sinks, KV-cache pruning, merging, offloading) and compare their memory profiles.

Result: Analogous aggregate accuracies mask distinct memory profiles: token pruning retains fine-grained detail and temporal structure better than token merging; quantized offloading supports ultra-long-term recall. All mechanisms fall short of real-time (>1s/frame); top methods cap ~45% accuracy, indicating substantial gaps in current architectures.

Conclusion: egostream serves as a diagnostic testbed to analyze and close gaps in streaming episodic memory, guiding architectural improvements and memory-management strategies for sustained, real-world performance.

Abstract: Continuous episodic memory is a core capability for autonomous agents operating in dynamic, real-world environments, yet current streaming video benchmarks provide limited tools for diagnosing what models remember and for how long. We introduce \egostream, a diagnostic benchmark for streaming episodic memory evaluation in egocentric vision. \egostream organizes 2,250 curated questions along seven cognitive dimensions: detail, spatial, temporal, event, social, causal, and prospective memory. We introduce the Answer Validity Window (AVW), which specifies the temporal span an answer remains valid as the observed scene evolves. This allows us to expand the questions into 8,528 recall-conditioned evaluations, enabling controlled testing from instant to ultra-long-term recall while separating genuine model forgetting from natural world-state changes. We rigorously establish baseline performance through a unified streaming MLLM framework that compares several state-of-the-art memory-management mechanisms, covering sliding windows, attention sinks, KV-cache pruning, merging, and offloading. Experiments within a unified Qwen3-VL backbone reveal that comparable aggregate accuracies mask starkly different memory profiles. For instance, token pruning preserves fine-grained details and temporal structure significantly better than token merging, while quantized offloading rescues ultra-long-term recall. Ultimately, all mechanisms operate well below real-time (>1s per frame), and top performing methods ceil at about 45\% accuracy, exposing critical gaps in current architectures. \egostream provides the diagnostic testbed needed to close these gaps.

</details>


### [107] [nuReasoning: A Reasoning-Centric Dataset and Benchmark for Long-Tail Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31572)
*Zhiyu Huang,Johnson Liu,Rui Song,Zewei Zhou,Ruining Yang,Yun Zhang,Tianhui Cai,Hanyin Zhang,Mingxuan Gao,Valeria Xu,Jiali Chen,Yishan Shen,Yiluan Guo,Tony,Qi,Jiaqi Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: nuReasoning is a large-scale, real-world dataset and benchmark for reasoning-centric autonomous driving, featuring 20k 20-second clips with multi-modal data and human-verified reasoning annotations, enabling both reasoning evaluation and planning evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving datasets primarily target perception, prediction, or planning with limited supervision for reasoning over realistic long-tail scenes; there is a need for a dataset that directly supports reasoning-centric evaluation and supervision to improve robustness and interpretability.

Method: Data collection of 20,000 clips (20s each) across multiple cities with synchronized multi-camera images, LiDAR, HD maps, object annotations, and human-verified reasoning annotations covering Spatial, Decision, and Counterfactual Reasoning; provides benchmarking for both reasoning and planning; experiments show benefits of reasoning supervision for VLM and VLA training.

Result: Fine-tuning vision-language models on nuReasoning improves driving-specific question answering; incorporating reasoning supervision into vision-language action (VLA) training enhances planning performance even when textual reasoning outputs are disabled at inference.

Conclusion: nuReasoning establishes a foundation for evaluating and improving robust, interpretable, reasoning-driven autonomous driving in realistic long-tail settings, by enabling direct study of reasoning supervision effects on downstream driving tasks.

Abstract: Reasoning is essential for autonomous driving (AD) in long-tail scenarios, where vehicles must apply commonsense knowledge, understand spatial relations, infer agent interactions, and make safe decisions. However, existing AD datasets and benchmarks mainly target perception, prediction, or planning, and provide limited supervision for reasoning over realistic long-tail driving scenes. We introduce nuReasoning, a large-scale real-world dataset and benchmark for reasoning-centric AD. Following the lineage of nuScenes and nuPlan, nuReasoning advances real-world AD datasets and benchmarks toward reasoning in long-tail driving scenarios. The dataset contains 20,000 clips, each 20 seconds long, collected across multiple cities, with synchronized multi-camera images, LiDAR data, HD maps, object annotations, and human-verified reasoning annotations spanning Spatial Reasoning, Decision Reasoning, and Counterfactual Reasoning. Unlike prior datasets that focus primarily on visual question answering, nuReasoning supports both reasoning evaluation and planning evaluation, enabling a direct study of how reasoning supervision affects driving performance. Experiments show that fine-tuning VLMs on nuReasoning substantially improves driving-specific question answering, while incorporating reasoning supervision into VLA training improves planning performance even when textual reasoning outputs are disabled at inference time. These results establish nuReasoning as a foundation for evaluating and improving robust, interpretable, reasoning-driven AD systems in realistic long-tail settings.

</details>


### [108] [Joint Multi-Camera LiDAR Extrinsic Calibration via Learned Pairwise Initialization and Geometric Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31576)
*Aziz Al-Najjar,Marzieh Amini,James R. Green,Felix Kwamena*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage joint calibration framework that refines per-camera CMRNext estimates via multi-frame bundle adjustment to ensure global consistency across a multi-camera LiDAR setup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-camera platforms, treating camera-LiDAR pairs independently yields accurate per-camera estimates but inconsistent system-wide extrinsics due to ignoring rigid geometric coupling.

Method: Independently apply CMRNext to each camera for initial extrinsics and dense 2D-3D correspondences; then jointly refine with a multi-frame bundle adjustment incorporating reprojection, per-camera priors, and relative-pose priors to enforce global consistency.

Result: Per-camera accuracy and inter-camera consistency improved; KITTI in-domain: translation 0.89 cm, rotation 0.038; Walkley out-of-domain: translation reduced from 108.6 cm to 3.1 cm.

Conclusion: Explicit multi-camera coupling yields robust global calibration, especially when single-camera predictions are unreliable.

Abstract: Most learning-based camera-LiDAR calibration methods treat each camera-LiDAR pair independently, ignoring the rigid geometric coupling in multi-camera platforms. As a result, per-camera estimates may be individually accurate yet inconsistent at the system level. We present a two-stage framework for joint multi-camera LiDAR extrinsic calibration that combines learned pairwise matching with geometric refinement. First, CMRNext is applied independently to each camera to produce initial extrinsic estimates and dense 2D-3D correspondences. These predictions are then jointly refined through a multi-frame bundle adjustment with reprojection, per-camera prior, and relative-pose prior terms. This approach converts pairwise predictions into a globally consistent multi-camera calibration. Experiments on KITTI (in-domain for CMRNext) and Walkley (out-of-domain) datasets show improved per-camera accuracy and inter-camera consistency. On KITTI, the method achieves 0.89 cm translation error and 0.038 rotation error. On Walkley, it reduces translation error from 108.6 cm to 3.1 cm, highlighting the benefit of explicit multi-camera coupling when single-camera predictions are less reliable.

</details>


### [109] [TunerDiT: Training-free Progressive Steering of Diffusion Transformer for Multi-Event Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31590)
*Ruotong Liao,Guowen Huang,Qing Cheng,Guangyao Zhai,Lei Zhang,Xun Xiao,Thomas Seidl,Daniel Cremers,Volker Tresp*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TunerDiT introduces a training-free progressive steering method for multi-event long-horizon T2V generation, using two mechanisms (Event-Partitioned Masking and Cross-Event Prompt Fusion) guided by intrinsic turning points in DiT denoising; paired with a benchmarking suite (Meve), it achieves state-of-the-art across 8 metrics and scales with event count, balancing video consistency and event separation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon text-to-video generation struggles to enforce clear event boundaries while preserving global layout; prior methods may require training or struggle with event transitions. The authors leverage diffusion process insights to identify turning points where text conditioning influences global vs. fine-grained details, enabling a training-free, progressive steering approach.

Method: TunerDiT applies two steering handles during inference: (1) Event-Partitioned Masking constrains event boundaries while allowing transition bands; (2) Cross-Event Prompt Fusion injects neighboring event semantics for late-stage refinement. It operates in a progressive, no-training framework and is evaluated against a self-curated benchmark suite (Meve for multi-event generation).

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance across 8 metrics, offering a tunable trade-off between video consistency and event separation. The gain in text alignment increases with the number of events, indicating scalability to more complex multi-event scenarios.

Conclusion: Training-free progressive steering can effectively enable long-horizon, multi-event T2V generation with controllable event boundaries and refined semantics. TunerDiT demonstrates clear scalability with event count and provides a benchmark suite (Meve) for evaluating multi-event capabilities; potential future work includes exploring broader event types and further quantifying trade-offs between consistency and semantic alignment.

Abstract: Text-to-video (T2V) generation faces challenging questions when generating videos with long horizons containing multiple events. Inspired by the intrinsics of the diffusion process, we probe video diffusion transformers (DiTs) and uncover intrinsic turning points in the DiT denoising trajectory where conditioning text affects generation from global layout to fine-grained details. Building on this finding, we present TunerDiT, a simple yet effective progressive steering method that requires no additional training for multi-event generation. TunerDiT comprises two steering handles: (1) Event-Partitioned Masking that enforces event boundaries while allowing cross-event transition bands; (2) Cross-Event Prompt Fusion that injects neighboring event semantics for late-stage refinement. We contribute a self-curated prompt suite for benchmarking multi-event generation, i.e., Meve. TunerDiT achieves state-of-the-art performance across 8 metrics and offers a tunable trade-off between video consistency and event separation, compared with other training-free methods. The improvement in text alignment increases with the event count, indicating a scaling possibility with increasing event count.

</details>


### [110] [SurGe: Improved Surface Geometry in Point Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31577)
*Karim Knaebel,Gonzalo Martin Garcia,Christian Schmidt,Ilya Fradlin,Lucas Nunes,Daan de Geus,Bastian Leibe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SurGe introduces a point map normal metric and two complementary components, a point gradient matching loss and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD), to improve local surface geometry in zero-shot monocular 3D reconstruction, achieving best global AbsRel and better local metrics on eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations understate local surface-geometry errors in feedforward 3D reconstruction; a metric that captures local orientation and a method to improve local geometry are needed.

Method: Introduce point gradient matching loss supervising depth-normalized 3D finite differences; implement Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and applies Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing; propose the point map normal metric for evaluation.

Result: On eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, SurGe attains the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal metrics.

Conclusion: The combination of a local-geometry-aware metric and targeted architectural/loss components yields improved local surface fidelity while maintaining strong global geometry, establishing SurGe as a new reference for zero-shot monocular 3D reconstruction.

Abstract: Recent feedforward 3D reconstruction methods predict point maps and estimate global 3D geometry remarkably well. However, their predictions still exhibit inaccurate local surface geometry, which is clearly visible qualitatively but only weakly reflected in common metrics. To make these errors more explicit in evaluation, we introduce a point map normal metric that evaluates the local surface orientation induced by neighboring 3D predictions. To reduce these errors, we propose two complementary components: a point gradient matching loss that supervises depth-normalized 3D finite differences, and a Neighborhood Attention Decoder (NAD) that progressively upsamples features and uses Neighborhood Attention for local feature mixing. Across eight zero-shot monocular geometry benchmarks, our model, SurGe, achieves the best average rank for global point map AbsRel and consistently improves local point map and point map normal evaluations.

</details>


### [111] [Lumos-Nexus: Efficient Frequency Bridging with Homogeneous Latent Space for Video Unified Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31603)
*Jiazheng Xing,Hangjie Yuan,Lingling Cai,Xinyu Liu,Yujie Wei,Fei Du,Hai Ci,Tao Feng,Jiasheng Tang,Weihua Chen,Fan Wang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lumos-Nexus introduces a training-efficient, two-stage unified video generation framework that achieves high visual fidelity and strong reasoning-driven control by training with a lightweight generator and progressively leveraging a large pretrained generator at inference via Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB); VR-Bench is proposed to evaluate reasoning-driven video generation, with code and models released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality, instruction-grounded video generation is hindered by the computational burden of integrating large, high-fidelity generators into a unified training loop. The work aims to preserve reasoning-driven controllability while boosting visual realism and temporal coherence.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) training with a lightweight generator aligned to the understanding block to learn semantically controlled video synthesis; (2) inference via Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB) to progressively transfer generation to a high-capacity pretrained generator in a shared latent space, enabling coarse-to-fine refinement. Additionally, VR-Bench is introduced to benchmark reasoning-driven generation; experiments measure performance on VBench (visual realism/temporal coherence) and VR-Bench (reasoning-based generation). Code and models are released.

Result: Experiments show substantial gains in visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench and strong performance on VR-Bench, indicating improved fidelity without compromising reasoning control.

Conclusion: Lumos-Nexus demonstrates training efficiency and high-quality, reasoning-aligned video generation through a two-stage design and UPFB, with VR-Bench filling a gap in reasoning-driven generation benchmarks; the authors provide code and pretrained models for replication.

Abstract: Connector-based video unified models have demonstrated strong capability in instruction-grounded video synthesis, but integrating a large high-fidelity generator into the unified training loop is computationally prohibitive, limiting achievable visual quality. We therefore propose Lumos-Nexus, a training-efficient unified video generation framework that facilitates the development of strong reasoning-driven generation capabilities while significantly enhancing visual fidelity. Lumos-Nexus adopts a two-stage design: 1) During training, only a lightweight generator is aligned with the understanding block to learn to take in reasoning-driven semantic control. 2) During inference, we introduce Unified Progressive Frequency Bridging (UPFB) to progressively hand off generation to a high-capacity pretrained generator in the shared latent space, enabling coarse-to-fine refinement and producing high-fidelity videos without compromising reasoning quality. To fill the gap in reasoning-driven video generation benchmarks, we introduce VR-Bench, which assesses a model's capability to translate inferred intent into coherent and semantically aligned video content. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Lumos-Nexus achieves substantial gains in visual realism and temporal coherence on VBench, while exhibiting strong reasoning-based generative performance on VR-Bench. Code and models are available at https://jiazheng-xing.github.io/nexus-lumos-home/.

</details>


### [112] [Recognizing Co-Speech Gestures in-the-Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31589)
*Sindhu B Hegde,K R Prajwal,Andrew Zisserman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Gesture Recognition in the Wild (GRW), a large-scale, frame-accurate dataset (156,688 clips, 150-word taxonomy) mapping unconstrained gestures to spoken words; used to train and benchmark models for gesture semantic detection, word recognition, and temporal localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses scarcity of precisely annotated data for semantic co-speech gestures, which limits multimodal models' ability to learn gesture-word mappings.

Method: Create manually annotated video dataset GRW with 156,688 clips and 150 action categories; define three tasks (semantic gesture classification, gesture-to-word recognition, temporal localization); establish benchmarks for these tasks.

Result: Dataset and task benchmarks are established; models can be trained to classify gestures as semantic or not, recognize corresponding words, and localize gestures in time.

Conclusion: GRW provides a foundational resource for semantic co-speech gesture understanding and a standard benchmark to advance multimodal gesture recognition research.

Abstract: While humans naturally gesture during speech, only a sparse subset of these movements are visually depictive and semantically linked to specific spoken words. Current multimodal models struggle to capture these semantic co-speech gestures, heavily bottlenecked by a lack of precisely annotated training data. To address this, we introduce the Gesture Recognition in the Wild (GRW) dataset, the first large-scale benchmark designed to map unconstrained human gestures to specific words with frame-accurate temporal boundaries. Comprising 156,688 manually annotated video clips, GRW spans a highly diverse 150-word taxonomy of physical actions, spatial descriptors, and abstract concepts. We leverage GRW to train video models to (a) classify gestures as semantic or not, (b) recognize the word corresponding to a co-speech gesture, and (c) temporally localize the gesture. We also use GRW to establish benchmarks for these three tasks.

</details>


### [113] [CoFiDA-M: Concept-Aware Feature Modulation for Cross-Domain Adaptation with Image-Only Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31591)
*Nurjahan Sultana,Moi Hoon Yap,Xinqi Fan,Wenqi Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A privileged-information distillation framework (CoFiDA-M) trains a teacher using MONET-derived concept scores to guide a FiLM-based feature editor, then distills this semantics into an image-only student for robust cross-dataset skin cancer screening without access to test-time metadata.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shift between expert dermoscopic (source) images and consumer-grade clinical (target) images impairs deployment; while semantic priors (concept probabilities) can guide learning, they are unavailable at test time, creating a deployment paradox for image-only systems.

Method: A teacher model leverages MONET concept probabilities to modulate visual features via FiLM, producing an semantically edited feature space. A lightweight image-only student is trained to imitate this edited representation (not only final predictions), effectively distilling semantic reasoning into weights.

Result: On a challenging multi-dataset benchmark, the image-only student substantially outperforms state-of-the-art, with notable gains in melanoma recall and strong cross-dataset robustness.

Conclusion: Privileged information during training can be leveraged to produce robust, deployable image-only models by distilling semantically edited representations, with potential applicability beyond dermatology and practical deployment advantages.

Abstract: Models for AI-based skin cancer screening suffer a severe performance drop when shifting from expert dermoscopic (source) images to consumer-grade clinical (target) images, hindering real-world deployment. Existing domain adaptation methods often ignore crucial semantic invariants, such as clinical concepts. While new foundation models like MONET can provide this semantic information as dense, probabilistic scores, this metadata is unavailable at test time, creating a deployment paradox for practical image-only screening tools. We address this gap by proposing CoFiDA-M, a privileged information framework that learns from concepts at training time but deploys as an image-only model. Our method trains a teacher network that uses MONET concept probabilities to guide a FiLM modulator, transforming visual features into a semantically ``edited" feature space. A lightweight, image-only student is then trained to reproduce this edited representation, not just the teacher's final predictions. This distillation ``bakes" the clinical reasoning into the student's weights. On a challenging multi-dataset benchmark, our image-only student significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, especially in melanoma recall. Our work provides a practical and generalizable framework for leveraging noisy, probabilistic metadata as privileged information, demonstrating strong cross-dataset robustness and potential for real-world deployment beyond dermatology. Implementation code is available at: https://github.com/mmu-dermatology-research/CoFiDA.git

</details>


### [114] [Learning Global Motion with Compact Gaussians for Feed-Forward 4D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31595)
*Mungyeom Kim,Minkyeong Jeon,Honggyu An,Jaewoo Jung,Hyuna Ko,Jisang Han,Hyeonseo Yu,Donghwan Shin,Sunghwan Hong,Takuya Narihira,Kazumi Fukuda,Yuki Mitsufuji,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: C4G achieves 4D dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video using timestamp-conditioned Gaussian tokens, with diffusion-based rendering and 4D feature lifting; fewer Gaussians, no camera poses, robust to temporal gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video is challenged by motion, view-dependent bias, and need for per-scene optimization; duplicative Gaussians hamper learning.

Method: Introduce C4G: a feed-forward framework with timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian query tokens that aggregate features across full temporal context to decode 3D Gaussians modulated by time; add a video diffusion-based rendering enhancement; extend to 4D feature field via feature lifting for point tracking and dynamic understanding; no camera poses required; fewer Gaussians.

Result: Strong novel-view synthesis performance with significantly fewer Gaussians; improved motion modeling and robustness to large temporal gaps; no camera pose; avoids per-scene optimization.

Conclusion: C4G enables globally coherent motion modeling and 4D scene understanding from monocular video, with diffusion-based rendering to enhance details; useful for tracking and dynamic scene comprehension.

Abstract: Dynamic scene reconstruction from monocular video remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. Existing feed-forward methods predict 3D Gaussians pixel-wise for each frame, suffering from duplicated Gaussians and view-dependent biases that hinder effective learning of scene motion. We present C4G, a feed-forward 4D reconstruction framework built upon a compact set of timestamp-conditioned learnable Gaussian query tokens. Each token aggregates corresponding features across the full temporal context and decodes a 3D Gaussian whose position is modulated by the target timestamp, enabling globally coherent motion modeling without per-scene optimization. To capture fine-grained details, we further introduce a video diffusion model-based rendering enhancement module. Since our framework effectively aggregates features into Gaussians, we extend this capability to feature lifting, producing a 4D feature field that supports point tracking and dynamic scene understanding. C4G achieves strong novel-view synthesis performance using significantly fewer Gaussians and without requiring camera poses, while exhibiting stronger motion modeling and robustness to large temporal gaps.

</details>


### [115] [SOCO: Benchmarking Semantic Object Correspondence in Vision Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31597)
*Olaf Dünkel,Basavaraj Sunagad,Haoran Wang,David T. Hoffmann,Christian Theobalt,Adam Kortylewski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SOCO introduces a taxonomy-driven, large-scale benchmark for semantic object correspondence with 100 categories, 1M keypoint pairs, and language descriptions to evaluate vision and multimodal models on part-level understanding; findings show strong semantic structure in backbones but limited cross-category transfer, LVLMs excel at language-grounded localization but lag in cross-image visual correspondence, and correspondence aligns with dense downstream task performance better than ImageNet accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable systematic, part-level evaluation of semantic object correspondence (SC) in vision foundation models, addressing inconsistent protocols and limited supervision for object parts.

Method: Create SOCO, a benchmark with a taxonomy of correspondence types, consistent functionally meaningful keypoint annotations across 100 categories, over 1M correspondence pairs, and keypoint language descriptions for evaluating LVLMs.

Result: Empirical findings: (i) vision backbones encode semantic structure but have poor cross-category transfer and partial object-part localization; (ii) LVLMs perform better in text-prompted part localization than in cross-image visual matching, indicating a language-grounded localization gap; (iii) correspondence performance correlates more with dense downstream tasks (segmentation, tracking, 3D pose/detection) than with ImageNet classification.

Conclusion: SOCO provides a principled benchmark for assessing structured, part-level representations in vision and multimodal models and reveals gaps between language-grounded localization and fine-grained visual correspondence.

Abstract: Measuring structured object understanding in vision foundation models remains challenging due to inconsistent evaluation protocols and limited part-level supervision. Semantic correspondence (SC) evaluates this capability by testing whether object parts can be matched across instances and categories under large variations in appearance, viewpoint, and geometry. To enable a systematic SC evaluation, we introduce SOCO, a new benchmark for Semantic Object Correspondence that introduces a taxonomy of correspondence types and provides consistent, functionally meaningful keypoint annotations across 100 categories and over 1M correspondence pairs. In addition, SOCO includes keypoint language descriptions, enabling the evaluation of large vision-language models (LVLMs) and their fine-grained part-level understanding. Comprehensive experiments reveal that (i) vision foundation backbones encode strong semantic structure but transfer correspondences poorly across related categories and only partially capture object-part position, (ii) LVLMs are stronger at text-prompted part localization than at visual-reference cross-image matching, exposing a gap between language-grounded localization and fine-grained visual correspondence, and (iii) correspondence performance predicts performance on dense downstream tasks, including segmentation, tracking, 3D pose estimation, and 3D detection, more strongly than ImageNet classification. Together, these findings position SOCO as a benchmark for structured, part-level representation quality in vision and multimodal foundation models.

</details>


### [116] [Representation Forcing for Bottleneck-Free Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31604)
*Yuqing Wang,Zhijie Lin,Ceyuan Yang,Yang Zhao,Fei Xiao,Hao He,Qi Zhao,Zihan Ding,Fuyun Wang,Shuai Wang,Youliang Zhang,Haoqi Fan,Xihui Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RF enables end-to-end pixel-space unified multimodal modeling by teaching the model to predict internal visual representations as intermediate tokens, removing the external generative latent space. It achieves competitive generation and improved understanding relative to VAE-based UMMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current UMMs rely on a frozen, pretrained VAE for image generation, creating a bottleneck. Removing it naively leads to a quality gap because the model must learn both high-level structure and low-level details from raw pixels. There is a need for bottleneck-free, end-to-end UMMs.

Method: Introduce Representation Forcing (RF): the decoder autoregressively predicts visual representations as intermediate tokens before pixels. These tokens remain in context to guide pixel diffusion within the same backbone. Perception outputs are repurposed as generation targets, eliminating the need for an external generative latent space.

Result: RF improves both tasks: for generation, a pixel-space model with RF matches state-of-the-art VAE-based unified models; for understanding, RF generally outperforms its VAE-based variant.

Conclusion: RF advances toward end-to-end, bottleneck-free unified multimodal models by removing dependence on external generative latent spaces, with strong gains in both generation quality and understanding performance.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) aim to handle perception and generation in a single model. Yet existing UMMs still rely on a frozen, separately pretrained VAE for image generation, imposing a structural bottleneck. Naively removing it introduces a quality gap, as the model must learn both high-level structure and low-level details from raw pixels. In this paper, we propose Representation Forcing (RF), a technique that closes this gap by making representation prediction a native capability of the model. Concretely, RF forces the decoder to autoregressively predict visual representations as intermediate tokens before pixels; these tokens then stay in context to guide pixel diffusion within the same backbone. By turning representations from perception outputs into generation targets, RF eliminates the need for any external generative latent space. We find that RF benefits both understanding and generation. On image generation, our pixel-space model with RF matches state-of-the-art VAE-based unified models. On image understanding, pixel-space RF generally outperforms its VAE-based variant. Together, these results offer an effective step toward end-to-end, bottleneck-free UMMs.

</details>


### [117] [KLIP: localized distribution shift detection via KL-divergence with diffusion priors in Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31596)
*Alireza Kheirandish,Jihoon Hong,Sara Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A KL-based OOD metric (KLIP) compares diffusion priors to posteriors to perform calibration-free detection of OOD in inverse problems, capable of identifying both whole-image and localized OOD regions, including subtle shifts; demonstrated on medical imaging with diverse models/datasets; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OOD methods for diffusion-based imaging often rely on calibrated data, assume knowledge of the shifted distribution, or miss subtle/localized shifts, and typically operate on full images rather than indirect measurements. There is a need for a calibration-free, measurement-domain OOD detector that can localize OOD regions.

Method: Compute the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the diffusion prior and the posterior conditioned on measurements; this KL divergence serves as an OOD score that does not require calibration data. The score can be computed across the image to localize OOD patches and works under various diffusion models, datasets, and inverse problems.

Result: Empirically detects subtle, semantically meaningful distribution shifts (e.g., healthy vs. tumor CT scans) and generalizes across diffusion models, datasets, and inverse problems.

Conclusion: KLIP provides a calibration-free, versatile OOD detection framework for diffusion-prior-based imaging, with localizable OOD detection, and broad applicability across model families and inverse problem settings.

Abstract: Diffusion models have shown promising performance as data-driven priors for computational imaging, as well as some capacity to detect out-of-distribution (OOD) images. However, existing approaches to OOD detection often require some knowledge of the shifted distribution, fail to detect subtle or localized distribution shifts, and operate on full images, rather than the indirect measurements available in inverse problems. We propose an OOD detection metric based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the diffusion prior and the posterior distribution, that (i) does not require any calibration data or knowledge of the shifted distribution, and (ii) can detect whole images as OOD as well as localize OOD patches within an image. Experimentally, we show that this metric can detect subtle yet semantically meaningful distribution shifts, such as the shift from healthy liver CT scans to those with tumors, and generalizes across different types of diffusion models, datasets, and inverse problems. Our code can be found at https://github.com/voilalab/KLIP.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [118] [PhyDrawGen: Physically Grounded Diagram Generation from Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30512)
*Nafiul Haque,Syed Nazmus Sakib,Shifat E Arman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic pipeline, PhyDrawGen, decouples semantic scene understanding from physical constraint satisfaction to generate physics-consistent diagrams, outperforming strong baselines on a 1,449-problem benchmark across mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-diagram models often hallucinate physical details and violate conservation laws. There is a need to enforce physical validity while preserving visual plausibility.

Method: 1) LLM extracts a typed scene graph from text; 2) a deterministic solver converts the graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG) encoding force balance, optical paths, and field topologies as exact geometric primitives; 3) a fine-tuned Qwen-VL model implements a visually grounded propose-verify loop to iteratively correct constraint violations.

Result: Evaluated on 1,449 problems across mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism; PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro, showing robust physical accuracy even on unusual-object problems.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic decoupling of semantic interpretation and constraint satisfaction yields higher physical fidelity in diagram generation; PSLG-based geometry provides exact constraint encoding; the propose-verify loop helps ensure violation-free outputs and suggests strong generalization within the tested domains.

Abstract: Generating physics diagrams from text requires strict adherence to physical laws. While current generative models produce visually plausible outputs, they systematically hallucinate force vectors, ignore conservation laws, and violate geometric constraints. We present PhyDrawGen, a neuro-symbolic pipeline that decouples semantic scene understanding from physical constraint satisfaction. First, a large language model extracts a typed scene graph from the problem text. A deterministic solver then converts this graph into a Planar Straight-Line Graph (PSLG), encoding force balance, optical paths, and field topologies as exact geometric primitives. Finally, a fine-tuned Qwen-VL model implements a visually grounded propose-verify loop to iteratively correct any constraint violations. Evaluated on a benchmark of 1,449 problems spanning mechanics, optics, and electromagnetism, PhyDrawGen significantly outperforms GPT-5-image, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3 Pro, demonstrating robust physical accuracy even on unusual-object problems.

</details>


### [119] [Transforming and Encoding FTS for SAT Solving: What Helps, What Hurts (Extended Version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30563)
*João Filipe,Álvaro Torralba,Gregor Behnke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces multiple SAT encodings for factored tasks (SAS+-like with disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and angelic nondeterminism), and analyzes how parallelism and task transformations affect SAT-based planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable effective SAT-based planning for factored tasks, addressing the current limitation to heuristic search methods.

Method: Develop several propositional encodings of the factored transition relation; investigate different strategies for translating the factored task into SAT; study parallelism at multiple levels; assess the impact of common task transformations on SAT-based planners.

Result: Characterizes the proposed encoding schemes, analyzes parallelization opportunities, and evaluates how task transformations influence SAT-based planning performance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that SAT-based planning for factored tasks is viable with appropriate encodings, and that exploiting parallelism and task transformations can enhance planner performance.

Abstract: Factored tasks are a classical planning representation that extends SAS+ with limited forms of disjunctive preconditions, conditional effects, and angelic nondeterminism. This allows for a more compact representation of tasks than traditional formalisms such as STRIPS or SAS+, and supports a wide range of task transformations. However, existing planning approaches for factored tasks have been limited to heuristic search methods.
  In this work, we investigate how to encode factored tasks in SAT. We propose several ways to encode the tasks, focusing on different strategies for translating the factored transition relation into propositional logic. We also analyze how to exploit parallelism at various levels in this setting and study the impact of common task transformations on the performance of SAT-based planners.

</details>


### [120] [Procedural Generation of First Person Shooter Maps using Map-Elites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30570)
*Simone de Donato,Pier Luca Lanzi,Daniele Loiacono*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAP-Elites with new FPS map representations yields higher diversity and quality in map generation, introducing two novel representations (Point-Line, Spatial-Layout) and usingMESB; analyzes topological and emergent metrics to guide illumination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve map design for FPS games by applying quality-diversity optimization to explore diverse, high-quality level layouts and understand which features best guide the MAP-Elites illumination process.

Method: Compare two existing FPS map representations (All-Black and Grid-Graph), introduce two novel ones (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout); define topological metrics (layout-based) and emergent metrics (require gameplay); identify best features for illumination; apply MAP-Elites with Sliding Boundaries (MESB) to evolve populations of maps.

Result: The novel representations yield higher diversity and quality in evolved FPS maps compared with representations previously used.

Conclusion: Adopting the new representations and a metrics-driven MESB MAP-Elites workflow improves the quality–diversity trade-off in FPS map design, enabling richer and more varied level layouts.

Abstract: We investigate the application of MAP-Elites (a well-known quality diversity algorithm) to design levels for First-Person Shooter (FPS) games. We consider two well-known map representations (All-Black and Grid-Graph) and introduce two novel representations (Point-Line and Spatial-Layout) that improve the characterization of FPS maps. We define a series of metrics to describe maps' topological properties (which solely depend on maps' layout), and emergent properties (which must be evaluated through actual gameplay). We perform an in-depth analysis to identify the most suitable features to guide MAP-Elites illumination process. We apply MAP-Elites with Sliding Boundaries (MESB) to evolve populations of FPS maps. Our results show that the new representations can generate maps with higher diversity and quality than the representations previously used for evolving FPS maps.

</details>


### [121] [Uncertainty-Aware and Temporally Regulated Expert Advice in Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30576)
*Ahmed Abouelazm,Felix Klingebiel,Philip Schörner,J. Marius Zöllner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware, expert-guided exploration with adaptive thresholds and shared replay boosts safe RL for autonomous driving using IQN, achieving notable improvement on unsignalized intersection navigation in CARLA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploration in RL for autonomous driving is inherently unsafe. The agent must experience novel behaviors to learn, but exploration can cause collisions or off-road events. The work proposes leveraging expert advice to steer exploration while avoiding long-term dependence.

Method: Trigger expert guidance when epistemic or aleatoric uncertainty exceeds adaptive thresholds derived from rolling buffers. Use a commitment-cooldown strategy with a stochastic early-stop to regulate the duration and frequency of guidance. Combine expert and agent experiences in a shared replay buffer within an off-policy implicit quantile network (IQN) backbone to efficiently reuse expert trajectories.

Result: In CARLA, the method outperforms the IQN baseline, increasing success by 5–7% and reducing failures, demonstrating safer and more efficient exploration for sensor-based RL policy learning in unsignalized intersection navigation.

Conclusion: Risk-sensitive uncertainty coupled with regulated expert integration enables safer and more efficient exploration for sensor-based RL in autonomous driving tasks, with effective transfer to unsignalized intersection navigation using IQN.

Abstract: Exploration in reinforcement learning for autonomous driving is inherently unsafe: agents must experience novel behaviors to learn, yet exploration can lead to collisions or off-road driving. We propose an uncertainty-aware framework that leverages expert advice to guide exploration while avoiding long-term dependence. Advice is triggered when epistemic or aleatoric uncertainty exceeds adaptive thresholds derived from rolling buffers, ensuring advice evolves with the agent's confidence. A commitment-cooldown strategy with a stochastic early-stop heuristic regulates the duration and frequency of guidance, exposing the agent to coherent maneuvers without exhausting the advice budget. Expert and agent experiences are combined in a shared replay buffer within an off-policy implicit quantile network (IQN) backbone, enabling efficient reuse of expert trajectories. Experiments in CARLA show that our method outperforms the IQN baseline, improving success by 5-7% and reducing failures, demonstrating that risk-sensitive uncertainty coupled with regulated expert integration enables safer and more efficient exploration for sensor-based RL policy learning in unsignalized intersection navigation.

</details>


### [122] [Harness Updating Is Not Harness Benefit: Disentangling Evolution Capabilities in Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30621)
*Minhua Lin,Juncheng Wu,Zijun Wang,Zhan Shi,Yisi Sang,Bing He,Zewen Liu,Tianxin Wei,Zongyu Wu,Zhiwei Zhang,Dakuo Wang,Xiang Zhang,Benoit Dumoulin,Cihang Xie,Yuyin Zhou,Suhang Wang,Hanqing Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Harness self-evolution updates from execution evidence yield similar gains across base capabilities; harness-benefit is strongest for mid-tier models and weaker for both weak and strong tiers, suggesting prioritizing the task-solving agent's capabilities over the evolver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether a model's base task-solving capability predicts effectiveness of harness self-evolution, by examining two capabilities—harness updating and harness benefit—across models.

Method: Empirical analysis across models with varying base capabilities. Measure harness-updating (useful persistent harness updates from evidence) and harness-benefit (improvement from updated harnesses in task solving). Compare model families (e.g., Qwen3.5-9B, Claude Opus 4.6) and analyze failure modes; provide publicly available source code.

Result: Harness-updating is flat across base capability: different capability tiers produce harness updates yielding similar gains. Harness-benefit is non-monotonic: weak-tier models gain little, mid-tier models gain most, strong-tier models gain less than mid-tier. Two failure modes for weak-tier: failing to activate relevant harness artifacts or activating but not following them faithfully. Implication: invest capability budget in the task-solving agent rather than the evolver; focus on harness invocation and long-horizon instruction following during training.

Conclusion: Focus on strengthening the task-solving agent and its use of harnesses; improve harness invocation and long-horizon guidance; code released at the linked repository.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as systems built around editable external harnesses, including prompts, skills, memories and tools, that shape task execution without changing model parameters. Harness self-evolution adapts such agents by updating these harnesses from execution evidence. Yet it remains unclear whether a model's base capability in task-solving predicts its capabilities in harness self-evolution: which models produce useful harness updates, and which actually benefit from them? We analyze two harness self-evolution capabilities: (i) harness-updating, the capability to produce useful persistent harness updates from execution evidence; (ii) harness-benefit, the capability to benefit from updated harnesses during task solving. Our analysis reveals two findings. First, harness-updating is flat in base capability: models from different capability tiers produce harness updates that lead to surprisingly similar gains; even Qwen3.5-9B's updates yield gains comparable to those of Claude Opus~4.6. Second, harness-benefit is non-monotonic in base capability: weak-tier models benefit little from updated harnesses, mid-tier models benefit most, and strong-tier models benefit less than mid-tier. We trace low gains at the weak tier to two failure modes: weak-tier models may fail to activate relevant harness artifacts, or activate them but fail to follow them faithfully. These findings suggest investing capability budget in the task-solving agent rather than the evolver, and targeting harness invocation and long-horizon instruction following in agent training. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/A-EVO-Lab/a-evolve/tree/release/harness-evolution.

</details>


### [123] [EHRBench: An Automated and Reliable EHR-based Benchmark for Clinical Decision Making with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30637)
*Yuzhang Xie,Keqi Han,Yunpeng Xiao,Hejie Cui,Guanchen Wu,Ziyang Zhang,Kai Shu,Jiaying Lu,Xiao Hu,Carl Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces EHRBench, a scalable, automated, EHR-grounded benchmark for evaluating LLM-based clinical decision-making via an EHR-LLM-KB pipeline, generating ~960k QA items across diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis from encounter trajectories with KB verification; benchmarks 30+ LLMs and analyzes performance/robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, reliable evaluation of LLM-based clinical decision-making on real-world, EHR-grounded tasks, addressing reliability gaps in existing benchmarks.

Method: An automated EHR-LLM-KB interaction pipeline converts encounter-level EHR trajectories into structured templates, deterministically instantiates templates into QA items, and applies KB-based verification/enrichment to filter hallucinations and ambiguities. This yields ~960,067 QA items across diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis. More than 30 LLMs are benchmarked.

Result: Construction of 960,067 QA items covering three core decision tasks; extensive benchmarking across 30+ LLMs; analysis reveals consistent capability trends and actionable gaps; demonstration of EHRBench reliability and scalability.

Conclusion: EHRBench provides a scalable, reliable benchmark grounded in real patient data for evaluating LLM-based CDM systems, highlighting practical gaps and guiding development toward clinically reliable LLMs.

Abstract: Clinical decision-making (CDM) is central to real-world clinical workflows, where clinicians infer diagnoses, select treatments, or anticipate future health outcomes under incomplete evidence. LLMs are increasingly used to support these decisions due to strong language capabilities, broad biomedical knowledge, and efficiency, yet the reliability of LLMs on real-world clinical decision tasks remains insufficiently understood. To evaluate CDM models, especially LLM-based models, an ideal and practical medical decision benchmark should be constructed via an automated yet reliable pipeline to ensure both scale and quality. Moreover, the grounding of a CDM benchmark in real patient EHRs can better support evaluation on practical CDM tasks that require substantive biomedical knowledge and clinical inference. To fill the gaps, we introduce EHRBench, an automated and reliable EHR-grounded benchmark for evaluating LLM-based clinical decision-making at scale. To ensure scalability and reliability, EHRBench is constructed through an EHR-LLM-KB(knowledge-base) interaction pipeline. For efficiency, we use a specialized LLM to automatically convert encounter-level EHR trajectories into structured templates and deterministically instantiate the templates into QA items. In parallel, we apply systematic KB-based verification and enrichment to filter hallucinated or ambiguous relations and to improve reliability. Using this pipeline, we construct nearly 1M (960,067) QA items spanning three core inference-required clinical decision tasks: diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis. We benchmark more than 30 representative LLMs on EHRBench and provide detailed analyses of performance and robustness. The results show consistent capability trends across settings, further validating the reliability of EHRBench and highlighting actionable gaps toward clinically reliable LLM systems.

</details>


### [124] [Structure-Induced Information for Rerooting Levin Tree Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30664)
*Jake Tuero,Michael Buro,Laurent Orseau,Levi H. S. Lelis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: We introduce a learned rerooter for the sqrt-LTS framework that implicitly decomposes tasks into soft subtasks, enabling scalable subgoal-based policy tree search with lower overhead. We propose three rerooter designs: clustering-based, heuristic-based (cost-to-go estimates), and a hybrid, and demonstrate improved scalability and online training efficiency over explicit-subgoal methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subgoal-based policy tree search relies on explicit subgoal generation, which incurs substantial overhead and impedes scalability in complex single-agent deterministic problems.

Method: Apply a sqrt-LTS-based rerouting mechanism with three rerooter designs: (i) clustering-based using global state-space structure, (ii) heuristic-based using learned cost-to-go estimates, and (iii) a hybrid combining both signals. This avoids explicit subgoal reconstruction while guiding search through soft subtasks.

Result: Rerooting-based methods scale to complex environments where traditional subgoal-based search fails, achieving significantly lower computational overhead and state-of-the-art online training efficiency on the tested domains.

Conclusion:  Learned rerooters within the sqrt-LTS framework provide scalable, efficient guidance for subgoal-based policy tree search by implicitly decomposing tasks into soft subtasks, eliminating the need for explicit subgoal generation.

Abstract: Subgoal-based policy tree search, which uses a policy to guide search, is effective for complex single-agent deterministic problems but often relies on explicit subgoal generation that can incur substantial overhead and hinders scalability. In this paper, we overcome these limitations by using a learned ``rerooter'' through the recently-introduced $\sqrt{\text{LTS}}$ algorithm. A rerooter implicitly decomposes the problem into soft subtasks. While previous work focused on the formal guarantees for given or handcrafted rerooters, in this work we propose three rerooter designs: (i) a clustering-based rerooter that exploits global state-space structure, (ii) a heuristic-based rerooter that leverages learned cost-to-go estimates, and (iii) a hybrid that combines both signals. Our framework avoids having to explicitly reconstruct and reason over generated subgoals, thereby enabling scalable allocation of search effort with significantly lower computational overhead. Empirically, our rerooting-based methods scale to complex environments where subgoal-based policy tree search fails, and achieve state-of-the-art online training efficiency on the domains tested.

</details>


### [125] [Healthcare Mechanisms from Policy-as-Code Search under Strategic Provider Response](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30680)
*Zihan Wang,Xiang Xu,Hongyuan Zha,Wenhao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reframes hospital mechanism design as program synthesis for language models, using a multi-agent simulator (Medi-Sim) to study incentive effects and pressure migrations; uses LLM-guided search to synthesize an objective program that mitigates up-coding and rejections while preserving profitability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the static evaluation of healthcare AI benchmarks by modeling strategic provider responses and examining how incentive structures shape observed performance and outcomes.

Method: Construct typed, inspectable rule programs for language models that are executed within Medi-Sim, a five-channel provider model (coding, selection, delay, effort, triage). Perform an incentive sweep to reveal classical health-economics regimes and use an audit lever to observe pressure migration. Employ LLM-guided evolutionary code search to optimize a mixed-objective rule program.

Result: Incentive sweeps reproduce known phenomena: up-coding, low-complexity patient selection under profit pressure, and Goodhart drift. An audit shows that closing coding shifts behavior to other channels, significantly increasing low-complexity selection. The evolved mixed-objective program reduces up-coding by half, reduces rejections by half, and retains most of the baseline profit.

Conclusion: Program-synthesized, inspectable rule-based mechanisms guided by LLM search can both diagnose the equilibrium effects of provider incentives and offer a practical, interpretable reform that mitigates gaming while preserving financial viability.

Abstract: Healthcare mechanisms are inseparable from the strategic provider response they induce: existing healthcare AI benchmarks hold this response fixed and so cannot evaluate mechanisms by the equilibrium they produce. We recast hospital mechanism design as program synthesis for language models: typed, inspectable rule programs are executed and scored by Medi-Sim, a multi-agent simulator with five strategic provider channels (coding, selection, delay, effort, triage). An incentive sweep recovers classical health-economics findings as adjacent regimes -- up-coding and low-complexity-patient selection under profit pressure, and Goodhart-style drift where measured performance becomes anti-correlated with true outcomes -- and a single audit lever exposes pressure migration: closing the coding channel more than doubles low-complexity selection. LLM-guided evolutionary code search over the same rule-program space then synthesizes an inspectable mixed-objective program that eliminates up-coding, halves rejection, and retains most of the profit-oriented baseline's funds.

</details>


### [126] [MAVEN: Improving Generalization in Agentic Tool Calling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30738)
*Omkar Ghugarkar,Vishvesh Bhat,Muhammad Ahmed Mohsin,Asad Aali*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAVEN introduces a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold for modular decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification in agentic tool-calling; it yields substantial gains on MAVEN-Bench (71% from 48%) and competes with frontier baselines using an open-weight backbone at low cost, highlighting the value of verification-centric design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization across agentic tool-calling environments is limited; current LLMs struggle to compose reasoning strategies, preserve intermediate states, and coordinate multi-step tool usage across domains. A verification-centered, modular scaffold is proposed to address these issues.

Method: Propose MAVEN (Modular Agentic Verification and Execution Network) as a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold enabling structured decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification. Evaluate on established tool-calling benchmarks (BFCL v3, TauBench, Tau2Bench, AceBench) and introduce MAVEN-Bench—a stress-test for multi-step mathematical/physical reasoning with explicit verification and adversarial task composition. Demonstrate performance gains on GPT-OSS-120b base from 48% to 71% accuracy; compare favorably to frontier proprietary baselines using an open-weight backbone with ~1/10 cost ratio.

Result: MAVEN yields a notable improvement in end-to-end task success and demonstrates that verification-centered scaffolds can strengthen compositional reasoning. The approach shows competitive performance against larger, private baselines at a fraction of the cost and uncovers a gap between partial reasoning quality and overall task success in multi-step tasks.

Conclusion: Lightweight, verification-centered modular scaffolds can enhance agentic reasoning across tool-calling tasks, improve end-to-end outcomes, and justify more process-aware evaluation of agents in real-world settings.

Abstract: Generalization across agentic tool-calling environments remains a central challenge for reliable agentic reasoning systems. Although large language models achieve strong results on individual benchmarks, their ability to compose reasoning strategies, preserve intermediate states, and coordinate tools across domains remains underexplored. We present MAVEN (Modular Agentic Verification and Execution Network), a lightweight symbolic reasoning scaffold for structured decomposition, adaptive tool orchestration, and intermediate verification. We evaluate MAVEN across established tool-calling benchmarks, including BFCL v3, TauBench, Tau2Bench, AceBench, and introduce MAVEN-Bench, a stress-test benchmark for multi-step mathematical and physical reasoning with explicit verification and adversarial task composition. MAVEN-Bench exposes a substantial gap between partial reasoning quality and end-to-end task success; in direct MAVEN-Bench runs, MAVEN improves its GPT-OSS-120b base model from 48% to 71% accuracy without additional training. It also remains competitive with frontier proprietary baselines while using an open-weight backbone with an estimated cost ratio of roughly 1/10, suggesting that lightweight verification-centered scaffolds can strengthen compositional reasoning and motivate more process-aware evaluation of agents in the wild.

</details>


### [127] [Learning Agent-Compatible Context Management for Long-Horizon Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30785)
*Lu Yi,Runlin Lei,Liuyi Yao,Yuexiang Xie,Yuyang Li,Wenhao Zhang,Zhewei Wei,Yaliang Li,Jian-Yun Nie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AdaCoM trains an external LLM to manage the context of a frozen agent via flexible edit actions and end-to-end reinforcement learning, yielding substantial gains on long-horizon tasks by preserving progress while pruning stale content; reveals a Fidelity–Reliability trade-off and demonstrates transfer mostly among similarly capable agents as a path toward reusable context managers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tasks (e.g., web search, deep research) cause context growth and degradation, leading to degraded reasoning. Existing fixes rely on agent-side control or fixed summarization trained for a specific agent, which is impractical for closed-source agents and lacks cross-agent generality.

Method: An external LLM acts as a context manager for a frozen agent, applying flexible modification actions to the context. The system is trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning, optimizing context preservation and content pruning across diverse agent baselines, without altering the agent itself.

Result: AdaCoM substantially improves performance across web search and deep-research benchmarks by preserving task constraints/progress while pruning stale content, outperforming baselines that rely on fixed strategies.

Conclusion: The approach reveals a Fidelity–Reliability trade-off: high-fidelity context preservation benefits higher-capability (vanilla ReAct) agents, while lower-capability agents benefit from more aggressive compression. Transfer experiments show AdaCoM generalizes best across agents with similar capability, suggesting reusable, external context managers as a practical component for diverse agent systems.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly face long-horizon tasks such as web search and deep research in real-world applications, where accumulated context can cause long-context degradation and reasoning failures. Prior work mitigates this through context management with agent-side context control or fixed strategies such as summarization, which require training the agent itself for adaptation - making it impractical for closed-source agents and ignoring that different agents may require different strategies. We introduce Adaptive Context Management (AdaCoM), which trains an external LLM to manage the context of a frozen agent through flexible modification actions and end-to-end reinforcement learning. Across diverse agents on web search and deep research benchmarks, AdaCoM substantially improves performance by preserving task constraints and progress while pruning stale content. The learned strategies reveal a Fidelity-Reliability Trade-off: agents with higher vanilla ReAct performance benefit from higher-fidelity context preservation, whereas lower-performing agents require more aggressive compression to stay within a reliable reasoning regime. Transfer experiments show that AdaCoM generalizes most effectively across agents with similar capability (measured by vanilla ReAct performance), suggesting a practical path toward reusable context managers for agent systems.

</details>


### [128] [PReMISE: Policy Rubrics as Measurement Specifications for LLM Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30803)
*Swastik Roy,Rajkumar Pujari,Tharindu Kumarage,Charith Peris,Rahul Gupta,Anna Rumshisky,Pradeep Natarajan,Venkatesh Saligrama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PReMISE discovers policy-level rubric sets from pairwise human preferences and audits rubric sets for four axes, with two repair operations improving judge accuracy and reducing exploitability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM judges' scores are heavily rubric-dependent; thus reusable rubrics may distort measurements of response quality. A robust framework is needed to discover, audit, and repair rubrics to ensure reliable evaluation of open-ended responses.

Method: Given pairwise human-preference data, PReMISE (i) discovers a policy-level rubric set, and (ii) audits any rubric set under LLM-judge use along four axes: structural adequacy, reliability, preference fit, and adversarial robustness. It also analyzes rubric sources and introduces two repair operations: preference-rank selection and reliability-constrained refinement.

Result: Across rubric sources, no raw source is simultaneously reliable, preference-predictive, and adversarially robust; high inter-rater agreement does not imply low exploitability. PReMISE uniquely scores well on applicability, specificity, and effective dimensionality. Preference-rank selection raises judge accuracy on paired responses from 65.0% to 68.6%; reliability-constrained refinement reduces exploitative scores from 46.4% to 36.0% with little change in inter-judge agreement (alpha 0.531 to 0.519).

Conclusion: PReMISE provides a workflow to discover and audit rubric sets from human preferences, and demonstrates targeted repairs that improve judge accuracy and reduce adversarial exploitation, highlighting the importance of auditing rubrics in LLM evaluation.

Abstract: LLM judges are increasingly used to evaluate open-ended responses, but their scores depend strongly on the rubrics that condition them. A vague rubric asking for a response to be ``helpful and factual'' can reward polished answers that invent facts or violate user intent. We treat reusable rubrics as measurement specifications: changing the rubric changes the response quality measurement induced by a fixed judge. We introduce PReMISE, a framework that, given pairwise human-preference data, (i) discovers a policy-level rubric set, and (ii) audits any rubric set under LLM-judge use along four axes: structural adequacy, reliability, preference fit, and adversarial robustness. Across rubric sources no raw source is simultaneously reliable, preference-predictive, and adversarially robust; and high inter-rater agreement does not imply low exploitability. PReMISE is the only rubric source to score non-trivially on applicability, specificity, and effective dimensionality simultaneously. We contribute two audit-targeted repair operations: preference-rank selection raises judge accuracy on paired responses from $65.0\%$ to $68.6\%$, competitive with the strongest rubric-discovery baselines and leading on two of three judges in our cross-judge sweep; reliability-constrained refinement reduces the rate at which exploit responses receive high scores from $46.4\%$ to $36.0\%$ with little change in inter-judge agreement ($α{=}.531\to.519$).

</details>


### [129] [Planner-Centric Reinforcement Learning for Deep Research with Structure-Aware Reward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30824)
*Mustafa Anis Hussain,Xinle Wu,Yao Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DecomposeR introduces a planner-centric framework that models research plans as typed DAGs, enabling explicit, rewardable planning separate from execution in long-form tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training regimes rely on short-form QA proxies or monolithic long trajectories, which blur planning and execution and hinder fine-grained credit assignment for planning.

Method: Represent research plans as typed DAGs and train a Qwen3-8B model in two stages: (1) planner RL to learn graph structure and query decomposition for planning, (2) answerer RL to learn branch-level execution and synthesis conditioned on the plan. Rewards are applied to explicit planner tokens and structured components to reward planning. Evaluation uses long-form benchmarks and compares against open baselines.

Result: DecomposeR-8B achieves 5.1–8.0 point gains on popular long-form benchmarks over strong comparable baselines, attributed to improved planning and answering capabilities.

Conclusion: A planner-centric decomposition with explicit reward signals for planning yields finer-grained optimization of planning, reduces end-to-end credit ambiguity, and improves long-form research task performance.

Abstract: Deep research tasks require LLMs to plan what to investigate, retrieve evidence, and synthesize long-form answers across multiple branches of inquiry. Existing training paradigms either rely on short-form verifiable QA as a proxy or optimize monolithic long trajectories, which makes planning and execution difficult to disentangle and yields weak credit assignment for the planning process. We propose DecomposeR, a planner-centric deep research framework that represents research plans as typed directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), allowing planning to be made explicit, structured, and rewardable. We train a Qwen3-8B model in two stages: planner reinforcement learning (RL) first learns graph structure and query decomposition to improve research planning, and answerer reinforcement learning (RL) then learns branch-level execution and final synthesis conditioned on the learned plan. By assigning rewards to explicit planner tokens and structured components rather than to a flat trajectory, DecomposeR enables finer-grained optimization of planning while reducing the ambiguity of end-to-end training. Experiments show that DecomposeR-8B improves over strong comparable open baselines by 5.1-8.0 points on popular long-form benchmarks due to improved planning and answering capabilities.

</details>


### [130] [SLAT: Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming for Efficient CoT Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30832)
*Jian Yao,Xiongcai Luo,Ran Cheng,Kay Chen Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes SLAT, an RL-based, segment-level adaptive trimming method to remove redundant high-probability segments in chain-of-thought, achieving ~50% shorter reasoning with competitive accuracy, improving the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CoT reasoning suffers from overthinking and computational overhead due to structural redundancy; token-length penalties are coarse and may discard useful reasoning.

Method: Develop segment-level adaptive trimming (SLAT) with a theoretical characterization of segment suboptimality under the correctness-length objective; train an RL framework to suppress redundant segments selectively based on marginal utility.

Result: Empirical results on standard benchmarks show SLAT achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, reducing reasoning length by 50% compared with uncompressed baselines while maintaining competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: Segment-aware, theoretically grounded trimming is a promising direction for efficient CoT in large language models.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Reasoning Models have significantly improved chain-of-thought (CoT) capabilities via reinforcement learning (RL). However, generated reasoning chains frequently suffer from structural redundancy (i.e., \emph{overthinking}), incurring high computational overhead without improving answer correctness. Existing mitigation strategies typically rely on token-uniform length penalties, which provide coarse, segment-agnostic pressure toward shorter outputs and can inadvertently suppress useful reasoning alongside redundancy. To address this, we demonstrate that inefficiency concentrates in high-probability segments with low marginal utility. We derive a theoretical characterization of segment suboptimality under the correctness-length trade-off objective and propose \textsc{SLAT} (Segment-Level Adaptive Trimming), an RL framework that selectively suppresses redundant segments based on this criterion. Empirical results on standard benchmarks indicate that \textsc{SLAT} establishes a superior accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, reducing reasoning length by $50\%$ relative to uncompressed baselines while maintaining competitive accuracy. Overall, our results suggest that theoretically grounded, segment-aware trimming is a promising direction for efficient CoT reasoning in large language models.

</details>


### [131] [COMPASS: Cognitive MCTS-Guided Process Alignment for Safe Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30838)
*Wenkai Shen,Pengyang Zhou,Jiahe Xu,Jiaming Qian,Haozhe He,Zhihao Huang,Chaochao Chen,Xiaolin Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COMPASS is a cognitive MCTS-guided framework for safety alignment in LLM-powered search agents, combining cognitive tree exploration to uncover stealthy attack paths and introspective step-wise alignment to supervise risky intermediate actions, yielding a favorable safety-utility trade-off with reduced data needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-induced safety degradation in multi-step LLM workflows occurs when harmful intents decompose into innocuous sub-queries; existing alignment methods fail to detect sparse, diverse violations across interactions.

Method: 1) Cognitive tree exploration (CTE) to efficiently synthesize stealthy attack trajectories; 2) Introspective step-wise alignment (ISA) to identify and supervise risky intermediate actions throughout the agent’s workflow; 3) Integration that preserves general utility while improving safety.

Result: Empirical evaluations indicate COMPASS achieves a favorable safety-utility trade-off and requires substantially less training data compared to baselines.

Conclusion: COMPASS presents a robust, data-efficient approach to safety alignment in multi-step LLM-powered agents by combining cognitive search with granular, step-wise supervision; further work could explore broader evaluation and scalability.

Abstract: LLM-powered search agents enable multi-step reasoning and tool use.
