However, these capabilities introduce retrieval-induced safety degradation, as harmful intents may decompose into seemingly innocuous sub-queries that lead to unsafe outcomes. Existing alignment methods struggle to capture sparse safety signals and fail to supervise diverse violations across multi-step interactions. We propose COMPASS, a Cognitive MCTS-Guided Process Alignment framework designed to achieve robust safety alignment throughout the agent workflow while preserving general utility. COMPASS integrates cognitive tree exploration (CTE) to efficiently synthesize stealthy attack trajectories, and introspective step-wise alignment (ISA) to isolate risky intermediate actions for fine-grained process supervision. Empirical results show that COMPASS achieves a favorable safety-utility trade-off while requiring substantially less training data.

</details>


### [132] [Distilling LLM Feedback for Lean Theorem Proving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30861)
*Gaetan Narozniak,Gérard Biau,Rémi Munos,Ahmad Rammal,Pierre Marion*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Feedback Distillation, a token-level self-distillation method using privileged feedback from a language model to improve post-training for reasoning models, yielding higher diversity, entropy, and better pass@k, and complementary to GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training for reasoning models suffers from sparse rewards, limited exploration, and mode collapse in traditional GRPO-based approaches; leveraging self-distillation with privileged feedback aims to inject external knowledge and maintain diversity.

Method: Train the model to imitate its own token-level distribution conditioned on privileged feedback from a language model (token-level supervision); can inject external knowledge; demonstrated on Lean4 theorem proving; GRPO can be initialized from Feedback Distillation for complementary gains.

Result: Feedback Distillation yields greater diversity in generated trajectories than GRPO, higher policy entropy, and better pass@k scaling; initializing GRPO from a Feedback Distillation checkpoint outperforms either method alone.

Conclusion: A promising direction for improving post-training of complex reasoning systems; Feedback Distillation and GRPO are complementary, with potential gains from combined usage and external-knowledge injection.

Abstract: Post-training for reasoning models typically combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards, most commonly with GRPO. However, this algorithm suffers from sparse rewards, limited exploration, and mode collapse. Building upon recent works on self-distillation, we propose Feedback Distillation, a training method where the model is trained to match, at the token level, its own distribution conditioned on privileged feedback produced by a language model. Feedback Distillation offers token-level supervision and can inject external knowledge. Evaluating our method for Lean4 theorem-proving, we find that Feedback Distillation maintains greater diversity in generated trajectories than GRPO, yielding higher policy entropy and better pass@k scaling. The two methods are complementary: initializing GRPO from a Feedback Distillation checkpoint outperforms either method alone. All in all, our results suggest a promising avenue to improve post-training for complex reasoning.

</details>


### [133] [UniScale: Adaptive Unified Inference Scaling via Online Joint Optimization of Model Routing and Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30898)
*Kaiyu Huang,Xingyu Wang,Mingze Kong,Zhubo Shi,Yuqian Hou,Hong Xu,Zhongxiang Dai,Minchen Yu,Qingjiang Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unifies model routing and test-time scaling (UIS) into a single optimization space and implements UniScale as an online contextual bandit (LinUCB) to adapt inference policies for LLMs in dynamic environments, achieving finer-grained, better quality-cost trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoupled routing (coarse, discrete changes) and per-model TTS (capacity ceilings, diminishing returns) hinder adaptability and efficiency in real-world, dynamic inference with LLMs.

Method: Formulate UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem; implement UniScale framework using LinUCB; incorporate efficiency-aware learning and cost modeling to manage high-dimensional action spaces and stabilize optimization.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows UniScale leveraging UIS synergy yields finer-grained control and consistently better quality-cost trade-offs across diverse dynamic inference scenarios.

Conclusion: A unified UIS space and online policy learning (LinUCB) enable adaptive, scalable, and efficient inference by combining routing and TTS, outperforming decoupled baselines.

Abstract: In real-world deployments of large language models (LLMs), balancing inference quality and computational cost has become a central challenge. Existing approaches tackle this trade-off along two largely independent dimensions: model routing, which switches among models of different scales to match request complexity, and test-time scaling (TTS), which adjusts inference-time compute within a fixed model for fine-grained control. However, this decoupled design introduces inherent limitations. Model routing yields coarse-grained, discrete performance changes due to the sparse set of model scales, while single-model TTS often encounters capacity ceilings and exhibits diminishing returns as compute increases. Moreover, treating the two mechanisms separately restricts adaptability in dynamic inference environments. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Unified Inference Scaling (UIS), which unifies model routing and TTS in a single optimization space. Building on this formulation, we propose UniScale, an online framework that models adaptive UIS as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem and learns inference policies via LinUCB. The framework incorporates efficiency-aware learning and cost modeling to ensure stable and scalable optimization over high-dimensional action spaces. Evaluation shows that UniScale effectively exploits the synergy in the UIS space to deliver a fine-grained and consistently better quality-cost trade-off across diverse, dynamic inference scenarios.

</details>


### [134] [BilliardPhys-Bench: Benchmarking Physical Reasoning and Visual Dynamics of Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30900)
*Ben Wang,Xiaogang Li,Ruochen Gao,Peiyao Xiao,Chengliang Xu,Zeyu Wang,Zichao Chen,Bing Zhao,Hu Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BilliardPhys-Bench provides a procedural, synthetic billiards environment to benchmark physical reasoning in multimodal LLMs, revealing time/complexity-dependent performance drops and a persistent 'stasis bias' where models opt for no interaction when inference is hard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal models exhibit strong static recognition but weak intuitive physical reasoning. A robust benchmark is needed to evaluate and guide improvements in dynamic physical inference.

Method: A procedural engine generates randomized billiard scenes with friction and elastic collisions. The benchmark evaluates three tasks: predicting ball-to-ball collisions, wall bounces, and final positions after motion stops. It assesses recent MLLMs from GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Qwen families across varying simulation times and geometric complexity.

Result: Model performance declines as simulation time increases and scene geometry becomes more complex. A consistent 'stasis bias' emerges, where, when the outcome is difficult to infer, models tend to predict no interaction. This reveals systematic weaknesses in current multimodal models for visual dynamics.

Conclusion: Current MLLMs struggle with dynamic physical reasoning; the work underscores the need for stronger physical inductive biases and better integration of dynamics-aware reasoning in multimodal architectures.

Abstract: Current multimodal models handle static image recognition well, but intuitive physical reasoning remains a weakness. Predicting how objects will move and interact from a single image is still difficult for these systems. We present BilliardPhys-Bench, a benchmark for physical reasoning in synthetic billiards environments. Its procedural engine generates randomized scenarios with friction and elastic collisions. The benchmark tests three abilities: (1) predicting ball-to-ball collisions, (2) reasoning about wall bounces, and (3) estimating final ball positions after motion stops. We evaluate recent MLLMs from the GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Qwen families. Performance drops as simulation time increases and scene geometry grows more complex. We also observe a consistent failure mode we call "stasis bias": when the correct physical outcome is harder to infer, models tend to predict no interaction. These findings show where current MLLMs break down on visual dynamics and point toward the need for better physical inductive biases in multimodal architectures.

</details>


### [135] [A Persona-Based Evaluation Framework for Pluralistic Alignment in Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31021)
*Atahan Karagoz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a state-space, persona-based evaluation framework for AI that captures diverse human perspectives, demonstrates that generative models can instantiate these evaluative personas but exhibit drift under sequential prompting, and argues for dynamic regulatory mechanisms and a dynamical-systems framing to enable adaptive, context-sensitive evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI alignment benchmarks rely on monolithic, aggregated judgments that fail to reflect cultural, demographic, and contextual diversity in human evaluation, potentially biasing or narrowing assessments of capability and alignment.

Method: Introduce a state-space constrained emulation framework that uses a structured manifold of synthetic cognitive profiles (evaluative personas). Assess a generative model’s ability to instantiate/maintain these personas and analyze stability under sequential inference and stochastic prompt perturbations. Examine degeneracy as state-space drift and semantic inconsistency, and argue for embedding viability-driven regulatory mechanisms. Frame persona-based evaluation as a structured dynamical system over latent manifolds.

Result: Generative architectures can instantiate and maintain evaluative personas with high consistency, enabling pluralistic benchmarking. However, sequential inference and prompt perturbations cause systematic degradation in persona coherence, manifested as state-space drift and semantic inconsistency.

Conclusion: Static alignment constraints are insufficient for robust, long-term evaluative behavior. Dynamic regulatory mechanisms are needed to sustain coherent cognitive emulation. Framing persona-based evaluation as a dynamical system over latent manifolds provides a foundation for adaptive, human-aligned, context-sensitive AI evaluation.

Abstract: Current alignment paradigms for generative artificial intelligence rely predominantly on monolithic benchmarking frameworks that reduce the plurality of human judgment to aggregated statistical baselines, thereby obscuring cultural, demographic, and contextual variability in evaluation. We introduce a state-space constrained emulation framework for AI evaluation that replaces singular assessment functions with a structured manifold of synthetic cognitive profiles representing diverse human perspectives. We show that modern generative architectures can instantiate and maintain these evaluative personas with high consistency, enabling a form of pluralistic, perspective-dependent benchmarking that more closely reflects real-world consensus variability. However, we further analyze the stability of these simulated evaluators under sequential inference and stochastic prompt perturbations, revealing systematic degradation in persona coherence that manifests as state-space drift and semantic inconsistency. These findings suggest that static alignment constraints are insufficient for sustaining robust evaluative behavior over time. Instead, we argue for the necessity of embedding dynamic, viability-driven regulatory mechanisms within generative systems to preserve coherent cognitive emulation. By framing persona-based evaluation as a structured dynamical system over latent representation manifolds, this study provides a foundation for more adaptive, human-aligned, and context-sensitive approaches to AI evaluation.

</details>


### [136] [HADT: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Differential Transformer for Autonomous Earth Observation Satellite Cluster](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31023)
*Mohamad A. Hady,Muhammad Anwar Masum,Siyi Hu,Mahardhika Pratama,Jimmy Cao,Ryszard Kowalczyk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Transformer-based model-free RL for autonomous resource management in heterogeneous satellite EO clusters; relational tokenization and differential attention enable robust performance and transferability across varying cluster sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limitations of model-based scheduling under dynamic uncertainty in space environments; need autonomous, real-time decision-making with minimal ground interaction.

Method: Introduce a transformer-based architecture designed for heterogeneous satellite clusters, employing relational observations-actions tokenization and a differential attention mechanism. Formulated as a sequential decision-making problem solved via model-free reinforcement learning; evaluated against baselines.

Result: Significant performance improvements over baselines; strong adaptability and transferability to varying numbers of satellite clusters.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture provides effective autonomous resource management for heterogeneous satellite EO missions, with scalable transferability and potential for broad deployment.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of autonomous resource management in heterogeneous satellite cluster conducting Earth Observation (EO) missions including optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) satellites. In autonomous operation mode, satellites are equipped with intelligent capabilities enabling real-time decision-making based on the latest conditions, while requiring minimal interaction with ground operators. Traditional scheduling approaches typically rely on mathematical models to represent satellite mission and resource management. Then, this problem is solved by using optimization algorithms. However, such solutions become less effective when the underlying models are not available, over complex, and inaccurate due to dynamic changes and uncertainties inherent in the space mission environment. A promising alternative is to reformulate the problem as a sequential decision-making process and apply model-free reinforcement learning techniques to enable adaptive and real-time resource management. To this end, we propose a novel transformer-based architecture tailored for heterogeneous satellite cluster autonomous EO Mission with relational observations-actions tokenization and differential attention mechanism. Our experimental results demonstrate significant performance improvements compared to the available baselines. Moreover, the proposed architecture exhibits strong adaptability and transferability with respect to varying numbers of satellite clusters.

</details>


### [137] [GraphARC: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Graph-Based Abstract Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31031)
*Saku Peltonen,August Bøgh Rønberg,Andreas Plesner,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GraphARC extends ARC to graph-structured data, assessing few-shot graph transformations and generalization across graph families; reveals model limitations and scaling issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate abstract reasoning on graphs beyond grids, and to quantify generalization with few-shot transformation learning in a scalable benchmark for graph-structured tasks.

Method: Construct tasks where a rule is inferred from a few input-output graphs and applied to a new test graph; covers local, global, and hierarchical graph transformations; generated at scale across diverse graph families and sizes; evaluate state-of-the-art language models on the full graph-transformation task.

Result: Language models can answer questions about graph properties but struggle with the full graph transformation task, revealing a comprehension-execution gap; performance further degrades on larger instances, indicating scaling barriers.

Conclusion: GraphARC provides a unified framework combining node classification, link prediction, and graph generation within a single benchmark, offering a promising testbed for future graph foundation models.

Abstract: Relational reasoning lies at the heart of intelligence, but existing benchmarks are typically confined to formats such as grids or text. We introduce GraphARC, a benchmark for abstract reasoning on graph-structured data. GraphARC generalizes the few-shot transformation learning paradigm of the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC). Each task requires inferring a transformation rule from a few input-output pairs and applying it to a new test graph, covering local, global, and hierarchical graph transformations. Unlike grid-based ARC, GraphARC instances can be generated at scale across diverse graph families and sizes, enabling systematic evaluation of generalization abilities. We evaluate state-of-the-art language models on GraphARC and observe clear limitations. Models can answer questions about graph properties but often fail to solve the full graph transformation task, revealing a comprehension-execution gap. Performance further degrades on larger instances, exposing scaling barriers. More broadly, by combining aspects of node classification, link prediction, and graph generation within a single framework, GraphARC provides a promising testbed for future graph foundation models.

</details>


### [138] [Vector Linking via Cross-Model Local Isometric Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31100)
*Ziying Chen,Yang Cao,He Sun,Beining Yang,Tianjian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method to link vectors across different encoders by seed anchors and geometric hashing, enabling cross-model linking with little supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable cross-model vector space alignment when only black-box encoders and partially-overlapping data are available, enabling tasks like vector DB integration and cross-model clustering.

Method: Iterative, reference-based geometric embedding hashing: each vector is represented by distances to sampled paired anchors; candidate links are proposed via hash-space matching; evidence across views is aggregated with a Beta-Bernoulli posterior to bootstrap new anchors from seed links; starts from a tiny seed set of paired anchors and grows the linking graph.

Result: The approach yields accurate and robust cross-model linking across multiple benchmarks and embedding model pairs, under varying data overlap, seed budgets, and out-of-domain anchors; short-range distances are approximately preserved up to a scale factor, while long-range distances show model-specific distortion; demonstrates practical effectiveness and robustness.

Conclusion: VecLinking provides a practical framework for vector-level cross-model linking with minimal initial supervision, enabling integration of vector databases and cross-model clustering; broad applicability and open-source code are provided.

Abstract: We study Vector Linking: given two embedding clouds produced by different black-box encoders over partially overlapping datasets, recover cross-model object correspondences using only vectors. Empirically and theoretically, we show that independently trained contrastive encoders exhibit local geometric consistency: short-range distances are approximately preserved up to a scale factor, while long-range distances are not due to model-specific distortion. Building on this, we propose an iterative, reference-based geometric embedding hashing that recovers vector links from a tiny seed set of paired anchors. It represents each vector by distances to sampled paired anchors, proposes candidate links via hash-space matching, and aggregates evidence across views in a Beta-Bernoulli posterior to bootstrap high-confidence links as new anchors. Experiments across multiple benchmarks and embedding model pairs demonstrate accurate and robust linking under varying overlap, seed budgets, and out-of-domain anchors, with applications to vector database integration and cross-model clustering. Code is available at https://github.com/DBgroup-Edinburgh/VecLinking.

</details>


### [139] [LLM-FACETS: A Privacy-Preserving Framework for Evaluating LLM Transparency and Accountability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31167)
*Tom Lucas,Alessio Buscemi,Alfredo Capozucca,German Castignani,Barbara Delacroix*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An open-source, browser-based framework (LLM-FACETS) for auditing LLM outputs across factual grounding, epistemic calibration, and reproducibility. It serves three practitioner profiles (technical, domain, compliance), keeps deterministic metrics local, exposes external LLM-judge metrics with credential controls, and provides transparent visualization and consensus mechanisms to localize hallucinations; supports a plug-in metric/dataset architecture and cross-validates 18 metrics against canonical libraries to ensure reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing LLMs is inaccessible to non-technical practitioners due to heavy setup and cloud-based data transmissions, hindering responsible AI oversight. A transparent, open, and modular tool that aligns with EU AI Act and NIST RMF roles is needed to enable domain experts, technical staff, and compliance officers to assess grounding, calibration, and reproducibility.

Method: An open-source framework with a browser-based interface and plugin architecture. Data flows are explicit: deterministic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) run locally with no outbound data; LLM-judge metrics call external APIs under user credential control. Provides token-level log-probability visualization, multi-judge consensus to mitigate bias, and RAG Triad metrics (Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, Context Relevance) to localize hallucinations. Plugins allow new metrics/datasets without changing the core pipeline. Cross-validation across 18 metric implementations against canonical libraries.

Result: The framework is implemented as an open-source tool enabling cross-checking across multiple metrics for the same property, promoting reproducibility and decoupling AI accountability from the evaluation teams. Validation shows 18 metric implementations align with canonical reference libraries.

Conclusion: LLM-FACETS advances transparent, reproducible, and accessible evaluation of LLMs for factuality, epistemic quality, and reproducibility, enabling domain experts, technical personnel, and compliance officers to audit and localize errors while maintaining control over data flows and credentials.

Abstract: Assessing whether Large Language Models outputs are factually grounded, epistemically calibrated, and methodologically reproducible is a prerequisite for responsible AI deployment. Yet auditing LLMs remains inaccessible to non-technical practitioners: existing tools require programming expertise and non-trivial environment setup, and cloud-hosted platforms transmit evaluation data to external services, creating barriers for domain experts and compliance officers legally responsible for AI oversight. We introduce LLM-FACETS (LLM FActuality Cross-EvaluaTion System): an open-source framework with a browser-accessible interface and a plugin architecture, structured around three practitioner profiles (technical experts, domain experts, compliance officers) that mirror the stakeholder categories identified in the EU AI Act and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. The architecture makes data flows explicit: deterministic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore) run entirely within the self-hosted server with no outbound transmission; LLM-judge metrics contact external APIs explicitly, with users retaining full credential control. The framework operationalizes transparency through three mechanisms: token-level log-probability visualization for epistemic uncertainty, multi-judge consensus to mitigate judge bias, and RAG Triad metrics (Faithfulness, Answer Relevance, Context Relevance) to detect and localize hallucinations. A plugin architecture allows any new metric or dataset to be integrated without modifying the evaluation pipeline. The open-source implementation enables cross-checking across multiple metrics targeting the same property, ensuring reproducibility and decoupling AI accountability from the teams building the systems assessed. We verify the framework through cross-validation of 18 metric implementations against canonical reference libraries.

</details>


### [140] [Formalizing and falsifying causal pathways of rare events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31254)
*Anahita Haghighat,Dominik Janzing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes a causal pathway abstraction for rare-event dynamics in structural equation models, deriving testable implications and showing they can depend only on the rare-event pathway rather than the full causal graph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge verbal causal explanations with detailed causal modeling by focusing on pathways of rare events and identifying when inferences are invariant to full graph structure.

Method: Define a causal pathway for rare events within SEMs, derive testable implications, and establish conditions under which these implications are determined by the pathway abstraction alone; propose a mapping from full causal structure to rare-event pathways.

Result: Characterizes conditions where causal implications follow from the rare-event pathway abstraction, and introduces an abstraction framework that connects simple verbal explanations to detailed causal models.

Conclusion: Pathway-based causal abstractions for rare events enable robust inferences and explanations with reduced dependence on the full causal graph, serving as a bridge between intuition and formal modeling.

Abstract: Building on recent formalizations of root cause analysis for rare events (``outliers'') in structural equation models, we propose a formal definition of a causal pathway and discuss its testable implications. We identify conditions under which these implications depend only on a causal abstraction defined by the pathway of rare events, rather than on the full causal graph of the underlying system. Accordingly, we introduce an abstraction of causal structure to pathways of rare events that bridges simple verbal causal explanations and detailed causal modeling.

</details>


### [141] [COLLEAGUE.SKILL: Automated AI Skill Generation via Expert Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31264)
*Tianyi Zhou,Dongrui Liu,Leitao Yuan,Jing Shao,Xia Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A trace-to-skill distillation system COLLEAGUE.SKILL to create portable, versioned person-grounded AI skills from heterogeneous human traces, enabling inspectable behavior and interaction patterns, with an open-source ecosystem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmentation of person-specific knowledge encoded in diverse traces (expertise, judgment, interaction style) and to provide an end-to-end workflow that yields inspectable, correctable, and deployable skills.

Method: Automated trace-to-skill distillation pipeline producing two coordinated tracks: a capability track (practices, mental models, and decision heuristics) and a bounded behavior track (communication style, interaction rules, and correction history). Outputs are versioned skill packages with an artifact contract, generation workflow, correction lifecycle, and deployment surface; includes domain presets and open-source implementation.

Result: Introduces COLLEAGUE.SKILL as an end-to-end system for distilling traces into portable, inspectable skill packages, with versioning, correction, rollback, cross-host installation, and an active open-source ecosystem (repositories, skill gallery).

Conclusion: Person-grounded skills can be represented as portable, correctable packages rather than opaque prompts or hidden memories, enabling scalable, controllable agent personalization.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly expected not only to complete isolated tasks, but also to carry bounded representations of human expertise, judgment, and interaction style. Building such person-grounded agents remains difficult because actionable knowledge associated with a person or role is usually embedded in heterogeneous traces rather than written as clean instructions. Existing memory and persona systems capture fragments of this evidence, while skill frameworks provide portable packaging formats; however, there is no end-to-end workflow for distilling these traces into inspectable, correctable, and agent-usable skills. We present an automated trace-to-skill distillation system for generating person-grounded AI skills via expert knowledge distillation. Given materials from a target person or role, COLLEAGUE.SKILL produces a versioned skill package with two coordinated tracks: a capability track for practices, mental models, and decision heuristics, and a bounded behavior track for communication style, interaction rules, and correction history. The package can be inspected, invoked, updated through natural-language feedback, rolled back, installed across agent hosts, and optionally prepared for controlled distribution. We describe the artifact contract, generation workflow, correction lifecycle, deployment surface, and domain presets implemented in the open-source system. At the time of writing, the public repository has approximately 18.5k GitHub stars; the gallery lists 215 skills from 165 contributors and more than 100k cumulative stars across listed skill cards. The system illustrates how person-grounded skills can be represented as portable, correctable packages rather than opaque prompts or hidden memories.

</details>


### [142] [Industrializing Prediction-Powered Inference: The GLIDE Library for Reliable GenAI and Agentic Systems Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31278)
*Grégoire Martinon,Ibrahim Merad,Mohammed Raki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GLIDE unifies prediction-powered inference (PPI) estimators and samplers under a SciPy-style API for mean estimation, with a reproducible validation suite, an empirical method-selection decision tree, and a case study showing annotation savings; open-source at github.com/EmertonData/glide.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable, unbiased evaluation of agentic systems requires valid uncertainty with manageable annotation costs. Existing practice relies on costly human annotation or biased LLM judgments, and PPI methods are scattered across papers without a common implementation or evaluation framework.

Method: GLIDE implements multiple PPI estimators (PPI++, Stratified PPI, Predict-Then-Debias with variants, Active Statistical Inference) and samplers (uniform, stratified, active, cost-optimal) with a SciPy-style API tailored to mean estimation. It includes a Monte Carlo validation suite, a data-driven decision tree for method selection, and an agentic evaluation case study.

Result: Provides a unified toolkit for debiased, uncertainty-valid mean estimation in agentic evaluation; enables reproducible experiments and demonstrates substantial annotation savings at equivalent precision in the case study.

Conclusion: GLIDE offers a standardized, open-source platform for prediction-powered inference methods and sampling strategies, enabling efficient, unbiased evaluation of agentic systems and facilitating method selection via a data-driven decision tree.

Abstract: Reliable evaluation of agentic systems requires unbiased estimates with valid uncertainty, but standard practice navigates between costly human annotation and biased LLM-as-judge proxies. Prediction-powered inference (PPI) combines both into debiased estimates with valid confidence intervals, yet its various methods remain scattered across papers under partial implementations. We introduce GLIDE, an open-source Python library that unifies state-of-the-art PPI estimators (PPI++, Stratified PPI, Predict-Then-Debias and its stratified variants, Active Statistical Inference) and samplers (uniform, stratified, active, cost-optimal) under a scipy-style API specialized to mean estimation. GLIDE ships with a reproducible Monte Carlo validation suite, an empirically grounded decision tree for method selection, and an agentic evaluation case study showing substantial annotation savings at equivalent precision. The GLIDE package is available at this URL: https://github.com/EmertonData/glide

</details>


### [143] [TraceGraph: Shared Decision Landscapes for Diagnosing and Improving Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31308)
*Junjie Nian,Kang Chen,Ge Zhang,Yixin Cao,Yugang Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceGraph establishes a graph-based diagnostic of multi-model agent trajectories, revealing nuanced navigation behaviors and enabling a trap-aware recovery pipeline that modestly improves resolved rates; it provides a vocabulary for benchmarking diagnostics and targeted improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond aggregate pass/reward metrics by capturing state-action-observation dynamics and trap-region structures in agent benchmarks, enabling deeper analysis and actionable improvements.

Method: Construct a graph from pooled action-observation states across rollouts before model identity; overlay outcome-informed productive cores and trap regions; summarize rollouts with Access, Trap exposure, and Repair; analyze across five benchmark splits to reveal hidden variations; apply a trap-aware recovery pipeline for SWE-bench using an aruntime detector on historical trap states and evaluate continuation policies from the same prefix; report improvements on official resolved rates.

Result: TraceGraph uncovers navigation differences masked by aggregate scores and shows splits differ in trap-reward dynamics. In SWE-bench, the trap-aware policy raises the official resolved rate from 40.4% to 43.5% (per-provider fired subset) and from 41.0% to 44.8% (common-fired instances), with provider-specific active components.

Conclusion: TraceGraph provides a reusable process vocabulary for diagnosing what benchmarks test, where models diverge on a shared landscape, and how failure regions can guide downstream improvement.

Abstract: Agent benchmarks increasingly record rich interaction trajectories, yet evaluation often reduces each rollout to a pass rate or reward score. We introduce TraceGraph, a graph-based framework that turns released multi-model agent trajectories into shared decision landscapes. For each task, TraceGraph builds a graph over observable action-observation states from pooled rollouts before model identity is introduced. It then overlays outcome-informed productive cores and trap regions, and summarizes each rollout with three events: Access, Trap exposure, and Repair. Across trajectories spanning five benchmark splits, TraceGraph profiles reveal navigation differences hidden by aggregate scores and show that splits differ in whether they reward avoiding traps or recovering from them. The same TraceGraph landscape also motivates a trap-aware recovery pipeline for SWE-bench: aruntime detector fires on states matching historical trap regions, then lightweight continuation policies are evaluated from the same prefix. On fired states, the best pooled single-factor policy raises official resolved rate from 40.4% to 43.5% on the per-provider fired subset and from 41.0% to 44.8% on common-fired instances, with provider-specific active components. Overall, TraceGraph provides a process vocabulary for asking what agent benchmarks test, where models diverge on a shared landscape, and how failure regions can guide downstream improvement.

</details>


### [144] [Diagnosing Failure Modes of Shared-State Collaboration in Resource-Constrained Visual Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31354)
*Yunpeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Low-capacity modular visual reasoning systems are prone to failure due to noise accumulation in shared working memory. The paper introduces CoSee to audit read–write–verify loops in document VQA, revealing that shared workspaces can amplify hallucinations. Two main failure modes are Noise Reinforcement and Policy Collapse. Higher compute without verification may worsen performance. The bottleneck is communication fidelity, not reasoning depth, with traceable diagnostics for modular design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how intermediate state evolution fails in modular visual reasoning when learners are weak (4B–8B) by analyzing information flow and error propagation in collaborative, multi-step reasoning.

Method: Develop CoSee, an auditing framework that formalizes the read–write–verify loop to trace information flow in document visual question answering across multi-page, chart, and web-based benchmarks. Analyze failure modes, quantify cost–accuracy tradeoffs, and provide trace-level diagnostics for modular design.

Result: Identification of counter-intuitive degradation where naive shared workspaces amplify hallucinations. Two dominant failure modes emerge: Noise Reinforcement (ungrounded notes reused as evidence) and Policy Collapse (added context leads to under-specified, short-form answers). Cost–accuracy Pareto analysis shows increased compute can harm performance without explicit verification. CoSee offers traceable diagnostics and a baseline for reliable modular design.

Conclusion: For resource-constrained agents, the bottleneck lies in communication fidelity rather than reasoning depth. The framework provides mechanistic, traceable insights and benchmarks to guide reliable modular architectures.

Abstract: Modular visual reasoning systems increasingly rely on shared working memory for multi-step collaboration, yet the failure dynamics of intermediate state evolution in low-capacity regimes remain underexplored. We study failure modes of collaborative reasoning with weak learners (4B--8B models) through the lens of noise accumulation. We introduce CoSee, an auditing framework that formalizes the read-write-verify loop to trace information flow in document visual question answering. Across multi-page, chart, and web-based benchmarks, we find a counter-intuitive degradation: naive shared workspaces often amplify hallucinations rather than resolve them. We identify two dominant failure modes: Noise Reinforcement, where ungrounded notes are reused as evidence, and Policy Collapse, where added context shifts the model toward under-specified, short-form answers. Using cost-accuracy Pareto frontiers, we show that increased compute can correlate negatively with performance without explicit verification. Our findings suggest that for resource-constrained agents, the bottleneck lies not in reasoning depth but in communication fidelity, providing trace-level diagnostics and a mechanistic baseline for reliable modular design.

</details>


### [145] [Learning to Adapt: Self-Improving Web Agent via Cognitive-Aware Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31365)
*Weile Chen,Bingchen Miao,Qifan Yu,Wendong Bu,Guoming Wang,Wenqiao Zhang,Shengyu Zhang,Juncheng Li,Siliang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SCALE introduces an autonomous learning framework for Multimodal LLM web agents using three adversarial roles (Selector, Predictor, Judger) to identify limitations and expand cognitive capabilities, plus SCALE-Hop for global graph-based exploration and SCALE-20k, a large-scale web-exploration dataset; results show improved performance and generalization across MLLMs in diverse web environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome reliance on handcrafted execution pipelines and expensive expert trajectories in web agents, enabling autonomous discovery of agent limitations and adaptive exploration to handle complex, dynamic environments.

Method: Propose SCALE with three adversarial roles (Selector, Predictor, Judger) for self-cognitive error discovery; SCALE-Hop graph exploration for global planning and avoidance of local traps; SCALE-20k dataset collected from 19 real-world websites with structured demonstrations from SCALE exploration traces.

Result: Experimental results show significant improvements in performance and generalization for multiple MLLMs across various web environments.

Conclusion: Presents a scalable, generalizable framework for truly autonomous and adaptive web agents, leveraging self-cognitive exploration and graph-based planning to broaden cognitive capabilities.

Abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have led to promising progress in web agents. However, existing web agents often rely on handcrafted execution pipelines or expensive expert trajectories, limiting their adaptability to complex, dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we propose SCALE (Self-Cognitive-Aware Learning and Exploration), which leverages three adversarial roles, Selector, Predictor, and Judger to autonomously discover the agent's limitations and expand its cognitive boundaries through environmental exploration. Moreover, we propose SCALE-Hop, a graph exploration strategy that facilitates global planning and helps agents avoid local exploration traps. To further support learning, we construct SCALE-20k, a large-scale dataset collected from 19 real-world websites, containing diverse task types and structured demonstrations generated from SCALE's exploration traces. Experimental results show that our approach significantly improves the performance and generalization of multiple MLLMs in various web environments. Our framework offers a scalable and generalizable solution for building truly autonomous and adaptive web agents.

</details>


### [146] [HypoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Interactive Abductive Hypothesis Generation over Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31370)
*Yisen Gao,Yixi Cai,Tianshi Zheng,Jiaxin Bai,Yangqiu Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HypoAgent is a three-agent framework for interactive abductive hypothesis generation over knowledge graphs, grounding user intent, controllable generation, and root-cause analysis to improve multi-turn dialogue performance and semantic similarity, achieving state-of-the-art results across settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing controllable hypothesis generation methods struggle to ground evolving natural-language intents in multi-turn dialogues and lack fine-grained diagnosis for failed hypotheses. There is a need for interactive, interpretable abductive reasoning over KG to adapt to dialogue history and provide refinements.

Method: HypoAgent combines three agents: (1) Intent Recognition Agent that grounds user utterances and dialogue history into executable KG conditions; (2) Hypothesis Generation Agent that generates hypotheses controllably according to extracted intent; (3) Root Cause Analysis Agent that diagnoses unreliable hypothesis fragments and uses KG neighborhood probing to identify supported refinements.

Result: Empirical evaluation on commonsense and biomedical KGs shows HypoAgent achieves state-of-the-art semantic similarity under single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings.

Conclusion: HypoAgent enables more grounded, controllable, and diagnosable abductive hypothesis generation over KGs in interactive settings; code is available at the provided repository.

Abstract: Abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs aims to generate logical hypotheses that explain observed entities or facts. Existing controllable hypothesis generation methods allow users to guide this process with explicit conditions, but they remain limited in interactive settings: they struggle to ground evolving natural-language intents across multi-turn dialogues and provide little fine-grained diagnosis when generated hypotheses fail. To address these limitations, we propose HypoAgent, an Agentic framework for interactive abductive Hypothesis Generation over knowledge graphs. HypoAgent integrates three agents: an Intent Recognition Agent that grounds user utterances and dialogue history into executable KG conditions, a Hypothesis Generation Agent that performs controllable hypothesis generation according to the extracted user intention, and a Root Cause Analysis Agent that diagnoses unreliable hypothesis fragments and leverages KG neighborhood probing to identify supported refinements. Experiments on commonsense and biomedical domain-specific knowledge graphs demonstrate that HypoAgent achieves state-of-the-art semantic similarity under single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/HypoAgent.

</details>


### [147] [FAM-Bench: A Multimodal Benchmark for Condition-Aware Food-as-Medicine Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31410)
*Mingyang Mao,Bhargav Rishi Medisetti,Utkarsh Grover,Tanvir Ibrahim,Wenyan Li,Tingting Zhang,Xiaomin Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces FAM-Bench, a 2,500-instance, multi-modal benchmark for judging dish suitability for health conditions and ranking dishes by condition-specific suitability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current food-AI benchmarks focus on recognition, recipe understanding, or nutrient estimation, lacking a health-aware decision layer that reasons about whether a concrete dish is appropriate for a given health condition.

Method: Construct 2,500 nutrition-expert-verified instances across 13 diet-related health conditions. Provide two tasks: (1) dish-level suitability assessment from image and ingredient list, judging if a dish is suitable for a condition; (2) comparative dish analysis ranking four candidate dishes by condition-specific suitability. Data integrate ingredient evidence, visual preparation cues, and clinical nutrition constraints for grounded health-aware reasoning in language and vision-language models.

Result: The abstract presents dataset construction and task definitions; it does not report empirical results or baselines.

Conclusion: FAM-Bench offers a standardized testbed to evaluate grounded health-aware reasoning in multimodal models, advancing the evaluation of the health- (Food-as-Medicine) decision layer beyond traditional dish-recognition or nutrition estimation tasks.

Abstract: Food-as-Medicine requires models to reason beyond what a dish is or what nutrition it contains: they must decide whether a concrete food choice is appropriate for a specific health condition. Existing food AI benchmarks primarily evaluate dish recognition, recipe understanding, nutrient estimation, or general nutrition question answering, leaving this health-aware decision layer largely untested. We introduce FAM-Bench, a multi-modal Food-as-Medicine benchmark with 2500 nutrition-expert-verified instances across 13 diet-related health conditions. The benchmark contains two complementary tasks: dish-level suitability assessment, where models judge whether a dish is suitable for a condition from its image and ingredient list, and comparative dish analysis, where models rank four candidate dishes by condition-specific suitability. Both tasks require integrating ingredient evidence, visual preparation cues, and clinical nutrition constraints, providing a standardized testbed for grounded health-aware reasoning in language and vision-language models.

</details>


### [148] [Answer-Set-Programming-based Abstractions for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31444)
*Rafael Bankosegger,Thomas Eiter,Johannes Oetsch*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ASP-based CARCASS abstractions for RL in first-order domains; evaluation on Blocks World and Minigrid shows feasibility and promise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL in realistic problems has enormous state spaces; relational abstractions and logical representations (CARCASS) help model MDPs in first-order domains; ASP offers a fully declarative alternative to Prolog.

Method: Implement CARCASS abstractions using Answer-Set Programming (ASP) and evaluate in two case studies (Blocks World and Minigrid) to assess feasibility and benefits when domain knowledge is available.

Result: ASP-based CARCASS is feasible and promising for constructing abstractions for RL; case studies validate the approach and show potential benefits when domain knowledge is available.

Conclusion: ASP-based CARCASS provides a viable, declarative framework for RL abstractions in first-order domains and merits further exploration across additional domains and datasets.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) enables autonomous agents to learn policies from experience, but realistic problems often involve enormous state spaces, making learning and generalisation challenging. Abstraction and approximation are therefore essential. Relational Reinforcement Learning (RRL) offers a way to reason about objects and their relations, and the CARCASS framework by Martijn van Otterlo demonstrates how logical representations can model Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) in first-order domains. Originally implemented in Prolog, CARCASS leverages domain knowledge to create powerful abstractions. We explore Answer-Set Programming (ASP), which is a rich and, contrary to Prolog, fully declarative modelling language, to realise CARCASS abstractions. We evaluate our ASP-based implementation in case studies of two domains, viz. Blocks World and Minigrid. Our results indicate that CARCASS with ASP provides a promising approach to constructing abstractions for RL, especially when domain knowledge is available.

</details>


### [149] [AutoSci: A Memory-Centric Agentic System for the Full Scientific Research Lifecycle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31468)
*Weitong Qian,Beicheng Xu,Zhongao Xie,Bowen Fan,Guozheng Tang,Jiale Chen,Xinzhe Wu,Mingtian Yang,Chenyang Di,Jiajun Li,Lingching Tung,Peichao Lai,Yifei Xia,Ziyi Guo,Yanwei Xu,Yanzhao Qin,Shaoduo Gan,Xupeng Miao,Bin Cui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoSci is a memory-centric, agentic framework for end-to-end scientific research. It unifies the full lifecycle with four modules: SciMem (memory architecture for knowledge and project artifacts), SciFlow (five-stage research lifecycle with state/control/verification/feedback/orchestration), SciDAG (DAG-shaped multi-agent operators and templates), and SciEvolve (feedback-driven versioned updates to memory, skills, and templates), enabling persistent execution, memory, and evolution across projects. Codebase at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in existing systems: they often address only parts of the research lifecycle or lack persistent, cross-project memory and self-improving procedures. A unified, memory-centric agentic system could automate the entire scientific workflow.

Method: Proposes a four-module architecture (SciMem, SciFlow, SciDAG, SciEvolve) with clear memory separation (Long-Term Knowledge Memory vs Active Research Memory), a five-stage SciFlow lifecycle with a control harness, DAG-based multi-agent operators and reusable templates for SciDAG, and feedback-driven evolution for SciEvolve, resulting in a persistent research environment across projects.

Result: Conceptual framework and architecture; claims a unified system (AutoSci) that can execute, remember, and evolve across research projects; provides a GitHub code repository but no reported empirical results in the abstract.

Conclusion: AutoSci offers a memory-centric, end-to-end solution for automated scientific research, addressing coordination, memory, and procedural improvements across projects through its modular design.

Abstract: Scientific research has traditionally been human-intensive, requiring researchers to coordinate literature, ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and review responses across long project cycles. The rise of LLM-based scientific agents creates an opportunity to automate this process. Such a system must support the full research lifecycle, maintain structured persistent memory across projects, and improve its own research procedures over time. However, existing systems either partially satisfy or fail to satisfy these requirements, leaving a gap for a unified automated scientific research system. As a result, we present AutoSci, a memory-centric agentic system for the full scientific research lifecycle. AutoSci is organized around four modules. SciMem provides schema-governed research memory, separating Long-Term Knowledge Memory for reusable scientific knowledge from Active Research Memory for project-level artifacts such as ideas, experiments, manuscripts, and reviews. SciFlow executes a five-stage lifecycle from literature understanding to rebuttal through a harness that controls state, context, verification, feedback, and orchestration. SciDAG augments difficult skills with DAG-shaped multi-agent operators and reusable stage-specific templates. SciEvolve converts feedback signals from users, experiments, reviews, and external environments into versioned updates to SciMem organization, SciFlow skills, and SciDAG templates. Together, these modules make AutoSci a persistent research environment that can execute, remember, and evolve across research projects. The code repository is available at https://github.com/skyllwt/AutoSci.

</details>


### [150] [LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31492)
*Liwei Kang,Yee Whye Teh,Wee Sun Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs' trace-based reasoning is not automatically superior to heuristic search; explicitly representing the search tree with parent pointers (LinTree) improves performance and efficiency over implicit traces and LLM-guided heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether access to full search trace/history improves reasoning, diagnose why traces help, and assess whether making the search structure explicit enhances utilization of history.

Method: Three controlled environments (Blocks World, grid Navigation, Sokoban). Compare: (1) trace-conditioned policies vs (2) best-first search using an LLM heuristic that only observes current state; then introduce LinTree with explicit parent pointers to represent the linearized search tree and compare performance/efficiency against implicit traces and the heuristic baseline.

Result: Raw access to search history alone does not reliably outperform heuristic search. Implicitly represented search trees limit the benefit of traces. Adding explicit tree structure via LinTree (with parent pointers) improves both task performance and search efficiency relative to both implicit reasoning models and LLM-heuristic-guided search.

Conclusion: Explicit, structure-aware representations of search history are beneficial for LLM reasoning. Making the search tree explicit helps leverage past exploration and backtracking decisions, suggesting future work on integrating tree-structured histories into LLM reasoning systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often solve reasoning problems by generating intermediate traces that explore and revise partial solutions. From a search perspective, these traces can be viewed as linearized search trees, where the model extends a partial solution, abandons it when it fails, and backtracks to try alternatives. Compared with traditional heuristic-guided search, such a policy has a potential advantage: it conditions on the whole search trace rather than only on the current local state. We first test whether LLMs utilize this advantage by comparing trace-conditioned reasoning policies against best-first search equipped with an LLM heuristic that only observes the current local state. Across three controlled reasoning environments, Blocks World, grid Navigation, and Sokoban, we find that raw access to search history alone is not enough to reliably outperform heuristic search. We then study one possible reason: in LLM reasoning traces, the underlying search tree is only implicitly represented, and when the model backtracks or switches branches, the trace does not explicitly identify which earlier search state is being revisited. We show that adding simple parent pointers to explicitly represent the linearized tree (LinTree) structure improves both task performance and search efficiency relative to implicit reasoning models and LLM-heuristic-guided search. These results suggest that search history becomes most useful when its tree structure is made explicit, motivating more structure-aware representations for LLM reasoning.

</details>


### [151] [Choosing the Lens: Strategic Perspective Activation in Context-Dependent Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31581)
*Albert Sadowski,Jarosław A. Chudziak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CDAFs extend abstract argumentation by making defeat context-sensitive via a defeat function; relevance sets and priorities derive defeats, enabling regime-based manipulation; a worked example shows context can flip acceptance; defines ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION with baseline complexity results;—but tight bounds and multi-agent variants remain open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model how external regimes influence evaluation of arguments, capturing strategic leverage of an agent who can affect the regime, which standard Dung frameworks miss.

Method: Extend Dung's abstract argumentation framework with a per-context defeat function (CDAF). Introduce perspective-labeled specialization to derive the defeat function from a relevance set ρ (agent's action space) and a priority π. Provide a small worked example illustrating context-dependent acceptance/rejection. Define the decision problem ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION and establish baseline complexity bounds. Note that tight bounds and multi-agent variants are left for future work.

Result: CDAFs yield context-sensitive defeat relations, enabling regime-dependent argument acceptance. The paper provides a worked example where full-relevance injective priority rejects the target argument, while partial activations can accept it. It formalizes ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION and presents baseline complexity bounds for this problem.

Conclusion: CDAFs offer a mechanism to model regime-driven argument evaluation and manipulation. The approach opens avenues for strategic analysis under context shifts, with initial complexity results provided; rigorous tight bounds and multi-agent scenarios remain open research directions.

Abstract: The same arguments often need to be evaluated under different external regimes. An agent with influence over the regime has a strategic lever that standard formalisms do not directly capture. We introduce context-dependent argumentation frameworks (CDAFs), an extension of Dung's theory in which a defeat function determines, per context, which attacks succeed. A perspective-labeled specialisation derives the defeat function from a relevance set $ρ$ and a priority $π$. The relevance set is the agent's action space. In a small worked example, the agent's target argument is rejected under every full-relevance injective priority, yet accepted under partial activations, one of which no VAF audience can mirror. We define the corresponding decision problem, ACTIVATION-MANIPULATION, and record baseline complexity bounds. Tight bounds and multi-agent variants are left open.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [152] [LongDS-Bench: On the Failure of Long-Horizon Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30434)
*Kewei Xu,Xiaoben Lu,Shuofei Qiao,Zihan Ding,Haoming Xu,Lei Liang,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongDS is a long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis benchmark (68 tasks, 2,225 turns, six domains) that reveals the challenge of maintaining analytical state; current models underperform, with state maintenance as the bottleneck, and code/data are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data analysis is iterative and context evolves over time; existing benchmarks largely assess isolated or short interactive tasks, failing to test long-horizon state tracking.

Method: Constructed 68 tasks from real Kaggle notebooks spanning Geoscience, Business, Education, etc.; designed around state-evolution patterns (counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition); collected 2,225 turns; evaluated five state-of-the-art models; analyzed dependency spans and effect of additional agent steps.

Result: Best model attains 48.45% average accuracy; performance drops about 47 points from early to late turns; long-horizon errors account for 52%–69% of failures; more interaction steps do not reliably improve performance.

Conclusion: LongDS enables research on reliable long-horizon, agentic data analysis; code and data will be released on GitHub for broader community use.

Abstract: Real-world data analysis is inherently iterative, yet existing benchmarks mostly evaluate isolated or short interactive tasks, leaving agents' ability to track evolving analytical context over long horizons untested. We introduce LongDS, a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis where agents must maintain, update, restore, and compose evolving analytical states. LongDS comprises 68 tasks constructed from real-world Kaggle notebooks, spanning 2,225 turns across six domains including Geoscience, Business, and Education. Tasks are designed around state-evolution patterns (e.g., counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition), with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models, we find that the best model reaches only 48.45% average accuracy, performance drops nearly 47 points from early to late turns, and long-horizon errors account for 52%--69% of failures. Further analysis shows that additional agent steps do not necessarily improve performance, suggesting that the key bottleneck is maintaining a correct analytical state rather than increasing interaction budget. We release LongDS to support research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/DataMind.

</details>


### [153] [Calibrated Preference Learning: The Case of Label Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30447)
*Santo M. A. R. Thies,Viktor Bengs,Timo Kaufmann,Sebastian J. Vollmer,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a formal calibration framework for probabilistic label ranking, establishing a hierarchy of calibration notions (full rankings, sub-rankings, top-k). Full-rank calibration implies the others but they are not mutually implied; sub-ranking and top-k calibrations are incomparable. Empirically, many label-ranking models are poorly calibrated, with notable gaps between sub-ranking and top-k metrics. In RLHF reward models, calibration correlates with accuracy but is not identical, indicating a distinct quality dimension. The work motivates methods to diagnose and correct miscalibration and suggests future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration is well-studied for classification/regression but not for probabilistic label ranking, where the target is a distribution over label orderings. Proper calibration for rankings could improve decision-making and reflect a meaningful quality dimension beyond accuracy.

Method: Formalize calibration notions for full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k predictions; prove a hierarchy of implications (full-rank ⇒ sub-ranking/top-k; others not implied); empirically evaluate popular label-ranking models and RLHF reward models using the proposed framework, comparing calibration with ranking metrics.

Result: The authors prove theoretical relationships: full-rank calibration implies sub-ranking and top-k calibration, but the reverse does not hold; sub-ranking and top-k calibrations are incomparable. Empirically, many models exhibit poor calibration and substantial gaps between sub-ranking and top-k performance. In RLHF rewards, calibration correlates strongly with accuracy but not perfectly.

Conclusion: The proposed calibration framework captures a meaningful quality dimension beyond standard accuracy and highlights potential downstream effects of miscalibration. This motivates developing methods to diagnose and correct calibration in label ranking and RLHF contexts, and suggests several avenues for future work.

Abstract: Calibration, the alignment of predicted probabilities with true outcome frequencies, is essential for reliable decision-making. While extensively studied for classification and regression, calibration has not been formally addressed for probabilistic label ranking, where the goal is to predict a distribution over orderings of a label set. Naively treating rankings as classes ignores their structure and fails to capture important modalities such as pairwise and top-k predictions. We formalize calibration for label ranking and develop a hierarchy of notions covering full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k rankings. We prove that full-rank calibration implies the others but not conversely, and sub-ranking and top-k calibration are incomparable. Empirically, we find popular label ranking models are often poorly calibrated, with substantial differences between sub-ranking and top-k metrics. Applying our framework to RLHF reward models, we find that calibration correlates strongly but not perfectly with benchmark accuracy, suggesting it captures a meaningful quality dimension beyond top-1 accuracy. These findings motivate future work on understanding the downstream effects of miscalibration and developing methods to correct it.

</details>


### [154] [Bounded Behavioral Indistinguishability for Black-Box LLM Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30448)
*Munawar Hasan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce bounded behavioral indistinguishability for black-box LLM distillation and show that improving semantic similarity via distillation (LoRA) does not eliminate all behavioral differences; requires adversarial, category-aware evaluation beyond output similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Output-only similarity is insufficient to ensure indistinguishability from the teacher. Distilled models may retain detectable behavioral traits that could reveal the teacher or alter behavior under adversarial probing.

Method: Define (ε,q,t,𝔸)-behavioral indistinguishability over an explicit prompt distribution. Evaluate Qwen and Llama teacher–student pairs with a 5,000-prompt behavioral probe suite. Compare base vs LoRA-distilled students using semantic similarity and adversarial probes (learned discriminators, category analysis, teacher-identification adversary). Extend with cross-family judges, A/B-swap consistency filtering, and query-budget experiments to compare disagreement-guided vs stratified sampling.

Result: LoRA distillation raises semantic similarity (Qwen 0.788→0.862; Llama 0.814→0.874). However, adversarial evaluation shows remaining behavioral differences: discriminators retain nonzero advantage, artifacts concentrated in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts; teacher-identification adversary confirms trend. Cross-family judge and filtering reduce distinguishing advantage (Qwen: 0.158 base→0.081 after LoRA). Disagreement-guided acquisition underperforms or is not consistently better than stratified sampling; coverage/diversity remain strong baselines.

Conclusion: Semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient for black-box distillation. Effective evaluation must be bounded, adversarial, and category-aware to ensure behavioral indistinguishability from the teacher.

Abstract: Black-box LLM distillation is usually evaluated as an output-matching problem: a student is considered successful when its responses are semantically similar to, or task-consistent with, those of a teacher. However, output similarity does not imply that the student is behaviorally indistinguishable from the model it imitates. We introduce bounded behavioral indistinguishability, formalized as $(ε,q,t,\mathbb{A})$-behavioral indistinguishability over an explicit prompt distribution, where $ε$ bounds distinguishing advantage, $q$ bounds oracle queries, $t$ bounds computation, and $\mathbb{A}$ denotes the adversary class.
  We instantiate this notion on Qwen and Llama teacher-student pairs using a controlled $5,000$-prompt behavioral probe suite. For each family, we compare the teacher with both the base student and the LoRA-distilled student, measuring whether distillation reduces distinguishability rather than merely improving similarity. LoRA raises semantic similarity from $0.788$ to $0.862$ for Qwen and from $0.814$ to $0.874$ for Llama. Yet adversarial evaluation reveals remaining behavioral differences: learned discriminators retain nonzero advantage, and pairwise category analysis shows artifacts concentrated in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts. A pairwise teacher-identification adversary confirms this trend. With a different-family Llama judge and A/B-swap consistency filtering, Qwen distinguishing advantage drops from $0.158$ for the base student to $0.081$ after LoRA distillation. Query-budget experiments show that disagreement-guided acquisition does not consistently outperform stratified random sampling, indicating that coverage and diversity remain strong baselines. Our results show that semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient: black-box LLM distillation requires bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation.

</details>


### [155] [VeriGate: Verifier-Gated Step-Level Supervision for GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30451)
*Aakriti Agrawal,Minghui Liu,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VeriGate is a verifier-gated extension of GRPO that adds continuation-aware credit via future-cumulated PRM rewards and group-normalized token-level advantages, achieving substantial accuracy gains and improved training stability over GRPO baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) suffers from sparse supervision: when all sampled trajectories receive the same verifier reward, the group-relative advantage collapses; outcome-only rewards provide no step-level credit assignment, hindering exploration and robust reasoning.

Method: Three design choices: (1) keep the verifier in charge when verifier rewards induce a meaningful preference among trajectories, using process supervision only when rewards are degenerate; (2) convert Process Reward Model (PRM) step scores into future-cumulated rewards to provide continuation-aware credit; (3) transform rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages to restore informative gradients and fine-grained credit assignment while reducing reward hacking risk.

Result: Empirical evaluation on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models across six reasoning benchmarks shows VeriGate improves average accuracy by ~20% (1.5B) and ~12% (7B), reduces zero-gradient failures, lowers reward-hacking behavior, and improves reasoning quality relative to outcome-only GRPO and PRM-as-outcome baselines.

Conclusion: VeriGate effectively mitigates sparse supervision and credit-assignment issues in GRPO, delivering stronger reasoning performance and training stability than existing baselines, and offering a robust approach to verifier-guided reasoning.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is an effective recipe for training reasoning models with verifier-based outcome rewards, but its supervision is sparse: when all sampled trajectories for a prompt receive the same verifier reward, the group-relative advantage collapses to zero and learning stalls. Outcome-only rewards also provide no step-level credit assignment, limiting exploration and making it harder to learn robust reasoning. We present VeriGate (Verifier-Gated Step-Level GRPO), a verifier-gated extension of GRPO that addresses these limitations with three design choices. First, VeriGate keeps the verifier in charge whenever verifier rewards induce a meaningful preference among sampled trajectories, and uses process supervision only when verifier rewards are degenerate. Second, instead of collapsing Process Reward Model (PRM) step scores into a single trajectory reward, VeriGate converts them into future-cumulated rewards to assign continuation-aware credit. Third, VeriGate transforms these rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages, restoring informative gradients and fine-grained credit assignment while remaining less susceptible to reward hacking than methods that optimize aggregated PRM scores. Empirically, training on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models and evaluating on six reasoning benchmarks, VeriGate improves average accuracy by about 20% and 12% for 1.5B and 7B models respectively, substantially reduces zero-gradient failures, decreases reward-hacking behavior, and improves reasoning quality relative to outcome-only GRPO and PRM-as-outcome baselines.

</details>


### [156] [A Unified Framework for Gradient Aggregation in Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30452)
*Zeou Hu,Kelvin Ho,Yaoliang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified gradient-aggregation framework for gradient-based multi-objective optimization (MOO) that establishes convergence guarantees to Pareto stationarity, introduces a sufficient alignment condition and dual-cone projection for feasibility, links existing methods via a primal perspective, and proposes capped MGDA (CVaR-based) for robustness, with empirical validation on synthetic and benchmark problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-objective ML problems require balancing trade-offs; existing gradient-aggregation methods are diverse, often analysed case-by-case, and lack a cohesive theory with convergence guarantees. A unifying framework can provide optimal convergence rates, clarify relationships among algorithms, and support robust/federated settings.

Method: 1) Derive a sufficient alignment condition for gradient directions in MOO. 2) Prove that choosing non-conflicting directions within the convex hull of gradients yields a fundamental convergence condition. 3) Demonstrate feasibility via projection onto the dual cone. 4) Present a primal optimization perspective that encompasses known MGDA-type algorithms, clarifying their relations and enabling new variants. 5) Introduce capped MGDA from a CVaR-based formulation to improve robustness. 6) Validate theory with experiments on synthetic problems and practical benchmarks.

Result: The framework yields optimal rates of convergence to Pareto stationarity. It provides a systematic method to ensure feasibility (dual-cone projection) and explains the theoretical connections among MGDA-like algorithms through a primal lens. The capped MGDA variant demonstrates robustness in adversarial federated learning. Empirical results on synthetic problems and benchmarks support the theoretical guarantees and practical viability.

Conclusion: A comprehensive, unifying approach to gradient aggregation in MOO that delivers convergence guarantees, clarifies relationships among existing methods, and supports robust designs (e.g., CVaR-based capped MGDA) for challenging settings such as adversarial federated learning; validated by experiments.

Abstract: Many machine learning problems involve multiple inherent trade-offs that are best addressed by gradient-based multi-objective optimization (MOO) algorithms. Existing methods are often proposed with various motivations, analyzed case by case, and differ algorithmically in how the component gradients are aggregated at each step. In this work, we develop a unifying framework for gradient aggregation in MOO, establishing (optimal) rates of convergence to Pareto stationarity, the standard measure of performance in MOO. Central to our analysis is a sufficient alignment condition, from which we derive a theorem showing that non-conflicting directions, when chosen within the convex hull of gradients, form a fundamental sufficient condition for convergence. We further show that feasibility can be ensured through projection onto the dual cone, broadening the scope of methods that admit convergence guarantees. In parallel, we present a primal optimization perspective of gradient aggregation that encompasses established algorithms, clarifies their theoretical relationships, and enables the design of new variants. As an illustration, we introduce capped MGDA, derived from a CVaR-based formulation, and demonstrate its robustness in adversarial federated learning. Finally, we validate our theory through experiments on synthetic problems and practical benchmarks.

</details>


### [157] [DisjunctiveNet: Neural Symbolic Learning via Differentiable Convexified Optimization Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30456)
*Shraman Pal,Can Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A differentiable end-to-end framework that enforces hard input-dependent MIL constraints in neural networks via disjunctive constraints and hierarchical convex relaxations to convex hulls, enabling exact rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse datasets in science/engineering limit data-driven methods, while rich domain knowledge manifests as rules (logical propositions, linear inequalities). Existing approaches use soft penalties or non-differentiable post-processing; hard, differentiable handling of mixed-integer rules remains challenging due to nonconvexity.

Method: Represent rules as disjunctive constraints and apply hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations. The resulting tractable linear constraints are embedded as differentiable optimization layers, enabling exact rule satisfaction within the neural network.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world datasets shows perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.

Conclusion: The framework enables exact enforcement of hard, input-dependent MIL constraints in neural nets via differentiable optimization layers, addressing nonconvexity challenges and achieving high performance with guaranteed rule satisfaction.

Abstract: Many learning tasks in science and engineering are characterized by sparse datasets, which limits the effectiveness of purely data-driven approaches. At the same time, these problems are often accompanied by rich domain knowledge derived from physical laws, operational requirements, and expert heuristics. Such knowledge is frequently expressed as rules involving logical propositions and linear inequalities. Existing neuro-symbolic methods typically enforce these rules approximately through soft penalties, assume input-independent rules when designing specialized architectures, or rely on non-differentiable post-processing at inference time to achieve hard constraint satisfaction.
