While recent advances in differentiable optimization layers enable end-to-end feasibility enforcement within neural networks, extending these approaches to logical or mixed-integer rules remains challenging due to inherent nonconvexity. In this work, we propose a unified end-to-end framework for enforcing hard, input-dependent mixed integer linear constraints within neural networks. Our approach represents rules as disjunctive constraints and applies hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations. These relaxations yield tractable linear constraints that can be embedded as differentiable optimization layers while enabling exact rule satisfaction. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on real-world datasets, achieving perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [158] [Scalable Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning via State Augmentation and Consensus for Separable Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30461)
*Santiago Amaya-Corredor,Miguel Calvo-Fullana,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable distributed MARL framework using neighbor consensus on Lagrange multipliers to enforce global constraints, enabling linear-scaling training/execution and feasibility, demonstrated on smart grid demand response.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global resource constraints with separable agent dynamics require coordination; independent learning yields infeasible solutions due to misaligned individual contributions; CTDE-based approaches scale poorly (quadratic in agents). A lightweight, distributed coordination mechanism is needed to ensure global feasibility without sacrificing scalability.

Method: Agents learn a single augmented policy offline, conditioned on local state and a dual variable encoding constraint feedback. During execution, agents reach agreement on the dual variable via neighbor-to-neighbor consensus over a communication graph of agents. The dual variables (Lagrange multipliers) enforce global constraints; under mild connectivity, consensus error is bounded, translating into bounded constraint violation that decreases with graph connectivity and the number of consensus rounds. Training and execution scale linearly with the number of agents, in contrast to CTDE's quadratic scaling.

Result: Empirical results on smart grid demand response show that consensus coordination is essential for feasibility: without consensus agents postpone demand to satisfy capacity, yielding a degenerate non-solution. With consensus, agents converge to a shared dual variable and satisfy both grid constraints and demand fulfillment. The method scales to thousands of agents, whereas CTDE baselines struggle beyond a few dozen.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a scalable, distributed MARL framework that achieves constraint satisfaction through lightweight neighbor consensus on dual variables, preserving the benefits of independent training while ensuring global feasibility. It provides theoretical bounds on consensus error and constraint violation and offers strong empirical performance in large-scale, constrained coordination tasks. Potential limitations include reliance on connectivity and communication to maintain the dual variable consensus and the assumed separable dynamics.

Abstract: We present a distributed approach for constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) that combines state-augmented policy learning with distributed consensus over dual variables. Our method targets systems where agents have separable dynamics but must coordinate to satisfy global resource constraints, a setting in which, as we demonstrate empirically, independent learning fails to produce feasible solutions because agents cannot determine appropriate individual contributions toward collective constraint satisfaction. The key technical contribution is showing that lightweight neighbor-to-neighbor consensus over Lagrange multipliers suffices for globally coordinated constraint enforcement while preserving the scalability of independent training. Each agent learns a single augmented policy offline, conditioned on both its local state and a dual variable encoding constraint feedback. During execution, agents reach agreement on this dual variable through local communication alone. We prove that under mild connectivity assumptions, the consensus error among agents' multipliers is bounded, and show that this translates to a bounded constraint violation that decreases with graph connectivity and the number of consensus rounds. Unlike centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) approaches, whose complexity grows at least quadratically with agent count, our method scales linearly in both training and execution. Experiments on smart grid demand response demonstrate that consensus coordination is \emph{essential for feasibility}: without it, agents satisfy grid capacity constraints only by indefinitely postponing demand, a degenerate non-solution. With consensus, agents converge to a shared dual variable and satisfy both grid constraints and demand fulfillment, scaling to thousands of agents while CTDE baselines are limited to dozens.

</details>


### [159] [idSCD: Identifying Training Datasets through Semantic Correlation Descriptors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30462)
*Andrada Gobeaja,Ionut Hodoroaga,Elena Burceanu,Marius Leordeanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCD-based semantic fingerprinting enables dataset-level membership inference by capturing and comparing the semantic correlation structures learned inside a model. The approach uses white-box descriptors to detect dataset traces, outperforming several black-box baselines and providing high ROC-AUC gains, especially when dataset groups exhibit distinct semantic particularities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether a model’s training data can be identified from the internal semantic relationships it learns, moving beyond behavioral signals to a dataset-level fingerprint that can reveal dataset membership.

Method: Introduce semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs) as a white-box fingerprint of a model’s learned semantic structure. Use leave-one-dataset-out diagnostics to show SCDs capture dataset-specific changes and can perfectly separate matching vs. non-matching dataset pairs. Propose a practical SCD-based membership score that, given only the model’s SCD and a standalone SCD for a target dataset, assesses whether the target dataset was part of the training mixture without requiring train-time re-analysis. Validate across three domains (NLI, emotion classification, medical text classification) under varying semantic separation and keyword support, comparing to RMIA, Attack-P, LiRA, and SIF baselines.

Result: SCDs successfully recover dataset-specific changes in leave-one-dataset-out tests and can distinguish matching from non-matching dataset pairs. The proposed membership score, relying solely on SCDs, achieves the best ROC-AUC and lowest variance across tasks, outperforming black-box baselines and the white-box SIF baseline. The largest relative gain (>60% in ROC-AUC) occurs when dataset groups exhibit distinct semantic particularities, demonstrating the effectiveness and limits of SCD-based membership inference under different semantic conditions.

Conclusion: Dataset membership can be traced through internal semantic correlations captured by SCDs. The white-box approach yields strong performance and robustness relative to multiple baselines, with notable gains under semantically distinct dataset groupings; performance naturally degrades as semantic separation and keyword support diminish.

Abstract: Can a dataset be recognized from the spurious correlations it induces during training? We argue that datasets leave dataset-specific traces in a model's learned semantic correlation structure: incidental regularities that are predictive within a dataset, but not causal for the underlying task, can be internalized during training. We use this insight to study dataset-level membership inference, moving beyond existing methods that rely on behavioral or distributional evidence such as confidence scores, losses, margins, generated samples, or query responses. We introduce a white-box semantic fingerprinting approach based on semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs), which capture the semantic correlation structure learned by a model and make it comparable across dataset mixtures. In a controlled leave-one-dataset-out diagnostic, SCDs recover dataset-specific changes and perfectly separate matching from non-matching dataset pairs. We then propose a practical SCD-based membership score that tests whether a target dataset is part of a model's training mixture using only the model's SCD and the target dataset's standalone SCD, without requiring leave-one-dataset-out models. Across three diverse experimental settings, with dataset groups for natural language inference, emotion classification, and medical text classification, we test both the advantages and limitations of SCD-based membership inference with different degrees of semantic separation and keyword support between dataset splits. On average, the classifier based on this score achieves the highest performance and the lowest std, outperforming black-box baselines RMIA, Attack-P, and LiRA, as well as the white-box SIF baseline. These results show that dataset membership can be traced through internal semantic correlations, with the largest relative gain exceeding 60% in ROC-AUC when dataset groups expose distinct semantic particularities.

</details>


### [160] [Can Subgraph Explanations Be Weaponized to Steal Graph Neural Networks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30470)
*Ojas Nimase,Jiate Li,Yue Zhao,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First black-box graph classification model extraction attack using explanations as guidance; exploits discrete labels and binary masks to locate decision boundaries and narrow search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability interfaces in Graph ML as a Service create potential attack surfaces; understanding vulnerabilities is essential for secure deployment and policy development.

Method: A two-part approach under strict black-box constraints: (1) Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation guided by explanation outputs toward decision boundaries with Hoeffding concentration guarantees; (2) leveraging explanation subgraphs to prune the boundary search space and accelerate execution.

Result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark graph datasets across domains shows superior performance against baselines, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of the attack; provides evidence of exploitable surfaces; code released.

Conclusion: Explainability interfaces in GMLaaS can be exploited for model extraction; findings inform defenses and policy frameworks for explainable AI mandates.

Abstract: Graph Machine Learning as a Service (GMLaaS) platforms increasingly implement explainability interfaces to meet regulatory transparency requirements. However, this transparency creates exploitable vulnerabilities for model extraction attacks. We present the first model extraction attack specifically designed for graph classification under strict black-box constraints where the attacker observes only discrete class labels and binary explanation masks (no probability scores, gradients, or confidence values). Our method (1) uses model explanation outputs to guide Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation toward decision boundaries, with Hoeffding concentration guarantees on estimation accuracy and (2) exploits explanation subgraphs to efficiently narrow the boundary search space. Extensive experiments on benchmark graph datasets across multiple domains demonstrate our method's superiority over comparable baselines. These findings demonstrate that such explainability interfaces create exploitable attack surfaces, informing both defensive mechanisms and policy frameworks for explainable AI mandates. The implementation code is provided in https://github.com/LabRAI/XSTEAL/.

</details>


### [161] [Universal Multiclass Transductive Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30479)
*Steve Hanneke,Hongao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dichotomy in learnability for universal transductive online classification with unbounded label space: only two optimal mistake-rate regimes exist (bounded or increasing logarithmic), characterized by a new Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree and the indifference property; results extend to agnostic and stochastic settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand when online learners can achieve sublinear mistakes when the label space is unbounded and the sequence of unlabeled instances is known in advance; identify the exact rate regimes and a combinatorial characterization.

Method: Introduce the Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree and the indifference property as the core combinatorial framework. Prove necessary and sufficient conditions for learnability in the realizable setting, establishing a dichotomy of optimal rates. Generalize to agnostic and stochastic sequence settings.

Result: Characterization of learnability: only two optimal rates for learnable classes—either bounded or increasing logarithmic. A new LCLL-tree structure with the indifference property captures learnability. Extension of the results to agnostic settings and to the case where only the stochastic process of instance generation is known.

Conclusion: The work provides a tight combinatorial criterion for learnability in universal transductive online classification with unbounded labels, identifying a sharp rate dichotomy and offering extensions to noisier (agnostic) and stochastic-process regimes.

Abstract: We consider the problem of universal transductive online classification with a possibly unbounded label space. This setting considers online learning, with the sequence of instances (without labels) known to the learner in advance. We say a concept class $\mathcal{H}$ is learnable if there is a learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$, such that for every realizable sequence, the number of mistakes made by $\mathcal{A}$ grows at most sublinearly with the number of predictions. We characterize the learnability of this setting and show that there are only two possible optimal rates for the learnable classes: either bounded or increasing logarithmically. We introduce a new combinatorial structure, called ``Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree'', which, along with the indifference property, characterizes the learnability. We also extend the learnability result to the agnostic case and the case where only the stochastic process that generates the instance sequence is known.

</details>


### [162] [Discovering a Zeta Map Algorithm on Dyck Paths via Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30482)
*Xiaoyu Huang,Blake Jackson,Kyu-Hwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A small one-layer, one-head encoder-decoder transformer learns the zeta map on Dyck paths; mechanistic interpretability reveals a level-based processing that yields a peak-centered scaffolding map, which is proved to coincide with the zeta map up to a labeling reversal; this demonstrates AI-assisted mathematical discovery producing a verifiable combinatorial algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demonstrate that ML can aid mathematics by yielding explicit, checkable constructions rather than only predictions, and study how interpretability translates neural computations into combinatorial algorithms.

Method: Train a compact encoder-decoder transformer on the zeta map for Dyck paths; analyze the model with decoder cross-attention, linear probing, and causal interventions; extract a level-based representation and derive a scaffolding map; prove equivalence with the zeta map up to labeling conventions.

Result: Uncovered a level-based mechanism: encoder representations expose path levels linearly; the decoder traverses input positions in a structured, selective way. This leads to the scaffolding map, an explicit peak-centered traversal algorithm that matches the zeta map (modulo a reversal convention in labeling).

Conclusion: Shows a controlled example of AI-assisted mathematical discovery where mechanistic interpretability converts model behavior into a precise, human-verifiable combinatorial algorithm.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly used in mathematical discovery, but in mathematics the desired output is often not a prediction itself, but an explicit construction that can be checked independently. We study this setting through the zeta map on Dyck paths, a classical bijection in the combinatorics of the q,t-Catalan numbers. We train a deliberately small one-layer, one-head encoder-decoder transformer on this map and analyze its learned computation using mechanistic interpretability tools, including decoder cross-attention analysis, linear probing, and causal intervention. The analysis reveals a level-based mechanism: encoder representations make path levels linearly accessible, while the decoder selects and traverses input positions in a structured way. Translating these signals into combinatorics leads to the scaffolding map, an explicit peak-centered traversal algorithm for Dyck paths. We prove that this algorithm agrees with the zeta map, modulo a reversal convention in the labeling. This gives a controlled example of AI-assisted mathematical discovery in which mechanistic interpretability turns model behavior into a precise, human-verifiable combinatorial algorithm.

</details>


### [163] [Graph-Conditioned Mixture of Graph Neural Network Experts for Traffic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30486)
*Amirhossein Ghaffari,Saeid Sheikhi,Ekaterina Gilman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-conditioned mixture of experts (GC-MoE) for node-wise spatio-temporal forecasting on sensor graphs. It uses frozen pretrained ST-GNN experts and a lightweight, input- and graph-aware router to assemble per-node predictions, optionally with a bounded output refinement layer and node-adaptive adapters. Achieves MAE gains over a zero-parameter ensemble baseline on four benchmarks with ~17k trainable parameters on top of 1.5M frozen weights; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In graph-based spatio-temporal forecasting, nodes (e.g., road segments) can exhibit diverse dynamics due to functional class, structure, and traffic patterns. A single backbone shared across all nodes may underutilize rich, region-specific dynamics. A node-wise mixture of specialized experts could leverage heterogeneity while remaining parameter-efficient.

Method: GC-MoE assigns each node a personalized convex combination of frozen forecasting experts, conditioned on graph topology and the recent input window. A graph-conditioned router learns to select/weight experts per node, while only a lightweight routing module is trained. The framework optionally includes a bounded graph-conditioned output refinement layer and uses node-adaptive ST-LoRA adapters as an ablation. Training optimizes ~17k parameters atop ~1.5M frozen weights.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four benchmarks (PEMS04, PEMS07, METR-LA, PEMS-BAY) shows GC-MoE improves MAE relative to a zero-parameter ensemble baseline and yields competitive RMSE and MAPE. The approach achieves this with a small trainable footprint (~17k parameters) on top of a large pool of frozen expert weights (1.5M).

Conclusion: A graph-conditioned mixture-of-experts approach enables per-node specialization in spatio-temporal forecasting with minimal trainable parameters, leveraging frozen architecture knowledge and a learnable routing module. The method demonstrates strong MAE gains on standard benchmarks and offers practical extensions (bounded output refinement, ST-LoRA adapters).

Abstract: Spatio-temporal forecasting on sensor graphs is commonly tackled with a single backbone architecture applied uniformly across all nodes, although graph regions can exhibit different dynamics. Road segments differ in functional class, structure, and traffic behavior, suggesting that node-wise expert specialization can be useful. We propose GC-MoE, a graph-conditioned mixture of experts framework that assigns each node a personalized combination of frozen forecasting experts based on graph topology and the recent traffic input window. GC-MoE combines frozen pretrained spatio-temporal GNN experts with an input-aware, spatially contextualized router while training only a lightweight routing module. We also study a bounded graph-conditioned output refinement layer as an optional extension and include node-adaptive ST-LoRA adapters only as an ablation diagnostic. Across four standard benchmarks (PEMS04, PEMS07, METR-LA, and PEMS-BAY), GC-MoE improves MAE over a zero-parameter ensemble baseline, with competitive RMSE and MAPE, while training only ~17K parameters on top of 1.5M frozen expert weights. The implementation is available at https://github.com/Ahghaffari/gc_moe.

</details>


### [164] [MAAT: Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30514)
*Suryash Yagnik,Shubham Gaur,Saksham Thakur,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha,Amitava Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Balanced evaluation reveals weaknesses in current machine unlearning methods for causal, Why-type questions; introduces 5WBENCH (5,000 samples across Who/What/When/Where/Why) and MAAT (a three-phase LoRA-based unlearning framework) that achieve simultaneous high forgetting and retention on Why-type causal knowledge, setting a new Pareto frontier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for machine unlearning are skewed toward non-causal, Why-type questions; This biases evaluation, hides forgetting failures, and impedes progress in truly unlearning causal facts. A balanced benchmark is needed to quantify forgetting and retention across causal-like reasoning tasks.

Method: Construct 5WBENCH with 1,000 examples per 5W category (5,000 total), with Why-type items focusing on multi-hop causal reasoning and long answer spans; Develop MAAT (Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning) operating on LoRA adapters, using gradient-projected ascent, SVD rank-dimension pruning, task vector negation, and hybrid KL-hidden-state retain repair to achieve targeted forgetting while preserving retained knowledge.

Result: 5WBENCH exposes that no existing baseline can simultaneously maximize forgetting and retention on Why-type questions; MAAT achieves high forgetting and high retention on Why-type causal knowledge, surpassing prior Pareto frontier limits; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: A balanced, Why-focused evaluation is essential for trustworthy unlearning research; MAAT demonstrates a viable path to simultaneously forgetting target causal facts while preserving unrelated knowledge, providing a robust framework and dataset for future investigation.

Abstract: Machine unlearning evaluation is structurally skewed: Why-type questions, which probe causal and relational knowledge, comprise less than 0.06% of CounterFact, 0.6% of ZSRE, and less than 1.3% of TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP-Cyber. This near-zero representation means that methods that fail on causal knowledge can score highly in aggregate, and this failure is undetectable without balanced evaluation. We present 5WBENCH, a balanced 5,000-sample benchmark with 1,000 examples per 5W category (Who, What, When, Where, Why), making causal unlearning failures quantifiable for the first time. Using 5WBENCH, we show that no existing baseline simultaneously achieves high forgetting and high retention on Why-type questions: aggressive forgetting degrades retained knowledge, while conservative methods fail to forget causal facts. Why-type difficulty stems from multi-hop reasoning chains (44% of Why entries vs. less than or equal to 2% for others) and gradient dilution over 40.1-token answer spans. We present MAAT (Multi-phase Adapter-Aware Targeted Unlearning), a three-phase framework operating on LoRA adapter weights, combining gradient-projected ascent, SVD rank-dimension pruning, task vector negation, and hybrid KL-hidden-state retain repair. MAAT is the first method to simultaneously achieve high forgetting and high retention on Why-type causal knowledge, reaching a new operating point on the forget-retain Pareto frontier. We make our code publicly available.

</details>


### [165] [Revisiting Padded Transformer Expressivity: Which Architectural Choices Matter and Which Don't](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30523)
*Anej Svete,William Merrill,Ryan Cotterell,Ashish Sabharwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Padded transformers with polynomial padding are equivalent to basic circuit classes: constant-precision padding aligns with L-uniform AC^0, growing precision with L-uniform TC^0; with log^d N looping, constant precision reaches FO-uniform AC^d, growing precision reaches FO-uniform TC^d. Increasing width or precision beyond logarithmic does not boost expressivity. The results hold for both softmax and average hard attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the robustness of transformer-to-circuit equivalences. Prior work linked transformers to boolean circuits but was sensitive to modeling choices and lacked exact characterizations. This work investigates padding, attention type, width, and uniformity to determine which factors genuinely affect expressivity.

Method: Theoretical, complexity-theoretic analysis establishing formal reductions between padded transformer models and circuit classes. The study considers two dimensions: precision (constant vs growing) and loop depth (log^d N), along with model width and L-uniformity. It analyzes both softmax and average hard attention to verify robustness across attention mechanisms.

Result: Found robust equivalences: (1) polynomially padded L-uniform constant-precision transformers ≡ L-uniform AC^0; (2) growing-precision transformers ≡ L-uniform TC^0; (3) with log^d N loops, constant-precision transformers ≡ FO-uniform AC^d; (4) with log^d N loops, growing-precision transformers ≡ FO-uniform TC^d. Importantly, increasing width or precision beyond logarithmic does not increase expressivity, and results hold for both softmax and average hard attention.

Conclusion: Padded transformer gadgets provide robust, depth- and precision-driven bridges to classical circuit complexity classes under broad conditions. The primary expressivity determinants are precision and depth, not width, and attention type is not a limiting factor. This deepens theoretical understanding of transformers by anchoring them to well-characterized circuit classes.

Abstract: Recent work describes what transformers can and cannot compute through connections to boolean circuits, but existing results lack exact characterizations and are sensitive to modeling choices. Padded transformers -- to whose input filler symbols such as ``...'' are appended -- emerge as a useful gadget for establishing equivalences to circuit classes by providing polynomial space for adaptive parallel computation. However, only a limited set of padded transformer idealizations has been studied, leaving open how robustly these equivalences hold under changes to attention type, model width, and uniformity. We find that, under practical assumptions, padded transformers are surprisingly robust to all of these, and identify numeric precision and model depth as the main factors affecting expressivity. Concretely, we prove that polynomially padded $\text{L-uniform}$ constant-precision transformers are equivalent to $\text{L-uniform AC}^0$, while growing-precision ones achieve $\text{L-uniform TC}^0$ regardless of width. Furthermore, looping enables sequential processing analogous to circuits: $\log^d N$-looped constant-precision transformers reach $\text{FO-uniform AC}^d$, and growing-precision ones reach $\text{FO-uniform TC}^d$. Interestingly, growing width or precision beyond logarithmic does not increase expressivity, and all our results hold for both softmax and average hard attention transformers.

</details>


### [166] [Representation Collapse in Sequential Post-Training of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30524)
*Yichen Liu,Mingyu Chen,Hao Wang,Xiaoran Xu,Chenxi Lin,Rui Zhang,Yutong Zhou,Yuxin Yang,Jiarui Wu,Wei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential post-training stages in LLMs tend to compress representations; a measurement suite reveals links to reduced plasticity, with proposed interventions to preserve future learnability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how multi-stage post-training shapes internal representations and downstream adaptability; address risks of over-concentration affecting plasticity, out-of-domain generalization, and calibration; explore mitigation strategies.

Method: Define measurement suite for hidden states, logits, token trajectories, LoRA updates; analyze various post-training regimes (supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, safety/refusal tuning, math/code specialization, long chain-of-thought tuning) under controlled stage orderings; test lightweight interventions (mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, LoRA decorrelation).

Result: Empirical findings align with the central hypothesis: increasing representation concentration tracks with reduced plasticity during later adaptation, weaker out-of-domain generalization, and poorer calibration; lightweight interventions show promise in preserving future learnability while retaining gains from post-training.

Conclusion: Sequential post-training drives representations toward low-rank, anisotropic, homogeneous spaces, risking degraded future adaptability; targeted interventions (replay, refresh, regularization, decorrelation) can mitigate these effects without sacrificing behavioral improvements.

Abstract: Large language models are now adapted through chains of post-training stages rather than through a single instruction-tuning pass. This paper studies whether such sequential post-training gradually compresses internal representations into low-rank, anisotropic, and homogeneous feature spaces. We define a measurement suite for hidden states, logits, token trajectories, and LoRA updates, and we use it to analyze supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, safety/refusal tuning, math and code specialization, and long chain-of-thought tuning under controlled stage orderings. The central hypothesis is that excessive representation concentration is not merely a geometric curiosity: it predicts reduced plasticity during later adaptation, weaker out-of-domain generalization, and poorer calibration. We further evaluate lightweight interventions, including mixed-domain replay, feature refresh, representation diversity regularization, and LoRA update decorrelation, as ways to preserve future learnability without giving up the behavioral gains of post-training.

</details>


### [167] [Measuring, Localizing, and Ablating Alignment Signatures in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30526)
*Aniket Anand,Janvijay Singh,Zhewei Sun,Dilek Hakkani-Tür,Nick Feamster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Aligned models exhibit AI-like stylistic patterns that shift text away from human-corpus style; a training-free method, PASTA, identifies and ablates the post-training alignment direction to reduce detector-visible AI-like text while preserving coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how post-training alignment shapes stylistic regularities and internal representations, and whether these effects are localized to specific internal directions.

Method: Compare human text, base-model generations, and aligned-model generations under matched prefixes; quantify human-corpus affinity and AI-detection rates; introduce PASTA to estimate an alignment signature from aligned-base residuals and perform decoding-time ablation along that direction across multiple models and detectors.

Result: Aligned generations show lower alignment with human text and higher AI-detection rates than base generations; PASTA reduces detector-detection rates across most aligned models and detectors, with transferability across detectors, outperforming random directions; qualitative analysis shows maintained relevance and coherence with increased stylistic variation.

Conclusion: Post-training alignment’s AI-like style is measurable, localized in a residual direction, and causally testable via ablation; PASTA provides a training-free tool to mitigate detectability while preserving content quality.

Abstract: Aligned language models often exhibit a recognizable AI-like style, yet its connection to post-training and internal representations remains poorly understood. In this work, we study whether post-training introduces or amplifies AI-like stylistic regularities and whether these regularities have a localized internal signature. To this end, we compare human text, base-model generations, and aligned-model generations under matched human-source prefixes. Aligned generations show lower human-corpus affinity and higher AI-detection rates than base generations, suggesting that post-training shifts generated text away from human-corpus style and toward detector-visible AI-like text. We then introduce PASTA (Post-training Alignment Signature Targeted Ablation), a training-free method that estimates a post-training alignment signature from aligned-base residual contrasts and ablates the corresponding direction during decoding. Across 11 aligned models and 6 AI detectors, PASTA lowers the detection rate for most aligned models; this effect transfers well across detectors and is not reproduced by random directions. Qualitative analysis suggests that PASTA generations remain relevant and coherent while exhibiting greater stylistic variation. Together, these results show that AI-like stylistic effects of post-training can be measured, localized, and causally tested through activation ablation.

</details>


### [168] [The Long-Term Effects of Data Selection in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30537)
*Yuxin Yang,Aoxiong Zeng,Xiangquan Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Long-horizon data selection reveals myopic gains; introducing LHAS improves future adaptation and reduces forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess how stage-wise data selectors affect future learning dynamics beyond immediate task performance.

Method: Unified multi-stage protocol; compare random, loss-, gradient-, diversity-, quality-, and utility-diversity selectors; formalize myopic selection; provide LHAS objective; local analysis.

Result: Myopic (short-sighted) selectors can improve current stage but slow future learning and increase forgetting; rank reversals observed; LHAS partially mitigates these effects by adding coverage and future-transfer terms.

Conclusion: Data selection should be evaluated as a long-horizon training intervention shaping the learning trajectory; adopt LHAS or similar objectives to balance immediate utility with future adaptability.

Abstract: Data selection is increasingly used to reduce the cost of large language model (LLM) fine-tuning, with recent methods prioritizing samples by current utility, diversity, quality, or influence. This paper studies a different question: when fine-tuning occurs over multiple stages, can selection strategies that look optimal now make the model less adaptable later? We introduce a long-horizon view of LLM data selection in which a selector is evaluated not only by immediate task performance, but also by future adaptation speed, forgetting, capability imbalance, and out-of-distribution robustness. We compare representative random, loss-based, gradient-based, diversity-based, quality-based, and utility-diversity selection families under a unified multi-stage protocol. Through controlled experiments designed to instantiate this protocol, we show how short-term selectors can exhibit rank reversal: they improve the current stage while slowing subsequent learning and increasing forgetting. We formalize this behavior as \emph{myopic selection}, provide a simple local analysis of why it can occur, and propose a diagnostic Long-Horizon Aware Selection (LHAS) objective that augments immediate utility with coverage, future-proxy transfer, and anti-concentration terms. The study argues that data selection should be evaluated as a training intervention that shapes the model's learning trajectory, rather than only as a local data-efficiency mechanism.

</details>


### [169] [DisasterLex: An Expert Concept-to-Schema Knowledge Graph for Geospatial Reasoning in Disaster Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30538)
*Yiming Xiao,Ankit Basu,Kai Yin,Sahil Vartak,Christian Swords,Ali Mostafavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DisasterLex uses a knowledge-graph-mediated framework to enable robust text-to-SQL over disaster-domain tables, improving routing and multi-table SQL composition via an Expert Knowledge Graph and staged grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disaster response relies on complex, heterogeneous geospatial tables; existing text-to-SQL methods struggle due to domain heterogeneity and need for causal reasoning.

Method: Introduce DisasterLex: an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) with typed causal edges; concept-to-table links; four-stage orchestration (identify query entities, route to domain, plan over causal edges, ground SQL) with schema restriction at each step; evaluation on disaster-analytics DB (36 tables, 150 columns; 107 concepts, 117 edges, 52 links) across 75 queries and seven base models; compare to LightRAG, HippoRAG 2, ReFoRCE, CHESS.

Result: DisasterLex outperforms four baselines by 1.4x–2.75x; absolute scores 1.65–3.56/5; errors mainly in routing and multi-table SQL composition, which the orchestration targets; code/data/EKG released.

Conclusion: A knowledge-graph-mediated orchestration improves accuracy and reliability of text-to-SQL in disaster analytics and can be generalized to heterogeneous, geospatial schemas; open resources support adoption.

Abstract: Disasters are inevitable and increasingly costly, and effective response depends on querying structured tabular data: precise, information-dense records of hazard, exposure, vulnerability, and lifeline infrastructure that underpin disaster management. Current text-to-SQL methods enable natural-language access to such tables but transfer poorly to the disaster domain, where queries span heterogeneous geospatial schemas and require reasoning over causal relations. We introduce DisasterLex, a knowledge-graph-mediated framework that inserts an Expert Knowledge Graph (EKG) of curated concepts and typed causal edges between the user query and the database, bridged to schema by concept-to-table links. The orchestration runs four stages (identifying query entities, routing to the operational domain, planning over causal edges, and grounding the SQL), restricting the schema passed to the model at each step. We instantiate it on a disaster-analytics database (36 geospatial tables, 150 columns) with an EKG of 107 concepts, 117 causal edges, and 52 concept-to-schema links, evaluated on a 75-query test set. On all seven base models spanning proprietary and open-weight families, DisasterLex beats four state-of-the-art baselines (LightRAG, HippoRAG 2, ReFoRCE, CHESS) by 1.4x to 2.75x, with absolute scores of 1.65 to 3.56 (of 5.0). Error analysis shows baseline failures cluster in routing and multi-table SQL composition, the operations our orchestration explicitly addresses. Code, data, and the EKG artifact are available at https://github.com/YimingXiao98/DisasterLex and on Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20388029.

</details>


### [170] [SubsurfaceGen: Procedural Generation of Field-Scale Earth Models and Seismic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30541)
*Joseph Stitt,Pratik Rathore,Madeleine Udell,Ching-Yao Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SubsurfaceGen provides a GPU-accelerated generator for realistic 3D velocity models and seismic data, releasing a large, field-scale ML dataset (4,276 2D velocity slices, 5 s wavefields, 8 s shot gathers from 42 3D models) across six geological settings to enable OOD testing and evaluation of ML-based FWI methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of existing datasets (e.g., Marmousi, SEAM, OpenFWI) in spatial/temporal extent, geological diversity, and physical realism to support machine-learning-based full waveform inversion (FWI) on field-scale problems.

Method: Develop SubsurfaceGen to generate 3D velocity models and corresponding seismic data on GPUs. Assemble a paired dataset of 4,276 2D velocity slices, 5 s wavefields, and 8 s shot gathers drawn from 42 realistic 3D velocity models spanning 10 km × 10 km lateral extent and 6.19 km depth at 10 m resolution, covering six geological settings (four generated with SubsurfaceGen, two from prior sources). Evaluate neural operators for wavefield prediction and encoder–decoder networks for end-to-end velocity inversion, using out-of-distribution testing by holding out one geological setting.

Result: Demonstrate field-scale failure modes for ML-based FWI and illustrate how SubsurfaceGen and the accompanying dataset can improve the training, evaluation, and benchmarking of ML methods for FWI.

Conclusion: SubsurfaceGen and the dataset provide a realistic, large-scale resource that enables rigorous ML-based FWI research, including OOD testing, through more diverse and physically realistic field-scale data.

Abstract: Full waveform inversion (FWI) is the gold standard for subsurface imaging, with applications from carbon sequestration to energy and mineral exploration to earthquake hazard assessment. Machine learning approaches to FWI need field-scale, geologically diverse, and physically realistic training data, but existing resources such as Marmousi, SEAM, and OpenFWI fall short on spatial extent, temporal extent, geological diversity, and physical realism. We address these limitations with SubsurfaceGen, a GPU-accelerated generator for 3D velocity models and seismic data. Along with SubsurfaceGen, we release a paired dataset of 4,276 2D velocity slices, 5 s wavefields, and 8 s shot gathers drawn from 42 realistic, field-scale 3D velocity models, each spanning 10 km x 10 km laterally and 6.19 km deep at 10 m resolution. The dataset spans six geological settings -- four built with SubsurfaceGen and two drawn from prior sources -- relevant for carbon sequestration and hydrocarbon exploration. We use this dataset to evaluate neural operators on wavefield prediction and encoder-decoders on end-to-end velocity inversion, holding out one geological setting for out-of-distribution testing. These experiments surface failure modes at field-scale and demonstrate how SubsurfaceGen and the associated dataset can impact ML-based FWI.

</details>


### [171] [Early Prediction of Future Behavioral Strategy from Process Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30550)
*Robert Kasumba,Dennis Barbour,Chien-Ju Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PLVM enables cross-task inference of person-level strategies from partial process traces, by fusing task-specific traces into a shared latent representation to predict behavior in a held-out task. Demonstrated in PowerWash Simulator with two source cleaning tasks predicting Fire Station level strategy; cross-task fusion helps when source tasks reveal complementary latent dimensions; supports early prediction when target-task data is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People exhibit stable, task-general tendencies that shape solution strategies, but such tendencies are hard to infer from a single task's outcomes or traces. Aggregated outcomes lose process detail, while single-task process models entangle traits with task specifics. The goal is to infer transferable person-level structure from partial traces across multiple tasks to enable early, cross-task prediction.

Method: Propose Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM) that encodes per-task process traces and fuses them into a shared latent representation capturing person-level tendencies. Train on two source tasks to predict strategy in a held-out target task. Validate with (a) controlled simulations using known latent types to assess when cross-task fusion provides advantages, and (b) naturalistic PowerWash Simulator data where partial traces from two cleaning tasks predict whether players will persist with Zone Planner vs switch to Zone Hopper in Fire Station level.

Result: Cross-task fusion improves prediction of locally persistent vs. frequent-switch strategies in the held-out task when target-task data is limited. Simulations show fusion helps when source tasks reveal complementary latent dimensions of the same underlying process. Partial source-task traces can thus support early target-task strategy prediction.

Conclusion: Process-level cross-task modeling is a promising approach for early prediction of target-task strategies under limited observation. It leverages complementary information across tasks to uncover stable person-level structure, with potential for broader applicability in adaptive systems and human-AI collaboration.

Abstract: Adaptive systems often need to make task-specific decisions about people from limited evidence: a tutor may need to anticipate how a learner will approach a new problem, a game may need to adapt when a player enters a new level, and a human-AI system may need to infer whether a partner will persist with a plan or switch goals. These decisions depend on person-level tendencies that shape how people solve related tasks, but such tendencies are difficult to infer from standard behavioral evidence. One approach is to use aggregate outcome summaries, such as scores, completion rates, or productivity; these summaries are compact and available across tasks, but can collapse distinct behavioral processes into similar outcomes. Another approach is to use process-level traces, which record how behavior unfolds; however, process modeling within one task can entangle stable person-level tendencies with task-specific layout and affordances. In this work, we study early cross-task behavioral inference: whether partial source-task process traces can reveal transferable person-level structure that predicts strategy in a held-out target task. We introduce a Process-Level Latent Variable Model (PLVM), which encodes task-specific traces and fuses them into a shared person-level latent representation for cross-task prediction. In PowerWash Simulator, a naturalistic telemetry dataset of human gameplay, PLVM uses partial traces from two cleaning tasks to predict locally persistent Zone Planner behavior versus frequent Zone Hopper behavior in the held-out Fire Station level. Controlled simulations with known latent types show that cross-task fusion helps when source tasks reveal complementary dimensions of a shared latent process. These results suggest that process-level cross-task modeling can support early prediction of target-task strategy when observing sufficient target-task behavior is impractical.

</details>


### [172] [Destruction is a General Strategy to Learn Generation; Diffusion's Strength is to Take it Seriously; Exploration is the Future](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30553)
*Pierre-André Noël*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion models are framed as a flexible destroy-then-generate withholding framework, enabling information withholding beyond hand-crafted methods; the abstract explores porting RL ideas to diffusion, raises diffusion-native exploration questions, and introduces a tutorial plus a novel probabilistic graphical model, offering directions rather than definitive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To position diffusion models within a broader information-withholding paradigm, argue for advantages in data-scarce settings, and seed diffusion-native approaches to RL-inspired exploration.

Method: A conceptual, tutorial-driven treatment: adopt a destroy-then-generate perspective; discuss cross-domain transfer of RL techniques to diffusion contexts; introduce a novel probabilistic graphical model to support exposition.

Result: Primarily conceptual and directional contributions: a theoretical framing, an outline for a tutorial, and the introduction of a new probabilistic graphical model, with no definitive empirical results.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based withholding is a flexible paradigm potentially advantageous in data-scarce regimes; addressing exploration in diffusion contexts via diffusion-native methods is a promising direction, warranting further research.

Abstract: I present diffusion models as part of a family of machine learning techniques that withhold information from a model's input and train it to guess the withheld information. I argue that diffusion's destroying approach to withholding is more flexible than typical hand-crafted information withholding techniques, providing a rich training playground that could be advantageous in some settings, notably data-scarce ones. I then address subtle issues that may arise when porting reinforcement learning techniques to the diffusion context, and wonder how such exploration problems could be addressed in more diffusion-native ways. I do not have definitive answers, but I do point my fingers in directions I deem interesting. A tutorial follows this thesis, expanding on the destroy-then-generate perspective. A novel kind of probabilistic graphical models is introduced to facilitate the tutorial's exposition.

</details>


### [173] [Supervised Training Rapidly Degrades Early Visual Cortex Alignment Across Biologically Plausible Learning Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30556)
*Nils Leutenegger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Untrained networks already align with early visual cortex; training alters V1 alignment differently by learning rule, with BP harming V1 alignment more than PC/STDP, and modestly increasing LOC alignment; suggests inductive biases drive low-level statistics and global error signals reshape early representations more than local rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why randomly initialized architectures can match brain representations and how different learning rules affect alignment to human fMRI across the visual hierarchy.

Method: RSA-based evaluation of model-brain similarity across training for four learning rules (BP, FA, PC, STDP) using 720 THINGS images and fMRI data from three subjects over six ROIs, at eight training checkpoints (epochs 0-40).

Result: (1) One epoch of training substantially reduces V1 alignment (25-90%, varies by rule). (2) BP causes the largest drop in V1 alignment (delta r = -0.080); PC and STDP preserve more (delta r ~ -0.04). (3) In LOC, BP shows the largest increase in alignment during training, though the absolute change is small.

Conclusion: Untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics via inductive biases; global error signals (BP) reshape early representations more aggressively than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better preserve brain-like structure.

Abstract: Random, untrained neural networks consistently match or exceed trained networks in representational similarity to early visual cortex. This puzzling finding challenges the assumption that learning improves brain alignment. We investigate it by tracking representational similarity analysis (RSA) alignment to human fMRI data across training for four learning rules: backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP). Using 720 object images from the THINGS database and fMRI data from three subjects across six visual ROIs, we measure Spearman correlations between model and brain representational dissimilarity matrices at eight training checkpoints (epochs 0-40). We find that (1) a single epoch of training reduces V1 alignment by 25-90%, depending on the learning rule; (2) backpropagation reduces V1 alignment most severely (delta r = -0.080), while predictive coding and STDP preserve substantially more (delta r ~ -0.04); and (3) a weaker, opposite tendency appears in object-selective cortex (LOC), where BP shows the largest increase in alignment during training, although the absolute change is small. These results suggest that untrained architectures capture low-level visual statistics through inductive biases alone, and that global error signals (BP) reshape early representations more aggressively than local learning rules (PC, STDP), which better preserve brain-like structure.

</details>


### [174] [Zeroth-Order Non-Log-Concave Sampling with Variance Reduction and Applications to Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30573)
*M. Berk Sahin,Behzad Sharif,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin sampler for high-dimensional, non-log-concave targets in black-box settings, with non-asymptotic guarantees (via ε-relative Fisher information and Poincaré inequality). Proposes ZO-APMC for posterior sampling with pre-trained score priors; supported by theory and experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of sampling from high-dimensional non-log-concave distributions when gradients are unavailable or costly, where standard Langevin dynamics has high variance and lacks non-asymptotic guarantees in black-box settings.

Method: Develops a variance-reduced zeroth-order gradient estimator for Langevin dynamics to reduce variance compared to classical batched zeroth-order estimators and to remove unfavorable dimensional scaling of batch size. Establishes non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling in terms of ε-relative Fisher information, and, under a Poincaré inequality, squared total variation distance. Introduces ZO-APMC, a posterior sampling algorithm for black-box inverse problems with pre-trained score-based generative priors, with non-asymptotic guarantees. Includes synthetic experiments and empirical validation on linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

Result: The paper provides the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling (via ε-relative Fisher information) and for squared TV under Poincaré. Demonstrates strong empirical performance on synthetic tasks and practical linear and nonlinear inverse problems when using the proposed variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin scheme and ZO-APMC.

Conclusion: Advances zeroth-order sampling by delivering non-asymptotic guarantees for non-log-concave distributions in black-box settings and enabling posterior sampling with score priors. The results bridge theory and practice, enabling stable, scalable sampling for complex inverse problems with inaccessible gradients.

Abstract: Sampling from high-dimensional, non-log-concave distributions with unnormalized densities remains a fundamental challenge in machine learning, particularly in black-box settings where gradient information is inaccessible or computationally prohibitive. While Langevin dynamics provides a principled framework for sampling when gradients are accessible, its extension to the black-box settings suffers from high variance and lacks non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for non-log-concave sampling. To address these limitations, we propose a variance-reduced zeroth-order Langevin sampling method. Our method employs a gradient estimator that substantially reduces the variance of the classical batched zeroth-order estimator and eliminates the unfavorable dimensional dependence of the batch size required for accurate estimation, enabling practical and stable sampling. We establish the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for zeroth-order non-log-concave sampling in terms of $\varepsilon$-relative Fisher information, and, under a Poincaré inequality assumption, squared total variation distance. We further propose ZO-APMC, a posterior sampling algorithm for black-box inverse problems with pre-trained score-based generative priors, establishing the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for such methods. We validate our theory through synthetic experiments and demonstrate strong empirical performance on practical linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

</details>


### [175] [Benchmarking Machine Learning Uncertainty Quantification Methodologies for Predicting Turbine Gas Temperature Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30585)
*Jostein Barry-Straume,Changmin Son,Adrian Sandu,Gavan Burke,Rekha Sundararajan,Andrew Rimell,James G. Steinrock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Comparative study of five neural-network prediction-interval methods for turbine gas temperature, assessing trade-offs in coverage, width, and stability within a unified cross-validated framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification in prognostics for engine health management to ensure safety and reliability.

Method: Implement Delta method, Bayesian Monte Carlo Dropout, Bootstrap, Lower-Upper Bound Estimation, and Mean-Variance Estimation within a unified framework. Use cross-validation for hyperparameter selection, repeated train-test splits for robustness, and multiple metrics (Coverage Probability, Normalized Interval Width, and Coverage Width-based Criterion) to evaluate accuracy and sharpness.

Result: Distinct trade-offs observed: methods differ in interval coverage, width, and stability. No single method dominates across all metrics; results guide practical selection and tuning of interval methods for engine health management.

Conclusion: Provides practical guidance for selecting and tuning prediction-interval methods to achieve interpretable and precise prognostics in real-world engine applications.

Abstract: Effective prognostics and health management of modern engines relies on accurate turbine gas temperature predictions and robust uncertainty quantification to ensure reliability and safety. This paper investigates five major approaches for constructing prediction intervals -- namely the Delta method, Bayesian Monte Carlo Dropout, Bootstrap method, Lower-Upper Bound Estimation, and Mean-Variance Estimation -- as a means of capturing the uncertainty in neural network predictions of turbine gas temperature. Each approach is implemented within a unified experimental framework that employs cross-validation for hyperparameter selection, repeated train-test splits for performance robustness, and multiple metrics to evaluate both the accuracy and tightness of the intervals. In particular, Coverage Probability, Normalized Mean Prediction Interval Width, and the Coverage Width-based Criterion are measured to comprehensively assess each method's reliability and sharpness. Experiments conducted on a representative turbine gas temperature dataset reveal distinct trade-offs among the five methods in terms of interval coverage, width, and stability. These findings provide a practical guide for selecting and tuning prediction interval methods in engine health management and prognostics, ensuring both interpretability and precision in real-world applications.

</details>


### [176] [Counterfactual Evaluation Reveals Hidden Capability Profiles in Clinical LLMs and Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30590)
*Matt Turk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Causal Sensitivity Score (CSS), an interventional metric to test whether clinical AI models adjust recommendations to counterfactual input changes. CSS diverges from a coverage-based CMS across frontier models, exposing safety blind spots and responsiveness gaps not captured by CMS; tool use can improve CSS for most models but some remain non-responsive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coverage-based evaluation (CMS) may miss whether models actually respond to clinically meaningful input perturbations. An interventional, pre-registered metric is needed to quantify model responsiveness and safety under counterfactual scenarios.

Method: Define five perturbation dimensions (biomarker flips, prior-treatment failures, biomarker removals, surgery-status changes, stage perturbations). Apply pre-registered mutations to 224 oncology cases. Score whether each model updates its recommendations in the pre-registered correct direction on a 0/0.5/1.0 scale. Benchmark with CMS across six frontier models from three labs in single-shot inference. Extend analysis to ReAct-style tool use, cross-judge replication, and three-rater medical validation.

Result: CSS and CMS yield nearly opposite model rankings; the CMS-worst model becomes CSS-best, and an upper-mid CMS model ranks last on CSS. All frontier models fail on surgery-status perturbations (up to 17.2% CSS on Family D), a blind spot CMS does not reveal. Tool use increases CSS for five of six models (+2.5 to +20.3 percentage points), but the lowest-CSS model may still fail to update. Cross-judge replication and three-rater validation support the findings. CSS offers a robust, complementary signal to CMS and suggests a potential dense reward for agentic RL.

Conclusion: Interventional, pre-registered metrics like CSS reveal model responsiveness and safety blind spots not captured by coverage-based metrics. CSS can supplement clinical AI evaluation and provide guidance for developing more responsive, potentially agentic systems.

Abstract: Two clinical AI systems can score nearly identically on coverage-based rubrics yet behave radically differently when their patient inputs change: one updates its recommendations to match the new clinical signal, while the other produces the same output regardless. We introduce the Causal Sensitivity Score (CSS), a pre-registered interventional metric that mutates oncology tumor-board cases along five clinically meaningful dimensions - biomarker flips, prior-treatment failures, biomarker removals, surgery-status changes, and stage perturbations - and scores whether each model updates its recommendations in the pre-registered correct direction using a {0, 0.5, 1.0} scale. Benchmarked against the Consensus Match Score (CMS), a coverage-based weighted recall metric, six frontier models from three labs evaluated in single-shot inference across 224 cases rank in nearly opposite orders: all six models change rank, the CMS-worst model becomes CSS-best, and one upper-mid CMS model ranks last on CSS. We further surface a universal safety blind spot: every frontier model fails on surgery-status interventions (at most 17.2% CSS on Family D), a finding CMS does not expose. The metric also transfers to tool-using agents: in a ReAct-style experiment, tool use improves CSS for five of six models (+2.5 to +20.3 percentage points), yet the lowest-CSS model retrieves the same chart sections and still fails to update its recommendations - revealing a structural responsiveness deficit visible only under counterfactual evaluation. Cross-judge replication and three-rater medical-professional validation confirm the aggregate findings. Interventional pre-registered metrics like CSS complement coverage-based evaluation for clinical AI agents: they capture responsiveness that coverage metrics miss and offer a candidate dense reward signal for future agentic RL systems.

</details>


### [177] [When LLM Reward Design Fails: Diagnostic-Driven Refinement for Sparse Structured RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28918)
*Youting Wang,Yuan Tang,Bowen Liu,Xuan Liu,Dingyan Shang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diagnostic-driven, iterative refinement framework improves LLM-assisted reward shaping for sparse, structured RL, using a failure-mode taxonomy and training diagnostics to guide targeted reward-function revisions. Demonstrates large gains on MiniGrid tasks with PPO; key failure modes identified; taxonomy prompts and dynamic labels are central; limitations include calibration, brain to dense rewards, and generalizability to interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fragility of LLM-generated reward shaping in sparse, structured RL by diagnosing failure modes and iteratively refining reward functions guided by diagnostics and a taxonomy.

Method: PPO agents trained on MiniGrid (DoorKey-8x8, KeyCorridor) and MuJoCo as a boundary stress test. An audit identifies dominant one-shot failure modes (reward flooding, semantic/API misunderstanding) and a rarer weak-shaping case. Proposes diagnostic-driven iterative refinement leveraging training diagnostics and a failure-mode taxonomy to guide targeted reward-function revisions. Extensive controls (metrics-only prompts, static vocabulary), budget-matched comparisons, component-removal tests, sensitivity analyses, and four crossed-variance designs. Boundary analyses examine success-based diagnostics vs return-trend feedback. Low-call protocol vs population-based reward search. Field considerations note sparsity and interface reliability; event_text considered as a potential factor.

Result: Refinement raised DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6% success and KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% with high seed variance. Controls show gains are not due to retrial or extra training; taxonomy prompts are a major mechanism; dynamic labels offer partial gains. In four environments, point estimates suggest larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates, but bootstrap intervals are wide. Boundaries indicate misfires in dense-reward locomotion; calibration limits revealed. The approach is bounded to sparse structured tasks with reliable interfaces under PPO; events like event_text may help/hurt. The low-call protocol contrasts with population-based search rather than a benchmark comparison.

Conclusion: Diagnostic-driven iterative refinement using a failure-mode taxonomy and training diagnostics is effective for robust LLM-guided reward shaping in sparse, structured RL. It yields substantial performance gains, but calibration, boundary effects, and task-domain limitations must be acknowledged; the method is not universally applicable beyond sparse, structured interfaces.

Abstract: For sparse, structured reinforcement-learning tasks with semantic reward-function interfaces, LLM-generated reward shaping is better framed as debugging than one-shot generation. We study PPO-trained agents using MiniGrid as core evaluation and MuJoCo as boundary stress test. Our audit finds two dominant one-shot failure modes -- reward flooding and semantic/API misunderstanding -- plus a rarer weak-shaping case. We propose diagnostic-driven iterative refinement, where training diagnostics and a failure-mode taxonomy guide targeted reward-function revision. Refinement improves DoorKey-8x8 from 2.3% to 97.6% and KeyCorridor from 31.2% to 86.7% with high seed-to-seed variance. Controls show these gains are not from retrying or extra training: metrics-only re-prompting yields large drops, while a static-vocabulary control recovers much of the gap (87.6%; 70.7%), showing the taxonomy prompt is a major mechanism and dynamic labels provide only partially isolated incremental evidence. Budget-matched and Best-of-3 comparisons separate refinement from selection and training-time effects. Component-removal tests, sensitivity analyses, and an audit against author labels provide converging evidence for the debugging interpretation while revealing calibration limits. Continuous-control results show the boundary: success-based diagnostics can misfire in dense-reward locomotion, and return-trend feedback removes one false-positive mechanism without robust gains. The low-call protocol is a cost contrast with population-based reward search, not a benchmark comparison. In four crossed-variance-design environments, point estimates suggest larger gains when LLM reward-function variance dominates but bootstrap intervals are wide. The method is bounded to sparse structured tasks with reliable interfaces under PPO; fields like event_text may help, hurt, or be neutral.

</details>


### [178] [Learning Transferable Predictability Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30592)
*Diyali Goswami,Auroop R. Ganguly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce Gauge-Fixed Ordinal Network (GON) to assign an interpretable, cross-system ordinal score of trajectory predictability, fixing gauge freedom via an anchor-and-variance objective, and demonstrate robust transfer across five dynamical systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a consistent numerical interpretation of predictability across diverse dynamical systems, addressing gauge ambiguity that arises when using ordinal supervision alone, and enabling cross-system transfer, model selection, and early-warning diagnostics.

Method: A temporal convolutional model (GON) operating on 2-jet trajectory features. Trained with an anchor-and-variance objective to align level-wise scores with shared coordinates across five predictability levels. Uses 2-jet features to capture local geometry; evaluated on five held-out dynamical systems with pretrained initialization vs. scratch training across varying window budgets.

Result: GON consistently outperforms scratch training across all window budgets on five held-out systems. Adaptation depth tracks geometric proximity to the training family. Zero-shot scores preserve ordinal structure near the stochastic boundary; pretrained initialization beats scratch across budgets. Cross-system transfer requires a stable score coordinate; pairwise discrimination and global order are distinct properties.

Conclusion: Gauge-fixed scoring enables consistent cross-system predictability assessment, model selection, and early-warning diagnostics by removing the gauge freedom inherent in ordinal scoring and providing a stable, interpretable score across diverse dynamical systems.

Abstract: We study the problem of assigning a scalar score to a short trajectory window that reflects its position on an ordered continuum of predictability regimes, spanning structured deterministic dynamics to unstructured stochastic noise. Existing methods address deterministic-versus-stochastic discrimination within a single system and do not produce scores with a consistent numerical interpretation across systems. We formalize this as ordinal estimation over a five-level predictability ladder and identify a structural source of cross-system ambiguity: ranking supervision alone leaves the score coordinate unfixed up to a monotone reparameterization, which we term the gauge freedom of ordinal scoring. We propose the Gauge-Fixed Ordinal Network (GON), a temporal convolutional model trained with an anchor-and-variance objective that pins level-wise score means to shared target coordinates. GON operates on 2-jet features that expose local trajectory geometry, preserved by smooth flows and disrupted by stochastic surrogate procedures. On five held-out dynamical systems, initializing from a pretrained GON checkpoint consistently outperforms training from scratch across all window budgets, with adaptation depth reflecting geometric proximity to the training family. Zero-shot scores retain ordinal structure at the stochastic boundary, where surrogate procedures most strongly disrupt nonlinear geometry, and pretrained initialization consistently beats scratch across all window budgets. Pairwise discrimination and globally coherent ordinal scoring are distinct properties requiring a stable score coordinate for cross-system transfer, with direct implications for predictability assessment, model selection, and early-warning diagnostics across natural and engineered dynamical systems.

</details>


### [179] [Unicorn: Scaling High-Dimensional Time Series Forecasting via Universal Correlation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30376)
*Haochen Yuan,Yichen Song,Yunbo Wang,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Unicorn, a latent prototype codebook approach for scalable, multi-dataset pretraining in high-dimensional time series that decouples correlation modeling from channel identities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the dichotomy between channel-independent models (scalable but ignore inter-channel dependencies) and channel-dependent models (expressive but dimension-bounded and hard to generalize across heterogeneous datasets).

Method: Introduce a latent prototype codebook that maps heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, enabling identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns that transfer across datasets with different dimensionalities; supports scalable, multi-dataset pretraining.

Result: Empirical results show Unicorn significantly surpasses state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, with pronounced gains in few-shot transfer scenarios.

Conclusion: Unicorn enables scalable multivariate time series foundation models by learning reusable, cross-domain interaction patterns that generalize across diverse channel configurations.

Abstract: Modern time series architectures face a fundamental trade-off: channel-independent models scale well with increasing data volume but ignore critical inter-channel dependencies, while channel-dependent models are expressive but remain ``dimension-bounded'', struggling to generalize across heterogeneous datasets.To bridge this gap, we introduce Unicorn (Universal Correlation Network), a framework for scalable, multi-dataset pretraining on high-dimensional time series. At the core of Unicorn is a latent prototype codebook that decouples correlation modeling from specific channel identities. By projecting heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, UniCorN learns identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns that transfer across domains with diverse dimensionalities and semantics. Extensive experiments show that Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, particularly in few-shot transfer scenarios, offering a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models.

</details>


### [180] [Scientific Machine Learning for Engine Health Management and Remaining Useful Life Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30593)
*Jostein Barry-Straume,Changmin Son,Adrian Sandu,Gavan Burke,Rekha Sundararajan,Andrew Rimell,James G. Steinrock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-task, uncertainty-aware turbine prognostics framework that jointly forecasts untrimmed turbine gas temperature (TGTU), Delta TGT (DTGT), and remaining useful life (RUL) with interval predictions, using a shared encoder and task-specific heads (including mean-variance regression and optional survival head); tunable for deployment and evaluated with point and interval metrics across flight phases and maintenance segments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world fleet data for turbine health are heterogeneous and non-stationary. Point predictions alone are insufficient for risk-aware maintenance decisions; practitioners need calibrated prediction intervals for RUL and thermal indicators.

Method: A multi-task architecture with a shared sequence encoder (convolutional front-end, residual bidirectional LSTM, and attention pooling) feeding task-specific heads. Heads include mean-variance estimation for probabilistic regression and an optional survival head for threshold-based event modeling. The framework offers practitioner-facing tunables (e.g., DTGT thresholding rules, RUL target construction) to align with internal policies. Evaluation uses both point and interval metrics (MAE, PICP, MPIW, CWC) and reports results aggregated and stratified by flight phase and maintenance segment.

Result: Prediction performance is assessed using both point and interval metrics; empirical coverage of prediction intervals is evaluated. Results are reported in aggregate and stratified by operational context (flight phase and maintenance segment) to illuminate context effects and support uncertainty-aware monitoring.

Conclusion: The framework enables uncertainty-aware turbine prognostics with calibrated interval predictions and practical tunability, supporting deployment in policy-aligned EHM workflows and uncertainty-aware maintenance decisions.

Abstract: Engine Health Management (EHM) depends on reliable forecasting of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) and on tracking thermal indicators such as turbine gas temperature (TGT). In practice, real-world fleet data are heterogeneous and non-stationary, and point predictions alone are insufficient for risk-aware maintenance decisions. This paper presents a multi-task scientific machine learning framework for turbine prognostics that jointly predicts turbine gas temperature untrimmed (TGTU), Delta Turbine Gas Temperature (DTGT), and RUL, with quantified uncertainty in the form of prediction intervals whose empirical coverage is evaluated. A shared sequence encoder (convolutional front-end with residual bidirectional LSTM layers and attention pooling) feeds task-specific heads, including mean--variance estimation for probabilistic regression and, optionally, a survival head for threshold-based event modeling. The framework is designed to be tunable via a small set of practitioner-facing parameters (e.g., DTGT thresholding rules and RUL target construction) so that deployment can align with in-house policies and proprietary criteria. The predictive performance of the proposed framework is evaluated using both point and interval metrics, including mean absolute error (MAE), prediction interval coverage probability (PICP), mean prediction interval width (MPIW), and the coverage--width criterion (CWC). Results are reported both in aggregate and stratified by flight phase and maintenance segment to highlight operational-context effects and to support uncertainty-aware monitoring.

</details>


### [181] [When LLMs Learn to Be Consistently Wrong: A Multi-Model Study of Linear Representations of Synthetic Deception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30381)
*Vahideh Zolfaghari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Synthetic dishonesty can be reliably detected in early transformer representations via simple linear probes after modest supervised fine-tuning; the direction of dishonesty consolidates in deeper layers, with model-dependent regimes of representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the representational basis and detectability of deceptive alignment by isolating synthetic dishonesty in a controlled setting and assessing generalization across architectures and distributions.

Method: Create honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) and fine-tune with LoRA on the same question distribution. Train linear and MLP probes on mean-pooled hidden states to detect dishonesty. Evaluate probe performance via AUC; compare logistic regression vs MLP; test generalization to TruthfulQA and MMLU; perform mechanistic analyses (Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, calibration (ECE)).

Result: Probes (especially linear) detect synthetic dishonesty with very high AUC (≥0.99) within early layers (1-3) for four models; Pythia lags (0.705 peak). Logistic regression often matches/exceeds MLP. Probes generalize to held-out MMLU with near-zero delta AUC.
