Gemma-2 models show strong noise robustness and high-dimensional representations, while Pythia/Llama/Qwen show representational collapse. The dishonesty direction strengthens in deeper layers; calibration improves (ECE <0.01 in most layers). Mechanistic analyses indicate two regimes across architectures and suggest potential for activation-based monitoring to detect deception.

Conclusion: Robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with distinct representational regimes per architecture. Linear probes suffice for detection, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis and informing monitoring strategies for AI safety.

Abstract: Deceptive alignment, in which models maintain accurate internal representations while deliberately producing false outputs, remains a central challenge in AI safety. While strategic deception is the primary long-term concern, synthetic dishonesty - induced via direct optimization on incorrect answers - provides a controlled testbed for studying the representational basis of learned deception. We introduce a multi-model paradigm in which honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) are fine-tuned using LoRA on the same question distribution. Linear probes trained on mean-pooled hidden states detect synthetic dishonesty with near-perfect AUC (greater than or equal to 0.99) as early as layers 1-3 in four architectures, while Pythia-1.4B reaches a peak of 0.705. Logistic regression probes consistently match or outperform MLP probes, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalize with near-zero loss (Delta AUC approx. 0) to held-out MMLU subjects. Late-layer representations show strong robustness to Gaussian noise, with Gemma-2 models exhibiting exceptional stability. Mechanistic analysis of Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, and calibration (ECE) reveals two regimes: representational collapse in Pythia/Llama/Qwen versus high-dimensional preservation in Gemma-2. Across all models, the dishonesty direction consolidates progressively in deeper layers, with optimal calibration (ECE less than 0.01 except Pythia) achievable in layers 1-4. These results demonstrate that robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with implications for activation-based monitoring.

</details>


### [182] [Improving Relative Representations with Learned Anchors and Whitened Inner Products](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30596)
*Oscar Thorsted Svendsen,Nikolaj Holst Jakobsen,Fabian Mager,Hiba Nassar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Robust, geometry-aware cross-model communication using learned semantic anchors and affine-invariant similarity to enable near-lossless transfer across heterogeneous architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Independently trained models converge to incompatible latent representations; existing Relative Representations with random anchors and cosine similarity fail to capture anisotropic geometry of modern architectures (e.g., Transformers).

Method: Learn anchors as robust semantic prototypes; employ a geometry-aware similarity that preserves magnitude and is affine-invariant; apply across vision and language tasks to enable cross-model communication.

Result: Significant performance and consistency gains; near lossless information transfer; stable zero-shot communication even between heterogeneous architectures, including small language models across scales.

Conclusion: The proposed RR framework with robust anchors and geometry-aware similarity improves cross-model compatibility, enabling modular AI and efficient information exchange across modalities and architectures.

Abstract: Independently trained neural models typically converge to incompatible latent representations, creating a fundamental barrier to highly modular AI systems. While Relative Representations (RR) address this by mapping absolute coordinates to a shared space defined by similarities to common anchor points, traditional implementations rely on randomly sampled anchors and cosine similarity, which frequently fail to capture the anisotropic geometries of modern architectures like Transformers. In this work, we propose a robust framework for cross-model communication based on two improvements. We learn anchors as robust semantic prototypes and utilize a geometry-aware similarity metric which preserves discriminative magnitude information and is invariant to affine shifts. Our approach demonstrates significant gains in performance and consistency across vision and language tasks. Notably, it enables nearly lossless information transfer and stable zero-shot communication even between highly heterogeneous architectures, such as small language models of varying scales.

</details>


### [183] [ScaleMAP: Preserving Local Density and Neighborhood Structure in Low-Dimensional Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30597)
*Rajas Poorna,Marcus T. Cicerone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ScaleMAP uses a scale-aware normalization by dividing pairwise embedding displacements by the geometric mean of endpoints' original-space local radii, reintroducing scale as a change of variables to preserve density without sacrificing neighborhood preservation; generalizes to PaCMAP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UMAP/PaCMAP-style locally adaptive normalization erases neighborhood scale and can suppress sparse structures and density features; a way to preserve density-scale information without compromising neighborhood structure is desirable.

Method: For each pair of points, scale the embedding displacement by the geometric mean of their original-space local radii, implementing scale reintroduction as a change of variables rather than a separate penalty; evaluated across standard benchmarks and scientific datasets (transcriptomics, hyperspectral imaging, flow cytometry); extends to PaCMAP.

Result: ScaleMAP matches DensMAP on density preservation while maintaining UMAP-level neighborhood preservation; recovers sparse bridges in transcriptomics that UMAP collapses; preserves density across 17 orders of magnitude in flow cytometry; demonstrates improved density preservation when applied to PaCMAP.

Conclusion: ScaleMAP offers a general, scale-aware normalization that preserves both local neighborhood structure and density information, with demonstrated gains across multiple data modalities and apparent generalizability beyond UMAP to PaCMAP.

Abstract: Nonlinear dimensionality-reduction methods such as UMAP and PaCMAP adaptively normalize local distances during graph construction, erasing neighborhood scale from the data. This distorts more than relative cluster sizes: sparse structures like bridges between transitioning cell types and narrow spectral spikes in hyperspectral images can be suppressed or lost entirely. DensMAP adds a density penalty to correct this, but this penalty competes with UMAP's attraction-repulsion forces, scattering points far from their neighborhoods. ScaleMAP takes a different approach: each pairwise embedding displacement is divided by the geometric mean of the two endpoints' original-space local radii, re-injecting scale information as a change of variables rather than as a competing objective. Across standard benchmarks and scientific datasets from transcriptomics, hyperspectral imaging, and flow cytometry, ScaleMAP matches DensMAP on density preservation while maintaining UMAP-level neighborhood preservation. In transcriptomic data, it recovers sparse bridges between cell populations that UMAP collapses; in flow cytometry, it faithfully represents density structure across 17 orders of magnitude. The same principle applied to PaCMAP yields consistently improved density preservation, suggesting the approach generalizes beyond UMAP.

</details>


### [184] [LLMs Without Deep Neural Networks: New Architecture, Benefits and Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30385)
*Vincent Granville*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes validating a DNN-free, RBF-like model for LLMs that achieves global loss minimization in a single closed-form iteration, with case studies and benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RBF networks are seen as explainable substitutes for DNNs with potential accuracy gains. The author independently derives a similar machinery and seeks validation of a one-shot, closed-form training alternative.

Method: Outline of a high-level architecture based on RBF-like machinery; derivation of a closed-form, one-iteration global optimum; includes a case study and comparisons to similar methods.

Result: Provides validation, a case study, and comparative analyses. The abstract asserts improved explainability and training efficiency, but concrete metrics are not provided.

Conclusion: The work presents a DNN-free alternative that claims one-shot, globally optimal training via closed-form solution, supported by case studies and method comparisons; further empirical validation is encouraged.

Abstract: The purpose of this article is to provide validation to my deep neural network alternative in the context of LLMs. Very recently, there has been a significant interest by Chinese researchers in a model called RBF network, as a substitute to standard DNNs, with increased explainability and higher accuracy. It turns out that my new model, discovered independently, is based on the exact same machinery. But with a major twist: it does not need DNN as it finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form, in one iteration, thus eliminating the tedious training step. Here I provide a high-level overview of my technology, with case study and comparison to similar methods.

</details>


### [185] [AMNESIA: A Large Scale Medical Unlearning Benchmark Suite with Disease-Informed Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30599)
*Saeedeh Davoudi,Reihaneh Iranmanesh,Ophir Frieder,Nazli Goharian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AMNESIA is the first large-scale, open-source benchmark for medical unlearning, featuring 70,560 QA pairs from 8,820 patient notes across 11 diseases; it covers factual recall and clinical inference and introduces a leakage-detection metric. Findings show that unlearning a single patient can erode knowledge about others with the same condition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical LLMs continually update knowledge; existing unlearning benchmarks use synthetic or small-scale data, leaving clinical unlearning underexplored. A realistic, large-scale benchmark is needed to study how unlearning affects shared medical knowledge and privacy.

Method: Construct AMNESIA with 70,560 QA pairs from 8,820 patient notes across 11 disease categories; include factual recall and reasoning questions; evaluate four widely used unlearning methods at random patient and disease levels; introduce a new metric to detect leakage of medical terminology.

Result: Evaluation across methods shows that unlearning individual patients can erode knowledge about other patients with the same condition; a leakage-detection metric reveals sharing of medical terminology; results indicate weak separation between patient-specific and shared clinical knowledge.

Conclusion: Calls for unlearning approaches that better disentangle patient-specific information from shared clinical knowledge; AMNESIA provides a realistic benchmark to advance medical unlearning research and evaluation; openness of data supports reproducibility.

Abstract: Medical knowledge is continuously evolving. This creates a need to update or selectively forget information encoded in already-trained medical LLMs. Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific training data from a model without full retraining. Yet, existing unlearning benchmarks rely on synthetic or small-scale general data, leaving clinical unlearning understudied. We introduce AMNESIA, the first large-scale, open source benchmark for medical unlearning, with 70,560 question-answer pairs from 8,820 patient notes across 11 disease categories. AMNESIA includes both factual questions testing direct recall and reasoning questions testing clinical inference. We use it to evaluate four widely used unlearning methods at both random patient and disease-level, and introduce a new metric for detecting leakage of medical terminology. We show that unlearning individual patients erodes knowledge of others with the same condition, calling for methods that can better separate patients from shared clinical knowledge.

</details>


### [186] [Functional MRI Time Series Generation via Wavelet-Based Image Transform and Spectral Flow Matching for Brain Disorder Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30387)
*Hwa Hui Tew,Junn Yong Loo,Fang Yu Leong,Julia K. Lau,Ding Fan,Hernando Ombao,Raphaël C. -W. Phan,Chee Pin Tan,Chee-Ming Ting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSFM uses dual spectral representations (DWT and DCT) with spectral flow matching to generate physiologically plausible, class-conditioned fMRI signals, improving brain network classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity and the non-stationary, complex spatiotemporal dynamics of BOLD signals to enable more faithful generative models for fMRI and enhance downstream analyses.

Method: Transform BOLD signals via discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to a wavelet map capturing multi-scale variations, then project into discrete cosine transform (DCT) space across regions and time. Train a spectral flow matching model to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representations, followed by inverse DCT and inverse DWT to reconstruct time-domain BOLD signals.

Result: Demonstrates improved performance in downstream fMRI-based brain network classification; code provided at the mentioned repository.

Conclusion: The dual-transform framework imposes structured frequency priors while preserving physiological brain dynamics, yielding more plausible and effective fMRI data generation for data-efficient brain analysis.

Abstract: Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) provides non-invasive access to dynamic brain activity by measuring blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signals over time. However, the resource-intensive nature of fMRI acquisition limits the availability of high-fidelity samples required for data-driven brain analysis models. While modern generative models can synthesize fMRI data, they often remain challenging in replicating their inherent non-stationarity, intricate spatiotemporal dynamics, and physiological variations of raw BOLD signals. To address these challenges, we propose Dual-Spectral Flow Matching (DSFM), a novel fMRI generative framework that cascades dual frequency representation of BOLD signals with spectral flow matching. Specifically, our framework first converts BOLD signals into a wavelet decomposition map via a discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to capture globalized transient and multi-scale variations, and projects into the discrete cosine transform (DCT) space across brain regions and time to exploit localized energy compaction of low-frequency dominant BOLD coefficients. Subsequently, a spectral flow matching model is trained to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representation. The generated samples are reconstructed through inverse DCT and inverse DWT operations to recover physiologically plausible time-domain BOLD signals. This dual-transform approach imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics. Ultimately, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through improved downstream fMRI-based brain network classification. The code is available at https://github.com/htew0001/DSFM.git .

</details>


### [187] [The Fast Mixing Mechanism for Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30600)
*Omri Lev,Moshe Shenfeld,Vishwak Srinivasan,Katrina Ligett,Ashia C. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fast-transform based DP sketching mechanism enables strong privacy guarantees and competitive runtime with Gaussian sketches, yielding the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares (OLS) by combining fast transforms with sketch-based DP linear regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, privacy-preserving optimization by reducing computational cost via structured sketches, addressing the runtime gap in existing DP sketching methods that rely on Gaussian sketches.

Method: Introduce a DP sketching mechanism based on fast transforms (e.g., Hadamard-based sketches). Derive privacy guarantees that are state-of-the-art and analyze regimes where the mechanism matches Gaussian sketch performance up to a constant factor. Integrate the sketch into recent DP linear regression frameworks to produce a fast, accurate algorithm for DP linear regression, and establish privacy and accuracy guarantees for this method.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art differential privacy guarantees for the sketching mechanism; in favorable regimes its privacy-utility guarantees match those of Gaussian sketches up to a constant factor; yields a new DP linear regression algorithm with improved runtime, and, to the best of our knowledge, the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares (OLS).

Conclusion: The fast-transform DP sketching approach provides a practically and theoretically advantageous route to fast, privacy-preserving regression, combining efficiency of structured fast transforms with strong DP guarantees and competitive accuracy.

Abstract: Randomized sketching is a central tool for compressing large-scale optimization problems while preserving accuracy. In particular, sketches that are based on structured matrices, such as the Hadamard matrix, can be applied efficiently and often yield solutions that approximate those of the original problem at much lower computational cost. In differential privacy (DP), Gaussian sketching has been used to solve DP linear regression, beginning with \citet{sheffet2017differentially, sheffet2019old} and later refined by \citet{lev2025gaussianmix, lev2026near}. However, although these methods achieve strong utility guarantees, they usually do not improve runtime over classical DP approaches. In this work, we introduce a new DP sketching mechanism based on fast transforms, which, in certain cases, matches the runtime of classical fast sketching methods. We prove state-of-the-art privacy guarantees for this mechanism and show that, in favorable regimes, they match those of the Gaussian sketch up to a constant factor. As an application, we combine this mechanism with recent sketch-based methods for DP linear regression to obtain a new algorithm with strong utility and improved runtime. We establish privacy and accuracy guarantees for this algorithm, yielding, to the best of our knowledge, the first fast method for DP ordinary least squares.

</details>


### [188] [NumLeak: Public Numeric Benchmarks as Latent Labels in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30393)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NumLeak introduces a hybrid measurement framework combining API-boundary probes with white-box validation to distinguish memorized recall from genuine skill in LLMs, revealing substantial memorization of finance and macro signals under closed APIs, and a strong defense via a simple system prompt.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the risk that public numeric benchmarks may reflect memorized date-correlated data rather than out-of-sample skill, leading to overestimation of model capability in time-sensitive tasks.

Method: Hybrid evaluation: (1) API-boundary probes on production models to assess recall and memorization via date-conditioned tasks; (2) white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM to reproduce dose–response, rank log-probabilities, and test prompts; (3) experiments across financial metrics (Fama-French factors, Mkt-RF, unemployment, CPI inflation, NOAA temperature) and a date-to-market sentiment regression; (4) parse-rate analyses on holdouts; (5) a one-line system prompt defense test against non-adaptive suffix attacks.

Result: Frontier LLMs exhibit very high recall of market excess returns (Pearson r = 0.97–0.99) and near-zero error (within ~0.15 or 25 bps) on related factors; parse rate declines to 21–57% on recent releases while preserving r≈0.99 for answered months; logprob ranking and white-box tests reveal memorization channels that are understated by black-box probes; system prompts block ~99.8% of a non-adaptive attack with negligible utility cost.

Conclusion: Evaluation of LLMs under API restrictions can substantially underreport memorization-driven performance; a white-box, causal-model validation and defense prompts are essential to accurately assess genuine skill versus memorized information, with practical defenses possible and robust to simple attacks.

Abstract: Public numeric benchmarks appear in pretraining, so an evaluation that conditions on a date may be measuring memorized recall rather than out-of-sample skill. We introduce NumLeak, a measurement framework that combines API-boundary probes on production models with a white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. Top-tier frontier LLMs recall the Fama-French market excess return at 3-seed pooled Pearson r=0.97-0.99 while staying within 0.15 within-25bps on the five sibling factors; comparable fidelity appears on U.S. unemployment, CPI inflation, and NOAA temperature. On a recent-release holdout, parse rate collapses to 21-57% but r stays at approximately 0.99 on months answered, the refuse-or-recall asymmetry a memorized channel predicts. The white-box experiment reproduces the dose-response, and logprob ranking detects memorization that open-ended generation misses, implying closed-API black-box probes understate the channel. A Sonnet "date to market-sentiment" regression that correlates with true Mkt-RF at r=0.74 collapses to r=0.02 once the model's own recall is residualized out. A one-line system-prompt defense blocks 99.8% of a non-adaptive single-turn suffix attack set at near-zero utility cost on conceptual and historical-narrative queries

</details>


### [189] [TASER: Task-Aware Stein Regularisation for Geometry-Driven Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30601)
*Michał Kozyra,Gesine Reinert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TASER (Task-Aware Stein Regularisation) is a training-time regulariser based on Langevin Stein operators that penalises Stein residuals to enforce data-density–aware anisotropic smoothing. It improves robustness to distribution shift and adversarial perturbations, with theoretical links to reduced first-order shift sensitivity, and shows CIFAR-10 gains across methods without significant loss in clean accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep networks remain fragile under distribution shift and adversarial perturbations due to unreasonable input sensitivity. Stein regularisation aims to align predictors with the data density geometry, yielding geometry-consistent, data-aware smoothness.

Method: Derive a training-time regularisation term from Langevin Stein operators that penalises pointwise Stein residuals under the training distribution, leading to anisotropic smoothing. Develop scalable implementation variants compatible with modern architectures and provide theoretical connections to reduced shift sensitivity.

Result: The approach yields improved robustness and stability across regression and vision benchmarks. In CIFAR-10 experiments, TASER consistently improves adversarial robustness of established training methods without causing statistically significant degradation in clean accuracy.

Conclusion: TASER provides a principled, scalable regularisation framework that imposes geometry-aware smoothness via Stein residues, with theoretical backing and practical robustness gains across tasks.

Abstract: Modern deep networks remain fragile under distribution shift and adversarial perturbations, often due to excessive or poorly structured input sensitivity. We introduce TASER (Task-Aware Stein Regularisation), a training-time regularisation framework derived from Langevin Stein operators. By penalising pointwise Stein residuals under the training distribution, TASER encourages geometric compatibility between predictors and data density, inducing anisotropic, data-aware smoothness. We provide theoretical links between Stein regularisation and reduced first-order shift sensitivity, develop scalable implementation variants compatible with modern architectures, and demonstrate improved robustness and stability across regression and vision benchmarks. Across CIFAR-10 experiments, TASER consistently improves the adversarial robustness of established training methods without incurring statistically significant clean-accuracy degradation.

</details>


### [190] [Constrained Flow Optimization via Sequential Fine Tuning for Molecular Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30610)
*Sven Gutjahr,Riccardo De Santi,Luca Schaufelberger,Kjell Jorner,Andreas Krause*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Constrained Generative Optimization and Constrained Flow Optimization (CFO) to balance reward enhancement and constraint satisfaction in diffusion/flow generative models; provides convergence guarantees and demonstrates improved reward with high constraint satisfaction in synthetic and molecular design tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reliably trade off maximizing task-specific rewards (e.g., binding affinity) with satisfying practical constraints (e.g., synthesizability) in generative models, addressing limitations of existing RL/control approaches in providing predictable trade-offs.

Method: Formulates a rigorous Constrained Generative Optimization framework and presents CFO, an algorithm that reduces the problem to sequential fine-tuning using scalable, established methods; provides convergence guarantees for constrained optimization and constrained generation.

Result: CFO achieves consistent reward gains while maintaining high constraint satisfaction across synthetic benchmarks and a molecular design task.

Conclusion: CFO offers automatic, provable balancing between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction with scalable training, validated on synthetic and molecular design experiments; advantageous for constrained generative optimization in scientific discovery.

Abstract: Adapting generative foundation models, in particular diffusion and flow models, to optimize given reward functions (e.g., binding affinity) while satisfying constraints (e.g., molecular synthesizability) is fundamental for their adoption in real-world scientific discovery applications such as molecular design or protein engineering. While recent works have introduced scalable methods for reward-guided fine-tuning of such models via reinforcement learning and control schemes, it remains an open problem how to algorithmically trade-off reward maximization and constraint satisfaction in a reliable and predictable manner. Motivated by this challenge, we first present a rigorous framework for Constrained Generative Optimization, which brings an optimization viewpoint to the introduced adaptation problem and retrieves the relevant task of constrained generation as a sub-case. Then, we introduce Constrained Flow Optimization (CFO), an algorithm that automatically and provably balances reward maximization and constraint satisfaction by reducing the original problem to sequential fine-tuning via established, scalable methods. We provide convergence guarantees for constrained generative optimization and constrained generation via CFO. Ultimately, we present an experimental evaluation of CFO on both synthetic, yet illustrative, settings, and a molecular design task. Across these evaluations, CFO achieves consistent increases in reward while ensuring high constraint satisfaction, showcasing its practical utility for constrained generative optimization.

</details>


### [191] [Improving Selective Classification with Pairwise Queries for Binary Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30615)
*Harsh Vardhan,Sunav Choudhary,Natwar Modani,Arya Mazumdar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A simple algorithm using pairwise queries to the same model improves selective classification by better detecting high-error samples, yielding a superior accuracy-cost tradeoff over relying solely on confidence estimates, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation on synthetic and in-context learning datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Confidence estimates in selective classification can be misaligned with actual predictions, especially in in-context binary classification with LLMs, causing higher error on non-rejected samples. Pairwise queries can reveal inconsistencies and reduce error.

Method: Introduce pairwise querying: query the same model twice or with varying prompts to detect inconsistent/high-error samples, and integrate this signal into the selective classification framework. Provide theoretical conditions under which this simple pairwise-query algorithm outperforms inconsistent confidence estimates. Validate experimentally on 1 synthetic and 4 real in-context learning-based binary classification datasets, comparing against baselines using raw confidence (e.g., next-token logits).

Result: The proposed pairwise-query method achieves a better accuracy-cost tradeoff than using only raw confidence estimates across the tested tasks, indicating effectiveness in detecting high-error samples and reducing non-rejected error.

Conclusion: Pairwise queries are a practical, theoretically justified enhancement for selective classification with LLMs, improving accuracy-cost efficiency. The approach shows promise for broader applicability beyond LLMs and synthetic settings.

Abstract: In selective classification, a model predicts the labels of data samples where it is confident, and abstains from predicting labels for samples on which it is not confident. The rejected samples are often labeled by an expert, which is expensive. The budget for the expert is best utilized when the model has low error on non-rejected samples. However, the estimate of a model's confidence might be inconsistent with the model's predictions, which can lead to high error on non-rejected points. Such situations can readily occur in in-context binary classification by LLMs. To remedy this, we propose making additional pairwise queries to the same model. These pairwise queries can detect high-error samples and be incorporated into selective classification techniques to reduce the error on non-rejected samples. Theoretically, we establish the conditions under which a simple algorithm using pairwise queries outperforms an inconsistent confidence estimate. We support this insight through extensive experiments for $1$ synthetic and $4$ in-context learning-based real binary classification datasets. In all these cases, we show that our algorithms, using pairwise queries, obtain a better accuracy-cost tradeoff than using only the raw confidence estimates, for instance, the LLM's next-token logits.

</details>


### [192] [Active Timepoint Selection for Learning Measure-Valued Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30625)
*Nicolas Huynh,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an active experimentation framework for inferring distributional trajectories from sparse, destructive measurements by mapping measure snapshots into a tangent space via Linearized Optimal Transport (LOT) and using a Gaussian Process surrogate to guide measurement times, achieving uncertainty-aware data acquisition and outperforming baselines on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruct continuous probability paths in Wasserstein space from sparse, destructive measurements where standard Euclidean metrics fail and uncertainty quantification is lacking; active learning for measure-valued data remains open.

Method: Transform distribution snapshots into LOT tangent space; fit a Gaussian Process surrogate on this space to model the probability path with epistemic uncertainty; design an acquisition policy that selects measurement times to minimize posterior uncertainty, iterating to refine the path.

Result: Empirically outperforms uncertainty-agnostic baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: LOT-based tangent-space GP surrogate enables tractable Bayesian active experimentation in the space of measures, providing an effective framework for planning measurements that reduce uncertainty about probability paths.

Abstract: Inferring continuous probability paths from sparse snapshots is a fundamental challenge in domains like single-cell biology, where high-fidelity data acquisition is often destructive and constrained by prohibitive sequencing costs. This motivates the need for active learning strategies to strategically select optimal measurement times. However, designing active learning policies for this setting remains an open problem: the target objects reside on the infinite dimensional Wasserstein space where standard Euclidean metrics are ill-defined, and current interpolation methods lack epistemic uncertainty quantification. We introduce a framework which extends active experimentation to the space of measures. By leveraging Linearized Optimal Transport (LOT), we map distributional snapshots into a tangent space amenable to Gaussian Process modeling, allowing us to construct a tractable probabilistic surrogate for the underlying probability path. This yields an acquisition policy that iteratively selects measurement times to minimize uncertainty. Empirical results demonstrate that our strategy outperforms uncertainty-agnostic baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [193] [CellBRIDGE: Learning Cellular Trajectories via Interaction-Aware Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30635)
*Silas Ruhrberg Estévez,Nicolas Huynh,Tennison Liu,Roderik M. Kortlever,Gerard I. Evan,David L. Bentley,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CellBRIDGE augments optimal transport with a ligand-receptor–driven interaction cost to align scRNA-seq snapshots, yielding more biologically meaningful couplings and enabling interpretable in silico perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inferring dynamics from destructive, snapshot-based scRNA-seq measurements; standard OT relies on gene-expression distances and treats cells as independent, neglecting structured cell–cell communication via ligand–receptor signaling.

Method: Propose CellBRIDGE: integrate a directed, typed interaction cost derived from ligand–receptor activity into feature-based OT; model cell–cell communication to improve cross-snapshot couplings; evaluate on synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets; use in silico perturbations by silencing LR pairs.

Result: Improved cross-snapshot couplings and trajectory estimates compared with feature-only baselines; on lung cancer data, silencing specific LR pairs induces trajectory shifts consistent with targeted pathway inhibition.

Conclusion: Incorporating cell–cell communication into OT yields more biologically meaningful trajectory inferences and enables mechanistically interpretable perturbations, with potential broad applicability to trajectory problems in scRNA-seq.

Abstract: Inferring dynamics from population snapshots is a fundamental challenge in machine learning and biology. In scRNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq), destructive measurements preclude direct tracking of individual cells across time, making trajectory inference underdetermined. Optimal Transport (OT) provides a principled framework for snapshot alignment, but a long-standing modeling question is which cost functions yield biologically meaningful couplings. Standard OT approaches rely on gene-expression distances, implicitly treating cells as independent points and neglecting structured cell-cell communication mediated by ligand-receptor signaling. We introduce CellBRIDGE (Cell-Based Regularized Interaction-Driven Gene Expression), which augments feature-based OT with a directed, typed interaction cost derived from ligand-receptor activity. By explicitly modeling cell-cell communication, CellBRIDGE improves cross-snapshot couplings and downstream trajectory estimates across synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets relative to feature-only baselines. Notably, CellBRIDGE enables mechanistically interpretable in silico perturbations: on lung cancer data, silencing specific ligand-receptor pairs induces trajectory shifts that recapitulate expected effects of targeted pathway inhibition.

</details>


### [194] [Score Broadcast and Decorrelation: A General Framework for Broadcast-Based Credit Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30638)
*Mustafa Uzun,Mete Erdogan,Cengiz Pehlevan,Alper T. Erdogan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SBD generalizes error broadcast across loss families via an orthogonality principle between the output score and hidden activations, enabling biologically plausible credit assignment with a three-factor rule; shows empirical gains on CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a loss-agnostic, biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation by grounding error broadcasting in a principled decorrelation framework that extends prior MSE-focused Error Broadcast and Decorrelation (EBD) work.

Method: Propose orthogonality between the output score (gradient of loss w.r.t. final-layer output) and hidden activations, valid when the optimal score has conditional mean zero. Unifies broadcast-based credit assignment across loss families (cross-entropy, Bregman divergences, proper scoring rules, exponential-family NLLs). Derive cross-entropy case explicitly, characterize admissible loss class, and introduce score vector expansion to enrich broadcast signals while preserving orthogonality. Ground three-factor learning rule with neuromodulatory factor equal to the broadcast score. Experimental implementation includes CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet, comparing to existing broadcast methods with improvements from score vector expansion.

Result: The work provides a theoretical framework for loss-score-based broadcasting applicable to a broad class of differentiable losses, along with explicit derivations for cross-entropy, a characterization of admissible losses, and a practical score-vector expansion technique. Empirically, SBD improves over prior broadcast methods on CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet, with additional gains from score-vector expansion.

Conclusion: Score-based broadcast serves as a unifying signal for credit assignment across differentiable losses, with a solid orthogonality foundation and a concrete three-factor learning rule; score-vector expansion further enhances decorrelation directions and performance.

Abstract: We introduce Score Broadcast and Decorrelation (SBD), a principled framework for broadcast-based credit assignment for general families of differentiable losses. Error broadcast is a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation that sends output information to hidden layers without weight transport. The Error Broadcast and Decorrelation (EBD) framework, recently introduced for the mean-squared-error (MSE) setting, grounded this mechanism in the stochastic orthogonality of optimal estimators, under which the optimal residual is orthogonal to functions of the input. We generalize that foundation by introducing an orthogonality principle between the output score (the gradient of loss with respect to the final-layer output) and hidden-layer activations, which holds whenever the optimal score has conditional mean zero. This single principle unifies broadcast-based credit assignment across the standard differentiable-loss families, including cross-entropy, Bregman divergences, proper scoring rules, and exponential-family negative log-likelihoods. The framework supplies a theoretical grounding for the three-factor learning rule under general losses, with the neuromodulatory factor derived as the broadcast loss score. We derive the cross-entropy case explicitly, characterize the admissible loss class, and introduce a score vector expansion technique that enriches the broadcast signal while preserving the orthogonality framework. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and Tiny ImageNet show that SBD substantially improves over existing broadcast approaches, with score vector expansion delivering further gains. Overall, this work identifies the loss score as the signal to broadcast, supplies the orthogonality theory and theoretical grounding for the three-factor learning rule from neuroscience, and shows how score vector expansion enriches the decorrelation directions of the resulting objective.

</details>


### [195] [CSULoRA: Closest Safe Update Low-Rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30640)
*Oleksandr Marchenko Breneur,Adelaide Danilov,Aria Nourbakhsh,Salima Lamsiyah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSULoRA is a post-hoc, closed-form method that corrects trained LoRA adapters by estimating a safety-aligned subspace and attenuating unsafe directions while preserving fully aligned updates, improving safety without sacrificing most utility gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA-based fine-tuning with adversarial or unsafe data can degrade model safety; existing safety-preserving methods rely on hard interventions that can hurt task performance or require extra tuning. A post-hoc, soft correction that preserves performance is desirable.

Method: 1) Build a safety-aligned subspace from weight displacement between a safety-aligned model and the base checkpoint. 2) Decompose each LoRA update into fully aligned, partially aligned, and off-subspace components. 3) Solve a closed-form penalized minimum-change problem to preserve the fully aligned part while smoothly attenuating unsafe directions based on their energy, instead of discarding the off-subspace components.

Result: CSULoRA substantially reduces attack success rate in adversarial fine-tuning experiments while preserving most of the utility gains from standard LoRA fine-tuning.

Conclusion: A practical post-hoc safety correction for LoRA that avoids extra training and minimizes interference with performance by projecting updates into a safety-aligned subspace and softly attenuating unsafe directions.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation has become a standard method for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models, but even small amounts of unsafe or adversarial fine-tuning data can substantially weaken the safety behavior of aligned models. Existing safety-preserving LoRA methods often rely on hard interventions such as projection, pruning, thresholding, or additional training objectives. While these methods can suppress unsafe update directions, they may also remove task-relevant information or require extra tuning. We introduce CSULoRA, a post-hoc method for correcting trained LoRA adapters through closest safe update estimation. CSULoRA estimates a safety-aligned subspace from the weight displacement between a safety-aligned model and its corresponding base checkpoint. It then decomposes each LoRA update into fully aligned, partially aligned, and off-subspace components. Instead of discarding components outside the estimated safety subspace, CSULoRA solves a closed-form penalized minimum-change problem that preserves the fully aligned component while smoothly attenuating potentially unsafe directions according to their relative energy. In adversarial fine-tuning experiments, CSULoRA substantially reduces attack success rate while preserving most of the utility gains obtained from standard LoRA fine-tuning.

</details>


### [196] [Diffusion Models Preferentially Memorize Prototypical Examples or: Why Does My Diffusion Model Love Slop?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30642)
*Marta Aparicio Rodriguez,Anastasia Borovykh,Grigorios A. Pavliotis,Daniel J. Korchinski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memorization in diffusion models trained on RHM-generated strings is biased toward samples built from common substrings, not rare ones. Deduplication at the data-point level does not provide a meaningful privacy guarantee. Fat-tailed data cause delayed memorization, and higher-level production-rule fat-tails amplify this effect. There exists an intermediate partial memorization regime where common substrings are learned early but overproduced later; stopping training in this regime yields bland, slop-like outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand when and how samples are memorized in generative models to address privacy concerns and to assess the role of dataset diversity in memorization dynamics.

Method: Train diffusion models on strings generated by Random Hierarchy Model (RHM) production rules. Analyze memorization patterns across datasets with varying diversification and fat-tailed properties. Observe generation to detect partial memorization regimes and effects of early stopping.

Result: Memorization preferentially targets samples composed of common substrings, even when all samples are unique; data-point deduplication does not reliably protect privacy. Fat-tailed distributions cause delayed memorization, and introducing fat-tails at higher abstraction levels amplifies this. An intermediate regime of partial memorization exists, with early learning of common substrings followed by overproduction; stopping training in this regime yields bland, slop-like outputs.

Conclusion: Dataset diversity, especially at higher levels of abstraction, mitigates memorization. Privacy guarantees require more than per-point dedup. Monitoring memorization dynamics is crucial, and early stopping can yield less diverse outputs.

Abstract: Generative models have a persistent limitation: their tendency to memorize training data can create legal liabilities and erode creative diversity. Understanding which samples are memorized in whole or in part, and under what conditions, therefore remains an important open problem. Here we answer the question "Are atypical or rare samples memorized first?" in the negative. We train diffusion models on strings generated according to the production rules of the Random Hierarchy Model (RHM), and find that samples composed of common substrings are preferentially memorized. This holds true even if the training data consists of entirely unique samples, indicating that deduplication at the data point level does not provide a meaningful privacy guarantee. Correspondingly we predict, then observe, delayed memorization for fat-tailed datasets (i.e., those with more atypical samples). This effect is amplified when fat-tails are introduced into high-level production rules. These together suggest that dataset diversity, particularly at higher levels of abstraction, plays an important role in staving off memorization. Finally, we identify an intermediate regime of partial memorization in which common substrings are learned first and subsequently overproduced during generation. If training is stopped in this regime, models will exhibit the reversion-to-the-mean blandness often derided as "slop".

</details>


### [197] [Convergence of Steepest Descent and Adam under Non-Uniform Smoothness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30648)
*Sharan Vaswani,Yifan Sun,Reza Babanezhad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Affine-curvature objectives (curvature affine in the objective value) yield linear convergence guarantees for first-order methods. The paper analyzes steepest descent and diagonal RMSProp/Adam, with sign GD outperforming GD in logistic regression on separable data and softmax policy gradient. It also shows linear convergence for a class of two-layer nets and provides a lower bound showing RMSProp/Adam beat AdaGrad, AMSGrad, GD, and heavy-ball under the assumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend convergence analysis of first-order methods to a broader class of objectives whose curvature scales with the objective value, reflecting practical non-uniform smoothness in ML loss landscapes.

Method: Assume curvature is an affine function of the objective value and impose gradient domination. Analyze convergence of (i) steepest descent, (ii) deterministic diagonal RMSProp, and (iii) Adam. Derive rates under these assumptions, with constant step-sizes and momentum where applicable, and compare to baseline methods. Prove linear convergence for specific ML settings and establish a lower bound relative to other algorithms.

Result: General convergence rate established for steepest descent and deterministic diagonal variants of RMSProp and Adam under affine-curvature and gradient-domination. Sign GD converges linearly and outperforms GD for logistic regression on separable data and softmax policy gradient. A class of two-layer neural networks on separable data admits linear convergence for RMSProp and Adam with fixed step-size and momentum. Lower bound shows RMSProp and Adam outperform AdaGrad, AMSGrad, GD, and heavy-ball in this regime.

Conclusion: Affine-curvature models capture a broad ML-relevant class of problems; under these conditions, first-order methods can achieve provably faster convergence than traditional GD and certain adaptive methods, with sign GD also beating GD in key settings. The results unify and extend known convergence properties to practical learning scenarios and provide a theoretical justification for the empirical success of RMSProp/Adam in non-strongly convex regimes.

Abstract: Recent work has analyzed the convergence of first-order methods under non-uniform smoothness assumptions that better model the loss landscape in machine learning tasks. We generalize this assumption to objectives whose curvature is an affine function of the objective value. This property is satisfied by a broad class of problems, including logistic regression, generalized linear models with a logistic link function, softmax policy gradient in reinforcement learning, and a class of neural networks. Under this assumption and gradient domination conditions, we establish a general convergence rate for the steepest descent method, and deterministic, diagonal variants of RMSProp and Adam. Our results imply that for logistic regression on separable data and the softmax policy gradient objective, sign GD converges linearly and is provably faster than GD. Furthermore, we show that for a class of two-layer neural networks on separable data, RMSProp and Adam can converge at a linear rate with a constant step-size and momentum parameter. Finally, we present a lower bound demonstrating that, under our assumption, RMSProp and Adam are provably faster than AdaGrad, AMSGrad, gradient descent, and heavy-ball momentum.

</details>


### [198] [LARK: Learnability-Grounded Trajectory Selection for Efficient Reasoning Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30651)
*Tianrun Yu,Kaixiang Zhao,Chih-Chun Chen,Amanda Hughes,Taylor W. Killian,Fenglong Ma,Weitong Zhang,Porter Jenkins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LARK is a learnability-grounded trajectory selection method for reasoning distillation that picks teacher trajectories the student can learn quickly while maintaining distributional coverage, using a learnability factor ρ estimated via a learnability proxy and a χ^2-regularized policy, with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over data-selection baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trajectory selection methods rely on trajectory quality or model confidence but do not explicitly account for whether the student can learn from a given trajectory. This can waste supervision on trajectories that are hard for the student to learn, hindering efficiency and generalization.

Method: Introduce a learnability factor ρ that quantifies the rate of decrease in the student training loss. Estimate this rate efficiently via a learnability proxy. Employ a χ^2-regularized selection policy to balance learnability and distributional coverage (i.e., keeping the training distribution representative). Provide theoretical guarantees on estimation error. Demonstrate that selecting trajectories via LARK yields faster supervised fine-tuning loss reduction and better downstream performance.

Result: Empirically, LARK outperforms data selection baselines across multiple base models and reasoning tasks. The LARK score correlates with downstream training utility, and trajectories selected by LARK induce faster loss reduction during supervised fine-tuning. The authors also release code for reproducibility.

Conclusion: A principled, learnability-guided trajectory selection framework for reasoning distillation that improves data efficiency and generalization by targeting trajectories the student can learn efficiently while preserving coverage, backed by theoretical error guarantees and strong empirical results.

Abstract: We study trajectory selection for reasoning distillation, where teacher-generated reasoning trajectories are selectively used as supervision for a student model. Existing methods rely on heuristics such as trajectory quality or model confidence, but they often overlook whether a trajectory is learnable by the student. In this paper, we present LARK, a learnability-grounded method for reasoning trajectory selection. LARK selects trajectories that the student can learn efficiently while preserving the generalization of the full training distribution. At the core of LARK is a learnability factor $ρ$, which characterizes the rate at which the student's training loss decreases. To estimate this rate efficiently and maintain generalization, we introduce a learnability proxy and a $χ^2$-regularized selection policy that balances learnability and distributional coverage, both with strong theoretical guarantees on their estimation error. Empirically, LARK consistently outperforms data selection baselines across multiple base models and reasoning tasks. Diagnostic analyses show that the LARK score predicts downstream training utility and that LARK-selected trajectories induce faster supervised fine-tuning loss reduction. Our code is available at https://github.com/Tianrun-Yu/LARK.

</details>


### [199] [BOKBO (Best of K Bad Options): Calibrated Abstention for VLA Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30660)
*Anya Singh,Cabrel Happi,Jai Relan,Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces BOKBO, a conformal abstention layer for K-sample vision-language-action (VLA) inference with global and Mondrian per-task variants, delivering finite-sample, distribution-free guarantees on executed-violation rate and improved task-hold coverage, while diagnosing and mitigating failure modes of nonconformity scores under perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate unsafe outcomes in test-time, K-sample VLA inference where all candidate actions can be unsafe or uncertain; provide strong, distribution-free calibration guarantees and robust abstention across tasks and perturbations.

Method: Develop a conformal abstention layer (BOKBO) for K-sample VLA inference with global and Mondrian (per-task) variants; augment with a learned violation predictor conditioned on semantic visual features and task identity; analyze failure modes of policy-internal nonconformity scores under perturbation-based K-sampling; test under token-level temperature sampling and policy-stochasticity-based sampling; evaluate using conditional coverage bounds (CRC), bootstrap splits, and distribution shifts; identify and correct a pitfall related to globally-set force thresholds.

Result: Finite-sample distribution-free guarantees on executed-violation rate; global and Mondrian variants with improved per-task calibration; reported empirical metrics: CRC bound holds on 86% of bootstrap splits with 78% coverage and 70% net task success; Mondrian-BOKBO raises per-task hold fraction from 0.71 to 0.93; results stable across seeds and distribution shifts; reveals and mitigates a pitfall where naive force thresholds inflate violations by ~5x.

Conclusion: BOKBO provides reliable abstention and calibration for K-sample VLA inference, improving safety and task success; Mondrian per-task calibration reduces conditional gaps on hard tasks; learned predictors help tighten calibration; but nonconformity scores can fail under certain perturbations, necessitating mechanism-aware sampling and careful threshold design; the study highlights practical pitfalls to avoid in deployment.

Abstract: Test-time scaling for vision-language-action (VLA) policies, methods such as RoboMonkey, SEAL, MG-Select, and V-GPS, samples K candidate action chunks at inference and executes the verifier-best. When all K candidates are unsafe, the system executes a violating action with no warning. We propose BOKBO, the first conformal abstention layer for K-sample VLA inference, providing finite-sample distribution-free guarantees on executed-violation rate. We provide both global and per-task (Mondrian) variants, with the per-task variant closing the conditional gap on the hardest tasks.
  Our analysis exposes a structural failure of policy-internal nonconformity scores under perturbation-based K-sampling: the base-policy confidence proxy and K-sample disagreement correlate at 0.98 with the action-noise hyperparameter $σ$, while correlating at the noise floor with actual safety violations. We test the failure's scope by replicating the analysis under token-level temperature sampling and find the failure is mechanism-specific and partially mitigated under policy-stochasticity-based sampling. A learned violation predictor conditioned on semantic visual features and task identity supports tight calibration: at $ε$ = 0.05 on libero_object_temp_x0.1 with OpenVLA-OFT, the conditional CRC bound holds on 86% of bootstrap splits with 78% coverage and 70% net task success. Mondrian-BOKBO raises the minimum per-task conditional hold fraction from 0.71 to 0.93. Results are stable across 5 training seeds, replicate within bootstrap noise on $π_0$-FAST, hold on libero_spatial_temp_x0.1 as a co-equal benchmark, and survive four within-suite distribution shifts. We additionally identify and correct a methodological pitfall: globally-set force thresholds well below expert-typical manipulation forces conflate unsafe behavior with normal manipulation, inflating violation rates by $5\times$.

</details>


### [200] [Bridging the Gap Between Natural Language and Market Dynamics via High-Dimensional Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30652)
*Yujin Jeong,Noelle Jung,Brian Y. C. Leung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: High-dimensional FinBERT embeddings via a Siamese-optimized approach improve short-term stock move predictions in a Transformer architecture, outperforming scalar sentiment baselines and other embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar sentiment scores discard nuanced information in financial news, leading to information loss. High-dimensional narrative representations may capture richer context to aid short-term forecasting.

Method: Replace discrete polarity with dense FinBERT embeddings within a Transformer-based forecasting model. Compare embedding strategies on the FNSPID dataset: raw embeddings, attention-weighted aggregation, and a Siamese-network-based approach. Evaluate their impact on short-term stock price movement prediction.

Result: Attention-based aggregation under low signal-to-noise typical of financial data underperformed. Siamese-optimized embeddings outperformed both the scalar baseline and raw embedding approaches, improving predictive accuracy for short-term movements.

Conclusion: Maintaining high-dimensional narrative context via Siamese-optimized embeddings yields better predictive accuracy in noisy financial forecasting tasks, suggesting a viable path beyond scalar sentiment scores.

Abstract: Traditional multi-modal financial forecasting often relies on scalar sentiment scores, which fail to capture the nuances of financial news. To address this information loss, this paper explores high-dimensional representation learning by replacing discrete polarity ratings with dense FinBERT embeddings within a Transformer-based forecasting architecture. We benchmarked various embedding strategies on the FNSPID dataset, including raw embeddings, attention-weighted aggregation, and a custom Siamese network. While the attention-based mechanism struggled with the low signal-to-noise ratio typical of financial data, the integration of Siamese-optimized embeddings outperformed both the scalar baseline and raw embedding approaches, demonstrating that preserving high-dimensional narrative context yields improved predictive accuracy for short-term stock price movements.

</details>


### [201] [Learning to Perceive the World Through Control: Empowerment-Based Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30656)
*Mahsa Bastankhah,Sophie Broderick,Benjamin Eysenbach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Empowerment-based representation learning yields two invariant, complementary representations (forward and backward) that focus on control-relevant state features; learning via interaction is essential for deriving a control-centric model, aligning with causal learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional observations often contain many control-irrelevant features. The empowerment objective, which promotes an agent’s influence over the environment, is proposed as a means to learn representations that retain only control-relevant information.

Method: The paper analyzes empowerment maximization and shows that it induces two distinct, complementary representations—forward and backward—that are invariant to control-irrelevant features. It argues that interaction-driven learning (not passive data) is crucial for obtaining these invariances and ties the approach to causal learning.

Result: Empowerment maximization yields two invariant, complementary representations (forward and backward) that capture relevant state aspects while disregarding irrelevant ones, effectively producing an implicit, control-centric model of the world. The analysis underscores the importance of active interaction for learning these invariances.

Conclusion: Maximizing empowerment guides learning toward a control-centric world model, with forward and backward representations providing robust, invariant encodings. Interaction-driven learning is essential for acquiring these invariances, aligning with causal learning perspectives.

Abstract: In many practical reinforcement learning environments, observations are far higher-dimensional than the variables that matter for control. In this work, we ask: can we learn representations that capture only control-relevant features of the environment? We study this question through the empowerment objective, which maximizes an agent's influence over the environment and is widely used for unsupervised skill learning. We show that empowerment agents induce two distinct representations -- forward and backward -- that capture complementary aspects of the state, and both of which are invariant to control-irrelevant features. Thus, empowerment maximization leads agents to learn an implicit, control-centric model of the world. Our analysis highlights the importance of learning representations through interaction rather than from passive datasets: interaction aimed at maximizing control is essential for learning useful invariance properties, a perspective that aligns closely with the causal learning literature.

</details>


### [202] [FLAG: Flow Policy MaxEnt-RL by Latent Augmented Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30749)
*Sungha Kim,Gawon Lee,Jusuk Lee,Jonghae Park,H. Jin Kim,Daesol Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLAG introduces a flow-based latent augmentation to MaxEnt-RL, localizing sampling to avoid importance-weight collapse, enabling expressive policies and high-dimensional control with state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MaxEnt-RL with expressive policies struggles due to importance weight degeneracy when sampling over large action spaces; Gaussian policies limit expressivity and sampling inefficiency hampers scalability.

Method: Add a flow latent variable to the state, forming a latent-augmented policy; optimize a provably consistent proxy MaxEnt-RL objective that focuses sampling in a localized region and leverages flow-based expressivity to model complex actions.

Result: Empirical results show FLAG enables expressive policy optimization with limited importance samples, scales to high-dimensional control tasks, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging benchmarks.

Conclusion: Localizing sampling with a latent-flow augmentation yields scalable, expressive MaxEnt-RL policies and strong empirical performance; the approach offers a practical path to high-dimensional, robust RL via constrained importance sampling.

Abstract: Maximum entropy reinforcement learning (MaxEnt-RL) enables robust exploration, yet practical implementations often restrict policies to simple Gaussians.
  While recent approaches incorporate expressive generative policies via importance-weighted supervised learning, they are prone to importance weight collapse, which limits their scalability in high-dimensional action spaces.
  Our key insight is to mitigate this limitation by localizing the sampling region, avoiding the weight degeneracy induced by importance sampling over the entire action space.
  To instantiate this insight, we introduce \textbf{FLAG} (\textbf{F}low policy with \textbf{L}atent-\textbf{A}ugmented \textbf{G}uidance).
  FLAG augments the state space with a flow latent variable and optimizes a provably consistent proxy MaxEnt-RL objective.
  We empirically demonstrate that FLAG enables expressive policy optimization with limited importance samples and scales to high-dimensional control tasks.
  Furthermore, FLAG achieves state-of-the-art performance across challenging benchmarks. Our project webpage: https://flag-rl.github.io/

</details>


### [203] [Spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based learning for infectious disease forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30662)
*Luz Stefani Sotomayor Valenzuela,Susanna Cramb,Darren Wraith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based model for forecasting infectious disease cases, adaptable to both large and small populations; evaluated on US COVID-19 and Hungary chickenpox data with competitive weekly forecasts, one-step delay, and reduced sensitivity to high-frequency variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the under-exploration of stochastic modelling within spatio-temporal graph-based disease forecasting and the challenge of scaling to large real-world datasets, to improve real-world forecast reliability.

Method: Introduces a spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based architecture that incorporates a stochastic formulation and uncertainty approximation; capable of encoding large and small geographical networks within a single model; evaluated on 3,218 US counties (COVID-19) and 20 Hungarian counties (chickenpox), benchmarked against four baseline spatio-temporal graph models.

Result: The approach shows enhanced performance for the 2022 first wave of COVID-19 in the US and competitive results for Hungarian chickenpox waves (2012-2014); overall weekly forecasts are competitive across all counties, though with a one-step delay and reduced sensitivity to high-frequency/low-amplitude variability.

Conclusion: A robust, scalable stochastic graph-based framework can effectively represent epidemic progression and deliver competitive forecasts across diverse scales while explicitly handling uncertainty, albeit with a small forecast delay.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal graph-based models have typically been used to forecast new cases of infectious diseases such as COVID-19 and chickenpox outbreaks. However, the use of stochastic modelling into their learning process has been surprisingly under-investigated and rarely considered entire data sets of large countries. As a result, it is unknown whether these models would provide accurate forecasts in real-world disease spread scenarios. In this work, we propose a spatio-temporal stochastic graph-based architecture that integrates a stochastic formulation and uncertainty approximation process to forecast new infectious disease cases. We find that our approach can adapt to encode large and small population geographical networks within a single model architecture. Using two real-world data sets, COVID-19 in the US and chickenpox in Hungary, we report an enhanced effect of the proposed architecture across predictions of the 2022 first wave for COVID-19 in the US and comparative results of chickenpox waves during 2012-2014 in Hungary. By benchmarking with four spatio-temporal graph-based models, quantitative results show competitive overall weekly performance of the proposed approach on forecasting new cases for all 3,218 US counties and all 20 Hungary counties. The proposed approach can represent overall epidemic progression relative to baselines, though with a one-step delay; while exhibiting a reduced sensitivity to high-frequency and low-amplitude variability.

</details>


### [204] [Universal Decision Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30694)
*Sridhar Mahadevan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified categorical framework (Universal Decision Learner) uses left/right Kan extensions to connect local decision data to globally coherent behavior across planning, reinforcement learning, causal intervention, online learning, and game-theoretic equilibrium.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a universal formalism to explain how local information can be canonically extended to global behavior across diverse decision-making paradigms, unifying algorithms under a common construction.

Method: Introduce Universal Decision Learner (UDL) by extending a partially specified decision functor from observed contexts to new contexts via left Kan extensions (rollout, aggregation, candidate generation) and right Kan extensions (consistency, constraint satisfaction, fixed-point semantics); define Kan-invariant behavioral equivalence and minimal abstractions; prove a universal comparison property.

Result: Abstract UDL construction defined; universal comparison property proven; Kan-invariant equivalence and minimal abstractions characterized; shows Bellman equations, planning recursions, causal interventions, online regret, and equilibria arise as special cases; reinforcement learning specialization discussed in supplementary material.

Conclusion: The framework provides a canonical way to extend local behavioral data to globally coherent decision policies; many decision-formalisms can be seen as instantiations of the same universal problem rather than all sharing one algorithm.

Abstract: Many theories of decision making -- planning, reinforcement learning, causal intervention, online learning, and game-theoretic equilibrium -- turn local information into globally coherent behavior. This paper proposes a common categorical formulation: a Universal Decision Learner (UDL) extends a partially specified decision functor from observed contexts to new contexts by a pair of universal constructions. Left Kan extensions express rollout, aggregation, and candidate generation; right Kan extensions express consistency, constraint satisfaction, and fixed-point semantics. The central claim is not that every decision problem has the same algorithm, but that many decision formalisms instantiate the same universal problem: extend local behavioral data canonically, then characterize the globally coherent extensions. We give the abstract UDL construction, prove its universal comparison property, define Kan-invariant behavioral equivalence and minimal abstractions, and show how Bellman equations, planning recursions, causal interventions, online regret, and equilibria arise as special cases. The supplementary material develops the reinforcement-learning specialization in more detail.

</details>


### [205] [A Context-Aware Middleware for Medical Image Based Reports: An approach based on image feature extraction and association rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30699)
*Erick O. Rodrigues,Jose Viterbo,Aura Conci,Trueman Mac Henry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A context-aware middleware for medical image routing infers the best-fit staff based on historical data and current context to improve workflow efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual forwarding of medical images to the appropriate specialist is time-consuming and inefficient; automation is needed to reduce decision time and improve staff utilization.

Method: The middleware collects data from analyses performed by medical staff; using this data and the current clinical context, it infers the most suitable recipient for an incoming image.

Result: The abstract claims the middleware can infer the best-fit staff to receive an incoming image, aiming to improve workflow efficiency.

Conclusion: Context-aware middleware can improve hospital workflow efficiency by intelligently routing medical images to the appropriate staff.

Abstract: This work proposes a context-aware middleware for medical workflow organization and efficiency improvement. In hospitals, laboratories and teleradiology companies, each physician or technician is specialized in a specific kind of diagnosis or analysis. Therefore, certain types of medical images are often forwarded to a certain physician or a certain group. This forwarding is time consuming. That is, repeatedly deciding who would be the best physician, whether he is available at a certain moment given a certain context is exhaustive and may be very inefficient. Thus, the proposed middleware has the ability to process and collect data from images analyzed by each medical staff. Based on the collected data and current clinical context, the middleware is able to infer who would be the best fit staff to receive a certain incoming medical image.

</details>


### [206] [Diversity Matters: Revisiting Test-Time Compute in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30713)
*Yijie Tong,Yifan Hou,Shaobo Cui,Antoine Bosselut,Mrinmaya Sachan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-based test-time combinations improve VLM ensembles by leveraging predictive entropy, outperforming majority voting and the best single model; smaller models can meaningfully boost larger ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time compute (TTC) strategies are effective for LLMs but underexplored for vision-language models (VLMs). The study investigates feature-based scoring and voting, identifies diversity as a key limitation, and proposes a principled alternative to harness ensemble strengths in VLMs.

Method: Systematic study across seven VLMs and six benchmarks, analyzing feature-based scoring and majority voting. Proposes Entropy-based TTC (ETTC) that selects the most confident prediction via predictive entropy. Theoretical analysis shows voting is limited when outputs are highly correlated; ETTC reduces to voting for single models and weights stronger models more in ensembles. Proves ETTC outperforms majority voting under mild assumptions and empirically demonstrates it surpasses both voting and the best individual model. Also observes that smaller models can synergistically enhance larger ones.

Result: Feature heuristics fail; voting yields only modest gains in single-model settings due to lack of prediction diversity. Multi-model ensembles are more diverse, but standard majority voting fails to account for varying model capabilities. ETTC outperforms majority voting and the best single model in experiments, and smaller models can contribute to gains when combined with larger ones, enabling ensembling benefits not achievable with standard strategies.

Conclusion: Entropy-based TTC provides a principled, confidence-driven ensemble mechanism for VLMs. By exploiting predictive entropy, ETTC leverages model heterogeneity to produce stronger predictions, offering consistent gains over voting and the best single model. The findings highlight the value of heterogeneous ensembles, including smaller models, for enhanced TTC performance in VLMs.

Abstract: Test-time compute (TTC) strategies have emerged as a lightweight approach to boost reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, their application and benefits for vision-language models (VLMs) remain underexplored. We present a systematic study of TTC across seven VLMs and six benchmarks, specifically analyzing feature-based scoring and majority voting methods. We find that feature heuristics fail and voting yields only modest gains in single-model settings. We theoretically show that this limitation stems from a lack of prediction diversity: when outputs are highly correlated, voting provides little benefit. In contrast, multi-model ensembles offer richer diversity, yet standard majority voting fails to account for varying model capabilities. To address this, we propose Entropy-based TTC (ETTC), which selects the most confident prediction based on predictive entropy. Our method reduces to majority voting in the single-model case, but in model ensembles, it leverages confidence disparities to prioritize stronger models. We prove that ETTC outperforms majority voting under mild assumptions and empirically demonstrate that it consistently surpasses both voting and the best individual model. Crucially, our results show that smaller models can synergistically enhance larger ones, unlocking ensembling gains not achievable with standard strategies.

</details>


### [207] [When are LLMs Sufficient Policy Optimizers for Sequential RL Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30719)
*Stephane Hatgis-Kessell,Emma Brunskill*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PromptPO shows LLMs can act as black-box policy optimizers, achieving competitive results with fewer environment interactions across several RL tasks, but struggles in fine-grained continuous control domains like MuJoCo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether LLMs can replace classical RL algorithms by generating executable policies from high-level environment descriptions, reducing sample complexity via prior knowledge encoded in the LLM.

Method: Iterative prompting: describe state/action/reward in Python to an LLM; LLM outputs executable policies or planning components; run rollouts, use feedback to refine; tasks include hard exploration, Meta-World robotics, real-world controls; no extra prompting beyond initial prompts to maximize return; outputs include PID-like controllers and planning algorithms such as value iteration.

Result: PromptPO often matches or exceeds standard RL baselines with substantially fewer environment interactions; policies range from simple controllers to planning-based approaches; LLM leverages prior environment/optimization knowledge; underperforms in MuJoCo domains.
