Conclusion: LLM-based policy optimization is a viable alternative in settings where the environment can be described and the task benefits from priors, but may be limited for tasks requiring fine-grained continuous control; further work needed on prompting reliability, generalization, and handling continuous control demands.

Abstract: We study when large language models (LLMs) can serve as effective black-box policy optimizers for reinforcement learning (RL) tasks, i.e., when can we replace classical RL algorithms with an LLM? We explore this question by introducing Prompted Policy Optimization (PromptPO), an iterative method that prompts an LLM with Python descriptions of the state space, action space, and reward function, then has it generate and refine executable policies based on rollout feedback. Across hard exploration environments, Meta-World robotics tasks, and several real-world control problems, PromptPO often matches or exceeds the performance of standard RL baselines while using substantially fewer environment interactions. To maximize expected return, and without further explicit prompting, the policies PromptPO outputs range from tuned proportional controllers or rule-based plans to policies that run planning algorithms like value iteration. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based policy optimization is sufficient when the LLM can leverage prior knowledge about the environment or optimization strategy. PromptPO underperforms standard RL baselines in MuJoCo domains. This demonstrates possible limitations of LLM-based policy optimization to settings that requiring fine-grained continuous control.

</details>


### [208] [Kalimati Vegetable Price Index Forecasting with a Momentum Corrected Online Stacking Ensemble](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30720)
*Sahaj Raj Malla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KVPI: an inverse-volatility weighted composite index (2013-2023, Kathmandu) of 135 wholesale commodities; 64 features; 14 models; Momentum-Corrected Online Stacking Ensemble best; RMSE 1.771, MAPE 0.68%, R2 0.845 at 90 days; open-source pipeline for Nepal's food security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high volatility and supply disruptions in emerging economies to produce a stable macro-level price signal to aid forecasting and policy.

Method: Construct KVPI by inverse-volatility weighting; generate 64 causally valid features including festival effects and calendar variables; evaluate 14 models across horizons; develop Momentum-Corrected Online Stacking Ensemble.

Result: Momentum-Corrected Online Stacking Ensemble achieves RMSE 1.771, MAPE 0.68%, R2 0.845 at 90 days; tree-based ensembles robust; transformers and classical stats less effective; open-source pipeline.

Conclusion: Tool supports policymakers and supply chain actors in Nepal and similar markets for anticipating price movements and enhancing food security.

Abstract: Forecasting agricultural commodity prices in emerging economies is difficult due to high volatility, frequent supply disruptions, and strong cultural influences on demand. This study introduces the Kalimati Vegetable Price Index (KVPI), a new inverse-volatility weighted composite index that aggregates 135 daily wholesale commodities from Kathmandu over ten years (2013-2023). By creating a stable macro-level signal, the KVPI reduces the noise inherent in modelling individual crops. A rich set of 64 causally valid features was developed, including festival lead-lag effects, rolling statistics, and calendar variables. Fourteen forecasting models spanning statistical, tree-based, deep learning, hybrid, and transformer architectures were rigorously evaluated across short (7-day), medium (14- and 30-day), and long-term (90-day) horizons. Tree-based ensembles proved notably robust, while classical statistical models and complex transformers struggled with the noisy dataset. The proposed Momentum-Corrected Online Stacking Ensemble achieved the strongest performance, yielding a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 1.771, an exceptionally low Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 0.68%, and explaining 84.5% of the variance (R-squared = 0.845) at the 90-day horizon. This open-source pipeline provides policymakers and supply chain actors in Nepal and similar markets with a practical, reliable tool for anticipating price movements and strengthening food security.

</details>


### [209] [Self-Certifying Transport MCMC via Dual Spectral-Gap Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30722)
*Jun Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces CerT-MCMC with two convergence certificates for learned-transport MCMC: a covering certificate providing full-support spectral-gap bounds with a dimension-dependent rate and a matching lower bound, and a quantile-core certificate focusing on a high-probability residual core using one-dimensional quantiles to yield non-vacuous bounds in higher dimensions; together, the first automatic, dimension-aware certification for this sampler, validated across D=2–20 problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide automatic, rigorous convergence guarantees for learned-transport MCMC, addressing the absence of reliable certificates for transport-based samplers in moderate-to-high dimensions.

Method: Use a normalising flow to map Gaussian to the target posterior; reuse the same flow as the independence Metropolis-Hastings proposal; derive two certificates: (a) covering certificate via finite-sample covering arguments to bound weight-ratio oscillation over the full proposal support, yielding full-support spectral-gap bounds when a gradient bound is available; correction term scales as O(n^{-1/D}) with an intrinsic Omega(n^{-1/D}) lower bound; (b) quantile-core certificate restricting to a high-probability residual core and controlling oscillation with one-dimensional empirical quantiles, with finite-sample slack O(n^{-1/2}) independent of ambient dimension.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic targets (D=2–20), structural-engineering posteriors (D=6,8), Heart Disease data (D=13), and synthetic Bayesian logistic regression (D=20) shows the quantile-core certificate yields non-vacuous spectral-gap bounds where the covering certificate is vacuous; the spectral-gap proxy tracks empirical ESS within ~7%; a negative control confirms the certificate discriminates flow quality by >10x while acceptance-rate differences are small (~1.15x).

Conclusion: The paper presents the first dual-certificate framework that provides automatic, dimension-aware convergence guarantees for learned-transport MCMC, enabling reliable detection of genuine transport failure versus proof-technique limitations and offering practical, dimension-sensitive certificates for certified Bayesian inference with learned proposals.

Abstract: We propose CerT-MCMC, a framework that equips learned-transport Markov chain Monte Carlo with automatic, rigorous convergence certificates. A normalising flow maps a Gaussian reference to an approximation of the target posterior; the same flow then serves as both the independence Metropolis-Hastings proposal and the basis for a computable spectral-gap bound. We develop two complementary certificates. The covering certificate bounds the weight-ratio oscillation over the full proposal support via finite-sample covering arguments, yielding full-support spectral-gap bounds when a conservative gradient bound is available; its correction term scales as O(n^{-1/D}), making it rapidly weak and eventually vacuous as dimension increases. We prove a matching Omega(n^{-1/D}) lower bound, establishing that this barrier is intrinsic to pointwise Lipschitz certification. The quantile-core certificate restricts attention to a high-probability residual core on which the oscillation is controlled by one-dimensional empirical quantiles, with a finite-sample probability slack of O(n^{-1/2}), independent of the ambient dimension. On synthetic targets (D=2-20), structural-engineering posteriors (D=6,8), real-data logistic regression on the Heart Disease data set (D=13), and synthetic Bayesian logistic regression (D=20), the quantile-core certificate delivers non-vacuous spectral-gap bounds where the covering certificate is vacuous, and its spectral-gap proxy tracks empirical effective sample sizes within 7%. A negative control experiment confirms that the certificate discriminates flow quality by a factor exceeding 10x, whereas acceptance rates differ by only 1.15x. To our knowledge, the dual-certificate framework is the first to provide automatic, dimension-aware convergence certificates for learned-transport MCMC, distinguishing genuine transport failure from proof-technique limitations.

</details>


### [210] [Reducing the GPU Memory Bottleneck with Lossless Compression for ML -- Extended](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30728)
*Aditya K Kamath,Arvind Krishnamurthy,Marco Canini,Simon Peter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Invariant Bit Packing (IBP): a lossless compression technique to reduce PCIe transfer bottlenecks in ML workloads by removing invariant bits across tensor groups, enabling GPU-optimized, warp-parallel decompression and asynchronous transfers. Demonstrates substantial speedups and provides framework APIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GPU memory capacity is often exceeded; ML workloads incur heavy PCIe data transfers. Lossy compression introduces accuracy/workload tradeoffs. This work seeks a lossless, deployment-friendly method to cut transfer time without affecting accuracy.

Method: IBP identifies invariant bits across groups of tensors, packs them into compact representations, and decompresses on the GPU using warp-parallelism, low-overhead bit operations, and asynchronous PCIe transfers. Provides easy-to-use APIs and integrates with GNN training, DLRM embedding lookups, and LLM inference.

Result: Average improvements reported: 74% faster GNN training, 180% faster DLRM embedding lookup, and 24% faster LLM inference.

Conclusion: Lossless IBP reduces interconnect transfer overhead with minimal interference to GPU computation and is practical for integration into common ML pipelines.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) training and inference often process data sets far exceeding GPU memory capacity, forcing them to rely on PCIe for on-demand tensor transfers, causing critical transfer bottlenecks. Lossy compression has been proposed to relieve bottlenecks but introduces workload-dependent accuracy loss, making it complex or even prohibitive to use in existing ML deployments. We explore lossless compression as an alternative that avoids this deployment complexity. We identify where lossless compression can be integrated into ML pipelines while minimizing interference with GPU execution. Based on our findings, we introduce Invariant Bit Packing (IBP), a novel lossless compression algorithm designed to minimize data transfer time for ML. IBP identifies and eliminates invariant bits across groups of tensors, improving throughput through GPU-optimized decompression that leverages warp parallelism, low-overhead bit operations, and asynchronous PCIe transfers. We provide easy-to-use APIs, showcasing them by adding IBP support to GNN training, as well as DLRM and LLM inference frameworks. IBP achieves, on average, 74% faster GNN training, 180% faster DLRM embedding lookup, and 24% faster LLM inference.

</details>


### [211] [SemStruct: Contextualizing Semantic Embeddings with Structural Information for Schema Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30729)
*Inwon Kang,Kavitha Srinivas,Nandana Mihindukulasooriya,Sola Shirai,Parikshit Ram,Horst Samulowitz,Oshani Seneviratne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SemStruct combines frozen PLMs with a GNN-based structural encoder to model tables as heterogeneous graphs, achieving state-of-the-art schema matching on Valentine and SOTAB-SM, with row-level context acting as topological conduits rather than semantic entities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the loss of row-level co-occurrence information when using serialized column descriptions in PLMs for schema matching; incorporate explicit structural context without fine-tuning language models.

Method: Model the table as a heterogeneous graph with columns and values as nodes connected by rows; use a frozen PLM to encode textual semantics and train a lightweight structural encoder (GNN) to propagate information through the structure; evaluate on Valentine and SOTAB-SM; perform ablations showing the importance of structural modeling.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming fully fine-tuned baselines on complex, semantically joinable datasets; ablations reveal row representations mainly serve as topological conduits, underscoring the necessity of explicit structural modeling for schema matching.

Conclusion: Explicit integration of structural context via GNNs with frozen PLMs yields substantial gains in schema matching, reducing reliance on LLM fine-tuning and highlighting the critical role of relational structure in data integration.

Abstract: Schema matching is a fundamental step in integrating heterogeneous data sources. While Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) have revolutionized this task by capturing linguistic semantics, they typically process tabular data as serialized text sequences of standalone column descriptions. This serialization discards critical structural information -- specifically, the row-level co-occurrences, i.e. the relational context -- forcing models to rely solely on column header semantics or standalone distributions. To bridge this gap, we propose SemStruct, a framework that joins the semantic power of frozen PLMs with the structural inductive bias of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). We model the table as a heterogeneous graph where columns and values are nodes connected by rows, allowing the GNN to propagate disambiguating context across the structure. Unlike other state-of-the-art methods that require proprietary LLM access and fine-tuning of language models, SemStruct keeps the language model frozen and trains only a lightweight structural encoder. Extensive experiments on the Valentine and SOTAB-SM benchmarks demonstrate that SemStruct achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming fully fine-tuned baselines on complex, semantically joinable datasets. Furthermore, our ablation studies reveal that row representations serve primarily as topological conduits rather than semantic entities, validating the necessity of explicit structural modeling in schema matching.

</details>


### [212] [Beyond Accuracy: Evaluating Efficiency, Robustness and Explainability in Deep Learning for Malaria Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30734)
*Olivier Kanamugire,Kerol Djoumessi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lightweight deep learning models can match heavier architectures for malaria diagnosis on the NLM-Malaria dataset, with no significant performance gap. However, robustness issues arise: model confidence degrades under image corruption faster than accuracy, and post-hoc explanations (XAI) are not robust to noise. CAM-based explanations align with diagnostically relevant regions, while fine-grained attributions are less effective for heavier backbones. The findings support deploying efficient models in resource-limited settings but highlight explainability reliability as a critical concern.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address urgent diagnostic needs for malaria in sub-Saharan Africa where limited computational resources hinder timely and accurate diagnosis; evaluate whether efficient models can maintain performance and deliver reliable explanations suitable for clinical adoption.

Method: Benchmark four deep learning architectures across a spectrum of capacities on the NLM-Malaria dataset. Assess predictive performance and robustness under three types of image corruption, and evaluate post-hoc explainability usingCAM-based XAI methods and fine-grained attribution approaches. Use Friedman test to compare model performance and analyze explanation localization and reliability under corruption.

Result: Lightweight, efficiency-oriented models achieve predictive parity with heavier architectures; Friedman test indicates no statistically significant differences among models. CAM-based explanations consistently localize diagnostically relevant regions; fine-grained attributions are less targeted, especially with heavier backbones. Under corruption, model confidence declines more rapidly than accuracy, and no XAI method maintains robustness to noise levels plausible in clinical practice, even when predictions remain accurate.

Conclusion: Deploy lightweight architectures for malaria diagnosis in resource-constrained settings, as they do not compromise accuracy. However, post-hoc explanations are vulnerable to corruption, underscoring the need for robust XAI strategies and cautious clinical deployment to ensure trustworthy decision support.

Abstract: Malaria remains a leading cause of mortality in sub-Saharan Africa, where scarce diagnostic infrastructure makes timely, accurate diagnosis particularly challenging. While deep learning offers a compelling path toward automated malaria screening, clinical adoption is hindered by computational cost and opacity in decision-making. This work benchmarks four deep learning models spanning a wide range of designed design architectures and model capacities on the NLM-Malaria dataset, jointly evaluating predictive performance, robustness, and post-hoc explainability. We find that lightweight, efficient-by-design models match their heavier counterparts in predictive performance, and the Friedman test confirms no statistically significant performance differences. CAM-based XAI methods consistently localize diagnostically relevant regions, while fine-grained attribution methods produce less targeted explanations, particularly with heavier backbones. Robustness evaluation under three types of image corruption further reveals that model confidence degrades faster than accuracy, providing a practical signal for human review. However, no XAI method is robust to corruption, with explanation reliability degrading at noise levels plausible in clinical practice, even when predictions remain accurate. These findings support the deployment of lightweight architectures for malaria diagnosis in resource-constrained settings, while highlighting the vulnerability of post-hoc explanations as an important consideration for responsible clinical deployment.

</details>


### [213] [OrcaRouter: A Production-Oriented LLM Router with Hybrid Offline-Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30736)
*Zhenghua Bao,Fengya Tian,Chris Zhang,Zhenjun Chen,Xile Ma,Yi Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrcaRouter is a production-ready LLM router that uses a LinUCB contextual bandit over lexical and embedding features, trained offline with a reward matrix and ridge regressors per model, and optionally updated online after deployment. It achieved strong RouterArena performance (second place) with 75.5% accuracy at $1/k queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently route user requests to the most appropriate model (balancing capability and cost) in production settings while preserving learnability and scalability.

Method: Offline phase obtains full-information feedback by evaluating each candidate model on curated routing prompts to build a reward matrix; fits one ridge regressor per candidate (arm). Deployed system initializes from these parameters and may continue learning online by updating only the selected model’s arm after observing reward; uses a LinUCB-based contextual bandit with lexical and sentence-embedding features.

Result: RouterArena, May 20, 2026: OrcaRouter-Adaptive ranked second with arena score 72.08; accuracy 75.54% at cost $1.00 per 1,000 queries.

Conclusion: Shows viability of production-oriented LLM routing with offline-online learning integration, achieving competitive accuracy-cost trade-offs. Highlights the importance of feature design (lexical + embedding) and per-arm online updates, while leaving room for examining generalization, drift, and robustness in real-world deployments.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models, each with distinct capabilities and inference costs, raises a practical deployment question: given an incoming request, which model should handle it? We present OrcaRouter, a production-oriented LLM router that combines a LinUCB-based contextual bandit over lexical and sentence-embedding features with a hybrid offline-online learning protocol. Offline, OrcaRouter obtains full-information feedback by evaluating each candidate model on a curated set of routing prompts, yielding a reward matrix used to fit one ridge regressor per arm. At deployment time, it initializes from these parameters and can optionally continue learning from bandit feedback, updating only the selected model's arm after observing its reward. At the time of our RouterArena submission (May 20, 2026), OrcaRouter-Adaptive ranked second on the public RouterArena leaderboard with an arena score of 72.08, achieving 75.54% accuracy at a cost of USD 1.00 per 1,000 queries.

</details>


### [214] [Chain-of-Thought and Compressed Looped Transformers: A Memory-Budget Separation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30757)
*Haozhou Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical comparison of three test-time memory regimes for Transformers—compressed latent loops, full sequence-state loops, and chain-of-thought scratchpads—shows a fundamental memory-budget separation: compressed loops are limited by their recurrent-state size and, under standard complexity assumptions, cannot solve P-complete problems under logspace reductions, whereas polynomial-length chain-of-thought can. Full sequence-state loops are memory-rich and resemble scratchpads; the separation arises from whether persistent state matches the task's working-memory demands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how different persistent-memory architectures in test-time reasoning affect computational power and efficiency, and identify the memory-budget constraints that govern when looped computation yields real reasoning benefits.

Method: Theoretical, complexity-theoretic analysis comparing three memory regimes. Defines compressed latent loops, full sequence-state loops, and chain-of-thought scratchpads; uses illustrative tasks (controlled pointer-chasing, associative-recall sweeps) to argue about memory budgets and performance.

Result: Compressed latent loops are bounded by the size of their recurrent state; extending the loop adds computation but does not create a growing scratchpad, so small-state loops remain small-space reasoners even with many steps and cannot solve P-complete problems under logspace reductions under standard assumptions. Polynomial-length chain-of-thought can overcome this limitation. Full sequence-state loops carry state at every input position and operate in a memory-rich regime similar to scratchpads. The separation is specific to compressed loops; performance depends on matching the persistent-state budget to the task’s working-memory demand.

Conclusion: Memory-budget considerations critically shape test-time reasoning capabilities. Compressed loops offer limited reasoning power dictated by recurrent-state size, while chain-of-thought scratchpads enable larger, polynomial-length reasoning. Designers should align persistent-state budgets with task requirements; the memory regime determines whether the model can reach higher computational thresholds.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought prompting and looped Transformers both give a fixed model more test-time computation, but they differ in what they remember. Chain-of-thought stores intermediate state in generated tokens that remain in the context, whereas a looped Transformer carries state through recurrent hidden activations. We argue that this persistent mutable memory is a central resource for test-time reasoning.
  We compare three memory regimes, the compressed latent loop, the full sequence-state loop, and the chain-of-thought scratchpad. Our main result shows that a compressed loop is limited by the size of its recurrent state. Running the loop longer adds computation but does not by itself create a growing scratchpad, so a loop with a small recurrent state remains a small-space reasoner even when run for many steps. Under a standard complexity assumption, such loops cannot decide problems that are P-complete under logspace reductions, whereas polynomial-length chain-of-thought can.
  The separation is specific to compressed loops, as full sequence-state loops carry state at every input position and live in a memory-rich regime closer to explicit scratchpads. Controlled pointer-chasing and associative-recall sweeps illustrate this memory-budget view, with performance sensitive to whether the persistent-state budget matches the task's working-memory demand.

</details>


### [215] [Efficient and Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Framework for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30776)
*Ha Manh Bui,Metod Jazbec,Eric Nalisnick,Anqi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DUAL is a diffusion-based uncertainty-aware framework for offline-to-online RL that distills a fast diffusion actor and transition model offline and uses Laplace-based epistemic uncertainty with a state-shift detector to guide online exploration, yielding higher online returns than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate distribution shift between offline data and online interactions in offline-to-online RL (O2O-RL) by leveraging diffusion priors and uncertainty quantification to improve data efficiency and stability.

Method: Offline phase distills a fast-sampling diffusion actor policy and a diffusion-based transition model using prior knowledge from the diffusion model; applies a Laplace approximation on the actor loss to quantify epistemic uncertainty; uses a distance-based transition-state-shift detector to identify shifts; online phase uses uncertainty signals to balance exploration vs exploitation.

Result: Empirically improves online expected return over O2O-RL baselines across multiple settings and environments.

Conclusion: DUAL demonstrates that combining diffusion priors with principled uncertainty quantification (Laplace-based epistemic uncertainty and shift detection) can improve O2O RL, with a theoretical link between the actor loss and epistemic uncertainty and practical gains in online performance.

Abstract: Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning (O2O-RL) leverages an offline, pre-trained policy to minimize costly online interactions. Although data-efficient, O2O-RL is susceptible to shifts between offline and online distributions. Existing work aims to mitigate the harm of this shift by finetuning the policy on trajectory data sampled from a diffusion model. Inspired by this line of work, we propose DUAL: an efficient \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{U}ncertainty-\textbf{A}ware framework for offline-to-online reinforcement \textbf{L}earning. DUAL utilizes the prior knowledge of the diffusion model to distill a fast-sampling diffusion actor policy and transition model in the offline phase. DUAL also employs a Laplace approximation and distance transition-state-shift detection, thereby using uncertainty quantification to improve exploration versus exploitation in the online phase. We formally show that our actor loss with the Laplace approximation provides a proxy for a principled estimate of epistemic uncertainty. Empirically, DUAL improves the online expected return over O2O-RL baselines across multiple settings and environments.

</details>


### [216] [AbstainGNN: Teaching Graph Neural Networks to Abstain for Graph Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30786)
*Xixun Lin,Zhiheng Zhou,Zhengyin Zhang,Yancheng Chen,Shuai Zhang,Ge Zhang,Shichao Zhu,Lixin Zou,Chuan Zhou,Peng Zhang,Shirui Pan,Yanan Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theory-driven graph classification framework with abstention (AbstainGNN) that jointly models prediction and abstention, grounded in PAC-Bayesian theory, with a two-stage training strategy (predictive warm-start and abstention calibration). Demonstrates improved reliability by rejecting uncertain predictions while maintaining competitive accuracy at fixed rejection rates across five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph classifiers often face high uncertainty; incorrect predictions can be harmful in safety-critical settings. Existing abstention methods are heuristic; there is a need for principled, theory-backed abstention in GNNs.

Method: Introduce abstention function alongside a predictive function within a GNN framework. Develop a unified objective derived from PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds to trade off classification error and rejection cost. Implement a two-stage training: first warm-starts the predictive function, then calibrates the abstention function using graph-structure information.

Result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmark datasets shows AbstainGNN surpasses existing abstention methods, achieving better classification performance at the same rejection rates.

Conclusion: A theory-guided abstention framework for graph classification that yields more reliable decisions by allowing the model to abstain under uncertainty, backed by PAC-Bayesian analysis and a practical two-stage training strategy.

Abstract: Graph classification is a core task in graph data mining with widespread real-world applications. Recent advances in graph neural networks (GNNs) have led to substantial performance improvements for graph classification. However, existing GNNs are typically forced to make predictions even under high uncertainty or unknown conditions, resulting in unreliable decisions that can severely impact downstream tasks, particularly in safety-critical scenarios. To address this critical limitation, we propose AbstainGNN, a novel and theory-driven framework for graph classification with abstention, which enables GNNs to reject uncertain predictions instead of producing incorrect decisions. Specifically, AbstainGNN explicitly models both the predictive function and the abstention function, allowing for effective utilization of graph structural information. Moreover, unlike existing heuristic abstention methods, we theoretically characterize the trade-off between classification errors and rejection costs from a PAC-Bayesian generalization perspective, and derive a unified learning objective for model optimization. Guided by this theoretical insight, we further develop an efficient two-stage training strategy consisting of predictive function warm-start and abstention function calibration. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets show that AbstainGNN outperforms existing abstention methods, achieving superior classification performance under the same rejection rates.

</details>


### [217] [Smaller Models are Natural Explorers for Policy-Level Diversity in GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30789)
*Yiming Ren,Yiran Xu,Zicheng Lin,Chufan Shi,Yukang Chen,Dingdong Wang,Tianhe Wu,Junjie Wang,Yujiu Yang,Yu Qiao,Ruihang Chu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S2L-PO leverages fixed small models as exploratory agents to train larger models in GRPO, shifting from token-level randomness to policy-level diversity. This yields temporally correlated, coherent exploration, faster convergence, and improved math-reasoning performance with reduced rollout cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GRPO approaches rely on token-level randomness to diversify rollouts, which introduces stepwise noise and can degrade trajectory coherence. There is underexplored potential in exploiting policy-level diversity across model sizes within the same family to improve gradient estimates and sample efficiency.

Method: Introduce S2L-PO: use small, fixed models as explorers to guide larger models. Implement a progressive annealing schedule that starts with offline small-model rollouts and gradually transitions to the large learner’s own sampling, balancing exploration and exploitation and avoiding mid-training drops.

Result: Empirical gains on diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., +8.8% on AIME 24 with a 1.7B explorer guiding an 8B model) and reduced rollout compute, indicating improved policy-level diversity leads to higher pass@k and faster convergence.

Conclusion: Policy-level diversity from smaller models provides a structured, temporally correlated signal that enhances gradient estimation and performance ceiling in GRPO. A carefully staged offline-to-online annealing (S2L-PO) yields faster convergence and higher accuracy with lowered compute.

Abstract: We identify a new dimension for enhancing rollout diversity in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for LLMs. While GRPO relies on diverse rollouts, prevailing strategies primarily increase diversity by injecting more token-level randomness, which may introduce step-wise noise and lead to incoherent trajectories. We uncover that smaller models within the same model family inherently exhibit higher policy-level diversity, indicated by their superior pass@k relative to larger counterparts as sample counts increase. Unlike token-level noise, this diversity is temporally correlated, preserves logical consistency, and provides structured exploration signals for gradient estimation. We thus propose S2L-PO (Small-to-Large Policy Optimization), a framework that leverages fixed small models as natural explorers to train larger models. To balance exploration and exploitation, we design a progressive annealing strategy that transitions from offline small-model rollouts to the large learner's own sampling. This shift elegantly avoids mid-training performance drops caused by the small model's capacity limits, achieving faster convergence and unlocking a higher performance ceiling. S2L-PO improves accuracy on diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., +8.8% on AIME 24 using a 1.7B explorer to guide the 8B model) while reducing rollout compute.

</details>


### [218] [Learning Permutation-invariant Macroscopic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30812)
*Zhichao Han,Mengyi Chen,Qianxiao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a permutation-invariant autoencoder to model macroscopic dynamics from unordered microscopic data by using a permutation-invariant encoder and a decoder that reconstructs mass distribution around observed points; jointly learns latent states and macroscopic observables; validated on particle systems, Lennard-Jones fluids, and polymer stretch dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of fixed input ordering in data-driven modeling of high-dimensional microscopic systems, especially when the microscopic state is inherently unordered (e.g., particle systems).

Method: Use a permutation-invariant encoder to obtain latent representations and design a decoder that reconstructs the mass distribution centered at observed points rather than per-sample reconstruction; jointly train to learn macroscopic dynamics of observables along with latent states.

Result: Demonstrates effectiveness and robustness across multiple microscopic settings: energy dynamics in interacting particle systems, mixing dynamics in Lennard-Jones fluids, and stretch dynamics from video data of polymers in elongational force fields.

Conclusion: Permutation-invariant latent representations with a mass-distribution-focused decoder enable robust learning of macroscopic dynamics from unordered microscopic data, generalizing across diverse physical systems.

Abstract: Accurately modeling the macroscopic dynamics of high-dimensional microscopic systems is of broad interest across the sciences. Many data-driven approaches learn a low-dimensional latent state through an autoencoder trained for pointwise input reconstruction. These methods typically assume a fixed ordering of microscopic degrees of freedom in the input. However, in many settings, such as particle systems, the microscopic state is inherently unordered. This motivates an autoencoder framework that learns permutation-invariant latent representations. To this end, we adopt a permutation-invariant encoder and design the decoder to reconstruct the mass distribution centered at the observed points rather than per-sample reconstruction. We then jointly learn the macroscopic dynamics of the observables together with the latent states. We demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method across a range of microscopic settings, including learning the energy dynamics in interacting particle systems, predicting mixing dynamics in Lennard-Jones fluids, and modeling the stretching dynamics from video data of polymers moving in an elongational force field.

</details>


### [219] [DARTS: Distribution-Aware Active Rollout Trajectory Shaping for Accelerating LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30859)
*Yujie Wang,Siwei Chen,Longzan Luo,Xinyi Liu,Xupeng Miao,Fangcheng Fu,Bin Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies intra-prompt long-tail inefficiencies in RL rollout distributions, caused by ineffective verbosity, and introduces active distribution shaping via distribution-aware trajectory sampling and adaptive redundancy allocation to create concise, certain rollouts, achieving up to 1.77x speedup with preserved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rollout efficiency is bottlenecked by long-tail response lengths; existing fixes target tail prompts but ignore the root cause—the distribution itself. A finer-grained analysis is needed to reduce overhead and improve efficiency.

Method: Propose distribution-aware trajectory sampling to choose trajectories from a redundant exploration space per prompt, coupled with an adaptive redundancy allocation scheme to maximize shaping effectiveness while maintaining system efficiency.

Result: Experiments demonstrate significant acceleration over state-of-the-art systems, up to 1.77x, without compromising model performance.

Conclusion: Active distribution shaping resolves tail-induced overheads in RL rollouts, offering substantial efficiency gains while preserving performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become pivotal for improving model capabilities yet suffers from rollout efficiency bottlenecks due to the long-tail response length distribution. While existing works mitigate the impact of long tails via prompt-level tail scheduling, we focus on the root source of inefficiency: the distribution itself. Specifically, we characterize the long-tail distribution at a finer granularity, identifying intra-prompt long tails, and revealing that they frequently consist of ineffective verbosity. To address this, we propose a novel paradigm of active distribution shaping to shape the rollout distribution towards conciseness and certainty, thereby fundamentally resolving tail-induced overheads. We achieve this through a distribution-aware trajectory sampling mechanism, which selects trajectories from a redundant exploration space for each prompt, and an adaptive redundancy allocation scheme to maximize both shaping effectiveness and system efficiency. Experiments demonstrate significant acceleration over state-of-the-art systems by up to 1.77x without compromising model performance.

</details>


### [220] [De-attribute to Forget for LLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30919)
*Xinyang Lu,Jiabao Pan,Rachael Hwee Ling Sim,See-Kiong Ng,Anthony Kum Hoe Tung,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes DareU, an LLM unlearning framework that uses data attribution rewards and reinforcement learning to de-attribute forget data, aiming to balance unlearning effectiveness with model utility; claims to outperform baselines using an attribution-approximation via an LLM classifier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses limitations of loss-based unlearning (e.g., over-forgetting, utility loss) by reframing unlearning as zeroing out data attribution rather than increasing prediction loss.

Method: Introduce DareU, a reinforcement learning framework that updates the LLM to reduce attribution scores of forget data owners. Uses an attribution estimator (an LLM classifier) as an efficient proxy for data attribution.

Result: Empirical results show DareU achieves effective unlearning while maintaining model utility, outperforming existing baselines.

Conclusion: Data attribution-based unlearning via RL is a promising direction that can balance unlearning quality and utility; warrants further evaluation and refinement of attribution estimators.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns on the use of inappropriate data for training, which has led to a growing interest in LLM unlearning. Many existing LLM unlearning approaches rely on optimizing prediction loss(es), such as maximizing the loss on the forget set, but often face critical issues like over-forgetting and poor model utility. To address them, this paper novelly frames the optimization objective for LLM unlearning as one of zeroing out data attribution instead. In particular, we propose the first LLM unlearning framework based on data attribution rewards called DareU that performs reinforcement learning to update the LLM by reducing the attribution score of its generated responses (i.e., de-attributing) to the forget data owners. Empirical evaluation using an LLM classifier as an efficient approximation of attribution shows that DareU outperforms existing baselines by achieving effective unlearning while balancing forget quality and model utility well.

</details>


### [221] [GlucoFM: A Dual-Stream Foundation Model for Continuous Glucose Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30865)
*Zechen Li,Keerthana Natarajan,Weizhi Zhang,Menglian Zhou,Simon A. Lee,Yuwei Zhang,Maxwell A. Xu,Zeinab Esmaeilpour,Flora D. Salim,Mark Malhotra,Lindsey Sunden,Shwetak Patel,Yuzhe Yang,Ahmed A. Metwally*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlucoFM is a lightweight CGM foundation model that realigns irregular CGM data to a 24-hour grid, decomposes into slow state and transient event streams, and pretrains with two objectives, achieving superior transferability and few-shot performance across cohorts and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CGM models treat glucose traces as entangled single streams and struggle with irregular sampling, limiting physiology-aware representation learning and transfer to diverse tasks.

Method: Align recordings to a 24-hour grid while preserving observation masks, decompose glucose dynamics into slow physiological state and transient event streams, and pretrain on 109,066 hours from 477 subjects using two objectives: masked contextual latent prediction over fused daily representations and temporal dynamics prediction over state and event streams.

Result: GlucoFM achieves the strongest subject-disjoint linear-probing performance among baselines, with an average PR-AUC gain of 4.1 points over the best CGM-specific foundation model. Gains are most pronounced on core metabolic outcomes, achieving PR-AUC improvements on diabetes-risk and β-cell dysfunction tasks and on most insulin-resistance tasks. It also demonstrates best cross-dataset transfer and strong few-shot adaptation, with consistent gains when aggregating multiple days for subject-level prediction.

Conclusion: Physiology-aware decomposition provides a beneficial inductive bias for transferable CGM representations, and GlucoFM delivers superior predictive performance and transferability across diverse cohorts and tasks.

Abstract: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) provides a dense view of daily metabolic physiology, yet existing generic time-series and CGM-specific foundation models often encode glucose traces as entangled single-stream sequences, leaving the distinct temporal structure of glycemic dynamics only implicitly modeled. We present GlucoFM, a lightweight CGM foundation model that aligns irregular recordings to a 24-hour chronological grid, preserves observation masks, and decomposes glucose dynamics into slow physiological state and transient event streams, capturing low-frequency glycemic baselines and short-term deviations that may reflect acute physiological responses or sensor artifacts. GlucoFM is pretrained on 109,066 hours of unlabeled CGM recordings from 477 subjects with two complementary objectives: masked contextual latent prediction over fused daily representations and temporal dynamics prediction over state and event streams. Across four diverse cohorts and seven clinical prediction tasks, GlucoFM achieves the strongest subject-disjoint linear-probing performance among evaluated baselines, improving average PR-AUC by 4.1 points over the best CGM-specific foundation model. Its gains are most pronounced on core metabolic outcomes, leading PR-AUC on all diabetes-risk and $β$-cell dysfunction tasks and on 3 of 4 insulin-resistance tasks. GlucoFM also achieves the best overall cross-dataset transfer performance and strong few-shot adaptation among evaluated methods, and consistent gains when aggregating multiple days for subject-level prediction, highlighting physiology-aware decomposition as an effective inductive bias for transferable CGM representation learning.

</details>


### [222] [DEM: A Distilled Explanation Model for Interpretable Anomaly Detection in Physiological Sensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31007)
*Jyotirmoy Singh,Anushka Roy,Shreea Bose,Chittaranjan Hota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Distilled Explanation Model (DEM), an intrinsically interpretable, three-stage framework that distills a gradient-boosting expert into a residual-based decision tree with a fidelity metric,
achieving high AUC on physiological anomaly detection and fast real-time inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve both high predictive accuracy and clinically interpretable explanations for anomaly detection in WBANs, where faults and missing data can cause false alarms.

Method: Three-stage glass-box pipeline: (1) train a gradient boosting expert; (2) fit an explanation tree on residuals relative to a linear baseline; (3) quantify fidelity of the explanation to the expert and provide depth-controlled, human-readable rules; evaluation with XGBoost distillation across four datasets (MIMIC-IV, WESAD, eICU, SmartNet).

Result: AUCs of 0.9964 (clinical contextual anomaly detection) and 0.9047 (wearable stress detection); inference time 0.17 ms per 1000 samples; DEM is ~1235x faster than SHAP; ablation shows gains from distillation; explicit accuracy-interpretability trade-off via tree depth.

Conclusion: DEM provides faithful, inherently interpretable predictions with a trustworthiness fidelity metric, enabling real-time, rule-based explanations and controlled trade-offs between accuracy and interpretability compared to post-hoc explanations.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in physiological sensor data from Wireless Body Area Networks (WBANs) can be caused by sensor faults, network disruptions, or missing data, leading to false alarms. Hence, it demands both high predictive accuracy and clinically interpretable explanations. Existing approaches rely either on black-box models that achieve strong performance but offer no transparency, or on post-prediction explanation methods such as SHAP and LIME. In this paper, we propose the Distilled Explanation Model (DEM), a three-stage glass-box framework that distills the non-linear knowledge of a gradient boosting expert into an interpretable decision tree operating on residuals relative to a linear baseline, so that the explanation is not an approximation but the prediction itself. DEM introduces a novel distillation fidelity metric that quantifies how faithfully the explanation tree captures the expert model's non-linear contribution, providing a principled measure of explanation trustworthiness absent from prior interpretable models. Evaluated across four physiological datasets, including MIMIC-IV, WESAD, eICU, and an in-house SmartNet WBAN corpus, DEM achieves an AUC of 0.9964 on clinical contextual anomaly detection and 0.9047 on wearable stress detection while producing human-readable if-then rules at a controllable depth. Inference requires 0.17ms per 1000 samples, rendering DEM 1235x faster than SHAP-based post-hoc explanation and suitable for real-time physiological monitoring. Ablation studies confirm that the XGBoost distillation step provides measurable gains over naive residual fitting, and depth-sensitivity analysis demonstrates an explicit, user-controlled accuracy-interpretability trade-off unique to DEM among existing intrinsically interpretable models.

</details>


### [223] [Bandwidth Allocation with Device Partitioning for Federated Learning over Industrial IoT networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30892)
*Kangmin Kim,Jaeyoung Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Partitioning-based bandwidth allocation for FL in IIoT: sequential, exclusive full-bandwidth access to device subsets reduces training time and energy, beating non-partitioning schemes and approaching the lower bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication is the main bottleneck in federated learning over wireless IIoT networks; device heterogeneity in compute capabilities requires a bandwidth policy beyond conventional QoS that minimizes total training time and energy.

Method: Devices are partitioned into ordered subsets, each granted exclusive access to the full bandwidth sequentially. The authors prove partitioning yields strictly lower training time than any non-partitioning scheme regardless of scheduler, and shorter transmissions also reduce uplink energy.

Result: The policy consistently reduces training time and energy consumption compared to existing schemes and approaches the theoretical lower bound on round time, validated on GC10-Det and CIFAR-10 datasets.

Conclusion: Partitioned, sequential bandwidth allocation leverages device heterogeneity to improve FL efficiency in IIoT, delivering faster convergence and lower energy use, with practical validation on real datasets.

Abstract: We consider a federated learning (FL) system in which Industrial Internet-of-Things (IIoT) devices collaboratively train a global model over wireless channels without sharing local data. In such systems, communication time is a primary bottleneck that constrains overall training efficiency. Unlike conventional networks that prioritize individual quality-of-service requirements, FL systems collectively aim to converge to an optimal global model as efficiently as possible, which calls for a fundamentally different approach to bandwidth allocation. In this paper, we propose a novel bandwidth allocation policy that exploits the heterogeneity of device computing capabilities to minimize total training time. Rather than distributing bandwidth among all selected devices simultaneously, the proposed policy partitions the participating devices into ordered subsets and sequentially grants each subset exclusive access to the full bandwidth. We formally prove that this partitioning-based policy achieves a strictly lower training time than any bandwidth allocation scheme without partitioning, irrespective of the underlying scheduling algorithm. Furthermore, by reducing per-device transmission duration, the proposed policy also minimizes uplink energy consumption, which is particularly beneficial for battery-constrained IIoT devices. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets - including GC10-Det, an industrial surface defect benchmark, and CIFAR-10, a standard image classification benchmark - demonstrate that the proposed policy consistently reduces training time and energy consumption compared to existing bandwidth allocation schemes, approaching the theoretical lower bound on round time.

</details>


### [224] [Zero Collapse: A Failure Mode of Policy Gradient Methods in Discontinuous Reward Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30896)
*Nishant Kumar,Enrique Areyan Viqueira,Amy Greenwald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies zero collapse in RL for auctions with discontinuous rewards, explains mechanism, analyzes how stochasticity and step size cause policies to enter zero-reward regimes, demonstrates across REINFORCE and actor-critic, and offers initialization/architecture-based mitigations plus a formal auction-RL framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of applying policy-gradient and value-based RL to repeated first-price auctions where rewards are discontinuous; such landscapes create flat regions with vanishing gradients, hindering learning.

Method: Provide mechanistic explanation of zero collapse; analyze interaction between policy stochasticity and step size; empirical comparisons across REINFORCE and actor-critic variants; propose mitigation strategies via initialization and architectural choices; formal RL framework for auction environments highlighting unique structural properties.

Result: Zero collapse phenomenon is demonstrated; biased value estimates can accelerate movement to unstable regions; mitigation strategies improve stability; framework to study auctions.

Conclusion: Offers practical guidelines and a formal framework; calls attention to structural properties of auction environments; lays groundwork for robust RL in bidding contexts.

Abstract: Bidding in repeated auctions is a central challenge for reinforcement learning (RL), combining continuous control with the strategic complexities of digital advertising. While policy gradient and value-based methods seem well-suited for these settings, they often struggle with the discontinuous, "cliff-like" nature of auction reward landscapes. In a first-price auction, for example, a bidder receives zero reward until they cross a specific threshold, after which the reward decreases as the bid increases. This creates a landscape of flat, zero-reward regions separated by sharp boundaries.
  We identify a fundamental failure mode in this setting termed "zero collapse." We show that stochastic exploration and gradient-based updates can cause policies to overshoot optimal high-reward regions and enter flat, zero-reward regimes. Once there, the lack of an informative gradient signal makes recovery extremely sample-inefficient, effectively trapping the agent. We find that actor-critic methods are particularly susceptible, as biased value estimates can accelerate this movement toward unstable regions.
  Our contributions include: (1) a mechanistic explanation of how discontinuous rewards lead to vanishing signals and zero collapse; (2) an analysis of the interaction between policy stochasticity and step size; and (3) an empirical demonstration of this phenomenon across REINFORCE and actor-critic variants. We propose practical mitigation strategies involving initialization and architectural choices to improve stability. Finally, we introduce a formal RL framework for auction environments highlighting their unique structural properties.

</details>


### [225] [Density-Guided Robust Counterfactual Explanations on Tabular Data under Model Multiplicity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30901)
*Jun Tan,Qing Guo,Zicheng Xu,Jinglin Li,Qi Fang,Ning Gui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DensityFlow uses Neural ODEs to generate counterfactual explanations that stay on the high-density data manifold by learning a differentiable density score via Noise Contrastive Estimation; introduces a (K+1)-way discriminator for density ratio estimation and a local proxy distillation for black-box models; shows improved validity under model multiplicity and lower query costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CEs are unreliable in low-density regions where model variance is high; ensemble-based stability methods are expensive; need a lightweight, reliable, and query-efficient approach that respects data density.

Method: Model counterfactual trajectories as continuous-time dynamics parameterized by Neural ODE; guide trajectories with a differentiable density score to avoid low-density areas; learn the density score through Noise Contrastive Estimation with a (K+1)-way discriminator; for black-box targets, perform local proxy distillation to align a lightweight surrogate only along the CE generation trajectory, enabling gradient-based optimization with few queries.

Result: Empirical results show DensityFlow achieves higher validity under model multiplicity and substantially reduces query costs compared to ensemble baselines; code released.

Conclusion: DensityFlow provides a robust, density-aware path to generating CEs, reducing reliance on ensembles and improving efficiency; potential for broader applicability and extension to other generative explanations.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CEs) are essential for actionable recourse, yet their reliability is often compromised in low-density regions, where classifiers exhibit high variance. Unlike existing methods that rely on expensive ensemble intersections to define stability, we propose \textit{DensityFlow}, a generative framework that constructs robust CEs by adhering to the high-confidence data manifold. Specifically, we model the counterfactual generation as continuous-time dynamics parameterized by Neural ODE, guided by a differentiable density score to actively avoid uncertain, low-density areas. This density score is learned via Noise Contrastive Estimation, effectively leveraging a $(K{+}1)$-way discriminator to estimate density ratios. For black-box settings, we introduce a local proxy distillation mechanism that aligns a lightweight surrogate with the target model strictly within the trajectory of CE generation, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization with minimal queries. Experiments demonstrate that \textit{DensityFlow} achieves superior validity under model multiplicity while significantly reducing query costs compared to ensemble-based baselines. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/G-AILab/DensityFlow.

</details>


### [226] [STEP: Learning STructured Embeddings for Progressive Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31061)
*Lucas Thil,Jesse Read,Rim Kaddah,Guillaume Doquet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-supervised method learns interpretable, geometry-based latent representations for progressive time series by placing observations on a manifold between two orthogonal prototypes; the latent polar coordinates (θ, r) read progression and mode without labels, enabling end-state prediction, multi-step forecasting, and interpretable phase separation, with competitive performance to deep models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To obtain transparent, interpretable representations for irreversible time-series dynamics (e.g., degradation, task completion) without relying on labeled data or proxy supervision.

Method: Train a self-supervised contrastive objective to map observations into a low-dimensional latent space whose geometry is anchored between two fixed orthogonal prototype vectors. Each observation is a point on a manifold between prototypes; a trajectory is a path across the manifold. Extract latent compass coordinates (θ, r) in polar form: θ tracks state progression; r identifies the active mode. No proxy labels are used.

Result: Evaluated on industrial degradation, robotic tasks, and neural activity. Demonstrated three capabilities: (1) end-state prediction, (2) multi-step forecasting, (3) interpretable phase separation. The method matches or improves over black-box counterparts across domains. A simple linear regressor on top of (θ, r) is competitive with deep architectures, indicating the state is encoded in a geometrically accessible form.

Conclusion: The approach yields an interpretable, geometry-grounded latent representation for progressive time series, enabling transparent state interpretation and competitive predictive performance with simpler models, across diverse domains.

Abstract: We present a novel method for learning interpretable representations of progressive time series, that is, data capturing irreversible state transitions such as degradation or task completion. Our approach uses a self-supervised contrastive objective to learn a low-dimensional latent space whose geometry is itself the interpretation: each observation becomes a point on a manifold anchored between two fixed orthogonal prototype vectors, and a trajectory becomes a path across that manifold. From this structure we read a latent compass, the polar coordinates (θ, r) of the latent vector, in which θ tracks the progression of the underlying state (e.g., from healthy to failed) and r identifies the active mode (e.g., the operating condition), without any proxy labels. We evaluate the approach against the state of the art on diverse domains, including industrial degradation, robotic tasks, and neural activity, validating three key capabilities: (1) end-state prediction, (2) multi-step forecasting, and (3) interpretable phase separation. Our method matches or improves over black-box counterparts on all of these while providing transparency about the underlying mechanisms. A simple linear regressor on top of the latent compass coordinates is competitive with deep architectures, direct quantitative evidence that the underlying state is encoded in a geometrically accessible form.

</details>


### [227] [Inverse Reinforcement Learning without an Optimal Demonstrator: A Feasible Reward Set Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30903)
*Kihyun Kim,Shripad Deshmukh,Nikos Vlassis,Jiawei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A feasible-reward-set (FRS) framework for IRL with multiple imperfect demonstrators: each demonstrator adds a linear suboptimality constraint, and the intersection of these sets yields a shrinking feasible reward set. The paper provides monotone shrinking proofs, exact conditions when a new demonstrator tightens the set, two recovery guarantees for the ground-truth reward, and practical offline algorithms with function approximation. Experiments on grid-world and LLM fine-tuning validate theoretical predictions and show improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IRL often presumes one optimal or near-optimal demonstrator, but real data come from multiple imperfect demonstrators with varying suboptimality. There is a need to integrate heterogeneous signals while accounting for suboptimality bounds to recover meaningful reward functions.

Method: Model suboptimality levels as linear constraints on the reward function; intersect feasible reward sets across demonstrators to obtain a joint feasible reward set (FRS). The authors analyze how the FRS shrinks as more data are added, and provide an exact criterion for when a new demonstrator strictly tightens the set. They derive two recovery guarantees for the ground-truth optimal demonstrator: (i) a bound that depends on proximity to the optimal occupancy distribution, and (ii) a bound requiring only sufficient state-action coverage without near-optimal demonstrator. They also propose strategies to resolve reward ambiguity and present an offline algorithm with function approximation for high-dimensional environments.

Result: The joint feasible set shrinks monotonically with added data; there is an exact condition under which a new demonstrator tightens the FRS. The paper establishes two recovery guarantees for recovering the ground-truth reward. Practically, it provides methods to address reward ambiguity and an offline scalable algorithm. Experiments in tabular grid-world and LLM fine-tuning align with theory and show improvements over baselines.

Conclusion: A principled framework for IRL with multiple imperfect demonstrators that yields theoretical guarantees and practical algorithms. The approach systematically leverages suboptimality information to tighten the reward uncertainty and demonstrates empirical effectiveness in both simple and large-scale settings.

Abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) typically assumes demonstrations from a single optimal demonstrator, but in many applications data come from multiple imperfect demonstrators with heterogeneous suboptimality levels. We study reward learning in this setting through a feasible-reward-set framework: for each demonstrator, we encode its declared suboptimality level as a linear constraint and intersect the resulting feasible sets across demonstrators. Our theoretical analysis shows that the joint feasible set shrinks monotonically as data are added, and we give an exact characterization of when a new demonstrator strictly tightens it. We further establish two recovery guarantees for the feasible reward set of the ground-truth optimal demonstrator: one bound depends on closeness to the optimal occupancy, while the other requires only sufficient coverage and no near-optimal demonstrator. On the practical side, we introduce strategies to address the inherent reward ambiguity in the obtained reward set and provide an offline algorithm with function approximation for high-dimensional environments. Experiments in tabular grid-world and large language model (LLM) fine-tuning settings are consistent with the theoretical predictions and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework over baselines.

</details>


### [228] [PINNs Failure Modes are Overfitting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30910)
*Nigel T. Andersen,Takashi Matsubara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINN failures stem from overfitting to collocation points; regularization and double backpropagation over residuals restore robustness, enabling state-of-the-art results with far fewer collocation points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and fix failure modes in PINNs, where low residual on training points does not guarantee a correct solution, hindering their reliability and efficiency.

Method: Visualize residuals to diagnose overfitting; apply regularization to loss; extend double backpropagation to the full residual set to improve gradient information; evaluate on standard failure-mode PDEs.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on four standard failure-mode equations with up to 23x fewer collocation points using a vanilla architecture.

Conclusion: Regularization and comprehensive second-order gradient (double backprop) updates mitigate PINN failure modes, enhancing robustness and sample efficiency without specialized architectures.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a common class of machine learning-based partial differential equation (PDE) solvers which train a network to represent a solution by minimizing a residual loss that encodes the PDE. Despite their successes, they are known to fail on certain simple equations, converging to an incorrect solution despite low loss. These failure modes have garnered significant attention in the literature over the past several years, motivating both architectural and optimization based solutions. By directly visualizing the residual, we show that failure modes are the result of overfitting: the loss is minimized on the collocation points, but not elsewhere. Applying regularization causes the failure modes to vanish. Finally, we extend double backpropagation over the full set of residuals, and use it to achieve state-of-the-art performance on four standard failure mode equations with up to $23\times$ fewer collocation points and a vanilla architecture.

</details>


### [229] [Automating Formal Verification with Reinforcement Learning and Recursive Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30914)
*Max Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR and verifier-guided inference advance LLMs on verified code and proofs; but spec hacking and weak benchmarks limit progress; results vary across datasets; need stronger evaluation and tool-use policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scarcity of data and the demand for machine-checkable correctness in formal verification with LLMs, by improving generation of verified programs and proofs via verifiers.

Method: Dafny: RLVR with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO); assemble candidate programs and score with compiler/verifier outcomes; multi-turn RLVR after filtering underspecified tasks. Lean: verifier-guided inference scaffold treating proof generation as structured search over subgoals, verifier feedback, diagnostics, and repair; fixed base model; VeriCoding pilot; VERINA dataset; Dalek-Bench benchmark derived from curve25519-dalek; preliminary results.

Result: Dafny: verified reward rises from 2.2% to 58.1%, later 31.1% after filtering; Lean: pass rate improves from 46.2% to 69.2% with proof reviser; on VERINA, 7 of 42 solved; Dalek-Bench: preliminary results weak.

Conclusion: Verifier-guided approaches show promise for verified-code and proof generation but face challenges from underspecified specs, spec hacking, and benchmarking; progress is highly dataset-dependent; future work needed on evaluation frameworks and task-specific tool-use policies.

Abstract: Automated formal verification remains challenging for large language models because data for proof assistants and verification-aware languages is scarce, and correctness depends on satisfying precise machine-checkable specifications rather than producing plausible code. This thesis studies how verifier environments can improve LLM generation of verified programs and proofs through reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) and verifier-guided inference-time search. First, we train open-source models in Dafny with RLVR using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and related variants, assembling generated candidates into complete programs and scoring them with compiler and verifier outcomes. Initial experiments on an APPS-derived Dafny dataset increased verified reward from 2.2% to 58.1%, but revealed specification hacking, where models exploit weak formal specifications instead of implementing the intended solutions. After filtering underspecified and vulnerable tasks, multi-turn RLVR on the refined benchmark improves the verified pass rate from 9.7% to 31.1%. Second, we develop a verifier-guided inference scaffold in Lean that treats proof generation as structured search over decomposed subgoals, verifier feedback, diagnostics, and repair. With a fixed base model, the full scaffold with proof reviser improves pass rate on an initial VeriCoding pilot set from 46.2% under direct repair to 69.2%. On the larger VERINA dataset, whole-task decomposition plus proof reviser solves 7 of 42 previously unsolved tasks. We also introduce Dalek-Bench, a repository-scale Lean benchmark derived from the Rust $\texttt{curve25519-dalek}$ verification project; preliminary results remain weak, indicating that stronger progress evaluation and task-specific tool-use policies are still needed.

</details>


### [230] [Spectral Anatomy of Quantum Gaussian Process Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30952)
*Jian Xu,Chao Li,Guang Lin,Yuning Qiu,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The normalized spectral entropy S(K)/log n governs when quantum Gaussian processes perform well or exhibit posterior pathologies, unifying HHL-based speedups and Bayesian-optimality failures; supported by theory (Nyström error tail bound, Bach’s d_sigma variance contraction) and a target-dependent entropy characterization, with kernel-agnostic empirical collapse and hardware demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve seemingly conflicting results about quantum speedups and Bayesian optimization pathologies by identifying a single, universal diagnostic quantity that governs kernel behavior across diverse quantum and classical kernels and hardware.

Method: Theoretical: derive a Cauchy–Schwarz tail bound for Nyström approximation error; establish a finite-sample variance-contraction identity via Bach’s degrees of freedom d_sigma(K); characterize the target-dependent optimal entropy through the intrinsic dimension of the target in the kernel eigenbasis. Empirical: test hardware-efficient, matchgate, IQP, and RBF/Matérn/RFF/deep kernels; perform dequantization, calibration-error (ECE), and variance-contraction analyses; validate the diagnostic on IBM Q (Heron) hardware across configurations (n_q=4, 24 configurations) with no error mitigation, and extend to a second backend and to n_q=6 scale-up.

Result: Across kernel families, S(K)/log n collapses onto identical curves for dequantization, ECE, and variance-contraction diagnostics. The NLL sweet spot occurs at high entropy for smooth targets and at low entropy for band-limited quantum targets. Hardware transfer shows median absolute error ~3.2% and mean ~5.2% in S/log n across 24 configurations at n_q=4, with a single outlier that improves on rerun; similar performance on a second IBM backend and at n_q=6, with mean error ~1.7%. No error mitigation used.

Conclusion: Normalized spectral entropy is a universal, target-aware diagnostic that governs QGP performance and governs when quantum speedups are possible or when posterior pathologies arise; the diagnostic is robust across kernel families and hardware, enabling kernel-agnostic predictions and practical hardware assessments.

Abstract: Two recent results have reshaped quantum Gaussian processes (QGPs). On the one hand, \citet{lowe2025assessing} rule out the exponential speedups claimed by HHL-based QGP regression in the typical, well-conditioned regime; on the other, an independent line of work shows that highly expressive quantum kernels suffer posterior pathologies that break Bayesian optimization. We show that these seemingly unrelated phenomena are governed by the same quantity: the normalized spectral entropy $S(K)/\log n$ of the kernel Gram matrix. We prove a Cauchy--Schwarz tail bound on Nyström approximation error, a finite-sample variance-contraction identity in terms of Bach's degrees of freedom $d_σ(K)$, and a characterization of the \emph{target-dependent} optimal entropy via the intrinsic dimension of the target in the kernel eigenbasis. Empirically, the diagnostic is kernel-agnostic: hardware-efficient, matchgate, IQP \emph{and} RBF/Matérn/RFF/deep-kernel families all collapse onto identical $S/\log n$ curves on dequantization, ECE, and variance-contraction panels. The NLL sweet spot lives at high entropy for smooth targets and at low entropy for band-limited quantum-data targets. The diagnostic transfers from simulator to IBM Heron hardware with median absolute error $3.2\%$ and mean $5.2\%$ in $S/\log n$ across $24$ configurations at $n_q = 4$, with matchgate and IQP within $5\%$ mean and a single HE configuration returning a $30\%$ outlier that drops to $0.5\%$ on rerun (attributed to calibration drift); the same diagnostic transfers to a second Heron backend (mean error $2.7\%$) and to a $n_q = 6$ scale-up on the original backend (mean error $1.7\%$). No error mitigation is applied throughout.

</details>


### [231] [Revisiting Zeroth-Order Hessian Approximation: A Single-Step Policy Optimization Lens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30960)
*Junbin Qiu,Zhaowei Hong,Renzhe Xu,Yao Shu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified framework reframes zeroth-order Hessian estimation as the Hessian of a smoothed policy optimization objective, yielding ZoVH: variance-reduced estimators for the full Hessian, its inverse, and the inverse Hessian–gradient product. Key innovations include an optimally derived baseline to minimize variance and a query-reuse scheme leveraging past queries. The work provides unbiasedness, variance bounds, and convergence guarantees, with strong empirical validation; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional zeroth-order Hessian estimation is noisy and sample-inefficient; existing estimators suffer high variance and poor scalability. A unifying perspective via policy optimization improves theoretical understanding and enables variance-reduction techniques.

Method: Establish equivalence between ZO Hessian estimators and the Hessian of a smoothed Policy Optimization (PO) objective; derive ZoVH—a suite of variance-reduced estimators for the full Hessian, the regularized inverse, and the inverse Hessian–gradient product. Introduce (1) an optimally derived baseline to provably minimize variance, and (2) a query-reuse strategy that leverages historical queries for improved sample efficiency without extra costs. Prove unbiasedness, variance optimality of the baseline, derive error bounds, and provide convergence guarantees for the curvature-aware ZO algorithm. Empirical validation supports theoretical claims; code provided.

Result: Theoretical: unbiased Hessian estimator; variance-optimal baseline; error bounds for ZoVH estimators; convergence guarantees for curvature-aware ZO optimization. Empirical: enhanced Hessian estimation accuracy and faster convergence across real-world tasks; code release.

Conclusion: ZoVH delivers a comprehensive, variance-reduced toolkit for zeroth-order curvature estimation, unifying classical estimators under a policy-optimization lens and delivering practical performance gains; broad applicability to derivative-free optimization tasks. Code available.

Abstract: Accurate Zeroth-Order (ZO) Hessian estimation is a cornerstone of derivative-free methods, essential for tasks such as bilevel optimization, Bayesian inference, and uncertainty quantification. However, obtaining a complete suite of low-variance estimators for the Hessian and its inverse in high-dimensional settings remains a significant challenge. To address this, we propose a unified framework that reinterprets ZO Hessian approximation through the lens of single-step Policy Optimization (PO). This perspective establishes a theoretical equivalence between general ZO Hessian estimators and the Hessian of a smoothed PO objective, unifying distinct classical randomized estimators as specific instances of baseline selection. Building on this foundation, we introduce ZoVH, a comprehensive suite of variance-reduced estimators for the full Hessian matrix, its regularized inverse, and the bias-corrected inverse Hessian-gradient product. ZoVH leverages two key techniques: (1) a unique optimal baseline derived to provably minimize variance, and (2) a query reuse strategy that incorporates historical function queries to enhance sample efficiency without inflating costs. Our rigorous theoretical analysis confirms the unbiasedness of the Hessian estimator, validates the variance optimality of our baseline, provides error bounds for the entire ZoVH suite, and establishes convergence guarantees for the resulting curvature-aware ZO algorithm. Extensive empirical results validate our theoretical findings, demonstrating that ZoVH achieves superior estimation accuracy and convergence performance in real-world applications. Code is available at https://github.com/Qjbtiger/ZoVH

</details>


### [232] [Parallel Tempering Initial Sampling in Inference-Time Reward Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30991)
*Myeongjun Oh,Gwangho Kim,Sungyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG
