TL;DR: PATHS leverages parallel tempering to initialize particles across a ladder of reward-tempered chains, addressing rare high-reward regions and multimodal landscapes in inference-time reward alignment, yielding better alignment on complex prompts without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SMC-based reward alignment initializes from a fixed prior and struggles to sample rare high-reward regions; reward landscapes are often multimodal, leading to mode trapping under finite budgets.

Method: PATHS (PArallel Tempering for High-complexity reward Sampling) couples multiple sampling chains via parallel tempering. It maintains a ladder of reward-tempered chains and periodically performs Metropolis swaps to enable cross-chain exploration, flattening the reward landscape and improving finite-budget sampling of high-reward regions.

Result: PATHS substantially enhances finite-budget exploration of rare, high-reward regions and yields consistent gains in alignment quality, with pronounced improvements on complex prompts in layout-to-image and quantity-aware generation tasks.

Conclusion: By mitigating mode trapping and promoting broader exploration, PATHS provides robust initialization for reward-aware sampling in diffusion/flow models, improving alignment performance without retraining.

Abstract: Inference-time reward alignment steers pretrained diffusion and flow-based generative models to satisfy user-specified rewards without retraining. Recently, Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) has emerged as a powerful framework for this task by iteratively filtering and propagating multiple particles. However, we show that standard SMC-based methods often suffer from poor performance because they initialize particles from a standard prior, whereas high-reward regions in complex reward landscapes are extremely rare. Further, we show that even recent reward-aware initial sampling approaches remain vulnerable to getting trapped in local modes, as complex reward landscapes are often multi-modal. To overcome these limitations, we propose PATHS (PArallel Tempering for High-complexity reward Sampling), a novel initialization method that couples multiple sampling chains through parallel tempering. PATHS maintains a ladder of reward-tempered chains and periodically performs Metropolis swaps, enabling efficient exploration across flattened reward landscapes, thereby mitigating the mode-trapping issues. Our analysis reveals that this mechanism substantially enhances the finite-budget exploration of rare, high-reward regions that are typically challenging to sample. Experiments on layout-to-image and quantity-aware generation show that PATHS achieves consistent gains in alignment quality, particularly on complex prompts.

</details>


### [233] [What changes after deployment? A survey on On-device Learning in TinyML](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31226)
*Massimo Pavan,Luca Pezzarossa,Fabrizio Pittorino,Manuel Roveri,Xenofon Fafoutis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Survey of ~70 TinyML on-device learning works categorized by distribution-change regimes; analyzes influence on on-device applicability, hardware, and solution architectures; identifies gap between benchmarks and real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-deployment distribution shift breaks static MCU models; on-device learning aims to adapt without cloud access; literature lacks a regime-based characterization linking change type to solutions; need guidance for real-world deployment.

Method: Systematic survey of ODL literature (≈70 works), organized around distribution-change regimes; analyzes how change type affects applicable applications, hardware platforms, and architectural patterns of proposed methods; synthesizes insights and identifies gaps.

Result: Distribution-change regime strongly informs what can be addressed on-device, the hardware used, and the structure of learning solutions; the regime-based lens yields a unified taxonomy across diverse ODL approaches; a persistent gap exists between benchmark-style evaluation and real-world deployment.

Conclusion: A regime-centric view clarifies the landscape of on-device learning under distribution change and highlights the need for benchmarks and deployments that reflect real-world constraints.

Abstract: Machine learning models on microcontroller-class devices (TinyML) face a fundamental challenge: post-deployment distribution change undermines static models. On-device learning (ODL) addresses this by running the learning process directly on the device. The existing literature has not characterized how distribution change occurs or how different change types require different solutions. Approximately 70 ODL works are surveyed under one principle: the distribution change regime. The survey analyzes how different types of distribution change influence the applications addressable on-device, the hardware employed, and the structure of the solutions. A persistent gap between methodological benchmarks and real-world deployment scenarios is also identified.

</details>


### [234] [EchoRL: Reinforcement Learning via Rollout Echoing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31228)
*Jinhe Bi,Aniri,Minglai Yang,Xingcheng Zhou,Wenke Huang,Sikuan Yan,Yujun Wang,Zixuan Cao,Michael Färber,Xun Xiao,Volker Tresp,Yunpu Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EchoRL enhances RLVR post-training by extracting an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts using step-level entropy and using it as auxiliary supervision, addressing policy-gradient vanishing due to advantage degeneration with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle gradient collapse in RLVR caused by advantage-degenerated rollouts where most self-generated rollouts appear verified-success, thereby zeroing advantages and shrinking learning signals. The authors hypothesize that valuable signals remain in such rollouts and can be harnessed by leveraging entropy patterns observed in expert trajectories.

Method: Identify an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts based on step-level entropy values and incorporate this clip as auxiliary supervision in the RL objective, resulting in a lightweight augmentation that reuses degenerate rollouts without significant computational cost.

Result: Across 10 benchmarks, 5 LLM backbones, and 4 RLVR post-training methods, EchoRL consistently improves RLVR post-training with minimal overhead.

Conclusion: EchoRL provides a lightweight, effective mechanism to exploit advantage-degenerated rollouts, boosting training performance in RLVR post-training.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards is an effective route for post-training to strengthen the reasoning capability of large language models. However, as training proceeds, the learning signal can collapse thus makes the training gain become marginal and ineffective. Specifically, a growing fraction of prompts' rollouts become advantage-degenerated: all the self-generated rollouts show verified-success, making the standard deviation over their rewards be zero; accordingly each rollout's advantage becomes degenerated (zero) as well. Given such rollouts' advantages, the policy-gradient for model optimization eventually vanishes, capping the training performance. We argue that some of these rollouts still contain valuable learning signals but unfortunately omitted with the existing RLVR methods. In this paper, inspired through analyzing the entropy pattern behind golden trajectories produced by external expert models, we propose EchoRL for better exploiting the advantage-degenerated rollouts to further improve the training performance. EchoRL is a lightweight module that first identifies an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts based on their step-level entropy values, and then feeds this clip back as an auxiliary supervision signal in the RL objective. Extensive experiments across 10 benchmarks, 5 LLM backbones, and 4 popular RLVR post-training methods demonstrate that EchoRL consistently improves RLVR post-training with minimal overhead.

</details>


### [235] [Learning Multi-Agent Coordination via Sheaf-ADMM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31005)
*Jeffrey Seely,Bartłomiej Cupiał,Llion Jones*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Differentiable ADMM-based multi-agent coordination using cellular sheaves for heterogeneous global consensus; end-to-end training via unrolled optimization; demonstrates improved robustness and solve rates; offers interpretable primal/consensus/dual dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between local, overlapping views and global coherence in multi-agent systems by enabling end-to-end trainable optimization with flexible consensus that can vary across agents and tasks.

Method: Decompose input into overlapping local views processed by agents solving convex subproblems parameterized by neural encoders. Agents coordinate via ADMM with inter-agent constraints defined by a cellular sheaf, enabling heterogeneous global consensus. Backpropagate through the unrolled optimization to train all components jointly.

Result: Empirical evaluation on maze pathfinding, image classification, Sudoku shows coordination emergent from local views producing correct global outputs. MNIST experiments indicate improved robustness to distribution shifts over standard CNNs. Sudoku achieves higher solve rates than parameter-matched MPNN baselines. The ADMM structure reveals distinct primal, consensus, and dual states enabling direct analysis and intervention, unlike standard message-passing architectures.

Conclusion: The proposed differentiable ADMM framework with a cellular sheaf provides flexible, analyzable coordination in multi-agent systems, achieving strong performance gains and enabling direct intervention on coordination dynamics.

Abstract: We present a differentiable optimization framework for multi-agent coordination. An input is decomposed into overlapping local views, each processed by an agent that solves a convex subproblem parameterized by a neural encoder. Agents coordinate through the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) with inter-agent constraints specified by a cellular sheaf. The sheaf specifies which aspects of neighboring solutions must agree, allowing for heterogeneous notions of global consensus. Backpropagating through the unrolled optimization jointly trains all components of the multi-agent system. We evaluate on maze pathfinding, image classification, and Sudoku, where agents with individually insufficient local views learn to coordinate to produce correct global outputs. On MNIST, the local-view decomposition yields improved robustness to distribution shifts relative to a standard CNN. On Sudoku, the optimization-derived structure yields markedly higher solve rates than parameter-matched MPNN baselines. Finally, the ADMM structure exposes distinct primal, consensus, and dual state variables, opening the coordination dynamics to direct analysis and intervention -- a property unavailable in standard message-passing architectures.

</details>


### [236] [Learning Cardiac Latent Representations in Vectorcardiogram Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31249)
*Bosong Huang,Panzhen Zhao,Zengxiang Li,Patricia Lee,Wei Jin,Alan Wee-Chung Liew,Ming Jin,Shirui Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First general self-supervised framework for ECG in Vectorcardiogram (VCG) space; LVCG learns view-invariant latent representations from the physical ECG signal, reducing redundancy and improving generalization, especially under domain shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ECG comprises multiple leads that are redundant projections of the same underlying cardiac activity. This redundancy can cause spurious correlations and overfitting when learning in the ECG signal space. A physically grounded latent space via the Frank vectorcardiogram (VCG) model should yield more compact, robust representations and better generalization across domains.

Method: Propose LVCG, a self-supervised representation learning framework that operates directly in the latent VCG space. It learns view-invariant latent VCG representations by aligning different lead projections into a single canonical VCG latent space, reducing lead-specific artifacts and redundancy.

Result: Empirically, LVCG outperforms ECG-space baselines across tasks and shows enhanced robustness and generalization, particularly under domain shift conditions.

Conclusion: Learning representations in the physically grounded VCG space reduces redundancy inherent in multi-lead ECG data and improves generalization, suggesting stronger transferability for downstream tasks such as diagnosis and clinical report generation.

Abstract: Electrocardiography (ECG) is a cornerstone of cardiac assessment, making the learning of informative ECG representations fundamental to tasks ranging from disease diagnosis to clinical report generation. However, existing methods operate almost exclusively in the observable ECG signal space. In practice, the standard twelve-lead ECG represents multiple projections of the same underlying cardiac electrical activity from different spatial orientations. Therefore, representation learning in the ECG space inevitably introduces substantial redundancy, which may lead to spurious correlations and increased risk of overfitting. To address this and motivated by the Frank vectorcardiogram (VCG) model, we propose learning a unified latent representation of cardiac electrical activity directly in the VCG space. We introduce LVCG, the first general self-supervised representation learning framework designed to operate in this physically grounded latent space. By learning view-invariant latent VCG representations rather than lead-specific artifacts, VCG minimizes redundancy and improves generalization. LVCG generally outperforms ECG-space baselines across tasks, demonstrating enhanced robustness and generalization, especially in domain shift settings.

</details>


### [237] [Augmented Lagrangian Predictive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31022)
*Jeffrey Seely,Julian Gould*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Augmented Lagrangian Predictive Coding (PC-ALM) enhances Predictive Coding by adding per-layer Lagrange multipliers that accumulate constraint errors, aligning local updates with backpropagation (BP) so BP gradients emerge via layer-local updates. In linear PC, PC-ALM converges to an equilibrium with exact BP gradients; in nonlinear networks up to depth 128, it matches BP performance across width-depth regimes, including deep narrow networks where PC struggles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the difficulty of obtaining BP-like credit signals with purely local, distributed updates in predictive coding, especially for deep networks, by embedding an augmented Lagrangian framework that preserves PC’s local inference while steering updates toward BP gradients.

Method: Introduce augmented Lagrangian for PC, maintain layer-local constraint errors, accumulate them into layer-local multipliers, and analyze both linear and nonlinear PC networks up to depth 128. Compare energy-dynamics: PC uses a scalar energy diffusion (heat flow); PC-ALM uses dual ascent on the augmented Lagrangian and yields recurrent per-layer activations. Assess convergence to BP gradients and performance across width-depth regimes.

Result: In linear PC networks, PC-ALM converges to an equilibrium with exact BP gradients distributed across layers via local updates. In nonlinear PC networks (depth up to 128), PC-ALM matches BP performance across width-depth regimes, notably correcting PC’s failures in deep narrow networks. Dynamics show ballistic credit propagation with signals evenly distributed across layers, unlike PC’s slow, diffusive propagation.

Conclusion: The augmented Lagrangian framework generalizes PC and can yield BP-like credit signaling through purely local dynamics, offering a potential path to distributed, scalable training of deep networks without a global backward pass.

Abstract: Predictive coding (PC) is a local-learning alternative to backpropagation (BP), training deep networks via local energy-minimization dynamics rather than a global backward pass. We introduce Augmented Lagrangian Predictive Coding (PC-ALM), which maintains PC's inference budget but aligns each weight update toward BP by accumulating per-layer constraint errors into a layer-local Lagrange multiplier. In linear PC networks, PC-ALM converges to an equilibrium with exact BP gradients distributed across the network via only layer-local updates. We analyze PC-ALM in nonlinear PC networks up to depth 128 and show that it matches BP performance across all width-depth regimes, notably in deep narrow networks where PC underperforms. PC-ALM introduces recurrent dynamics in each layer's activations. Compared to PC's heat flow on a scalar energy, PC-ALM dynamics are driven by dual ascent on the augmented Lagrangian. We observe "ballistic" credit propagation across very deep networks, with credit signals evenly distributed across layers, compared to PC's slow, diffusive credit propagation. Beyond the algorithm itself, the augmented Lagrangian framework offers a generalization of PC, and may yield insights into how distributed systems could compute and propagate BP-like credit signals through purely local dynamics.

</details>


### [238] [Multi-Scale Separable Fourier Neural Networks for Solving High-Frequency PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31027)
*Qihong Yang,Qiaolin He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MS-SFNN is a fixed-weight, randomized, separable Fourier neural network for high-frequency PDEs that uses least-squares to combine basis functions formed from per-coordinate subsections, with analytic derivatives and memory-efficient QR, achieving superior accuracy to PINN and SV-SNN.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of solving high-frequency linear and nonlinear PDEs efficiently and accurately with neural methods, by reducing memory usage, increasing spectral expressivity, and avoiding expensive training of weights.

Method: Construct d independent subnetworks (one per coordinate). Outputs are multiplied element-wise to form basis functions. Solutions are a linear combination of basis functions with coefficients solved via least squares. Weights/biases are randomly initialized and fixed. Introduce per-subnetwork frequency scaling; embed Fourier features via cosine activations; use analytically derived derivatives instead of automatic differentiation; apply memory-efficient batched QR for LS. Evaluate on challenging PDEs against PINN and SV-SNN.

Result: Empirical results claim unprecedented accuracy across a range of linear and nonlinear high-frequency PDEs, significantly outperforming PINN and SV-SNN.

Conclusion: MS-SFNN provides a scalable, memory-efficient, fixed-parameter neural framework with strong spectral capabilities for high-frequency PDEs, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy relative to contemporary neural PDE solvers.

Abstract: We propose a novel neural network architecture, termed Multi-Scale Separable Fourier Neural Networks (MS-SFNN), for the accurate and efficient solution of linear and nonlinear high-frequency partial differential equations (PDEs). MS-SFNN exploits a separable representation: given a $d$-dimensional input, it employs $d$ independent subnetworks -- each acting on a single coordinate -- and constructs basis functions via element-wise multiplication of their outputs. The PDE solution is approximated as a linear combination of these basis functions, with coefficients determined by least squares. Critically, all network weights and biases are randomly initialized once, from a uniform distribution with unit variance, and remain fixed thereafter. To enhance expressivity, a tunable scaling factor is introduced in each subnetwork to modulate the frequency content of the resulting basis functions. Fourier features are explicitly embedded through cosine activations, endowing the method with strong spectral approximation capabilities. To mitigate the memory bottleneck associated with dense collocation in high-frequency or three-dimensional problems, we replace automatic differentiation with analytically derived basis function derivatives and develop a memory-efficient batched QR decomposition algorithm for solving large-scale least-squares systems. Numerical experiments demonstrate that MS-SFNN achieves unprecedented accuracy across a range of challenging PDEs, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods such as Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN) and Separated-Variable Spectral Neural Networks (SV-SNN).

</details>


### [239] [Annealed Softmax Greedy in Many-Armed Bayesian Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31034)
*William Overman,Mohsen Bayati*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Annealed softmax over a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit yields near-optimal Bayes regret under β-regularity, explaining why uncertainty-agnostic RL updates can work. With m ~ √T arms, regret scales as Ť√T; many near-optimal arms underpin robust performance, unlike very small arm sets where regret can be linear. The work draws a structural analogy to RLVR where a base policy has nonzero probability of correct completions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a theoretical explanation for why uncertainty-agnostic RL updates (e.g., RLVR/GRPO) can be effective by analyzing a stylized model that captures the role of near-optimal arms and exploration via softmax under a Bayesian prior that promotes β-regularity.

Method: Analyze an annealed softmax (Boltzmann) policy in a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit with a linear upper-tail prior (β=1, β-regularity). Derive Bayes regret bounds for the policy, showing that many near-optimal arms lead to favorable regret; compare to empirical-mean greedy.

Result: Bayes regret bound of Ť(m + T/m); in particular Ť(√T) when m = Θ(√T). This is near-optimal in this regime and matches empirical-mean greedy. Under β-regularity, many arms stay near the optimum, so sampling a non-best arm tends to be another near-optimal arm. With few arms, softmax can yield linear regret. The analysis provides a structural analogy to RLVR.

Conclusion: The analysis suggests that uncertainty-agnostic updates can be effective when the hypothesis class contains many near-optimal arms (β-regularity); the mechanism is analogous to RLVR where a base policy has non-negligible probability of producing correct completions, supporting why such updates can work despite lacking explicit epistemic uncertainty tracking.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and group-based policy optimization methods such as GRPO update a stochastic policy by sampling multiple completions per prompt and increasing the policy's probability on those with higher reward, regularized by a KL penalty toward a reference policy. These updates do not include explicit mechanisms that track epistemic uncertainty. This paper studies a stylized explanation for why such uncertainty-agnostic updates can nevertheless be effective. We analyze an annealed softmax (Boltzmann) policy that selects actions according to a softmax of empirical mean rewards in a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit. Under a linear upper-tail condition on the prior (the $β=1$ case of $β$-regularity), which implies an abundance of near-optimal arms, we prove that annealed softmax greedy achieves Bayes regret $\tilde{O}(m + T/m)$, and in particular $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ when the number of arms scales as $m = Θ(\sqrt{T})$. This is the near-optimal Bayes regret rate in this regime, attained also by empirical-mean greedy. Under $β$-regularity, many arms maintain empirical means close to the optimum throughout learning, so when softmax samples an arm other than the empirically best, that arm tends to be another near-optimal one rather than a clearly inferior one. By contrast, with a small number of arms, the same kind of softmax policy can suffer linear regret. The result also provides a structural analogy to RLVR, where a base policy with a non-negligible probability of producing a correct completion plays the role of $β$-regularity.

</details>


### [240] [Learning to Solve and Optimize by Evolving Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31049)
*Veronika Semmelrock,Benedetta Strizzolo,Francesco Zuccato,Gerhard Friedrich,Patrick Rodler,Konstantin Schekotihin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Algorithm generation via code evolution guided by formal specifications and NL prompts; CHECKMATE; outperforms state-of-the-art solvers on configuration and scheduling problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on expert-crafted heuristics by shifting focus from 'how' to 'what'; enable automatic generation of correct and performant solvers.

Method: Code evolution (likely genetic programming) evolves solver programs under a formal correctness specification; natural-language descriptions steer the search; applied to industrial configuration and scheduling problems.

Result: Evolved algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art solvers on the tested problems.

Conclusion: Formal methods + program evolution can guide automatic generation of effective solvers for complex real-world combinatorial problems.

Abstract: Combinatorial and optimization problems are fundamental to many industrial AI applications. Solving large-scale real-world instances of such problems typically requires careful problem formalization, specialized solvers, and expert-designed heuristics. Thus, experts need to specify not only what solutions are, but also how they are derived. By introducing the tool CHECKMATE, we show that algorithm generation via code evolution represents a paradigm shift by eliminating the need to formulate the how. CHECKMATE solely relies on the what. Specifically, a formal specification ensures solutions' correctness and enables systematic performance evaluation of the generated programs, while a natural language description guides the evolutionary process. The effectiveness of our method is demonstrated on selected problems from two industrial domains: configuration and scheduling. In all cases, the evolved algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art solvers. This underscores the potential of formal methods in guiding code evolution for automatically solving complex real-world problems.

</details>


### [241] [Scaling Higher-Order Graph Learning with Maximal Clique Complexes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31373)
*Antoine Vialle,Aref Einizade,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros,Jhony H. Giraldo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces simplified and factored cellular WL tests (sCWL and fCWL) and a maximal clique complex to enable scalable higher-order graph learning; also proposes CliqueWalk, a biased random-walk sampler for maximal cliques, yielding linear-time scaling and strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs model only pairwise interactions; higher-order methods via cellular WL are more expressive but suffer from poor scalability. The work aims to balance expressivity with computational efficiency and enable scalable topological learning.

Method: Define sCWL and fCWL to preserve the expressivity of the cellular WL test while improving efficiency; introduce the maximal clique complex to support scalable higher-order graph representations; develop CliqueWalk, a biased random walk that samples maximal cliques without explicit enumeration, achieving linear scaling with graph size.

Result: A scalable CWN framework is proposed that maintains strong empirical performance while reducing time/memory complexity; CliqueWalk enables sampling of maximal cliques without enumeration and scales linearly with graph size.

Conclusion: The paper presents a scalable topological learning framework for higher-order graph representation, combining expressivity-preserving tests (sCWL/fCWL), a maximal clique complex, and an efficient clique sampling method (CliqueWalk) to achieve practical scalability.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are limited to modeling pairwise interactions, while higher-order models based on cell complexes achieve greater expressivity but often suffer from poor scalability. We introduce simplified and factored cellular Weisfeiler Leman tests (sCWL and fCWL), which preserve the expressivity of the CWL test while improving computational efficiency. We further introduce the maximal clique complex, enabling scalable CWNs with reduced time and memory complexity while retaining strong empirical performance. To avoid explicit clique enumeration, we propose CliqueWalk, a biased random walk that samples maximal cliques and scales linearly with graph size. These contributions yield a scalable topological learning framework for higher-order graph representation.

</details>


### [242] [Best-Arm Identification-Based Trust Region Selection for Bayesian Optimization on Multimodal Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31050)
*Nobuo Namura,Sho Takemori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Trajectory-aware, BAI-guided trust-region Bayesian optimization for multimodal problems, using extrapolated trajectories to prune suboptimal local optimizers, achieving faster convergence to the global optimum.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian optimization (BO) struggles with multimodal and high-dimensional objectives. Trust-region techniques focus search locally to improve efficiency; selecting the best region can be cast as a multi-armed bandit (MAB) problem.

Method: Maintain multiple locally initialized optimizers within overlapping trust regions; extrapolate their optimization trajectories to predict final outcomes; apply best-arm identification (BAI) to progressively discard suboptimal candidates; integrate BAI with trust-region BO to guide search.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows that BAI-guided BO converges faster to the global optimum under mild assumptions; empirical experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate improved efficiency and competitive performance.

Conclusion: A trajectory-aware BAI-guided trust-region BO framework effectively handles multimodality, offering faster convergence and robustness compared to conventional BO.

Abstract: Gaussian process-based Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular approach for expensive black-box optimization, but its performance often degrades on complex multimodal or high-dimensional problems. Trust region-based BO mitigates this issue by focusing on local regions, and recent studies suggest that selecting an effective region can be formulated as a multi-armed bandit problem. We propose a trajectory-aware framework that integrates best-arm identification (BAI) with trust region-based BO to efficiently solve multimodal optimization problems. Our method extrapolates the optimization trajectories of multiple locally initialized optimizers to predict their final performance and progressively eliminates suboptimal candidates via BAI. We theoretically show that the proposed BAI-guided BO converges faster to the global optimum than conventional BO under mild assumptions, and demonstrate its effectiveness through extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.

</details>


### [243] [PithTrain: A Compact and Agent-Native MoE Training System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31463)
*Ruihang Lai,Hao Kang,Haozhan Tang,Akaash R. Parthasarathy,Zichun Yu,Junru Shao,Todd C. Mowry,Chenyan Xiong,Tianqi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Agent-native MoE training framework PithTrain and ATE-Bench benchmark to measure agent-task efficiency; achieves competitive throughput and reduced agent overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models require scalable, production-grade training stacks. Evolving these stacks with AI coding agents introduces hidden costs not captured by throughput alone. A new metric—agent-task efficiency (ATE)—is needed. The work presents a compact, agent-native framework and benchmark to study this dimension.

Method: Design four agent-native design principles; implement PithTrain as a compact, agent-native MoE training framework; develop ATE-Bench to cover real-world training-framework tasks; evaluate throughput and agent-task efficiency.

Result: PithTrain matches the throughput of production frameworks; on ATE-Bench, it enables higher agent-task efficiency with up to 62% fewer Agent Turns and 64% less Active GPU Time.

Conclusion: Agent-native design and ATE benchmarking offer a holistic approach to ML-framework development with coding agents, enabling competitive performance while reducing agent overhead; PithTrain demonstrates viability of the approach.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the dominant architecture for frontier language models. To meet this demand, production frameworks have built optimized MoE training stacks over years of engineering effort. Yet evolving these stacks for new architectures and system optimizations remains expensive. With the rise of AI coding agents, they could automate parts of training-framework development and accelerate this evolution. But applying them to these existing frameworks carries hidden costs, invisible to today's throughput-only evaluations. We name this missing dimension agent-task efficiency (ATE): the cost of using coding agents to understand, operate, and extend a framework. Grounded in four agent-native design principles, we build PithTrain, a compact, agent-native MoE training framework. We further introduce ATE-Bench, covering real-world training-framework tasks. Our evaluation shows PithTrain matches the throughput of production frameworks, and on ATE-Bench, PithTrain enables higher agent-task efficiency, with up to 62% fewer Agent Turns and 64% less Active GPU Time.

</details>


### [244] [GPU Forecasters: Language Models as Selective Surrogates for Kernel Runtime Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31464)
*Zaid Khan,Justin Chih-Yao Chen,Jaemin Cho,Elias Stengel-Eskin,Mohit Bansal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs can accurately forecast GPU kernel performance and, with reinforcement learning, improve forecast accuracy and calibration, serving as selective surrogates to enable larger kernel searches within the same GPU budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GPU kernel evaluations are expensive due to compilation and execution overhead; a cheap, accurate surrogate that knows when to defer to real hardware is highly desirable for efficient kernel optimization.

Method: Forecast relative kernel performance using LLMs; assess accuracy and calibration of forecasts; study reinforcement learning to improve forecast accuracy and confidence calibration; integrate the surrogate into a kernel search to evaluate more candidates under a fixed GPU budget.

Result: LLMs provide accurate forecasts of relative kernel performance; reinforcement learning improves forecast accuracy and confidence calibration; using the surrogate within kernel search enables evaluating more candidates per budget and yields faster kernels compared to an equal-budget baseline.

Conclusion: LLMs can act as virtual models of a GPU, not just kernel generators, broadening their role in accelerator optimization by enabling selective, calibrated surrogate evaluations that improve kernel search efficiency.

Abstract: GPU kernels are the workhorse of modern deep learning, and optimizing them (via evolutionary search or coding agents) usually requires repeated measurement on target hardware. While these measurements provide the ground-truth signal necessary for kernel search, they are costly, because each evaluation of a kernel requires compilation and repeated execution on a GPU. As improvements in LLM inference reduce the cost of writing novel kernels and LLM-driven searches scale to large search budgets, on-device evaluation becomes a bottleneck. To address this, we study how LLMs can serve as selective GPU surrogates for kernel evaluation, by forecasting the performance of proposed kernels. A useful surrogate should be accurate, and it should be selective, by knowing when it could be wrong, and deferring to the GPU. To evaluate surrogates, we measure whether their forecasts are accurate, calibrated, and practically useful for recovering fast kernels under limited GPU-measurement budgets. Next, we study whether reinforcement learning can improve forecast accuracy and confidence calibration. Our experiments demonstrate that LLMs can accurately forecast relative kernel performance, that their utility can be improved through reinforcement learning. Used inside a kernel search, the surrogate lets the search consider several times as many candidates under the same GPU evaluation budget, and that leads to finding faster kernels than an equal-budget baseline. These results suggest that LLMs can play a broader role in kernel optimization, by acting as virtual models of a GPU rather than solely as kernel generators for search.

</details>


### [245] [Scalable Bayesian Inference for Nonlinear Conservation Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31127)
*Tim Weiland,Philipp Hennig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A numerically conservative, uncertainty-aware solver for nonlinear conservation laws using Gaussian-process priors and sparse approximations; achieves accurate forward simulations with calibrated uncertainty and fast inverse inference of nonparametric source fields, outperforming neural baselines in speed and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty in nonlinear conservation laws due to sparse/noisy measurements leads to ill-posed inverse problems; existing solvers and neural methods struggle to treat uncertainty in a physics-consistent way.

Method: Recast classical numerical solvers as Bayesian inference with Gaussian process priors to enforce physics; introduce a numerically conservative scheme; apply sparse approximations to scale to large forward and inverse problems; for forward problems output calibrated uncertainties; for inverse problems compute posteriors over nonparametric sources in seconds.

Result: Forward simulations retain accuracy of classical solvers with structured uncertainty; inverse posteriors computed rapidly (seconds) with improved accuracy over neural baselines which are slower and less accurate.

Conclusion: This framework provides a scalable, physics-informed probabilistic approach for nonlinear conservation laws, enabling efficient uncertainty quantification in forward and rapid Bayesian inversion of source terms.

Abstract: Nonlinear conservation laws are at the heart of many of the most important dynamical systems in science and engineering. In practical applications, such systems are often subject to various sources of uncertainty, e.g. due to sparse or noisy measurements. Inferring physical quantities and fields of interest then becomes an ill-posed problem which both classical numerical methods and modern deep learning-based methods struggle to treat appropriately. Recent work has framed classical numerical methods as Bayesian inference under Gaussian process priors, resulting in a physics-aware treatment of uncertainties. Following this line of work, we develop a novel numerically conservative method for uncertainty-aware simulations of nonlinear conservation laws. We use recent sparse approximation techniques to scale up to large-scale forward and inverse problems. For forward simulation, we inherit the accuracy of classical solvers while providing structured uncertainty quantification. On inverse problems, we recover posteriors over nonparametric source fields in seconds -- outperforming neural baselines that take minutes to produce a less accurate point estimate.

</details>


### [246] [Generalizing Multi-Scale Time-Series Modeling with a Single Operator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31129)
*Cheonwoo Lee,Dooho Lee,Doyun Choi,Jaemin Yoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SiGMA introduces a learnable discrete Gaussian (LDG) kernel to enable distance-aware, generalized multi-scale scaling for time-series forecasting, unifying prior scaling methods. It achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on short- and long-term benchmarks while delivering large speedups and memory savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-scale time-series models rely on fixed, discrete scaling, which lacks principled flexibility. A unified, principled scaling framework grounded in scale-space theory can enable distance-aware, learnable scaling across scales.

Method: Propose SiGMA (Single Generalized Multi-scale Architecture) that uses a learnable discrete Gaussian (LDG) kernel to implement distance-aware, generalized scaling within a scale-space-inspired framework. Integrate this operator into a forecasting model, train end-to-end, and evaluate on long- and short-term forecasting benchmarks against state-of-the-art multi-scale baselines; release code.

Result: SiGMA outperforms all baselines on both short- and long-term forecasting tasks; achieves the best performance in 13 of 16 long-term evaluation settings. It also delivers substantial efficiency gains: training speed up to 5.3x and memory reduction up to 3.8x compared to strongest competitors.

Conclusion: LDG-based distance-aware scaling within a generalized multi-scale framework yields superior accuracy and efficiency for time-series forecasting, validating the principled use of scale-space-inspired operators. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: Multi-scale modeling has emerged as an effective design principle for time-series forecasting by capturing temporal dynamics at multiple resolutions. As no principled foundation has been established in the literature, we unify existing scaling methods into a scaling operator family, revealing a fundamental limitation of existing approaches: reliance on fixed and discrete scaling. To address this limitation, we propose SiGMA (Single Generalized Multi-scale Architecture), which enables distance-aware scaling via the learnable discrete Gaussian (LDG) kernel grounded in scale-space theory. We evaluate SiGMA comprehensively on long- and short-term forecasting benchmarks against state-of-the-art multi-scale baselines. SiGMA outperforms all competitors on both tasks, especially achieving the best performance in 13 out of 16 long-term evaluation settings. Beyond accuracy, SiGMA significantly improves training speed by up to 5.3 times and reduces memory consumption by up to 3.8 times over the strongest competitors. Code is available at https://github.com/cheonwoolee/SiGMA.

</details>


### [247] [Positional versus Symbolic Attention Heads: Learning Dynamics, RoPE Geometry, and Length Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31558)
*Felipe Urrutia,Juan José Alegría,Cinthia Sanchez Macias,Jorge Salas,Cristian B. Calderon,Cristobal Rojas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shows that decoder-only transformers learning on two structurally equivalent multi-hop tasks develop pure attention heads that are either positional or symbolic. Despite equivalence, tasks demand different mixes of head types; symbolic heads extrapolate more reliably to longer sequences, while positional heads struggle. The paper provides geometric/ROPE-based constructions for these functions and defines a discrepancy metric to quantify robustness across sequence lengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the internal mechanisms by which Transformers solve structured tasks, differentiate positional and symbolic reasoning in attention heads, and evaluate robustness to longer sequences for safe deployment.

Method: Train a GPT-J decoder on two structurally equivalent multi-hop tasks (a number task for positional reasoning and a letter task for symbolic reasoning). Use a metric to classify attention-head behavior as positional or symbolic per prompt. Analyze emergence of pure heads, identify computational roles, and provide theoretical constructions showing RoPE-based attention can realize these functions via query/key/value operations. Introduce a discrepancy measure for robustness to sequence length, and validate predictions in controlled and real-world models.

Result: Learning coincides with the emergence of pure heads. The number task needs both positional and symbolic heads, while the letter task relies on symbolic heads alone. The authors map Head functions to geometrically interpretable RoPE-based operations and show a quantitative separation in robustness to longer sequences: symbolic heads extrapolate better, positional heads have tighter limits. They validate predictions empirically in both controlled and real-world models.

Conclusion: There exists a quantitative separation between positional and symbolic mechanisms in robustness to sequence length. Symbolic mechanisms offer more reliable extrapolation to longer sequences, while positional mechanisms exhibit sharper limitations. The work provides a mechanistic account of how single-layer RoPE-based attention can implement these functions and offers a framework for interpreting and predicting Transformer behavior on structured tasks.

Abstract: Transformer-based language models are widespread in today's society. As such, understanding the mechanisms by which they solve structured tasks and predicting how they may behave in novel scenarios is of great importance for safe deployment. We study the learning dynamics of attention heads in a controlled setting by training a decoder-only Transformer (GPT-J) on two structurally equivalent multi-hop reasoning tasks: a number task requiring positional reasoning and a letter task requiring symbolic reasoning. Using a recently introduced metric that classifies attention-head behavior as positional or symbolic for a given prompt, we show that successful learning is associated with the emergence of pure heads, i.e., heads that express themselves as either positional or symbolic. Despite the tasks' structural equivalence, they impose different mechanistic demands: the number task requires both positional and symbolic heads, whereas the letter task requires only symbolic heads. We then identify the computational roles of these heads, characterize the basic functions they implement, and give theoretical constructions showing how single-layer RoPE-based attention can realize these functions through geometrically interpretable query, key, and value operations. This analysis yields a quantitative separation between positional and symbolic mechanisms in their robustness to longer sequences, formalized through a novel notion of discrepancy. We empirically validate the resulting predictions in both controlled and real-world models, showing that symbolic mechanisms extrapolate more reliably to longer sequences while positional mechanisms face sharper limitations.

</details>


### [248] [TabCausal: Pretraining Across Causal Environments for Tabular Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31156)
*Zi-Rong Li,Si-Yang Liu,Tian-Zuo Wang,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes TabCausal, a data-driven Causal Discovery Foundation Model trained via broad causal pretraining across diverse environments and a dynamic task construction strategy; demonstrates superior macro-averaged performance and robust structure recovery, especially with interventional data, on synthetic and semantic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of existing CDFMs, notably the bottleneck in constructing effective causal pretraining tasks, and the gap between synthetic generators and realistic causal reasoning. Aim for transferable, amortized causal discovery.

Method: Train TabCausal with broad causal pretraining over diverse graph priors, structural mechanisms, noise models, dimensions, sample sizes, and intervention regimes. Use a dynamic task construction strategy to compose diverse discovery tasks with observational and mixed-interventional data. Introduce a protocol-guided, LLM-audited semantic causal environment benchmark using domain-grounded SCMs for OOD analysis.

Result: TabCausal achieves better macro-averaged performance than diverse causal discovery baselines on large-scale synthetic benchmarks. Demonstrates robust structure recovery across both synthetic and semantic environments, with particular strength under interventional evidence. Semantic benchmark aids bridging synthetic generators and realistic reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Broad causal pretraining over diverse causal environments is a key ingredient for transferable amortized causal discovery, enabling robust structure recovery and improved generalization beyond synthetic data.

Abstract: Causal discovery aims to recover directed causal relations from observational and interventional data, providing a basis for mechanistic understanding and reliable decision-making. Causal discovery foundation models (CDFMs) seek to amortize this problem by mapping a dataset directly to a causal graph in a single forward pass, avoiding per-dataset testing, search, or optimization. However, existing CDFMs remain limited, often failing to consistently match strong classical methods, and we find that a key bottleneck is how causal pretraining tasks are constructed. Based on this observation, we propose TabCausal, a data-driven CDFM trained with broad causal pretraining over diverse graph priors, structural mechanisms, noise models, dimensions, sample sizes, and intervention regimes. A dynamic task construction strategy composes these causal environments into varied discovery tasks, enabling more transferable structural learning from observational and mixed-interventional data. On large-scale synthetic benchmarks, TabCausal achieves better macro-averaged performance than a diverse set of causal discovery baselines. To further bridge abstract synthetic generators and realistic causal reasoning scenarios, we introduce a protocol-guided and LLM-audited semantic causal environment benchmark, where domain-grounded SCMs generate interpretable observational and interventional datasets for out-of-distribution analysis. Across both synthetic and semantic environments, TabCausal demonstrates robust structure recovery, especially under interventional evidence, highlighting broad causal pretraining as a key ingredient for transferable amortized causal discovery.

</details>


### [249] [Convergence of Two-Timescale Markovian Stochastic Approximations with Applications in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31172)
*Vagul Mahadevan,Claire Chen,Shuze Daniel Liu,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends two-timescale stochastic approximation to Markovian noise without projections, proving stability and convergence and achieving the first almost sure convergence of TDC with eligibility traces for off-policy linear function approximation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Relax the noise assumptions from i.i.d. to Markovian to better reflect RL scenarios; remove projection requirements; enable rigorous convergence guarantees for off-policy actor-critic/TDC methods with function approximation.

Method: Developed a novel stability bound by controlling the fast timescale parameter with the running maximum of the slow timescale, rather than the current slow iterate; analyze two-timescale SA under Markovian noise without compactness assumptions; use stochastic approximation and ODE techniques to establish almost sure convergence.

Result: Proves stability (boundedness) and convergence of two-timescale SA under Markovian noise; demonstrates first almost sure convergence of TDC with eligibility traces in off-policy linear function approximation.

Conclusion: The running-max control strategy is key to handling Markovian noise without projections, broadening applicability of two-timescale SA in RL; results support reliable off-policy learning with TDC and eligibility traces under linear function approximation and may extend to broader settings.

Abstract: This work studies the convergence of two-timescale stochastic approximations (SA), a class of iterative algorithms that update two sets of parameters in fast and slow timescales respectively. Notable examples of two-timescale SA in reinforcement learning (RL) include temporal difference learning with gradient correction (TDC) and actor-critic methods. Previously, the stability (i.e., boundedness) and convergence of two-timescale SA were only established under i.i.d. noise. This work instead establishes the stability and convergence of two-timescale SA under Markovian noise, a setup that is more realistic in RL. Notably, we do not need to use any projection operator and the noise does not need to live in a compact space. Our key technical novelty is to control the fast timescale parameter with the running max of the slow timescale parameter, instead of with the current slow timescale parameter, as most prior works do. As a key application, we establish the first almost sure convergence of TDC with eligibility traces under off-policy learning with linear function approximation.

</details>


### [250] [Geometry-based Schrödinger Bridges for Trustworthy Multimodal Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31193)
*Jiayu Xiong,Jing Wang,Qi Zhang,Wanlong Wang,Jun Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GMF introduces a geometry-based reliability signal for multimodal fusion by measuring latent-space transport corrections via Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow; squared initial velocity serves as an efficiency score to flag low-quality inputs independent of model confidence, improving robustness to noise and conflicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current trustworthy fusion methods rely on model prediction confidence, which can be misleading when the model is confident but wrong, creating a circular dependency. There is a need for an independent reliability signal to detect input quality.

Method: In latent space, apply Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow. The squared initial velocity of transport serves as a learned correction score: low values indicate valid data, while noisy, incomplete, or conflicting data require stronger transport corrections. This geometry-based score acts as an independent judge of data quality during fusion.

Result: GMF demonstrates significantly improved robustness against severe sensor noise and semantic conflicts compared to confidence-based baselines, across extensive experiments.

Conclusion: Geometry-based reliability signals can effectively flag unreliable inputs and break the circular dependency on model confidence, leading to more robust multimodal fusion in real-world settings.

Abstract: Real-world multimodal systems must be robust against low-quality data, such as sensor noise, incomplete multimodal data and conflicting inputs. However, existing trustworthy fusion methods rely on the model's own prediction confidence to judge data quality. This creates a circular dependency: when a model is confident but wrong, these methods fail to detect the error. To break this loop, we propose Geometry-based Multimodal Fusion (GMF). Instead of relying on predictions, we evaluate reliability by measuring how much transport correction the input needs in latent space. We implement Diffusion Schrödinger Bridge transport with Rectified Flow, where the squared initial velocity gives an efficient learned correction score. Valid data has low squared velocity magnitude, while noisy, incomplete data or conflicting data requires stronger transport correction. This geometry-based reliability signal acts as an independent judge, effectively flagging unreliable inputs even when the classifier is fooled. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GMF significantly improves robustness against severe sensor noise and semantic conflicts compared to confidence-based baselines.

</details>


### [251] [Beyond Additive Decompositions: Interpretability Through Separability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31200)
*Jinyang Liu,Munir Eberhardt Hiabu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tensor Separation Learning (TSL) offers a regression model that expresses the target as a sum of rank-1 feature interactions learned via a stagewise greedy algorithm with orthogonal refitting, enabling separability and faithful visualizations. It addresses limitations of additive explainability methods in the presence of interactions and provides theoretical approximation guarantees, while performing competitively with black-box models on regression tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome signal cancellation and off-support extrapolation in additive explainability (GAMs, SHAP, functional ANOVA) when strong feature interactions are present, by enforcing a separable, low-rank interaction structure that preserves information from higher-order terms.

Method: Learn a sum of rank-1 products of univariate per-feature functions using a stagewise greedy procedure with orthogonal refitting. Enforce separability to avoid information loss from marginalizing higher-order interactions. The model can be reconstructed from first-order partial dependence functions up to constants, and a stage-wise correspondence ensures faithful visualizations of components.

Result: Theoretical approximation-rate guarantees are established for functions with bounded mixed p-th order partial derivatives. Empirically, TSL competes with black-box models on regression benchmarks, suggesting strong performance while retaining interpretability.

Conclusion: TSL provides an interpretable, structurally faithful regression framework that mitigates issues of additive explainability in the presence of strong interactions, offers reconstructibility from PDPs, and delivers competitive predictive performance with theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: Interpretable machine learning requires models that are accurate and structurally faithful to the data. Existing explainability methods rely heavily on additive representations (e.g., Generalized Additive Models (GAMs), SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), functional ANOVA), which can suffer from signal cancellation and off-support extrapolation in the presence of strong interactions. We propose Tensor Separation Learning (TSL), a regression model that learns a sum of rank-1 products of univariate per-feature functions via a stagewise greedy procedure with orthogonal refitting. By enforcing separability, TSL avoids the information loss inherent in additive projections caused by marginalizing higher-order interactions. The learned TSL model can be fully reconstructed from first-order partial dependence functions, up to constant factors. This stage-wise correspondence ensures that the resulting visualizations are faithful to the fitted components. We establish approximation-rate guarantees for functions with bounded mixed $p$-th order partial derivatives and demonstrate that TSL competes with black-box models on regression benchmarks.

</details>


### [252] [Fixed-Point Masked Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31215)
*Andrea Miele,Yiming Qin,Alba Carballo-Castro,Justin Deschenaux,Pascal Frossard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FP-MGMs (CoFRe) enable adaptive-depth masked generation by substituting part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver and introducing cross-step consistency and 3SR, yielding cheaper training and better low-budget quality; pre-trained MGMs can be converted with small fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MGMs achieve strong performance but rely on full-sequence bidirectional transformers and fixed-depth denoisers, leading to high training cost and suboptimal performance at low sampling budgets. There is a need for adaptive depth and more efficient denoising.

Method: Replace part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver operating over shared attention layers; introduce cross-step consistency loss to align hidden representations across neighboring denoising steps; propose three-state reuse (3SR) to warm-start the solver by classifying tokens as unchanged, still-masked, or newly revealed; train within the CoFRe framework that enables training-to-inference with fixed-point masked generation; show that MGMs can be converted to FP-MGMs with short fine-tuning.

Result: Across OpenWebText: parameter count reduced by 38.8%, training time by 11.5%, VRAM by 16.9%; perplexity improves from 830.8 to 101.8 at 96 transformer-block forward passes versus MDLM. ImageNette: training time reduced by 48.6% and VRAM by 50.7%, with improved FID across budgets.

Conclusion: CoFRe provides a practical framework for cheaper training and stronger low-budget masked generation across modalities; enables converting existing MGMs to FP-MGMs with minimal fine-tuning and yields favorable quality–cost trade-offs.

Abstract: Masked Generative Models (MGMs) enable parallel decoding and achieve strong performance across modalities, but require full-sequence bidirectional transformers at every step, making training costly and degrading quality under low sampling budgets. Existing work improves efficiency via better samplers or cheaper fixed-depth denoisers, but they still allocate a fixed amount of denoiser computation to each refinement step. We introduce Fixed-Point Masked Generative Models (FP-MGMs), which replace part of the denoiser with a fixed-point solver over shared attention layers to enable adaptive depth with fewer parameters. To make it more effective for masked generation, we first introduce a cross-step consistency loss, which aligns hidden representations at neighboring denoising steps and, second, three-state reuse (3SR) which warm-starts the solver using the previous solution by treating differently unchanged, still-masked, and newly revealed tokens respectively. Together, these components define our complete training-to-inference framework for fixed-point masked generation, \emph{CoFRe}. We also show that pre-trained MGMs can be converted into FP-MGMs with short fine-tuning, avoiding full retraining. Across modalities, CoFRe improves the quality and cost trade-off. On OpenWebText, CoFRe reduces parameters by 38.8\%, training time by 11.5\%, and VRAM by 16.9\%, while improving generative perplexity from 830.8 to 101.8 at a budget of $96$ transformer-block forward passes, compared to MDLM. In ImageNette, CoFRe reduces training time by 48.6\% and VRAM by 50.7\%, while improving FID in all sample budgets tested. Overall, CoFRe offers a practical framework for cheaper training and stronger low-budget masked generation.

</details>


### [253] [Multivariate Distributional Reinforcement Learning Using Sliced Divergences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31222)
*Baptiste Debes,Tinne Tuytelaars*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDRL: a projection-based multivariate distributional RL framework; extends one-dimensional divergences via slicing; provides contraction guarantees under specific discounting; analyzes divergences; evaluated on toy, gridworld, Atari subset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenge of extending distributional RL to multivariate returns, where standard metrics and contraction guarantees fail, and matrix discounting complicates learning.

Method: Define sliced/maximum-sliced multivariate return distributions by projecting onto univariate slices; prove contraction for uniform slicing with shared scalar discounting and for maximum-slicing under general dense discount matrices; analyze base divergences (Wasserstein, Cramér, MMD) and compatibility with single-sample Bellman update; empirical evaluations on toy chain, gridworld, Atari subset.

Result: Contraction guarantees established for specified slicing regimes; broad class of base divergences supported; characterized conditions for compatibility with standard Bellman updates; empirical results demonstrate feasibility on simple tasks and Atari subset.

Conclusion: SDRL provides a tractable, theoretically grounded approach to multivariate distributional RL via projection/slicing, enabling contraction guarantees and flexible divergence choices; suitable for practical RL tasks with multivariate returns.

Abstract: Distributional reinforcement learning (DRL) models the full return distribution rather than expectations, but extending it to multivariate settings remains challenging. Many common metrics do not naturally generalize beyond one dimension or lose computational tractability, and the multivariate case introduces additional difficulties such as general matrix discounting, for which no contraction results are available. We introduce Sliced Distributional Reinforcement Learning (SDRL), which lifts tractable one-dimensional divergences to multivariate return distributions via projections. We prove Bellman contraction for uniform slicing under shared scalar discounting, and introduce a maximum-slicing variant with contraction under general dense discount matrices. SDRL supports a broad class of base divergences; we analyze Wasserstein, Cramér, and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), and characterize which SDRL variants suit the standard single-sample Bellman update used in distributional RL. We evaluate SDRL on a toy chain problem and a gridworld image-based environment as well as a subset of Atari games.

</details>


### [254] [Bifurcated Remaining Useful Life Prediction: A Hybrid Approach for Realistic Uncertainty Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31241)
*Xabier Belaunzaran,Antonio Nappa,Arkaitz Artetxe,Basilio Sierra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid, uncertainty-aware RUL prognosis for turbofan using state-aware healthy/degraded regimes; combines LSTM autoencoder, Conditional Weibull, and Probabilistic NN with MC dropout; outputs physically consistent uncertainty bands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need reliable Remaining Useful Life predictions with calibrated uncertainty for aerospace maintenance. The regime-splitting into healthy/degraded aims to optimize accuracy and uncertainty representation by leveraging nominal data and explicit probabilistic modeling.

Method: Train an LSTM-based autoencoder on nominal data (RUL>150) to monitor reconstruction error as a state classifier; convert error to continuous state probability via a calibrated sigmoid; healthy regime uses Conditional Weibull Survival Analysis for Mean Residual Life estimation; degraded regime uses a Probabilistic Neural Network with Monte Carlo Dropout to capture both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties; ensemble prediction is dynamically weighted by the state probabilities.

Result: Generates physically consistent uncertainty bands, with high confidence near end-of-life and realistic variance during early operation, enabling robust risk-informed maintenance.

Conclusion: A regime-aware, probabilistic framework that fuses data-driven and survival-analysis components to deliver uncertainty-aware RUL estimates on C-MAPSS data, suitable for maintenance decision support.

Abstract: This study presents a novel hybrid prognostic framework for uncertainty-aware Remaining Useful Life (RUL) estimation in turbofan engines using the NASA C-MAPSS dataset. The framework employs a state-aware strategy that bifurcates the engines operational lifespan into "healthy" and "degraded" regimes. An LSTM-based autoencoder, trained strictly on nominal data (RUL > 150 cycles), monitors reconstruction error to act as a robust state classifier. For the healthy regime, a Conditional Weibull Survival Analysis is used for Mean Residual Life estimation. For the degraded regime, a Probabilistic Neural Network with Monte Carlo Dropout captures both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. Rather than using rigid binary labels, a calibrated sigmoid function converts the autoencoders output into continuous state probabilities, dynamically weighting the final ensemble prediction. The primary strength of this framework is its generation of physically consistent uncertainty bands, yielding high-confidence predictions near end-of-life while accurately reflecting the inherent variance of early operation, providing a robust tool for risk-informed maintenance.

</details>


### [255] [Spectral Reach: Understanding Neural Scaling as Progress into the Spectral Tail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31244)
*Konstantin Nikolaou,Jonas Scheunemann,Sven Krippendorf,Samuel Tovey,Christian Holm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable metric (spectral position) tracks which eigenmodes of the empirical NTK drive learning; training shifts toward weaker spectral modes with larger models reaching deeper into the tail (spectral reach); feature learning amplifies gradients to sustain progress, suggesting architecture/optimizer design interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the mechanisms behind neural scaling laws and why larger models perform better, addressing the lack of scalable tools to analyze the eNTK during training.

Method: Introduce spectral position as a scalable measure of which eNTK eigenvalues contribute to loss reduction. Apply it to scaling experiments, tracking eigenmode contribution over training across model sizes; analyze how learning engages spectral tail and the role of feature learning in sustaining gradient growth.

Result: Spectral position decreases during training; learning shifts from dominant eigenmodes to the spectral tail. Larger models access more tail modes, showing a size-dependent 'spectral reach'. Feature learning emerges as a key enabler by adaptively amplifying gradient magnitudes, allowing progress when representations are frozen, pointing to concrete design implications.

Conclusion: The spectral reach of larger models helps explain lower losses and predicts actionable design changes in architecture and optimization to promote feature learning and tail engagement.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws describe predictable power-law relationships between model size, dataset size, compute, and performance. While these laws guide the development of modern foundation models, the mechanisms underpinning them remain poorly understood, in part due to the absence of scalable analysis tools. To close this gap, we introduce "spectral position": a scalable measure of which eigenvalues of the empirical neural tangent kernel (eNTK) currently drive loss reduction. Applying this measure to scaling experiments, we find that spectral position decreases throughout training: learning shifts from dominant eigenmodes into the spectral tail. Larger models reach further into the tail than smaller models, revealing a size-dependent capacity we call "spectral reach". This suggests why larger models achieve lower losses: they sustain learning on weak spectral signals inaccessible to smaller models. We further identify feature learning as a key enabler of spectral reach. It adaptively amplifies gradient magnitudes as learning advances, sustaining progress where frozen representations stall. This points to concrete interventions through architecture and optimizer design.

</details>


### [256] [Toward Identifiable Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31245)
*Walter Nelson,Theofanis Karaletsos,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAEs are unstable across training runs; authors propose minimal architectural/training tweaks yielding two iSAE variants with better reconstruction and stability, supported by a link to dictionary learning and an approximate RIP condition that yields near-identifiable sparse codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to understand why SAEs lack stability/identifiability in practice and to design fixes that make learned dictionaries and codes more stable and interpretable.

Method: analyze factors causing instability in sparse autoencoders, implement minimal architectural and training modifications, propose two identifiable SAE (iSAE) variants built on TopK SAE, and connect their behavior to dictionary learning theory.

Result: Two iSAE variants with lower reconstruction error and improved stability; learned dictionaries satisfy an approximate restricted isometry property, making the sparse codes near-identifiable.

Conclusion: iSAEs improve identifiability and stability of SAEs and connect practical SAE training to dictionary learning theory, with RIP-like conditions ensuring near-identifiability of sparse codes.

Abstract: Recently, sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as an attractive tool for interpreting and interacting with representations in practical neural networks. While it is common empirical folklore, we also show theoretically that SAEs are highly unstable: different training runs are likely to produce different concept dictionaries and sparse codes. We characterize the model properties that hinder the stability of real-world SAEs, and address each of these problems through minimal changes to the architecture and training procedure. Together, these changes yield two versions of an \textbf{i}dentifiable SAE (iSAE), a variant of the standard TopK SAE with lower reconstruction error and improved stability. We explain this improvement theoretically by connecting SAEs with traditional dictionary learning approaches, and show that the dictionaries learned in practice satisfy an approximate restricted isometry condition, rendering the corresponding sparse codes in those models near-identifiable.

</details>


### [257] [Fraud Type Decomposition and the Observation-Mechanism Taxonomy:Class-Specific Detection Limits in Payment Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31257)
*Gaurav Dhama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Separating fraud-rate estimation by a five-class observation-mechanism taxonomy strictly dominates pooling over a single homogeneous fraud variable; the efficiency gap is a Jensen penalty from heterogeneous observation rates, with per-class lower bounds on detection due to endogeneity, non-observability, and non-informativeness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud labels in payment networks come from heterogeneous, imperfect observation processes. Treating fraud as a single homogeneous binary variable mis-specifies the data-generating process and leads to inefficiency.

Method: Introduce an observation-mechanism taxonomy that partitions fraud into five classes, each with distinct censorship and labeling pipelines; prove that class-wise estimation dominates pooled estimation; quantify the efficiency gap via a Jensen penalty; derive binding per-class constraints (endogenous label corruption, structural non-observability, feature non-informativeness).

Result: Estimation should be conducted separately for each class; pooling incurs a Jensen-penalty inefficiency; the aggregation across classes is not optimal; each class has explicit theoretical detection limits.

Conclusion: Fraud detection is fundamentally a collection of distinct estimation problems aligned with the underlying observation structure; effective detection requires acknowledging class-specific observation mechanisms and limits.

Abstract: Fraud detection in payment networks relies on labels generated through heterogeneous and imperfect observation processes, yet existing approaches treat fraud as a homogeneous binary variable. We show that this assumption is structurally incorrect and leads to provable inefficiency. We introduce an observation-mechanism taxonomy that partitions fraud into five classes, each defined by a distinct censorship and labeling pipeline. We prove that estimating fraud rates separately by class and aggregating strictly dominates pooled estimation, with the efficiency gap characterized as a Jensen penalty arising from heterogeneous observation rates. For each class, we derive the binding theoretical constraint on detection, including endogenous label corruption, structural non-observability, and feature non-informativeness. These results establish that fraud detection is fundamentally a collection of distinct estimation problems, each governed by its own observation structure and detection limit.

</details>


### [258] [Student Capacity Moderates Knowledge Distillation Effectiveness: A Systematic Study Across ResNet Teacher-Student Pairs on CIFAR-10](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31191)
*Umut Onur Yasar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Teacher-student capacity affects knowledge distillation (KD) effectiveness in ResNet on CIFAR-10; three pairs (R50->R18, R34->R18, R50->R34) show capacity-dependent KD gains, a gradient clipping bug inflated conclusions about Feature-KD until fixed, and a stem/input-resolution correction is needed for KD to be meaningful; code/results available online.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand under what conditions KD is beneficial, with a focus on teacher-student capacity relationships, and address practical issues (implementation bugs, input-resolution adjustments) that influence reported gains.

Method: Empirical comparison of Logit-KD and Feature-KD across three teacher-student pairs on CIFAR-10 using ResNet variants (R50, R34, R18) with 3 seeds; report mean±std and three key findings.

Result: 1) KD gains scale with student capacity; strongest gain: +0.30pp (R50->R34 Feature-KD); +0.18pp (R34->R18 Feature-KD); +0.00pp (R34->R18 Logit-KD). 2) A gradient clipping bug in Feature-KD suppressed its performance; after correction, Feature-KD matches/outperforms Logit-KD in two of three pairs (e.g., 95.55% on R50->R34 vs 95.25% baseline). 3) Input-resolution-aware architecture is crucial; correcting the ResNet stem for 32x32 inputs raises teacher accuracy by >5pp, exceeding any observed KD gains. Code and results available at GitHub link.

Conclusion: KD effectiveness is moderated by student capacity, requires precise implementation, and is highly sensitive to input-resolution architecture. Reproducible practices and code availability support robust comparisons.

Abstract: We investigate how teacher-student capacity relationships modulate knowledge distillation (KD) effectiveness in ResNet-based image classification on CIFAR-10. Across three teacher-student pairs -- R50->R18, R34->R18, and R50->R34 -- we compare Logit-KD and Feature-KD under controlled, reproducible conditions (3 seeds, mean+/-std reported throughout). We report three main findings. First, student capacity is a key moderating factor in distillation gain: R34 students benefit substantially more from KD than R18 students even when teacher-student accuracy gaps are comparable, with the strongest gain of +0.30pp observed for R50->R34 Feature-KD versus +0.18pp for R34->R18 Feature-KD and +0.00pp for R34->R18 Logit-KD. Second, implementation correctness critically affects Feature-KD: a gradient clipping bug that excluded projection layers suppressed Feature-KD performance and produced misleading comparisons with Logit-KD. After correction, Feature-KD matches or outperforms Logit-KD in two of three pairs, reaching 95.55% on R50->R34 against a baseline of 95.25%. Third, input-resolution-aware architecture is a prerequisite for effective distillation: correcting the ResNet stem for 32x32 inputs raises teacher accuracy by over 5pp -- an order of magnitude larger than any KD gain. All code and results are available at github.com/umutonuryasar/kd-capacity-gap.

</details>


### [259] [Lightweight CNN-Based Anomaly Detection for High Voltage Converter Modulators in the Spallation Neutron Source](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31259)
*Alberto D. Cencillo,Leonardo Concepción,Julián Luengo,Isaac Triguero*

Main category: cs.LG
