TL;DR: Architectural bias in multi-channel fault-detection for HVCMs: decoupling temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing with per-pulse channel reweighting improves detection; achieves high AUC-PR and AUC-ROC on SNS subsystems; ablations highlight 3 dominant channels and fault-type dependent representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DL approaches entangle temporal and cross-channel features, obscuring channel independence and structured interactions; fault precursors vary by fault type, requiring model architectures that respect channel structure.

Method: Systematically vary the order of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing; implement per-pulse adaptive channel reweighting; evaluate on public HVCM dataset across RFQ, DTL, CCL, SCL; compare with state-of-the-art; perform ablations to identify dominant channels and fault-family behavior.

Result: Best variant achieves pooled AUC-PR 0.816 and AUC-ROC 0.934; outperforms state of the art on most subsystems and five of six fault families; three dominant input channels identified; fault-family performance linked to amplitude shifts vs joint patterns.

Conclusion: Channel-aware architectural bias and adaptive reweighting enhance fault detection in HVCMs; results suggest targeted channel selection and interaction modeling are crucial; supports design of specialized DL architectures for accelerator diagnostics.

Abstract: Unscheduled trips of high-power pulsed converters are a leading source of downtime at large accelerator facilities. At the Spallation Neutron Source (SNS), the High Voltage Converter Modulators (HVCMs) are consistently the second-largest contributor to lost beam time. Each HVCM pulse is recorded across sensor channels spanning currents, voltages, and magnetic fluxes, whose mutual interactions encode the operating state of the system. Fault precursors do not manifest uniformly across these channels: depending on fault type, they may alter the temporal structure of individual signals, change the statistical dependencies among channels, or both. Existing deep-learning approaches typically process multi-channel signals with standard convolutional pipelines that entangle temporal and cross-channel operations from the first layer, giving the model no explicit mechanism to represent channel independence or structured inter-channel interaction. We hypothesise that architectural inductive bias, specifically the ordering of temporal filtering and cross-channel mixing, plays a central role in detection performance on this class of data. To test this, we vary the order in which these two operations are applied, and examine whether per-pulse adaptive channel reweighting further improves sensitivity. Evaluated on the public HVCM dataset across all four SNS subsystems (RFQ, DTL, CCL, SCL), our best variant achieves a pooled AUC-PR of 0.816 and AUC-ROC of 0.934, outperforming the state of the art on most subsystems and five of the six fault families. Ablations identify three dominant input channels and link per-fault-family performance to whether precursors manifest as amplitude shifts in individual channels or as subtler patterns requiring joint channel representations to surface.

</details>


### [260] [Interpretability Without Tradeoffs: Disentangling Polysemanticity At Equal Predictive Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31304)
*Doğukan Bağcı,Bernt Schiele,Simone Schaub-Meyer,Jonas Fischer,Robin Hesse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ELUDe enables explicit, lossless unsupervised disentanglement of latent representations to yield interpretable sub-units without changing model behavior. It reorganizes information flow between layers to re-route concept-specific contributions, preserving outputs and requiring no training or labels; applicable to pretrained vision models like DINOv2 and ViT-B/16.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural networks encode multiple concepts within single neurons, making interpretability hard. Prior disentanglement methods (e.g., sparse autoencoders) can improve semantics but often degrade performance due to model modification. A need exists for interpretable representations without harming downstream accuracy or requiring labels.

Method: ELUDe reorganizes inter-layer information flow to separate concept-specific contributions while preserving the original computation. It is explicit, lossless, unsupervised, and can be applied post hoc to pretrained models without additional training or labels. The approach yields sub-units that behave like interpretable features without altering model outputs.

Result: Applied to several vision models (including DINOv2 and supervised ViT-B/16), ELUDe increases interpretability while maintaining downstream accuracy, operates efficiently, and enables practical uses such as steering model representations.

Conclusion: ELUDe delivers interpretable, scalable, and actionable insights with negligible to no loss in performance, achieving explicit disentanglement without retraining or labels and without sacrificing functional equivalence.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are widely used, but interpreting what they actually learn remains difficult. A major obstacle is that individual neurons often encode multiple unrelated concepts, obscuring the decision process of the network. While prior work, such as sparse autoencoders, can separate these mixed signals into more meaningful, "monosemantic" features, this typically requires altering the model in ways that can degrade downstream performance. To overcome this, we introduce ELUDe (explicit, lossless, unsupervised disentanglement), a method for improving the interpretability of DNNs while preserving their functional equivalence. ELUDe breaks latent representations into clear, inspectable sub-units that behave like interpretable features, while guaranteeing that the model's outputs remain exactly the same. It requires no explicit training, no labels, and can be applied to pretrained models. ELUDe works by reorganizing how information flows between layers, re-routing concept-specific contributions while preserving the original computation by construction. Across several vision models, including DINOv2 and supervised ViT-B/16, ELUDe improves interpretability, keeps downstream accuracy unchanged, runs efficiently, and supports practical uses such as steering model representations. In short, ELUDe offers interpretability (almost) without a tradeoff: clearer, scalable, and actionable model insights with no loss in performance.

</details>


### [261] [Algorithmic Recourse of In-Context Learning for Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31272)
*Wenshuo Dong,Jiaming Zhang,Shaopneg Fu,Hongbin Lin,Di Wang,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces ASR-ICL, a zeroth-order, adaptive subspace recourse framework for in-context learning on tabular data that yields sparse, actionable recourse for black-box ICL models; provides theoretical guarantees of bounded recourse and convergence to classical solutions as context grows; empirically achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods with fewer queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a growing need for post-hoc, actionable recourse for individuals affected by tabular decision models, particularly when decisions are made via in-context learning where recourse remains underexplored and challenging to compute efficiently.

Method: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of algorithmic recourse under in-context learning on tabular data and introduces ASR-ICL, a zeroth-order optimization framework that adaptively selects a subspace of features to generate sparse, actionable recourse. The approach extends to multi-class tasks and relies on model-agnostic querying for a black-box ICL model.

Result: ASR-ICL achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods while using significantly fewer queries; experiments across multiple real-world datasets and models validate the theoretical convergence of recourse toward classical solutions as context size increases.

Conclusion: ASR-ICL offers a principled, efficient, and scalable solution for algorithmic recourse under in-context learning on tabular data, bridging theoretical guarantees with practical performance and extending to multi-class problems.

Abstract: As predictive models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings such as credit approval, there is a growing need for post-hoc methods that provide recourse to affected individuals. Many such models operate on tabular data, where features correspond to real-world attributes. Recently, in-context learning (ICL) has enabled large language models to perform tabular prediction by conditioning on labeled examples at inference time, without explicit training. However, algorithmic recourse for tabular decision-making under ICL remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first study of algorithmic recourse for tabular data under ICL. We carry out a theoretical analysis, showing that recourse remains well-defined and bounded, and we characterize how recourse converges toward classical solutions as the context size increases. In practice, we propose a novel zeroth-order recourse framework, Adaptive Subspace Recourse for In-Context Learning (ASR-ICL), that efficiently generates actionable and sparse recourse for black-box ICL models. The proposed framework naturally extends to multi-class tabular tasks. Experiments across multiple real-world datasets and models demonstrate that ASR-ICL achieves recourse quality comparable to existing methods with fewer queries and empirically confirm the predicted convergence behavior, supporting our theoretical analysis.

</details>


### [262] [Survival Reinforcement Learning: Toward Scalable Self-Supervised RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31273)
*Franki Nguimatsia-Tiofack,Fabian Schramm,Théotime Le Hellard,Justin Carpentier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Survival Reinforcement Learning (SRL), a classification-based online alternative to contrastive RL (CRL) designed for long-horizon, goal-conditioned planning by maximizing dwell time at goals. It claims to bypass CRL constraints and reduce undesired bang-bang behavior, achieving parity with CRL on manipulation and 2x–8x gains on long-horizon locomotion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CRL scales depth but struggles with long-horizon goal-conditioned planning due to the uniformity-tolerance trade-off in contrastive losses and fragile survival-based behaviors.

Method: Introduce SRL as an online classification-based extension of survival value learning that maximizes dwell time at target goals, avoiding CRL’s structural constraints and bang-bang tendencies; trained end-to-end in a online fashion.

Result: SRL matches state-of-the-art CRL on manipulation tasks and outperforms CRL by 2x–8x on stable, long-horizon locomotion across diverse robotic benchmarks.

Conclusion: Classification-based methods may be a key primitive for scalable RL, with SRL offering a practical alternative to CRL for long-horizon planning and improved locomotion performance.

Abstract: While self-supervised Contrastive Reinforcement Learning (CRL) has shown remarkable depth-scaling capabilities, successfully using networks over 64 layers, scaled CRL still struggles with long-horizon goal-conditioned planning due to the uniformity-tolerance dilemma inherent in contrastive losses. We introduce Survival Reinforcement Learning (SRL), an online classification-based alternative that extends the survival value learning framework by maximizing the agent's dwell time at target goals. SRL bypasses the structural constraints of CRL and mitigates the "bang-bang" control solutions inherent to survival frameworks, which often induce undesirable behavior in complex dynamical systems. Evaluated across diverse robotic benchmarks, scaled SRL matches state-of-the-art CRL on manipulation tasks and outperforms it by 2x to 8x on stable, long-horizon locomotion tasks. Our results provide strong additional evidence that classification-based methods may serve as a key primitive in the broader effort to scale reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [263] [Learning Parametric Nitrogen Fertilizer Response Curves Using Neuro Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31276)
*Giorgio Morales,John Sheppard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic regression (SR) for nitrogen-response curves enables learning interpretable, parametric, site-specific N-response relationships without assuming a fixed form. A transformer-based skeleton discovery across management zones is coupled with a genetic-algorithm fitter, achieving robust, interpretable expressions that outperform standard parametric models on real data and resist data-scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches rely on predefined parametric forms or opaque ML models, limiting interpretability and discovery of site-specific functional relationships in precision agriculture. There is a need for interpretable, flexible models that capture spatially varying N-response across management zones.

Method: Propose a neuro-symbolic SR pipeline. Use a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction to discover shared functional skeletons across multiple management zones. Create diverse input subsets and enforce consistency to recover robust symbolic skeletons, which are then fitted to observed data with a genetic algorithm.

Result: Validated on synthetic 1D problems, demonstrating recovery of correct expressions under epistemic uncertainty, especially in data-scarce regimes. Applied to real winter-wheat data, learning distinct parametric N-response curves for different management zones. Discovered expressions yield lower fitting errors than quadratic-plateau and exponential models and reveal varied functional behaviors across spatial regions.

Conclusion: Neuro-SR can reveal site-specific agronomic relationships, enhancing interpretability and decision-making in precision agriculture by providing flexible, interpretable N-response curves across management zones.

Abstract: Accurately modeling crop response to Nitrogen (N) fertilization is a fundamental challenge in precision agriculture, as it impacts both economic returns and environmental sustainability. Existing approaches either rely on predefined parametric forms or opaque machine learning models, limiting their ability to interpret or discover site-specific functional relationships from data. In this work, we propose a neuro symbolic regression (SR) approach to learn parametric N-response curves without assuming a predefined functional form. Our approach integrates a transformer-based Multi-Set Symbolic Skeleton Prediction strategy, enabling the discovery of shared functional structures across multiple subdomains or management zones (MZs). By constructing diverse input subsets and enforcing consistency across them, the method recovers robust symbolic skeletons that are subsequently fitted to observed data using a genetic algorithm. This framework was first evaluated on synthetic one-dimensional problems to assess its robustness under varying levels of epistemic uncertainty. The results demonstrate the ability of the proposed SR approach to recover correct expressions even in data-scarce regimes. In this work, we present the results of applying our method to real-world winter wheat data, learning distinct parametric N-response curves for different MZs within a field. The results show that the discovered expressions not only achieve lower fitting errors than traditional models such as quadratic-plateau and exponential functions, but also capture diverse functional behaviors across spatial regions. This demonstrates the potential that neuro SR has to enable the discovery of site-specific agronomic relationships and support informed decision-making in precision agriculture.

</details>


### [264] [Non-Asymptotic Convergence of Stochastic Iterative Algorithms: A Lyapunov Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31309)
*Zaiwei Chen,Siva Theja Maguluri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A survey of Lyapunov-based finite-time analysis for stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms, introducing generalized Moreau envelopes as universal Lyapunov functions; derives mean-square convergence for SGD, linear SA, Q-learning, and TD learning; discusses extensions to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive/dissipative operators, and high-probability bounds; aims to provide a unified roadmap for SA and reinforcement learning analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified, self-contained finite-time analysis framework for SA algorithms under noisy access to a fixed-point operator, with broad applicability to reinforcement learning.

Method: Use generalized Moreau envelopes as universal Lyapunov functions to obtain finite-time and mean-square convergence guarantees in the standard contractive, i.i.d.-noise setting; apply the framework to SGD, linear SA, and value-based RL (Q-learning, TD learning); extend the analysis to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive and dissipative operators, and derive high-probability bounds.

Result: Mean-square convergence guarantees for SA-based algorithms (including SGD, linear SA, Q-learning, TD learning) with a unified finite-time analysis; a roadmap that connects these analyses under a single Lyapunov-function framework; extensions to more general noise and operator classes; discussion of open problems in high-probability bounds and non-contractive settings.

Conclusion: The generalized Moreau envelope–based Lyapunov framework yields a cohesive, self-contained roadmap for finite-time SA analysis and its reinforcement-learning applications, and points to fruitful directions for non-contractive settings and high-probability guarantees.

Abstract: We survey Lyapunov-based techniques for the finite-time analysis of stochastic iterative algorithms, also known as stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms, for solving fixed-point equations $\bar{F}(x)=x$, where the operator $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ can only be accessed through a noisy oracle. We first focus on the standard setting in which $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ is contractive with respect to some norm and the noise is i.i.d., and explain how generalized Moreau envelopes serve as universal Lyapunov functions, regardless of the underlying norm. We then show how this framework yields mean-square convergence guarantees and applies to stochastic gradient descent, linear SA, and value-based reinforcement learning algorithms such as Q-learning and temporal-difference learning. Finally, we discuss extensions to Markovian noise, seminorm-contractive operators, dissipative operators, and high-probability bounds, and conclude with open problems. The goal is to present a unified and self-contained roadmap for the finite-time analysis of SA and its applications, especially in reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [265] [Graph Neural Networks Are Not Continuous Across Graph Resolutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31315)
*Christian Koke,Yuesong Shen,Abhishek Saroha,Marvin Eisenberger,Bastian Rieck,Michael Bronstein,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNNs are not continuous with respect to natural graph convergence; similar graphs at different resolutions can yield very different embeddings; identifies a structural obstruction in information-propagation schemes; proposes a principled architectural modification to enforce continuity across scales; validates theory with extensive experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure stable, cross-scale representations and reliable generalization of GNNs when graphs are observed at varying resolutions; address the observed discontinuities in existing propagation schemes.

Method: Analyze the source of discontinuity in common information-propagation schemes; derive a principled modification to standard GNN architectures to enforce continuity across scales; enable coherent integration of distinct resolutions.

Result: The modification yields continuity across scales and enables consistent integration of distinct resolutions; extensive experiments confirm improved stability and cross-scale generalization across diverse datasets.

Conclusion: Continuity across graph scales is achievable via a principled architectural change, mitigating a fundamental limitation of conventional GNNs and enabling reliable multi-resolution processing.

Abstract: We show that contrary to conventional wisdom in the community, graph neural networks (GNNs) are not continuous with respect to all natural modes of graph convergence. As a result, GNNs may generate substantially different latent representations for graphs that are very similar. In particular they assign vastly different latent embeddings to graphs that represent the same underlying object at different resolution scales. We trace this failure of continuity back to a structural obstruction arising from commonly used information-propagation schemes. Building on this insight we then derive a principled modification to standard GNN architectures which equips models with continuity across scales. The proposed modification enables consistent integration of distinct resolutions and reliable generalization between them. We systematically validate our theoretical findings in a wide range of numerical experiments.

</details>


### [266] [Generalized Intention Modeling in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31318)
*Mateusz Odrowaz-Sypniewski,Jasmine Bayrooti,Ajay Shankar,Amanda Prorok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Task-adaptive opponent modeling with a performance-driven mixture of intent representations and a mutual-information–maximizing representation, improving ego-agent performance across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods rely on a single, preselected intent signal, which empirical evidence shows is not universally representative; intents are task- and environment-dependent, necessitating adaptive modeling.

Method: Introduce a framework that learns a mixture of multiple intent representations guided by performance (task-adaptive). Also propose a new intention representation optimized to maximize mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns.

Result: Empirically matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and provides insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

Conclusion: Adaptive, MI-informed intent representations are effective; modeling intent as a task-dependent mixture enhances robustness and interpretability of opponent modeling.

Abstract: Modeling an opponent's intent is critical for effective decision-making in non-cooperative, competitive, and general-sum multi-agent reinforcement learning. Existing opponent modeling methods encode intent using an embedding derived from episode information chosen a priori, such as the opponent's next action or a future environment state, and use this to guide the ego-agent's behavior. These approaches assume that the chosen information is universally representative of intent; however, we show empirically that this is not the case as intentions are often task- and environment-dependent. To address this, we introduce a task-adaptive opponent modeling framework that learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations. We further introduce a new intention representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns, thereby capturing opponent information that is most directly relevant to performance. Our approach consistently matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and yields insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

</details>


### [267] [dashi: A Python library for Dataset Shift Characterization to Support Trustworthy AI Development and Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31360)
*David Fernández-Narro,Pablo Ferri,Ángel Sánchez-García,Juan M. García-Gómez,Carlos Sáez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: dashi is an open-source Python library to explore, quantify, and characterize dataset shifts in health AI, using unsupervised information-geometry methods and supervised performance metrics across temporal and multi-source batches; demonstrated in gestational diabetes, COVID-19, and emergency medical dispatch to improve trustworthiness of AI life cycles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset shifts threaten performance and safety in health AI; need accessible tools bridging theory to practice for covariate, prior, and concept shifts; support data quality, model reliability, and responsible deployment.

Method: Dual unsupervised/supervised approaches. Unsupervised: information geometry, non-parametric manifolds; metrics/visualizations such as Information Geometric Temporal plots, Global Probabilistic Deviation, Source Probabilistic Outlyingness; Supervised: quantify model performance degradation; applicable to temporal and domain/source batches; open-source; interactive visuals.

Result: Empirical demonstrations on three health AI scenarios (gestational diabetes, COVID-19, emergency medical dispatch) using simulated and real-world data; shows utility in assessing data variability and its impact on performance; aids trustworthiness and robust pipelines.

Conclusion: dashi provides a practical, interpretable toolkit for diagnosing dataset shifts, enabling safer ML life cycle management in health AI by aligning data coherence with AI performance.

Abstract: The Artificial Intelligence (AI) life cycle requires a thorough understanding of the underlying data dynamics for robust, safe and cost-effective AI development and use. Dataset shifts are defined as changes between train and test data distributions. Whether occurring over time (temporal) or across different sites (multi-source), they can severely degrade model performance and compromise data quality. This is particularly important in health AI, where the safety and fundamental rights of patients can be severely affected by uncontrolled shifts both at training and operational stages. While the theoretical foundations of covariate, prior, and concept shifts are well established, there is a lack of accessible and comprehensive software tools to perform their analysis. We introduce dashi, an open-source Python library designed for the exploration, quantification, and characterization of dataset shifts. dashi provides a dual approach: an unsupervised approach that leverages information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds to data variability characterization and analysis (e.g., Information Geometric Temporal plots and Multi-Source Variability metrics like Global Probabilistic Deviation and Source Probabilistic Outlyingness), and a supervised approach that quantifies and characterizes model performance degradation. Both unsupervised and supervised approaches work across user-defined temporal and domain/source batches. We demonstrate the utility of dashi on three simulated and real-world health AI case studies on gestational diabetes mellitus, COVID-19 and emergency medical dispatch. By providing interactive visual analytics and variability metrics, dashi supports trustworthiness of AI life cycle stages enabling robust and safe machine learning pipelines through the assessment of data coherence and AI performance.

</details>


### [268] [Trading Complexity for Expressivity Through Structured Generalized Linear Token Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31367)
*Erwan Fagnou,Paul Caillon,Blaise Delattre,Alexandre Allauzen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified framework for token mixers that separates direct input influence from recurrent propagation, generalizes recurrence to multiple past states, and introduces structured patterns with provable complexity and expressivity insights, validated on synthetic tasks and language modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the speed–memory trade-offs in token mixing layers for long-range dependencies, and provide a unifying lens across architectures (attention, state-space models) to guide efficient and expressive design.

Method: Propose a framework that decouples one-step input-to-output influence from the recurrent propagation of past outputs; generalize recurrence equations to allow dependence on multiple past states; construct structured recurrence patterns with provable complexity bounds; analyze expressivity; validate empirically on synthetic tasks and language modeling.

Result: Establishes provable complexity guarantees and trade-offs between runtime and expressivity; offers a unified toolkit for designing efficient, expressive token mixers across model families; empirical results on synthetic tasks and language modeling support viability.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled, architecture-agnostic approach to designing and analyzing token mixers, enabling principled speed–accuracy trade-offs and cross-model comparisons.

Abstract: Token mixing layers play a key role in how language models can learn and generate long-range dependencies. Their efficiency relies on the necessary trade-off between decoding speed and the memory requirements, along with the cache size. Considering causal generation, this paper explores new trade-offs thanks to a unified framework which separates two crucial features: (i) the direct influence of inputs on outputs in one generation step; (ii) the recurrent propagation of information through past outputs. This framework encompasses major architectures such as attention and state-space models, but also generalizes the recurrence equations by allowing each state to depend on multiple past states rather than only the immediate predecessor. By introducing structure, we design new recurrence patterns that provably achieve the desired complexity, while providing theoretical insights on their expressivity -- trading runtime for expressivity in a principled way. Empirical validation is performed on synthetic tasks, along with language modeling. Together, these results provide a unified toolkit for the understanding and design of efficient and expressive token mixers across model families.

</details>


### [269] [Softsign: Smooth Sign in Your Optimizer For Better Parameter Heterogeneity Handling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31371)
*Dmitrii Feoktistov,Timofey Belinsky,Andrey Veprikov,Amir Zainullin,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SoftSignum and SoftMuon propose soft-sign relaxations of sign-based optimizers, with adaptive temperature scheduling and a geometry-relaxation theory based on Fenchel conjugates; they achieve improved convergence and empirical performance over AdamW and hard sign methods, demonstrated on diverse DL tasks including LLM pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hard sign-based and LMO-inspired optimizers offer low memory but fixed update magnitudes, ignoring gradient magnitudes and parameter heterogeneity, causing oscillations and poor terminal convergence; a continuous, adaptive relaxation could reconcile stability with gradient-aware updates.

Method: Replace hard sign with a temperature-controlled soft-sign; introduce adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule; extend to matrix-valued optimizers (SoftMuon); build a generalized geometry-relaxation framework using strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates; prove convergence in stochastic non-convex settings.

Result: Theoretical convergence under the proposed framework; empirical improvements over hard sign-based optimizers and AdamW across tasks, including LLM pretraining; consistent performance gains and reduced oscillations.

Conclusion: SoftSignum/SoftMuon offer a flexible, convergent alternative to sign-based optimizers; the geometry-relaxation framework unifies updates under convex-analytic principles; future work could explore broader architectures, schedule tuning, and tighter convergence bounds.

Abstract: Sign-based and LMO-inspired optimizers have recently attracted substantial attention in deep learning due to their strong performance and low memory footprint. However, their fixed-magnitude updates can hurt terminal convergence: they decouple update mechanisms from gradient magnitudes and fail to account for parameter heterogeneity, often leading to oscillation rather than convergence. We propose SoftSignum, a smooth relaxation of sign-based optimization that replaces the hard sign map with a temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, enabling a parameter-wise transition from sign-like updates to magnitude-sensitive SGD-like steps. We complement it with an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule and extend the same principle to matrix-valued optimizers, obtaining SoftMuon. We also develop a generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates, proving convergence in stochastic non-convex setting. Experiments on diverse deep learning tasks, including LLM pretraining, show that SoftSignum and SoftMuon consistently improve over their hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW.

</details>


### [270] [Constrained Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning with Max-Min Criterion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31388)
*Giseung Park,Hyunyoung Nam,Woohyeon Byeon,Amir Leshem,Youngchul Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Constrained max-min MORL framework integrating explicit constraint satisfaction with fairness, backed by theory, convergence analysis, and empirical validation in tabular MDPs across building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion, and greenhouse-gas-emission-aware traffic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of standard max-min MORL in incorporating hard constraints while maintaining fairness across multiple objectives.

Method: Propose a constrained max-min MORL framework. Develop theoretical foundations, provide convergence analysis, and implement/validate the algorithm in tabular MDPs. Demonstrate applicability to three domains (building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion, and emission-aware traffic).

Result: Convergence analysis supports the proposed algorithm. Empirical results in tabular settings show balanced trade-offs between fairness (max-min criterion) and constraint satisfaction across the three domains.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a principled approach to constrained fairness in MORL, with broad applicability to multi-objective decision-making tasks where constraints are essential.

Abstract: Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) extends standard RL by optimizing policies with respect to multiple, often conflicting, objectives. While max-min MORL has emerged as an effective approach for promoting fairness, its applicability remains limited, particularly when constraints must be incorporated. In this paper, we propose a MORL framework that integrates the max-min criterion with explicit constraint satisfaction. We establish a theoretical foundation for the proposed framework and validate the resulting algorithm through convergence analysis and experiments in tabular settings. We further demonstrate the practical relevance of our approach in simulated building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion control, and greenhouse-gas-emission-aware traffic management. Across these domains, our method effectively balances fairness and constraint satisfaction in multi-objective decision-making.

</details>


### [271] [DG-CoLearn: An Efficient Collaborative Learning Framework for Dynamic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31427)
*Ashley Hoi-Ting Au,Zikun Zhang,Ligang He,Qiang Ni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DG-CoLearn is a client-oblivious collaborative dynamic graph learning framework that incrementally processes graph snapshots to improve efficiency and privacy in cross-partition settings, achieving substantial speedups and improved predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic graph learning on partitioned data suffers from end-to-end retraining on full snapshots and privacy concerns when sharing graph structure; there is a need for scalable, privacy-preserving collaborative learning for evolving graphs.

Method: Incremental graph snapshot processing focusing computation on regions affected by temporal updates, preserving history via temporal modeling; server-mediated embedding exchange enables multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client graph structure; client-oblivious design applied across the processing pipeline.

Result: Experiments show up to 33.8x training speedup, 27.4x reduction in communication, node classification F1 improvement up to 13.36%, and link prediction MAP improvement up to 8.27%.

Conclusion: DG-CoLearn offers an effective balance of efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning, enabling better performance with reduced communication and computation.

Abstract: Dynamic graph learning (DGL) is essential for modelling evolving graph data, but existing methods suffer from significant computational overhead due to repeated full-snapshot retraining and are not well-suited for collaborative settings with partitioned data. In realistic graph systems, cross-partition edges are unavoidable, but direct sharing of graph structure between clients may violate privacy constraints. We propose DG-CoLearn, a client-oblivious collaborative dynamic graph learning framework built on incremental graph snapshot processing, which focuses computation on graph regions affected by temporal updates while preserving historical information through temporal modelling. This incremental design is consistently applied across the entire graph processing pipeline, including a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism to enable accurate multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DG-CoLearn achieves up to 33.8$\times$ speedup in training time and 27.4$\times$ reduction in communication overhead, while consistently improving predictive performance on both node classification (up to 13.36% F1 improvement) and link prediction (up to 8.27% MAP improvement) tasks. These results highlight the effectiveness of DG-CoLearn in bridging efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

</details>


### [272] [DRIFT: Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Efficient Multi-Turn Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31455)
*Jian Mu,Tianyi Lin,Chengwei Qin,Zhongxiang Dai,Yao Shu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRIFT decouples rollout from optimization, using return-based importance weighting on offline trajectories to perform weighted supervised fine-tuning, achieving RL-like performance with the efficiency of SFT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online RL for multi-turn LLM control is accurate but expensive; offline SFT is efficient but prone to distribution shift and collapse. A method translating KL-regularized RL into weighted SFT could combine benefits.

Method: Sample offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, compute return-based importance weights, and optimize the policy via weighted supervised fine-tuning on the resulting dataset, effectively decoupling rollout from optimization.

Result: DRIFT matches or exceeds multi-turn RL baselines while preserving SFT-like training efficiency and simplicity.

Conclusion: DRIFT provides a practical, theoretically grounded framework that achieves RL-like performance through decoupled rollouts and importance-weighted fine-tuning, with publicly available code.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed in multi-turn interactive settings where users or environments can iteratively provide lightweight feedback. Unfortunately, optimizing such behavior presents a sharp dilemma in practice: online reinforcement learning is able to effectively address multi-turn dynamics but is prohibitively expensive due to the cost of generating full correction trajectories at every update, whereas offline supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is efficient but suffers from distribution shift and behavioral collapse. To this end, we novelly propose DRIFT (Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning), a framework that operationalizes the theoretical insight that the KL-regularized RL objective is equivalent to importance-weighted supervised learning. DRIFT decouples rollout from optimization by sampling offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, deriving return-based importance weights, and optimizing the policy via weighted SFT on the resulting dataset. Empirically, we demonstrate that DRIFT matches or exceeds the performance of multi-turn reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining the training efficiency and simplicity of standard supervised fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/2020-qqtcg/DRIFT.

</details>


### [273] [Graphical einops: bridging tensor networks and computation graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31485)
*Vincent Wang-Maścianica,Nikhil Khatri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A formal graphical calculus for tensor programming underlying einops using nested graded tubes to encode tensor axes, enabling diagrammatic proofs via grade-naturality and applying it to convert attention masks into preprocessing for efficient sparse attention implementations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Architecture and tensor-diagram representations are widespread but largely prose-based; a proof-enabling, diagrammatic foundation is needed for tensor-axis identities and equivariances in tensor programming.

Method: Represent tensor axes as nested graded tubes around a base type; use the tube boundary to reflect undirected tensor-network axes and the interior to capture the operational aspects of computation graphs; introduce grade-naturality (sliding spectacles over tubes) as the key rewrite rule to enable proofs.

Result: Standard equivariance proofs can be performed as concise diagrammatic derivations; the framework can convert attention masks into preprocessing operations, yielding efficient sparse-attention implementations.

Conclusion: The proposed rewrite system offers a rigorous, flexible diagrammatic toolkit for tensor programming that both clarifies theoretical identities and guides practical, efficient implementations of tensor-based architectures.

Abstract: Architecture diagrams are ubiquitous in deep learning, but they are usually only representational: the tensor-program identities they suggest are still proved by prose and tensor-axis manipulation. We introduce a formal graphical calculus for the structural fragment of tensor programming underlying einops, making such diagrams proof-enabling. Our calculus represents tensor axes as nested graded tubes around a base type. The tube boundary recovers the undirected tensor-network view of axes, while the directed interior retains the operational reading of computation graphs. The key rewrite is grade-naturality: sliding spectacles over tubes. Standard equivariance proofs become short diagrammatic derivations. We additionally demonstrate how our rewrite system may be applied to convert attention masks into pre-processing operations, recovering efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.

</details>


### [274] [Assign and Add: A Mechanistic Study of Compositional Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31497)
*Brady Exoo,Alberto Bietti,John Sous*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Small transformers show compositional generalization in a controlled setting via a shared modular addition mechanism; training dynamics reveal phased learning and a theoretical framework links internal mechanism compositionality to generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover how compositionality arises in transformers by isolating a simple compositional task (variable assignment and modular addition) and inspecting reliance on a shared module.

Method: Partition training data into disjoint sets, train small transformers on the task; perform mechanistic analysis to see whether the same modular addition MLP is used across direct and indirect inputs; analyze training dynamics to identify learning phases; develop theoretical framework explaining how compositionality emerges from training dynamics.

Result: Models generalize to unseen variable-number combinations; the modular addition MLP is reused regardless of input pathway; three learning phases observed (learning modular addition, building variable assignment structure, refinement to harder sequences); framework explains emergence of compositionality.

Conclusion: Compositional generalization in transformers can be a natural consequence of the internal modularity and training dynamics, not requiring specialized architectures or curricula.

Abstract: Large language models are able to compose skills in order to perform complex tasks, many of which might not have been seen during training. The details of how exactly this composition occurs remain elusive. In this paper, we study a mechanism for compositional generalization in transformers by considering a simple controlled setting involving variable assignment and modular addition. By partitioning our training data into disjoint sets, we observe that small transformers are able to generalize to previously unseen combinations of variables and numbers. Our mechanistic analysis shows that the same ``modular addition'' MLP module is used whether the inputs are given directly or indirectly through a separate variable assignment mechanism. We also analyze the training dynamics from an empirical lens, which reveals three phases of learning: first, modular addition is learned, then the structure required for variable assignment, and finally a refinement phase where the model generalizes to some hard sequences not seen in training. Finally, we provide a theoretical framework to explain how compositionality emerges from training dynamics. These results suggest that compositional generalization can be a natural consequence of the compositionality of internal mechanisms in~transformers.

</details>


### [275] [Scalable Inference-Time Annealing with Surrogate Likelihood Estimators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31498)
*Daniel Peñaherrera,Rishal Aggarwal,David Ryan Koes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SITA retrains flow-based models to perform inference-time annealing across temperatures using an energy-based surrogate likelihood, avoiding score-divergence terms and enabling scalable Boltzmann sampling; achieves state-of-the-art results on Alanine dipeptide and alanine tripeptide.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently sampling Boltzmann distributions in molecular systems remains challenging with diffusion-based finetuning due to intractable divergences when estimating importance weights. A scalable, divergence-free alternative is needed.

Method: Sequential retraining of flow-based models at progressively lower temperatures along a temperature ladder, guided by an energy-based model that provides fast surrogate likelihoods to facilitate sampling without computing score-field divergences during inference-time annealing.

Result: State-of-the-art sampling performance reported for Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide; code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: SITA offers a scalable, divergence-free approach to inference-time annealing for molecular sampling, potentially generalizable to larger biomolecular systems and complex Boltzmann distributions.

Abstract: A long standing challenge in computational chemistry and biophysics is efficiently sampling the Boltzmann distribution of molecules. Advances in generative modeling have been proposed to address the limitations of conventional sampling techniques by eliminating the computational cost of simulation. A promising direction is iteratively finetuning diffusion models along a temperature ladder whereby training data is generated via importance sampling during inference-time annealing. Unfortunately, these methods require computing a divergence over the score field to estimate importance weights, rendering them intractable for larger systems. Here we present scalable inference-time annealing (SITA), which retrains flow-based models to generate samples at progressively lower temperatures using an energy-based model to facilitate fast surrogate likelihoods. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms. Our code is available at: https://github.com/countrsignal/sita.git

</details>


### [276] [On Efficient Scaling of GNNs via IO-Aware Layers Implementations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31500)
*Daria Fomina,Daniil Krasylnikov,Alexey Boykov,Andrey Dolgovyazov,Vyacheslav Zhdanovskiy,Fedor Velikonivtsev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GPU-centric kernels for three GNN kernel families (SpMM, reductions, attention) to reduce I/O and improve locality; achieve large speedups over baselines across graphs; reordering benefits neighbor-parallel kernels; release as drop-in, hardware-aware accelerations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs are limited by sparse, irregular memory accesses and edge-wise intermediates in popular frameworks, causing high memory traffic and poor scalability. An I/O- and arithmetic-intensity perspective suggests kernel specialization to reduce data movement.

Method: Develop GPU kernels for SpMM-based convolutions, reduction-based aggregations, and attention-based layers (GATv2/Graph Transformer); study graph reordering effects; create fused attention kernels (including Tensor Core block-sparse variants); design degree-aware reductions; leverage cuSPARSE; evaluate across realistic graphs; release as drop-in replacements.

Result: Fused attention kernels achieve up to 3.9x speedup for Graph Transformer (median 1.6x) with Tensor Core variants up to 7.3x on locally dense graphs; GATv2 up to 8.5x speedup with peak memory reductions up to 76x (median 6x); degree-aware reductions up to 10x; SpMM-based layers with cuSPARSE up to 8x speedup over DGL and beating baselines in most evaluations.

Conclusion: Provide reusable, hardware-aware GNN accelerations as drop-in implementations to enable reproducible performance improvements across frameworks and graph characteristics.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are bottlenecked by sparse, irregular memory access. Popular frameworks such as DGL and PyTorch Geometric support general message passing, but complex layers often materialize edge-wise intermediates, increasing memory traffic and limiting scalability on large graphs. We take an I/O- and arithmetic-intensity--centric view and show that widely used layers fall into three kernel families: SpMM-based convolutions, reduction-based aggregations, and attention-based layers (GATv2/Graph Transformer). For each family, we develop GPU kernels that reduce data movement, improve locality, and remain robust across realistic graphs. We also study graph reordering and find that its impact depends on the kernel mapping: it benefits neighbor-parallel (gather-dominated) kernels more consistently than feature-parallel designs. Empirically, our fused attention kernels reach up to $\textbf{3.9}\times$ speedup for Graph Transformer (median $\textbf{1.6}\times$), with Tensor Core (block-sparse) variants up to $\textbf{7.3}\times$ on locally dense graphs; for GATv2 we reach up to $\textbf{8.5}\times$ speedup (median $\textbf{2.0}\times$) while reducing peak memory by up to $\textbf{76}\times$ (median $\textbf{6}\times$). Our degree-aware reduction kernels achieve up to $\textbf{10}\times$ speedup (median $\textbf{2.6}\times$). For SpMM-based layers, properly cached cuSPARSE achieves up to $\textbf{8}\times$ speedup over DGL and outperforms evaluated custom baselines in the majority of evaluations. We release our implementations as drop-in replacements to support reproducible, hardware-aware GNN acceleration.

</details>


### [277] [Skill Reuse as Compression in Agentic RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31509)
*Zhikun Xu,Yu Feng,Jacob Dineen,Taiwei Shi,Jieyu Zhao,Ben Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReuseRL grounds agentic RL in the MDL principle by learning a shared skill dictionary from successful trajectories and adding a segmentation penalty to compress behavior into reusable patterns, with a PAC-Bayes generalization bound; empirically improves in- and out-of-distribution success across multiple tasks beyond vanilla baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL-trained LLM agents tend to latch onto brittle, task-specific shortcuts. The authors hypothesize that generalization improves when successful trajectories are compressible into a small set of reusable patterns, enabling structured, reusable skills.

Method: Introduce ReuseRL that (1) extracts a shared skill dictionary from successful trajectories; (2) augments the RL objective with a segmentation cost to penalize idiosyncratic, poorly encoded behaviors; (3) provides a PAC-Bayes generalization bound for the compression penalty.

Result: Across ALFWorld, TextWorld-Cooking, and Countdown-Stepwise, ReuseRL achieves higher in- and out-of-distribution success than vanilla GRPO and strong round-length baselines.

Conclusion: MDL-based compression via a reusable skill dictionary improves generalization for agentic RL with theoretical guarantees and demonstrated empirical gains across multiple environments.

Abstract: Large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning (RL) often learn brittle, task-specific shortcuts. We hypothesize that agents generalize better when their successful trajectories are structurally compressible, decomposed into a small set of reusable abstract patterns. To formalize this, we introduce ReuseRL, which grounds agentic RL in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. ReuseRL extracts a shared skill dictionary from successful trajectories and augments the RL objective with a segmentation cost, explicitly penalizing idiosyncratic behaviors that encode poorly. We prove a PAC-Bayes generalization bound for this compression penalty. Across ALFWorld, TextWorld-Cooking, and Countdown-Stepwise, ReuseRL improves in- and out-of-distribution success over vanilla GRPO and strong round-length baselines.

</details>


### [278] [On the Relationship Between Activation Outliers and Feature Death in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31518)
*Elana Simon,Etowah Adams,James Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation outliers cause feature death in sparse autoencoders; a gamma-based metric predicts initial death rates, and mean-centering activations eliminates the issue across diverse models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse autoencoders often waste dictionary capacity due to feature death, where many learned features never activate. This problem varies wildly across models with identical configurations and leans on an unexplained mechanism tied to how features align with the activation mean.

Method: Define gamma = ||μ|| / ||σ|| where μ is the per-dimension activation mean and σ is per-token variation; assess its ability to predict death rates using two criteria (dead-by-TopK and dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer configurations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models. Analyze whether dimensions with large mean-relative-outlier activation suppress pre-activations at initialization, causing permanent non-activation, and examine the recovery dynamics during training. Evaluate mean-centering (subtracting activation mean) as a remedy and test its effect across various models.

Result: Gamma predicts initial death rates with Spearman ρ = 0.89 for dead-by-TopK and ρ = 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU; dead features can revive only slowly as SAE bias learns the activation mean, especially at high γ. Mean-centering of activations eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models, validating the proposed mechanism and demonstrating a principled preprocessing step to prevent feature death.

Conclusion: Outlier severity (γ) explains feature death in SAEs and serves as a strong predictor of death rates across domains. Mean-centering activations provides a robust, principled remedy, removing the need for reliance on initialization and enabling the full utilization of the learned feature dictionary.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose neural network activations into interpretable features, but many learned features never activate, a problem called feature death that wastes dictionary capacity and can reintroduce superposition. Death rates vary dramatically between models: near-zero on GPT-2, over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations. We find that dimension-level activation outliers (dimensions whose mean magnitude is large relative to per-token variation) cause this by shifting pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean. Features anti-aligned with the mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire. We formalize outlier severity as $γ= \|μ\|/\|σ\|$; it predicts initial death rates (Spearman $ρ= 0.89$ for dead-by-TopK, $0.82$ for dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer combinations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models. Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, a process that is prohibitively slow at high $γ$. Mean-centering (subtracting the activation mean) sidesteps this and eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models, confirming the mechanism and providing a principled basis for when and why this preprocessing step is necessary.

</details>


### [279] [Chem-PerturBridge: a harmonized compendium of small molecule perturbation transcriptomic effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31522)
*Artur Szałata,Olga Novitskaia,Maiia Shulman,Matthew Mella,Altynbek Zhubanchaliyev,Fabian J. Theis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chem-PerturBridge is a harmonized, multi-dataset perturbation transcriptomics resource enabling cross-dataset agreement assessment and improved molecule representation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling are fragmented across technologies and pipelines, hindering large perturbation models; a harmonized, well-annotated resource with replicate-aware conditions and standardized identifiers is needed.

Method: Assembled Chem-PerturBridge: >37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, 1.25M transcriptomic samples, eight assay types; standardized metadata/identifiers; replicate-aware condition-level effects. Evaluated cross-dataset matched-condition agreement and replicate agreement; pretrained embeddings on the resource using a compound-held-out OP3 split; extensive holdout evaluations across 11 datasets.

Result: Matched same-compound conditions show weak agreement in fine-grained log fold-change (logFC) rankings/magnitudes across dataset pairs and often fall below same-context different-compound baselines; logFC directionality agreement is more stable and frequently exceeds baselines. Pretraining with Chem-PerturBridge improves over L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and descriptor-free baselines on compound-held-out OP3 evaluations; molecule-holdout evaluations show models trained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match non-Chem-PerturBridge models.

Conclusion: Chem-PerturBridge enables diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and supports model reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data for improved representation learning.

Abstract: Large perturbation models require training data encompassing chemical, cellular, and assay diversity. Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling, however, are fragmented across technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines. We introduce Chem-PerturBridge, a harmonized multi-dataset resource comprising over 37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types, with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. We use the resource to evaluate matched-condition agreement across datasets and replicate agreement within datasets. Matched same-compound conditions generally show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings and magnitudes across most dataset pairs, often falling below same-context different-compound baselines. In contrast, logFC direction agreement is substantially more stable and usually exceeds these baselines. We further evaluate Chem-PerturBridge as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning. Under a compound-held-out OP3 evaluation split, embeddings pretrained on Chem-PerturBridge improve over L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and the descriptor-free OP3 baseline across metrics. An extensive molecule-holdout evaluation across 11 datasets further shows that models trained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match those that are not. Chem-PerturBridge therefore supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data.

</details>


### [280] [Value Functions as Supermartingale Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31524)
*Alessandro Abate,Daniel Contro,Mirco Giacobbe,Agustín Martínez-Suñé,Diptarko Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical link between reinforcement learning for ω-regular tasks and stochastic certification: the value function of a policy that almost surely satisfies an ω-regular property forms a Streett supermartingale certificate; validated on finite MDPs and applicable to finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces, enabling certificate synthesis via RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile formal verification guarantees for ω-regular properties with reinforcement learning, across general state spaces, addressing the lack of guarantees for learned policies.

Method: Introduce a theoretical connection: with an appropriate reward, the value function of a policy that almost surely satisfies an ω-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for that specification; provide formal derivations and conditions under which this encoding holds.

Result: Theoretical result supported by experimental validation on finite MDPs; extension of validity to finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces demonstrated conceptually, suggesting a principled route to certificate synthesis via RL.

Conclusion: Establishes a principled pathway to synthesize certificates via reinforcement learning for ω-regular specifications, with potential applicability to broader state-space settings and future work on practical algorithms.

Abstract: Certification methods for stochastic systems provide sufficient proof rules, based on real-valued supermartingale certificates, to determine the almost-sure satisfaction of $ω$-regular properties (and therefore of linear temporal logic) over general state spaces, encompassing both countably infinite and continuous state spaces. Conversely, reinforcement learning (RL) methods for $ω$-regular tasks have received considerable attention, but they typically lack formal guarantees that the learned policy satisfies the specification, except possibly for finite state and action spaces. We bridge these two lines of research by establishing a novel theoretical connection: under an appropriate reward, the value function associated to a policy that almost surely satisfies an $ω$-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for that specification. Our results, validated experimentally on finite Markov decision processes, hold for finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces, suggesting a principled route to certificate synthesis via RL.

</details>


### [281] [The Dynamic-Probabilistic Consistency Gap in Chaotic Surrogate Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31547)
*Andre Herz,Matthijs Pals,Daniel Durstewitz,Georgia Koppe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies a dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap in dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR) where finite-horizon probabilistic objectives destabilize learned dynamics or decouple predictive uncertainty from local tangent dynamics. Introduces KAFFEE, an EKF-based differentiable training framework that leverages local innovations and Jacobians to produce dynamics-consistent uncertainty. Demonstrates improvements on chaotic Lorenz-96 and cross-system adaptation across 13 chaotic systems, enabling in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DSR aims for surrogate models with reliable uncertainty estimates that reflect the true dynamics. Current probabilistic objectives (especially open-loop, finite-horizon rollouts) can distort dynamics or misalign uncertainty with local expansions, undermining trustworthiness in deployment. There is a need for training approaches that couple uncertainty estimation with local dynamical structure.

Method: KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation) is a differentiable extended Kalman filter–based training scheme. It evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) and propagates covariance through learned local Jacobians. This creates a dynamic-probabilistic consistency by aligning uncertainty with local tangent dynamics during learning, enabling in-context Bayesian filtering across systems.

Result: Empirical results on stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96 show KAFFEE reduces the identified failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants compared to open-loop objectives, and maintains competitive predictive accuracy. Across 13 chaotic systems, the DPC gap emerges when adapting a DSR foundation model; KAFFEE enables in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.

Conclusion: KAFFEE mitigates the DPC gap by tightly coupling probabilistic training with local dynamical structure via differentiable EKF updates. It yields more physically consistent uncertainty estimates and supports cross-system adaptation without sacrificing baseline predictive performance.

Abstract: Dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR) aims to learn surrogate models that capture the dynamics underlying time-series data. Reliably deploying these surrogates requires uncertainty estimates consistent with the learned dynamics. We expose a dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap: the pursuit of finite-horizon probabilistic objectives can degrade dynamics or decouple predictive uncertainty from the local tangent dynamics it ought to reflect. We isolate three mechanisms behind this gap: core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty. Specifically, we show that open-loop Gaussian rollout objectives can penalize Jacobian-generated covariance growth in chaotic systems, encouraging optimization shortcuts that weaken physical expansion or decouple uncertainty from it. To mitigate this gap, we propose KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation), a differentiable extended Kalman filter-based training framework that evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) while transporting covariance through learned local Jacobians. On stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces the identified failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants relative to open-loop objectives, and maintains competitive predictive scores. We further show that the DPC gap appears when probabilistically adapting a DSR foundation model across 13 chaotic systems, where KAFFEE enables in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.

</details>


### [282] [Functional Attention: From Pairwise Affinities to Functional Correspondences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31559)
*Jiefang Xiao,Maolin Gao,Simon Weber,Guandao Yang,Daniel Cremers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Functional Attention, reinterpreting attention as a functional map between adaptive bases to capture global structure in operator learning; replaces softmax with structured linear operators, yielding a compact, resolution-invariant representation that generalizes across discretizations; achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance on PDE solving, 3D segmentation, and regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of token-wise attention in infinite-dimensional function spaces; preserve global functional structure and discretization-robustness in operator learning.

Method: Reinterpret attention as a functional correspondence; leverage geometric functional maps; substitute softmax affinities with structured linear operators; use adaptive bases to capture global dependencies.

Result: Matches or approaches state-of-the-art on operator learning tasks (solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, regression); robust to varying discretizations; compact representation; resolution-invariant; supported by experiments.

Conclusion: Functional Attention provides a geometry-inspired, principled approach to operator learning with improved global dependency modeling and discretization robustness; broadly applicable to PDEs and geometric tasks; project page available.

Abstract: Learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces, or operator learning, is essential for many machine learning applications. Although transformer-based operators are popular, they often rely on token-wise attention. These methods treat continuous fields as discrete tokens and usually ignore the global functional structure. We introduce \emph{Functional Attention}, which reinterprets attention as a functional correspondence between adaptive bases. Inspired by geometric functional maps, our method replaces softmax affinities with structured linear operators. This yields a compact, generalizable, resolution-invariant representation that explicitly captures global dependencies. Experiments demonstrate that \emph{Functional Attention} can match state-of-the-art performance in many operator learning tasks, including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, while remaining robust to varying discretizations. Project page is available at https://github.com/xjffff/FUNCATTN.

</details>


### [283] [Effective Biological Representation Learning by Masking Gene Expression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31562)
*Kian Kenyon-Dean,Alina Selega,Ihab Bendidi,Jordan M. Sorokin,Luca Bertinetto,David Errington,Hayley Donnella,Oren Kraus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TxFM is a self-supervised masked autoencoder for RNA-seq data that emphasizes inductive representation learning; trained on DiverseRNA-1.4M; achieves high-quality gene representations that outperform FM baselines trained on atlas-data sizes >100x larger.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The performance gap between transcriptomic foundation models and simple baselines is likely due to technical noise, batch effects, and limited transfer ability. The work investigates whether inductive, self-supervised representation learning can yield superior gene representations for RNA-seq data.

Method: Proposes TxFM, a masked autoencoding model tailored for count-based RNA-seq data. Conducts ablation studies to identify architecture configurations critical for transfer performance. Curates DiverseRNA-1.4M as a training corpus. Evaluates inductive transfer performance against baselines.

Result: TxFM trained on DiverseRNA-1.4M yields high-fidelity gene representations that surpass foundation models trained on atlas-scale corpora more than 100x larger in transfer tasks. Ablation highlights key architectural choices for strong transfer.

Conclusion: Inductive self-supervised learning is a viable approach for transcriptomics representation when combined with careful architectural design and data curation; gains are achievable even against substantially larger FM baselines.

Abstract: RNA sequencing produces rich and diverse datasets of gene expression, offering compelling insights into cellular state and function that have many applications in drug discovery. Modeling such data is challenging due to inherent technical noise and experimental batch effects, as evidenced by many existing transcriptomic foundation models (FMs) underperforming relative to linear baselines. Such results raise the question of whether deep representation learning provides a distinct advantage over the direct use of raw transcript counts. Our work explores this by developing a new self-supervised model, TxFM, with a focus on inductive representation learning evaluations. TxFM employs a masked autoencoding approach tailored to diverse RNA-seq count data, and our ablation study empirically identifies crucial architecture configurations required for strong transfer performance. Additionally, we curate a public training corpus, DiverseRNA-1.4M, and find that TxFM trained on this curated dataset yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform FMs trained on atlas-scale corpora over 100x larger. Overall, our results indicate that inductive self-supervised learning is a viable modeling approach for transcriptomics representation, provided a careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation.

</details>


### [284] [Giving Sensors a Voice: Multimodal JEPA for Semantic Time-Series Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31580)
*Utsav Dutta,Gerardo Pastrana,Sina Khoshfetrat Pakazad,Henrik Ohlsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHARM introduces a channel-aware representation model for heterogeneous multivariate time series that uses channel-level text descriptions, a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order, and a JEPA-based training objective with latent-space prediction and description-aware gating, yielding robust, interpretable embeddings usable by a linear probe across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series, addressing sensitivity to channel order and lacking interpretability, while enabling cross-dataset generalization via textual channel descriptors.

Method: A Transformer encoder that is equivariant to channel ordering, integrated with channel-level textual descriptions. Training uses Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) plus a latent-space prediction objective and a description-aware gating mechanism to learn inter-channel relationships and robust embeddings.

Result: Embeddings achieve strong performance across anomaly detection, classification, and short-/long-term forecasting with only a linear probe. Performance is primarily driven by the JEPA objective and conditioning architecture; text descriptions act as channel identifiers enabling cross-dataset generalization.

Conclusion: Channel-aware conditioning with textual descriptors yields robust, interpretable representations for heterogeneous time series, transferable across datasets and tasks, with JEPA as the key driver of performance.

Abstract: Transformer-based architectures have advanced sequence modeling in language and vision, yet general-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series remains underexplored. We introduce CHARM (Channel-Aware Representation Model), which incorporates channel-level textual descriptions into a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order. CHARM is trained with a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and a novel loss promoting informative, temporally stable embeddings; latent-space prediction encourages robustness to sensor noise while description-aware gating provides interpretability through learned inter-channel relationships. Across anomaly detection, classification, and short- and long-term forecasting, the learned embeddings achieve strong performance using only a linear probe. Performance is driven primarily by the JEPA objective and conditioning architecture, with text descriptions serving as channel identifiers for cross-dataset generalization.

</details>


### [285] [A Tight Theory of Error Feedback Algorithms in Distributed Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31594)
*Daniel Berg Thomsen,Adrien Taylor,Aymeric Dieuleveut*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides tight convergence guarantees for two error-feedback gradient compression schemes (EF and EF21) in distributed optimization by deriving optimal step sizes and Lyapunov functions; guarantees are independent of the number of agents and align with the best single-agent rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication costs dominate distributed learning; gradient compression helps but harms convergence; error-feedback methods aim to fix this, yet their relative performance is unclear; the paper seeks to establish tight, comparative analyses.

Method: Constructs tailored Lyapunov functions for EF and EF21; derives optimal step-size rules; provides convergence proofs yielding tight bounds; analyses possibly under standard smoothness and convex/nonconvex assumptions; results cover multi-agent and reduce to single-agent optimal rates.

Result: Tight convergence guarantees for EF and EF21; optimal step sizes; optimal Lyapunov functions; independence from the number of agents; recover best single-agent guarantees; practical guidance on parameter choices.

Conclusion: EF and EF21 are each optimally robust to compression under the studied conditions; results unify understanding of their performance; potential follow-up includes stochastic settings, non-uniform data, asynchronous networks, and broader objective classes.

Abstract: Communication costs are a major bottleneck in distributed learning and first-order optimization. A common approach to alleviate this issue is to compress the gradient information exchanged between agents. However, such compression typically degrades the convergence guarantees of gradient-based methods. Error feedback mechanisms provide a simple and computationally cheap remedy for this issue, but numerous variants have been proposed, and their relative performance remains poorly understood. This paper provides tight convergence analyses for two of the main error-feedback algorithms from the literature, the classic Error Feedback method (EF) and Error Feedback 21 (EF21), by identifying optimal step-size choices and constructing optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each method. The results hold independently of the number of agents and recover the known best guarantees possible in the single-agent regime.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [286] [Structured interactions improve distributed coordination beyond model scaling in a real-world multi-robot system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30383)
*Junping Wang,Zhizhong Zhang,Yongqiang Tang,Geng Zheng,Jiaming Zhang,Shiji Song,Yanmei Li,Yushan Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Topology redesign via modular hierarchical communication yields much larger gains than increasing onboard model size in a real-world multi-robot task (47-point vs 9-point on 0-100 scale). Nested mixed-effects models favor topology over scale; results replicated; hardware results align with sim extrapolations showing saturation beyond 1024 hidden units.
