Generalization remains uncertain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess system-level design choices under fixed hardware budgets to determine whether communication topology or onboard model capacity provides larger performance gains in real-world multi-robot coordination.

Method: Empirical study with 10 physical robots (5 runs per condition, 60 total). Compare fully connected versus modular hierarchical interaction topologies. Compare doubling neural network hidden size. Use nested mixed-effects models to assess effects on performance. Validate findings via independent SMAC replications and heterogeneous benchmark reanalyses; include simulation-calibrated extrapolation to assess scaling and hardware relevance.

Result: Switching from fully connected to modular hierarchical topology improved normalized performance by 47 points on a 0–100 scale; doubling hidden size improved by at most 9 points. Nested mixed-effects models show larger improvement in model fit for topology than for scale. Pattern replicated in SMAC replications; reanalyses provide secondary consistency checks. Hardware results align with simulation-based extrapolations showing saturation beyond 1024 hidden units. Broad generalization remains unproven.

Conclusion: Interaction structure (communication topology) can dominate performance within the tested system and task. System-level design choices may trump increased onboard model size under fixed hardware budgets. Generalization to other tasks and broader settings requires further validation.

Abstract: Scaling individual robot capabilities is common but costly. Here we investigate a system-level design question in real-world multi-robot coordination: given matched hardware budgets, does restructuring communication among robots yield larger gains than increasing onboard model size? Using a representative transport-and-mapping task with 10 physical robots (5 runs per condition, 60 runs total), we find that switching from fully connected to modular hierarchical interactions improves normalised performance by 47 points (0--100), whereas doubling neural network hidden size yields at most 9 points. Nested mixed-effects model comparisons show a substantially larger improvement in model fit for topology than for scale. The pattern is confirmed in independent SMAC replications; heterogeneous benchmark reanalyses provide secondary supporting consistency checks rather than primary evidence. Performance saturation beyond 1024 hidden units is observed in simulation-calibrated extrapolation, not directly on hardware. These results indicate that interaction structure can play a dominant role within the tested system and task setting, while broader quantitative generalisation remains to be established.

</details>


### [287] [Learning-Based Navigation for Indoor Mobile Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30468)
*Tri-Tin Nguyen,Tien-Dat Nguyen,Gia-Uy Le,Vinh Nguyen,Vinh-Hao Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots combining a supervised neural global planner trained on cost-aware A* trajectories with a learning-based DWA local planner; policy trained by behavior cloning then refined by PPO with feasibility-aware masking; validated in simulation and real environments; source code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust, safe indoor navigation by integrating learning-based global planning with reinforcement-learning-refined local control, addressing limitations of purely model-based or purely learned components.

Method: Global planning: supervised neural global planner trained on cost-aware A* expert trajectories. Local planning: Learning-Based DWA local planner as discrete candidate selection over the DWA action lattice; policy learned via behavior cloning and refined with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under feasibility-aware masking. Evaluation in both simulated and real-world indoor environments.

Result: Experiments show feasible global routes and reliable local motion commands for safe goal-directed navigation in obstacle-rich environments, demonstrating the benefit of integrating learning-based global planning with RL-refined local control.

Conclusion: The integrated learning-based navigation framework is effective for indoor mobile robot navigation; source code will be released at the provided URL.

Abstract: This paper presents a learning-based navigation framework for indoor mobile robots. The proposed method combines a supervised neural global planner, trained from cost-aware A* expert trajectories, with the proposed Learning-Based DWA local planner, which is formulated as discrete candidate selection over the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) action lattice. For local planning, the policy is first trained by behavior cloning and then refined by Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under feasibility-aware masking. The framework is implemented and evaluated in both simulated and real-world indoor environments. Experimental results show that the proposed method generates feasible global routes and reliable local motion commands for safe goal-directed navigation in the presence of obstacles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating learning-based global planning with reinforcement-learning-refined local control for indoor mobile robot navigation. The source code will be released at https://ntdathp.github.io/rl_robot_web/.

</details>


### [288] [ELAN4D: Embodiment-Centric 4D Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Models via Plug-and-Play Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30484)
*Zeyuan He,Bowen Yang,Zhirui Fang,Keru Zhou,Lei Jiang,Jingjing Qian,Fan Mo,Junchi Yan,Philip Torr,Xiu Li,Li Jiang,Jialin Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training (ELAN4D) enhances VLA policies by injecting predicted 4D robot keypoint tracks as supervision, improving robustness to perturbations without changing the base policy at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reactive VLA policies regressing actions from current observations struggle to generalize under out-of-distribution perturbations due to lacking explicit future dynamics modeling.

Method: Compute 3D keypoint displacement tracks from forward kinematics using proprioceptive states. Introduce a lightweight track decoder as an auxiliary branch to inject this 4D supervision into the action expert while preserving the pretrained Vision-Language backbone via gradient isolation; the decoder is discarded at inference, leaving the base policy interface unchanged.

Result: On LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0 and real-world manipulation tasks, ELAN4D yields consistent improvements over strong VLA baselines, with best overall performance and substantial gains under perturbations such as camera, background, and layout shifts.

Conclusion: Embodiment-centric 4D supervision effectively improves robustness and generalization of manipulation policies by leveraging 4D embodiment signals derived from forward kinematics, without requiring external trackers or reconstruction at inference.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise for robotic manipulation, yet most existing policies operate reactively by directly regressing actions from current observations, without explicitly modeling future dynamics. This limits their ability to generalize under out-of-distribution perturbations. To address this issue, we propose ELAN4D, an embodiment-centric, 4D-aware training framework that enhances VLA policies with future robot keypoint tracks as predictive spatio-temporal supervision. Using only forward kinematics from proprioceptive states, we derive 3D displacement tracks of robot keypoints, such as joints and the end-effector, with negligible preprocess cost. These tracks provide metric and compact supervision without requiring external trackers or reconstruction. A plug-and-play auxiliary branch with a lightweight track decoder injects this 4D signal into the action expert while preserving the pretrained vision-language backbone through gradient isolation. The track decoder is discarded during inference, leaving the base policy interface unchanged. Extensive experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin2.0 and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that ELAN4D consistently improves over strong VLA baselines, achieving the best overall performance and substantial gains under out-of-distribution perturbations, including camera, background, and layout shifts. These results highlight the effectiveness of embodiment-centric 4D supervision for building more robust and generalizable manipulation policies.

</details>


### [289] [Caspar: CUDA Accelerator for Symbolic Programming with Adaptive Reordering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30583)
*Emil Martens,Aaron Miller,Matias Varnum,Annette Stahl*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Caspar is a symbolic-GPU optimization library that compiles Python-defined symbolic residuals into CUDA kernels to enable fast nonlinear optimization (e.g., bundle adjustment) on GPUs; it delivers significant speedups with lower memory and comparable accuracy, demonstrated on BAL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between expressive symbolic programming in Python and high-performance GPU runtimes in C++, enabling automatic generation of optimized GPU kernels for nonlinear optimization in robotics.

Method: Extend SymForce to Caspar, support symbolic residuals and Lie group operations, use symbolic differentiation to generate CUDA kernels, and provide a solver interface. Evaluate on BAL dataset, benchmarking against state-of-the-art bundle adjusters.

Result: Caspar achieves 5–20x speedups over the best alternatives, uses less memory, and attains similar accuracy on BAL bundle adjustment tasks.

Conclusion: Caspar validates the effectiveness of symbolic GPU programming for nonlinear optimization in robotics, is open-source as part of SymForce, and lowers the barrier to deploying GPU-accelerated optimization.

Abstract: We present Caspar, a library that makes the power of modern GPUs more accessible in robotics and provides a state-of-the-art nonlinear GPU solver that can be applied to a wide range of different optimization problems. Caspar bridges the gap between expressive symbolic programming in Python and high-performance GPU runtimes in C++ by automatically generating optimized CUDA kernels from symbolic expressions. Building on the SymForce library, users can easily define and combine symbolic expressions, including Lie group operations, to generate custom CUDA kernels. To use Caspar as a solver, users need only define the symbolic residual functions; Caspar then uses symbolic differentiation to generate the necessary GPU kernels and interfaces to perform nonlinear optimization.
  In this paper, we present the core components of Caspar and showcase its performance by performing bundle adjustment on the Bundle Adjustment in the Large (BAL) dataset. We benchmark Caspar against other state-of-the-art bundle adjusters and show that it is 5 to 20 times faster than the best alternative, requires less memory, and achieves similar accuracy. This illustrates the benefit of our symbolic GPU programming approach. Caspar is released as part of SymForce and is freely available at https://github.com/symforce-org/symforce

</details>


### [290] [Exploiting Chordal Sparsity for Globally Optimal Estimation with Factor Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30617)
*Avinash Subramanian,Connor Holmes,Timothy D. Barfoot,Frank Dellaert,Frederike Dümbgen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Auto-generates convex SDP relaxations for factor-graph state estimation within GTSAM and exploits Bayes-tree based chordal decomposition to achieve faster global estimation, demonstrated on ring-graph SLAM and chain-graph localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve global optimality guarantees in state estimation without the heavy cost of general SDP solvers, by automating relaxation construction and leveraging problem structure in a familiar framework (GTSAM).

Method: Integrates automatic SDP relaxation construction for common factor/variable types into GTSAM; uses Bayes-tree decompositions to factor the SDP according to chordal sparsity; tests on 3D pose-graph ring and 2D chain localization; releases code.

Result: Shows favorable scaling and speedup over local solvers for the tested cases; demonstrates feasibility of faster global estimation by exploiting structure.

Conclusion: Structure-exploiting SDP relaxations within a standard robotics framework offer practical global estimators, broadening robustness of perception/navigation, with caveats about SDP size and generalization to other factor types; code available.

Abstract: Robust and efficient state estimation is crucial for perception, navigation, and control in robotics. State estimation problems are conveniently modeled using the factor-graph framework as enabled by modern software packages such as GTSAM or g2o. However, the standard solvers included in such frameworks are local and may converge to poor local minima, posing significant safety concerns. Conversely, techniques based on convex relaxations have been shown to provide a means of globally solving or certifying many state estimation problems. However, these relaxations 1) often require substantial effort to formulate, and 2) may incur significantly higher cost compared to efficient local solvers, as they require solving a large semidefinite program (SDP). In this work, we address both shortcomings by 1) creating a new procedure within the GTSAM framework for automatically constructing convex SDP relaxations for any factor graphs with common factor and variable types, and by 2) exploiting the Bayes tree constructions native to GTSAM to decompose the SDP problem, leading to significant speedup in solver time for chordally sparse problems. We demonstrate the favorable scaling of this structure-exploiting global estimator compared to standard local solvers for two case studies: A 3D pose-graph SLAM problem with a ring factor graph and a 2D localization problem with a chain factor graph. The software framework is available at https://github.com/borglab/gtsam.

</details>


### [291] [Bidirectional Incremental Generalized Hybrid A*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30647)
*Sidharth Talia,Oren Salzman,Siddhartha Srinivasa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bi-IGHA* extends IGHA* by bidirectional search over a hierarchy of resolutions to reduce search expansions while preserving monotonic cost improvement and termination guarantees in kinodynamic planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient anytime kinodynamic planning for systems with complex dynamics in unstructured environments where precomputing motion primitives is infeasible. The curse of dimensionality and coupling between search and discretization in Hybrid A* can hinder performance; freezing vertices in IGHA* may hide solution-supporting vertices in some iterations.

Method: Extend IGHA* to a bidirectional setting (Bi-IGHA*). Conduct search over a hierarchy of discretization resolutions in both forward and backward directions, freezing vertices (not pruning) to maintain search progress while mitigating the hiding effect of frozen vertices. Preserve IGHA* guarantees on monotonic cost improvement and termination.

Result: Empirical evaluation on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems shows substantially reduced expansions. Bi-IGHA* achieves equivalent closed-loop performance to kinodynamic planning for high-speed off-road autonomy while requiring significantly fewer expansions.

Conclusion: Bi-IGHA* provides improved computational efficiency for anytime kinodynamic planning in high-dimensional, unstructured environments by combining bidirectional search with hierarchical resolution management, preserving theoretical guarantees and reducing search effort.

Abstract: We focus on the problem of efficient anytime kinodynamic planning for systems with complex dynamics in unstructured environments that make precomputing motion primitives infeasible. Directly applying A* to such problems is computationally infeasible due to the curse of dimensionality. Methods such as Hybrid A* addressed this burden by discretizing the state space, but in turn creating a coupling between tree discovery and the discretization resolution. The Incremental Generalized Hybrid A* (IGHA*) performs search over a hierarchy of resolutions in an anytime fashion to break this coupling, by freezing vertices to use in later search iterations rather than pruning them. However, the frozen vertices can hide solution-supporting vertices from the search at a particular iteration. While classical bidirectional search is motivated by the reduction of search depth, extending IGHA* into the bidirectional setting (termed Bi-IGHA*) obtains additional benefit by fundamentally mitigating the behaviour induced by frozen vertices hiding solutions. We show that Bi-IGHA* preserves IGHA*'s guarantees on monotonic cost improvement and termination. We empirically show that Bi-IGHA* substantially reduces expansions on R3, R4, and R6 planning problems, and achieves equivalent closed-loop performance with kinodynamic planning for high-speed off-road autonomy while requiring significantly fewer expansions. Website: https://personalrobotics.github.io/IGHAStar/biighastar.html

</details>


### [292] [Primitive Subspaces Mediate Few-Shot Transfer in VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30695)
*Anya Singh,Cabrel Happi,Jai Relan,Varun Nair,Vidyut Baradwaj*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Primitive-aware training yields a reusable sub-skill library that enables few-shot transfer for VLA policies, achieving ~3x better sample efficiency than flat training across tasks, seeds, and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial VLA policies require rapid acquisition of new tasks with minimal fine-tuning; learning transferable primitive sub-skills could enable zero- to few-shot generalization without weight updates.

Method: Train two VLA architectures (OpenVLA and π0.5) with matched LoRA fine-tuning on REASSEMBLE; compare flat trajectories vs primitive-segmented episodes with language prompts; hold out 6 object-task combos; evaluate with m demonstrations (0,1,3,5,10) without updates; replicate across seeds and test on LIBERO-Long; ablate primitive-decodable subspace; audit evaluation methodology for chunked policies (action-range gates).

Result: Primitive-trained models reach 78% of fine-tuned upper bound with m=3 demonstrations; flat-trained models require m=10 for the same level; consistent across seeds, architectures, datasets; causal ablation shows removing primitive subspace degrades transfer by 32 percentage points, while random subspace has no effect; identified and corrected a methodological pitfall inflating single-step gate failure rates.

Conclusion: Primitive representations function as a causal, transferable sub-skill library that improves sample efficiency for unseen tasks in VLA policies; careful evaluation of chunked policies is essential to avoid inflated failure rates; results generalize across multiple architectures and datasets.

Abstract: Deploying vision-language-action (VLA) policies in industrial environments requires the ability to teach new tasks at low cost, a property current VLAs lack, since each new task requires fine-tuning. We investigate whether primitive-aware training produces a transferable artifact: a learned library of sub-skills that can be composed at inference time, conditioned on a small number of demonstrations, to perform tasks the policy was never trained on. We train two VLA architectures with different inductive biases, OpenVLA and $π_{0.5}$, on the REASSEMBLE contact-rich assembly dataset under matched LoRA fine-tuning recipes and locked hyperparameters, varying training between flat trajectories and primitive-segmented episodes with primitive-specific language prompts. We hold out 6 object-task combinations from training and evaluate few-shot transfer: models receive $m \in \{0, 1, 3, 5, 10\}$ demonstrations of a held-out task and attempt execution without weight updates. We replicate across three training seeds and validate on a second dataset (LIBERO-Long). Primitive-trained models reach 78% of fine-tuned upper-bound performance with only m=3 demonstrations, while flat-trained models require m=10 demonstrations to reach the same level -- a $3\times$ sample efficiency gap that replicates across seeds, architectures, and datasets. To establish causation, we ablate the primitive-decodable subspace of hidden states and show few-shot transfer degrades by 32 percentage points while ablating a random subspace of equal dimensionality has no effect, indicating primitive representations are causally necessary rather than incidentally correlated with transfer. We identify and correct a methodological pitfall in evaluating chunked policies: family-wise inflation of single-step action-range gates produces order-of-magnitude higher false-failure rates against ground-truth human demonstrations.

</details>


### [293] [Geometry-Aware Control Barrier Functions for Collision Avoidance via Bernstein Polynomial Approximations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30696)
*Siwon Jo,Yanze Zhang,Yupeng Yang,Wenhao Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A geometry-aware CBF using Bernstein polynomial SDFs to unify robot/obstacle representations and enable differentiable barrier constraints for safe navigation in single- and multi-robot scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome conservatism and high constraint counts of simple shape surrogates in unstructured environments by adopting a unified, differentiable obstacle representation.

Method: Model robots and obstacles with Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs); formulate a unified minimum-distance CBF; exploit Bernstein polynomial differentiability to enforce control constraints in closed-loop; validate via simulations in single and heterogeneous multi-robot settings.

Result: Demonstrates efficient computation and effective safety guarantees in simulations for both single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance.

Conclusion: BP-SDF-based geometry-aware CBF provides a unified, differentiable barrier representation that enables safe, real-time control in complex, unstructured scenes.

Abstract: Safe navigation often relies on well-defined conditions based on the shape of robots and obstacles, and can be challenging when they have irregular geometries. While Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) offer an efficient mechanism to enforce safe set forward invariance, common shape surrogates (e.g., spheres or super-ellipsoids) either are overly conservative in unstructured scenes or require many local primitives, which inflates constraint counts and degrades real-time performance. In this paper, we introduce a novel geometry-aware Control Barrier Function (CBF) based on Bernstein-Polynomial Signed Distance Fields (BP-SDFs). It provides a unified way to represent the obstacles and robots, so as to represent the barrier function with a unified minimum distance. Benefiting from the differentiability of the Bernstein polynomials, one can easily enforce the control constraints in a closed loop. We validate the method's efficiency and performance to guarantee safety in single-robot navigation and heterogeneous multi-robot collision avoidance via simulations under different environments.

</details>


### [294] [GSAM: A Generalizable and Safe Robotic Framework for Articulated Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30740)
*Beichen Shao,Mengying Xie,Heng Su,Wanyi Zhang,Mingyan Li,Yan Ding,Fausto Giunchiglia,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GSAM is a generalizable and safe framework for articulated-object manipulation that combines vision-based perception, a fine-tuned VLM refiner with chain-of-thought reasoning, an interaction-constraint generator, LLM-guided constraint functionalization for planning, and a kinematic-aware planner; it shows improved generalization and safety across diverse hinge tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limited generalization and collision risks caused by diverse articulated objects and complex end-effector/handle interactions in manipulation tasks.

Method: 1) Vision-based perceiver outputs initial kinematic parameters. 2) Fine-tuned VLM refiner uses chain-of-thought commonsense reasoning to refine perception. 3) Interaction constraint function generator integrates articulated object, interaction pose, and obstacle avoidance into a base. 4) LLM functionalizes these constraints and applies them to trajectory and posture planning. 5) A kinematic-aware manipulation planner verifies reachability for trajectory and posture. 6) Experimental evaluation on 50 hinge tasks across 5 object categories with 50 random end-effector–handle configurations.

Result: GSAM reduces perception dispersion by 3.1% and increases manipulation success rate by 36.0% relative to the best baseline.

Conclusion: GSAM demonstrates superior object generalization and interaction safety in practical articulated-object manipulation scenarios.

Abstract: Articulated object manipulation is a unique challenge for service robots. Existing methods employ end-to-end policy learning, visionmotion planning, and large-language/visual-language model (LLM/VLM), but often overlook the diversity of articulated objects and the complexity of interactions between end-effector and handle, leading to limited generalization and destructive collisions. To address this, we propose GSAM, a generalizable and safe robotic framework for articulated object manipulation. Specifically, a vision-based perceiver generates the kinematic parameters. Considering that pre-trained markers in perceiver yield raw estimations that may deviate from commonsense, we present a f ine-tuned VLM-based refiner, using chain-of-thought (COT) commonsense reasoning to refine perception. To prevent destructive collisions, we design an interaction constraint function generator, integrating articulated object, interaction pose, and obstacle avoidance knowledge into a base. LLM then functionalize these constraints and apply them to trajectory and posture planning. A kinematic-aware manipulation planner verifies reachability for trajectory and posture. Experiments on 50 hinge tasks across 5 object categories and 50 randomly initialized end-effectorhandle configurations show that GSAM reduces standard deviation by 3.1% and improves manipulation success rate by 36.0% compared to the best baseline, respectively demonstrating the superior object generalization and interaction safety of GSAM in practical scenarios.

</details>


### [295] [SSR: Scaling Surefooted and Symmetric Humanoid Traversal to the Open World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30770)
*Ruiqi Yu,Yiwen Wang,Yuan Hao,Jun WU,Qiuguo Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end SSR framework for egocentric vision-based humanoid traversal using imagined foothold guidance, equivariant latent-space augmentation, and multi-discriminator priors to ensure safe, stable, human-like locomotion across diverse terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable practical deployment of humanoid robots in open human environments by addressing vision-based planning, dynamic motion, and natural behavior across heterogeneous terrains.

Method: Three components: (1) imagined foothold guidance predicting forthcoming swing-foot contacts and evaluating their support to guide pre-touchdown swings to stable regions; (2) equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation to induce bilateral coordination under high-dimensional visual observations; (3) terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to encourage human-like behavior across scenes; evaluated end-to-end on real-world terrains including stairs with varied structures, wide gaps, and high platforms; demonstrates safe, stable locomotion and long-horizon traversal.

Result: Demonstrates safe, stable, high-quality locomotion on diverse real-world terrains; robust to stairs with varied structures, wide gaps, high platforms; supports reliable long-horizon traversal in open outdoor environments.

Conclusion: SSR provides an efficient, end-to-end framework for vision-based humanoid traversal that achieves practical deployment by integrating foothold-informed swing planning, symmetry-aware coordination, and scenario-adaptive motion priors, enabling natural and reliable locomotion across open-world terrains.

Abstract: Extending humanoid traversal to the open world is key to practical deployment in human environments, but remains challenging. The robot must use vision to ensure safe and reliable foot placement on heterogeneous terrain under highly dynamic motion, while producing coordinated, natural whole-body behaviors. We propose SSR, an efficient end-to-end framework for egocentric vision-based humanoid traversal that jointly learns these capabilities. SSR introduces imagined foothold guidance, which learns to model forthcoming swing-foot contacts and evaluates their support to guide pre-touchdown swings toward stable regions, reducing edge slips. It further employs equivariant latent-space symmetry augmentation to efficiently induce bilateral coordination under high-dimensional visual observations, and uses terrain-specific multi-discriminator motion priors to encourage human-like behavior across scenes. Extensive experiments show that SSR achieves safe, stable, and high-quality locomotion on diverse real-world terrains, including stairs with varied structures and extreme challenges such as wide gaps and high platforms, while enabling reliable long-horizon traversal in open outdoor environments.

</details>


### [296] [Object-Informed Model Predictive Path Integral Control for Non-Prehensile Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30778)
*Nikola Raicevic,Bharath Raam Radhakrishnan,Chenbin Yu,Ki Myung Brian Lee,Nikolay Atanasov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical MPPI framework uses an object-level plan to steer robot-level planning for long-horizon non-prehensile manipulation, improving success and computation efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon planning for non-prehensile, underactuated, and discontinuous interactions is challenging; decoupling object dynamics from robot dynamics enables efficient, non-myopic planning.

Method: Solve a simplified object-only plan assuming direct object actuation; use the object trajectory as reference for joint robot-object planning via a hierarchical MPPI; evaluate on a 6-DoF xArm6 pushing task with static obstacles.

Result: Simulation: 40% higher task success and 26% faster control frequency. Real hardware: 20% improvement with comparable computation to regular MPPI.

Conclusion: Object-informed hierarchical MPPI enhances long-horizon planning efficiency and success in non-prehensile manipulation, demonstrated with hardware validation and modest computational overhead.

Abstract: Long-horizon planning for non-prehensile robot manipulation is challenging due to underactuated and discontinuous interactions. We propose a hierarchical formulation of model predictive path integral (MPPI) control that guides robot-level planning with a separately computed object-level plan to achieve efficient long-horizon prediction. We first solve a simplified object-only problem, assuming the object can be actuated directly, and use the planned object trajectory as a reference in solving the joint robot-object planning problem. We evaluate our method in both simulation and hardware using a 6-DoF xArm6 manipulator to perform object pushing tasks in which the target object must reach a goal while avoiding static obstacles, necessitating non-myopic reasoning. Our object-informed MPPI increases task success by 40\% with a 26\% faster control frequency in simulation, and by 20\% in real experiments with similar computation as regular MPPI.

</details>


### [297] [Two Degree-of-Freedom Vibratory Transport in a Grasp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30780)
*C. L. Yako,Shenli Yuan,Kenneth Salisbury*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Demonstrates two-degree-of-freedom in-hand manipulation of grasped parts using asymmetric vibrations. A closed-loop moving surface creates periodic stick-slip waves; analytical relations link sticking/slipping accelerations to mean part velocity against gravity; experiments validate with controlled squeeze force and high-resolution encoder. Introduces a 2-DoF vibratory surface (translation and rotation) and uses two such surfaces in a parallel jaw gripper to bidirectionally translate and rotate grasped parts; the same waveform trends apply to rotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable dexterous, robust in-hand manipulation of grasped objects using vibro-friction (stick-slip) dynamics, with simple, actuator-efficient 2-DoF manipulation.

Method: Model stick-slip dynamics under periodic stick-slip waveforms generated by a closed-loop actuated moving surface. Analyze how sticking and slipping accelerations affect average velocity against gravity. Validate analytically with experiments where squeeze force is controlled and part motion is measured by a high-resolution encoder. Develop a 2-DoF vibratory surface (translation + rotation about the surface normal). Combine two such surfaces in a parallel jaw gripper to achieve bidirectional translation and rotation of grasped parts; test that translation trends extend to in-plane rotation.

Result: Analytical trends predicted; experimental results validate the impact of stick/slip accelerations on velocity; the 2-DoF vibratory surfaces enable both translation and rotation; two-surface gripper achieves bidirectional manipulation; waveform trends persist for in-plane rotation.

Conclusion: Asymmetric vibratory actuation provides a feasible 2-DoF in-hand manipulation mechanism through vibro-friction; oscillator parameters govern average motion, and the approach extends to rotational as well as translational motions with multi-surface grippers.

Abstract: In this paper, we use asymmetric vibrations to demonstrate two degree-of-freedom (DoF) in-hand manipulation of grasped parts. The asymmetric vibrations are achieved through closed-loop position control of a moving surface, which applies a periodic stick-slip waveform to the part to be manipulated. We show analytically how two vibratory waveform parameters, the sticking acceleration and the slipping acceleration, affect average part velocity when moving against gravity. The theoretical trends are then validated using an experimental setup where the squeeze force is controlled and part motion is recorded by a high-resolution encoder. We also develop a 2-DoF vibratory surface capable of translation in one direction and rotation about the surface normal. Using two of these 2-DoF surfaces in a parallel jaw gripper configuration, we bidirectionally translate and rotate a variety of grasped parts, as well as demonstrate that the same waveform trends for translation also persist for in-plane rotation.

</details>


### [298] [Feat2Go: Visual Feature-Grounded Value Estimation for Embodied Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30795)
*Junyang Shu,Zhiwei Lin,Bingqing Wei,Yongtao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Feat2Go introduces a fine-grained value estimation approach for embodied RL in VLA models. It derives a continuous progress signal from a pretrained visual world model, partitions episodes into semantic stages, and trains a value model to predict progress to reshape rewards, improving PPO/GRPO pipelines without manual reward engineering. Empirical gains on ManiSkill3 and RoboTwin 2.0 are substantial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address sparse supervision and difficult reward design in long-horizon VLA tasks; enable informative shaping signals without manual engineering.

Method: Compute patch-level similarity to subgoal states to obtain a continuous progress target; segment episodes via trend-based clustering into semantic stages; train a value model to predict this progress from current observation and instruction; use predicted value to reshape terminal rewards during policy optimization; plug into existing PPO/GRPO pipelines.

Result: Significant performance gains: on ManiSkill3, OpenVLAOFT improves OOD success from 17.5% to 82.9% (96.9% ID); on RoboTwin 2.0, average success 88.8% in domain-randomized tasks; consistent improvements in single-arm and bimanual settings.

Conclusion: Feat2Go demonstrates that fine-grained structure-aware value estimation can enhance VLA RL without manual reward shaping and is compatible with standard policy learners.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is a promising approach for improving the capabilities of vision-language-action (VLA) models while avoiding the heavy data requirements of imitation learning. However, its effectiveness for VLA models is often constrained by sparse supervision and the difficulty of designing informative reward signals for long-horizon manipulation. In this work, we present Feat2Go, a fine-grained value estimation framework for embodied reinforcement learning. Specifically, Feat2Go first derives a continuous progress target from a pretrained visual world model by measuring patch-level similarity to subgoal states and partitioning episodes into semantic stages with trend-based clustering. We then train an embodied value model to predict this structural progress from the current observation and task instruction, and use the predicted value to reshape terminal rewards during policy optimization. The proposed framework is compatible with existing VLA policy reinforcement learning pipelines, including PPO and GRPO, and does not rely on manual reward engineering. Extensive experiments on ManiSkill3 and RoboTwin 2.0 demonstrate that Feat2Go consistently improves the performance of existing VLA models under both single-arm and bimanual manipulation settings. More specifically, on ManiSkill3, Feat2Go improves OpenVLAOFT from 17.5% to 82.9% average out-of-distribution success while retaining 96.9% in-distribution performance. On RoboTwin 2.0, Feat2Go achieves an average success rate of 88.8% in domain-randomized task settings, outperforming prior reinforcement learning methods.

</details>


### [299] [Hide-and-Seek in Trajectories: Discovering Failure Signals for VLA Runtime Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30834)
*Seongheon Park,Wendi Li,Changdae Oh,Samuel Yeh,Zsolt Kira,Michael Hagenow,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes Hide-and-Seek, a coarsely supervised failure-detection framework for Vision-Language-Action robots that localizes failures without step-level labels via inter- and intra-trajectory contrastive learning; achieves state-of-the-art detection with favorable accuracy–timeliness trade-offs and strong generalization across seen/unseen tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA systems exhibit failure modes in real-world deployment. Existing detection methods are either computationally expensive (action resampling) or rely on external models, and uniform trajectory-level labeling obscures localized failure signals. There is a need for lightweight, localized, temporally structured failure signals derived from trajectory-level supervision.

Method: Hide-and-Seek uses inter-trajectory and intra-trajectory contrastive objectives to infer temporally structured, localized failure signals from trajectory-level supervision without step-level annotations. It localizes failure-indicative actions within trajectories and provides a misbehavior signal suitable for conformal prediction. Evaluated on LIBERO, VLABench, and a real robotic platform across OpenVLA, π0, and π0.5 policies.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art multi-task failure detection performance with a favorable accuracy–timeliness trade-off under conformal prediction. Demonstrates strong generalization to seen and unseen tasks across multiple datasets and policies.

Conclusion: Hide-and-Seek offers an annotation-light, effective solution for VLA failure detection, enabling robust deployment by producing localized, temporally structured failure signals and maintaining performance under conformal prediction across diverse tasks and policies.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to follow natural language instructions and generalize across diverse tasks, but they remain vulnerable to execution failures that compromise reliability in real-world deployment. Detecting such failures during execution is therefore critical for the robust deployment of embodied systems. Existing failure detection methods either rely on expensive action resampling or external models, while alternatives propagate trajectory-level labels uniformly across every timestep, obscuring localized failure signals. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Hide-and-Seek}, a framework that formulates VLA failure detection as a coarsely supervised learning problem. By combining inter-trajectory and intra-trajectory contrastive objectives, Hide-and-Seek localizes failure-indicative actions and induces temporally structured failure signals from trajectory-level supervision alone, without any step-level annotation. We evaluate Hide-and-Seek on LIBERO, VLABench, and a real-world robotic platform across three representative VLA policies: OpenVLA, $π_0$, and $π_{0.5}$.Our method achieves state-of-the-art multi-task failure detection performance with a practical accuracy--timeliness trade-off under conformal prediction, and generalizes well to both seen and unseen tasks.

</details>


### [300] [High-Load-Density Electro-Permanent Magnetic Foot with Controllable Adhesion for Quadruped Wall-Climbing Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30849)
*An Li,Bo Tao,I-Ming Chen,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A CHN-EPM adhesion unit with flux-concentrating 3D magnetic circuit enables high-load, robust adhesion for quadruped climbing on ferromagnetic surfaces, achieving >1000 N adhesion and >200:1 L/W, with magnetization control and contact-force feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable, robust climbing locomotion of quadruped robots on ferromagnetic surfaces despite air-gap variations and partial contact.

Method: Develop circular Halbach-net electro-permanent magnets (CHN-EPM) with a 3D flux-concentrating magnetic circuit; implement a magnetization driver with a two-stage pulse current control; integrate a flexible pressure sensor for contact force feedback; deploy on a commercial quadruped (Unitree GO2) and validate on ceilings, vertical walls and various ferromagnetic surfaces.

Result: Maximum adhesion force >1000 N; load-to-weight ratio >200:1; adhesion maintained under partial contact and uncertain contact; robust adhesion switching demonstrated.

Conclusion: The CHN-EPM with flux-concentration design and force-feedback control enables reliable high-load adhesion for climbing on ferromagnetic surfaces, enabling stable locomotion on complex surfaces such as painted, perforated, and curved ferromagnetic substrates.

Abstract: To enable reliable climbing locomotion of quadruped robots on ferromagnetic surfaces, this paper presents a high-load-density electro-permanent magnetic foot with controllable adhesion, featuring force-feedback circular Halbach-net electro-permanent magnet (CHN-EPM) adhesion units and a magnetization control system. Due to its three-dimensional magnetic circuit structure and flux-concentration effect, the CHN-EPM enables a distributed parallel magnetic flux path with enhanced flux utilization, resulting in reduced sensitivity to air-gap variations and allowing effective adhesion to be maintained even under partial contact conditions. The proposed CHN-EPM generates a maximum adhesion force exceeding 1000 N with a load-to-weight ratio over 200:1. A magnetization driver and a two-stage pulse current control strategy are developed to regulate the excitation current amplitude and duration, enabling accurate and reliable magnetization. By incorporating a flexible pressure sensor for contact force feedback, the system can effectively monitor attachment and detachment states, ensuring robust adhesion switching under uncertain contact conditions. The proposed system is integrated into a commercial quadruped robot (Unitree GO2), demonstrating high-load adhesion on ceiling and vertical-wall surfaces and stable locomotion on painted, perforated, and curved ferromagnetic surfaces.

</details>


### [301] [Wall-OSS-0.5 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30877)
*Ryan Yu,Pushi Zhang,Starrick Liu,Brae Liu,Miracle Kang,Shalfun Li,Lights Shi,Ellie Ma,Ping Yang,Chris Pan,Jerry Chen,Dongxiu Liu,Rain Sun,Miles Guo,Byron Zhang,Hugo Zhou,Zach Xu,Vincent Chen,Harrison Huang,James Wang,Dance Kuzi,Andy Zhai,Hang Su,Roy Gan,Lucy Liang,Hao Wang,Qian Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 4B VLA model pretrained across 20+ embodied Robo setups demonstrates zero-shot real-robot behavior and improved fine-tuned performance on 15 tasks, indicating VLA pretraining provides actionable robotic capability, not just better initialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether large-scale VLA pretraining yields executable robotic behavior on real hardware and generalizes across embodiments, rather than serving solely as an initialization for downstream learning.

Method: Gradient-bridged co-training with three objectives: discrete action prediction (injects VLM-grade gradients into the backbone), multimodal prediction (preserves grounded vision-language understanding), and continuous flow matching (defines deployment-time action interface). Pretraining spans >20 embodiments with ~1M trajectories per epoch, using a 3B VLM backbone plus action-generation components for a 4B VLA setup.

Result: Before fine-tuning, the model exhibits non-trivial zero-shot real-robot behavior, solving tasks including a held-out deformable manipulation task with high progress on a 17-task suite. After fine-tuning, average task progress reaches 60.5% on 15 real-robot tasks, outperforming π_0.5 by 17.5%. Multimodal evaluation indicates action training preserves broad V-L abilities while strengthening embodied grounding.

Conclusion: VLA pretraining can be directly tested on hardware and serves as a substantive source of robot capability, reframing pretraining from a mere initialization to an actionable foundation for robot policy.

Abstract: Large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) pretraining is increasingly adopted as the foundation for robot policies, yet the evidence for pretrained VLAs is almost invariably reported after task-specific fine-tuning. This leaves a foundational question unanswered: does VLA pretraining itself yield executable robot behavior, or does it merely furnish a better initialization for downstream policy learning? We present Wall-OSS-0.5, an open-source 4B VLA built upon a 3B VLM backbone augmented with action-generation components, designed so that pretrained robotic capability is directly measurable on physical hardware. The model is pretrained across more than 20 embodiments, processing over one million robot trajectories per epoch alongside a grounded multimodal corpus. We adopt a gradient-bridged co-training recipe in which three objectives play distinct and complementary roles: discrete action prediction routes strong VLM-native gradients into the backbone, multimodal prediction preserves grounded vision-language understanding, and continuous flow matching serves as the deployment-time action interface. Before task-specific fine-tuning, the pretrained checkpoint achieves non-trivial zero-shot real-robot behavior, completing several tasks, including a held-out deformable manipulation task, at high task progress on a 17-task suite. After fine-tuning, the same checkpoint serves as a stronger adaptation prior, reaching 60.5% average task progress on 15 real-robot tasks and outperforming π_0.5 by 17.5%. Multimodal evaluations further confirm that action training does not erode grounded vision-language competence: the model preserves broad vision-language ability while strengthening embodied grounding. Together, these results reposition VLA pretraining from an initialization strategy to a directly testable, already useful source of robot capability.

</details>


### [302] [Trajectory Planning for Non-Communicating Mobile Robots using Inverse Optimal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30906)
*Nina Majer,Yannick Epple,Xin Ye,Stefan Schwab,Sören Hohmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A combined trajectory planning and prediction framework for non-communicating mobile robots using inverse optimal control to infer goals and joint prediction to improve planning; results show 9.8% faster median time to goals and robust solvability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient collision-avoiding interaction among non-communicating mobile robots with unknown goals.

Method: Use inverse optimal control to estimate each robot's unknown goal from past trajectories. Each robot accounts for others by self-prediction and solves a joint prediction problem using the estimated goals. Predictions are then used for planning.

Result: In 2–8 robot simulations, the median time to all robots reaching goals improved by 9.8% over planning with constant-acceleration estimated goals; the planner/predictor never fails to find a solution.

Conclusion: The approach yields improved planning efficiency and reliability in multi-robot collision avoidance by integrating goal inference with multi-agent prediction.

Abstract: To enable an efficient interaction of non-communicating mobile robots in collision avoidance scenarios, we present a novel combined trajectory planning and prediction algorithm. Inverse optimal control is used to estimate unknown goal states of all robots based on observed past trajectories. Each robot also takes the perspective of other robots in considering self-prediction and solves a joint prediction problem using the estimated goal states. The resulting predictions are then considered for planning. Simulation results of scenarios with 2-8 robots show that the median of the durations until all vehicles reach their goals is 9.8 % faster compared to planning with constant acceleration based estimated goal states. Moreover, the proposed approach never leads to the solver being unable to find a solution to the planning or prediction problem.

</details>


### [303] [Enhancing Human-Likeness in Reinforcement Learning Agents via Hierarchical Macro Action Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30928)
*Usman Nizamani,M. Shaheer Luqman,Fawad Javed Fateh,Ali Shah Ali,Murad Popattia,M. Zeeshan Zia,Quoc-Huy Tran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces HiMAQ, a hierarchical macro action quantization framework for human-like RL that encodes human demonstrations into two-level vector-quantized macro actions to improve human-likeness without sacrificing reward performance; validated on D4RL and generalizes across IQL, SAC, and RLPD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between human-like interpretability and reward-driven RL performance by learning action sequences aligned with human behaviors, enhancing interpretability and reliability.

Method: Two-stage vector quantization (HiMAQ) that encodes actions into macro actions: a lower level maps actions to fine-grained subaction clusters, a higher level aggregates subactions into action clusters, forming hierarchical macro actions. The human-like RL framework is evaluated against a non-hierarchical MAQ baseline and tested across RL algorithms (IQL, SAC, RLPD) on D4RL benchmarks.

Result: The hierarchical HiMAQ approach achieves higher human-likeness scores than the non-hierarchical MAQ baseline while maintaining comparable or better success rates. Improvements generalize across multiple RL algorithms (IQL, SAC, RLPD) and datasets (D4RL).

Conclusion: Hierarchical macro action quantization enables learning action sequences that closely resemble human behavior without sacrificing reward optimization, offering improved interpretability and reliability and broad applicability across RL algorithms.

Abstract: Human-like agents are a long-standing goal of artificial intelligence. Despite strong performance, most reinforcement learning (RL) agents remain reward-driven and often exhibit behaviors that differ from humans, limiting interpretability and reliability. In this work, we introduce a novel human-like RL framework that predicts action sequences closely aligned with human behaviors while maximizing rewards. Specifically, we encode human demonstrations into macro actions using a hierarchical macro action quantization approach (termed HiMAQ) consisting of two successive levels of vector quantization. The lower quantization level maps input actions to fine-grained subaction clusters, while the higher quantization level aggregates these subaction clusters into action clusters. Extensive evaluations on the D4RL benchmarks show that our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline (MAQ), achieving better human-likeness scores while maintaining comparable or better success rates than previous RL agents. The improvements generalize across integrations with various RL algorithms, namely IQL, SAC, and RLPD.

</details>


### [304] [RDGen: Demonstration Generation for High-Quality Robot Learning via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30957)
*Zijian Zhu,Menglin Zou,Zhuang Li,Yaojie Tu,Xinhai Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RDGen uses sim-to-real RL to generate high-quality robot demonstrations for VLA training, yielding smoother trajectories and better downstream performance than teleoperation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality robot trajectory data are scarce and teleoperation is labor-intensive, costly, and hard to scale; scalable, reliable supervisory signals are needed for VLA training.

Method: Leverage an RL policy trained in simulation as a trajectory generator, with a VLM-based task parser for identifying task-relevant objects and a Grounding DINO-based localizer for object localization; transfer the RL policy to a real robot to collect rollouts as demonstrations and use simulation to additionally generate trajectories at low cost.

Result: On a pick-and-place task, the transferred RL policy achieves a high task success rate; RDGen-produced demonstrations are significantly smoother than teleoperation and lead to superior downstream VLA performance.

Conclusion: RL-generated demonstrations can serve as more reliable and consistent supervisory signals for robot policy learning, enabling scalable, high-quality data for VLA training via sim-to-real RL.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for general-purpose robot control. However, their performance remains fundamentally constrained by the availability of high-quality robot trajectory data. In current robot learning practice, such data are primarily collected through human teleoperation, which is labor-intensive, costly, and difficult to scale. In this paper, we propose RDGen, a sim-to-real reinforcement learning framework for generating high-quality robot demonstrations. Rather than employing reinforcement learning solely as the final control policy, RDGen leverages trained RL policies as a structured trajectory generator. The system consists of a VLM-based task parser that identifies task-relevant objects, a Grounding DINO-based object localizer, and an RL policy transferred from simulation to the real robot. Successful rollouts are then harvested as clean, high-quality demonstrations for downstream VLA training, while the simulation stage further provides a scalable source of additional trajectories at little marginal cost. Experiments on a pick-and-place task demonstrate that the transferred RL policy achieves a high task success rate. Compared with human teleoperation, RDGen produces significantly smoother trajectories and yields superior downstream VLA performance. These results indicate that RL-generated demonstrations can serve as more reliable and consistent supervisory signals for robot policy learning.

</details>


### [305] [A study on a Real-Time VR-Based Teleoperation Framework for Manipulator in Dynamic Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30989)
*InGyu Choi,GeonYeong Go,SunWoo Ahn,HyoJae Kang,Min-Sung Kang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A VR teleoperation framework enabling real-time, collision-aware manipulation of a 7-DoF manipulator by integrating GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization within a VR interface, capable of handling static and moving obstacles with low latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VR teleoperation research often focuses on data collection for imitation learning and neglects dynamic obstacles, collision risks, and operator safety in real-time deployment.

Method: Integrate GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization into a VR interface to generate feasible joint commands at each control cycle under robot constraints; evaluate in three environments (obstacle-free, static obstacle, moving obstacle) with a 7-DoF arm.

Result: Demonstrates stable online behavior and collision-aware motion generation; commands align with operator input while producing safe detours around interfering obstacles across all three scenarios.

Conclusion: The framework provides real-time, collision-aware VR teleoperation that preserves operator intent and improves safety in dynamic environments; potential for safe deployment in hazardous settings.

Abstract: Robot teleoperation enables safe, non-contact task execution in hazardous environments where direct human access is difficult, and its application has expanded with recent VR technologies. Many VR teleoperation studies, however, have primarily served as data-collection tools for robot imitation learning, so they often do not explicitly address dynamic obstacles, workspace changes, or collision risks during operation. For real deployment aimed at operator safety, teleoperation must react to dynamic situations with low latency and remain robust to mistakes made by inexperienced operators. This paper presents a VR teleoperation framework that supports real-time manipulation while handling collisions with both static and moving obstacles. The framework integrates GPU-accelerated inverse kinematics and trajectory optimization within a VR interface to generate feasible joint commands at each control cycle under robot constraints. Experiments with a 7-DoF manipulator demonstrate stable online behavior and collision-aware motion generation across three scenarios: obstacle-free, static-obstacle, and moving-obstacle environments. The results indicate that the proposed approach generates motion consistent with the operator's command while producing safe detours when obstacles interfere with the commanded path.

</details>


### [306] [Can Aerial VLA Models Cooperate? Evaluating Closed-Loop Air-Ground Coordination with CARLA-Air](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31066)
*Tianle Zeng,Yanci Wen,Xueang Yu,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Aerial VLA models show limited transfer to cooperative air-ground tasks; CARLA-Air enables unified evaluation; results reveal need for explicit partner grounding, fast coordination, and team-level objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if single-UAV vision-language-action capabilities transfer to cooperative air-ground tasks and to establish a physically consistent evaluation platform for UAV-UGV collaboration.

Method: Introduce CARLA-Air, unifying CARLA and AirSim inside Unreal Engine with shared world state, physics tick, and sensing to enable physically consistent UAV–UGV interaction. Evaluate representative aerial VLA and planning baselines on two diagnostic tasks (moving-platform landing and occlusion-recovery escort), measure alignment and coordination latency, test state prompts and bidirectional interaction.

Result: Aerial VLA models can often track or follow a ground partner but struggle to produce stable cooperative behavior. State prompting provides limited benefit; naive bidirectional interaction can fail to improve and may amplify errors. Three components beyond the current paradigm are needed: explicit partner-state grounding, low-latency action coordination, and team-level objective alignment.

Conclusion: Zero-shot cooperative air-ground VLA with current text-based cues is insufficient; progress requires explicit grounding, fast coordination, and aligned team objectives. CARLA-Air provides a valuable evaluation platform; code is released for further research.

Abstract: Recent aerial vision-language-action (VLA) models show promising single-UAV capabilities, such as tracking moving objects and navigating to language-specified landmarks. However, it remains unclear whether these capabilities can transfer to air-ground cooperation, where a UAV and a UGV must act jointly in a shared, closed-loop physical world.
  We study this question with CARLA-Air, a single-process air-ground evaluation environment that unifies CARLA and AirSim inside one Unreal Engine runtime. By sharing the same world state, physics tick, and sensing pipeline, CARLA-Air enables physically consistent UAV--UGV interaction and precise measurement of simulation-timestamp alignment and effective coordination latency.
  Using CARLA-Air, we evaluate representative aerial VLA and planning baselines on two complementary diagnostic tasks: moving-platform landing and occlusion-recovery escort. The results show that current aerial VLA models can often track or follow a ground partner, but struggle to convert this single-agent competence into stable cooperative behavior. State prompting provides limited benefit, and naive bidirectional interaction fails to consistently improve performance and can amplify errors for most baselines. These findings suggest that, under the tested text-based cue interfaces, zero-shot cooperative air-ground VLA requires three components beyond the current paradigm: explicit partner-state grounding, low-latency action coordination, and team-level objective alignment. Our code is available at https://github.com/louiszengCN/CarlaAir.

</details>


### [307] [Seeing Fast and Slow: Bimodal 3D Scene Graphs for Open-set Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31067)
*Marcel Bartholomeus Prasetyo,Shrutika Vishal Thengane,A Manicka Praveen,Yi Loo,Malika Meghjani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BiMoSG introduces a bimodal 3D scene graph generation framework for open-set tasks that can switch between a fast coarse mode and a slow fine mode, achieving significant speedups and enabling real-time integration with task execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-set task execution in robotics benefits from adaptive representations that balance speed and granularity. Starting with a coarse scene graph and refining only in regions likely to contain task-relevant objects reduces computation while maintaining effectiveness.

Method: BiMoSG implements a fast default mode to quickly generate a coarse 3D scene graph and a switchable slow mode to generate a finer, open-vocabulary 3D scene graph for task-relevant objects, enabling bimodal, on-demand granularity.

Result: The approach is demonstrated to be significantly faster than open-source state-of-the-art methods, enabling integration of scene graph generation with task execution for real-time deployment.

Conclusion: BiMoSG provides an efficient, adaptable 3D scene graph generation framework suitable for open-set tasks, allowing dynamic switching between coarse and fine representations to support real-time robotic exploration and task execution.

Abstract: Open-set task execution can significantly benefit from seamlessly switching between coarse and fine scene representations depending on the context and the evolving information as the robot explores the environment. For example, it is often sufficient to start with a coarse scene representation initially and only employ a finer, more granular scene representation when the robot encounters regions which are likely to contain the task relevant objects. Hence, in this work, we propose BiMoSG, a bimodal 3D scene graph generation approach for open-set tasks. BiMoSG employs a "fast" mode by default to efficiently generate a coarse 3D scene graph and can switch to a "slow" mode for generating a finer open vocabulary 3D scene graph of task relevant objects. We demonstrate that our proposed 3D scene graph generation approach is significantly faster than the open-source state-of-the-art approaches. This allows us to integrate the scene graph generation process with task execution for real-time deployment.

</details>


### [308] [Building Generalization Into Behavior Generation Via Adaptive Compositions of Regularities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31110)
*Aravind Battaje,Malte Bernhard,Vito Mengers,Oliver Brock*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive composition of regularities in a differentiable network enables generalization in robotics; AICON encodes regularities as interacting processes whose composition is realized by sensory feedback and gradient descent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization in robotics requires context-dependent structure; identifying a robust inductive bias to compose regularities adaptively across situations.

Method: Represent regularities as interacting processes in a differentiable network (AICON). Use sensory feedback to realize composition; optimize via gradient descent. Test on a simple simulated problem with all relevant regularities identifiable; evaluate on broad novel conditions; perform ablations to assess informativeness-based modulation.

Result: Context-appropriate behavior emerges across most novel conditions; one case fails due to insufficient encoded regularities; ablations show the network modulates influence of regularities based on informativeness.

Conclusion: Adaptive composition of regularities constitutes a strong inductive bias for generalization in behavior generation.

Abstract: Generalization in robotics requires prior knowledge about how the world is structured, yet this structure changes from one situation to the next. This paper investigates the proposition that generalization arises from adaptively composing regularities -- predictable relationships within the robot-environment system -- into situation-appropriate structures for behavior generation. We examine this proposition by analyzing the mechanism in AICON (Active InterCONnect), a framework representing regularities as interacting processes in a differentiable network, where sensory feedback realizes composition and gradient descent generates behavior. To isolate adaptive composition as the key mechanism, we study a simple simulated problem in which all relevant regularities can be identified. We expose the resulting model to a wide range of novel conditions not considered during design, and we find that it generates context-appropriate behavior in all but one case, where encoded regularities are provably insufficient. Ablations reveal that the network automatically modulates which regularities influence behavior based on their informativeness. These results suggest that adaptive composition of regularities constitutes a powerful inductive bias for building generalization into behavior generation.

</details>


### [309] [Don't Fool Me Twice: Adapting to Adversity in the Wild with Experience-Driven Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31119)
*Navin Sriram Ravie,Andrew Jong,Krrish Jain,John Liu,Omar Alama,Bijo Sebastian,Sebastian Scherer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A continual-learning framework enabling mobile embodied agents to online-learn disturbances, attribute anomalous behaviours to causes via semantics, and improve prediction and planning, using VLMs, kernel-regression for few-shot disturbance modeling, and semantic voxel-centric uncertainty; validated in simulation and on hardware across embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unseen unstructured environments induce embodiment-specific dangers that are not exhaustively describable. Large foundation vision-language models (VLMs) alone struggle to capture interaction-driven adversities; there is a need for online, few-shot, semantics-guided reasoning to improve perception, prediction, and planning for mobile robots.

Method: Don’t Fool Me Twice: observe disturbances and describe their effects; augment this description with visual context to query a VLM for probable causes; characterize the local disturbance with kernel regression for efficient few-shot modeling; use semantic voxel-centric modeling to estimate epistemic uncertainty and treat interaction-driven disturbances as learnable spatial behaviors; formulate four hypotheses and validate them in simulation and on hardware across embodiments and adversity modes.

Result: Validation of the four hypotheses in both simulation and hardware, demonstrating online continual learning that improves prediction and planning, and enabling richer recovery via semantic uncertainty; robustness across embodiments and adversity modes.

Conclusion: The framework advances embodied AI by fusing semantics, online learning, and uncertainty reasoning to cope with unseen disturbances. It highlights the potential of combining VLM-based cause reasoning, kernel-based disturbance modeling, and semantic voxel representations for resilient planning. Limitations may include dependence on VLM quality, real-time computational constraints, and the need for diverse disturbance coverage; future work could address scalability, grounding of semantics, and broader embodiment applicability.

Abstract: In robotics, dangers and adversity modes are often embodiment-specific and relative to each agent. A frontier of autonomous mobile robotics is to enable agents to operate effectively in the wild in unseen unstructured environments. A significant challenge in unseen unstructured environments is that it may not be possible to predict all the dangers to the specific robot. Although recent work has used large foundation vision-language models (VLMs) to preemptively predict an exhaustive list of common-sense dangers, it remains difficult to capture possible interaction and embodiment-dependent adversities. We propose a continual learning framework for a mobile embodied agent to learn online from disturbances and attribute anomalous behaviours to causes through semantics, enabling better prediction and planning of the world in the future. Our framework, "Don't Fool Me Twice", first observes disturbances and describes their effects on the robot; this description is augmented with visual context to query a VLM to predict possible causes; the local disturbance is characterized using kernel regression, which allows for efficient, few-shot modeling of transient anomalies. We leverage semantic voxel-centric modeling to estimate epistemic uncertainty, enabling richer downstream recovery by treating interaction-driven disturbances as learnable spatial behaviors. We present four hypotheses and validate them in simulation and on hardware across embodiments and adversity modes.

</details>


### [310] [TARIC: Memory-Augmented Traversability-Aware Outdoor VLN under Interrupted Semantic Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31121)
*Tianle Zeng,Hanjing Ye,Jianwei Peng,Jingwen Yu,Hanxuan Chen,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified outdoor VLN framework maintains traversability-consistent executable guidance during cue-free phases by grounding semantic bearings into a 3D cue memory with uncertainty-aware readout, bridging the gap between cue interruptions and feasible navigation, and showing improved success rates in both sim and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outdoor vision-language navigation suffers from semantic-cue interruptions where goal cues become sparse or occluded. Memory-based approaches struggle when traversability constraints force detours, causing cue-free degradation. Traversability should be treated as a stability condition for continuous goal-directed guidance, not merely a safety concern.

Method: Extract semantic bearings from visibility-gated cues and ground them into executable headings using a real-time near-field traversability profile. Lift intermittent 2D cues into a world-aligned 3D cue memory with uncertainty-aware readout to keep guidance reachable and stable during movement.

Result: Evaluated on quadrupedal and wheeled platforms over 600–1000 m routes. Simulation success rate improves by >10 percentage points over the strongest baseline; real-world success rate reaches 40% (vs. 17.5% for the strongest baseline), with notably higher robustness during extended cue-free intervals.

Conclusion: The framework delivers traversability-consistent, executable guidance through cue-free phases, enhancing robustness and real-world performance in outdoor VLN across long-range routes.

Abstract: Outdoor vision-language navigation (VLN) in long-range, open-world environments is frequently disrupted by semantic-cue interruptions, where informative goal cues become sparse, occluded, or leave the field of view. Once such cues disappear, agents enter a cue-free phase and often degrade into backtracking, oscillatory headings, or aimless exploration. While memory-based methods attempt to bridge these gaps, they often fail under traversability-driven detours: the remembered cue direction may be infeasible, forcing detours that prolong cue-free phases and gradually render robot-centric cues stale and implicit histories blurred. This makes traversability a stability condition for maintaining goal-directed guidance, rather than merely a local safety concern.
  We propose a unified outdoor VLN framework that survives semantic-cue interruptions by maintaining traversability-consistent executable guidance throughout prolonged cue-free phases. Specifically, our method extracts semantic bearings from visibility-gated goal or exploration cues and grounds them into executable headings using a real-time near-field traversability profile, providing goal-consistent feasible guidance beyond reject-only safety filtering. To prevent guidance degradation during detours, we lift intermittent 2D evidence into a world-aligned 3D cue memory with an uncertainty-aware readout mechanism, ensuring guidance remains continuously reachable and stable as the robot moves.
  We evaluate the framework on quadrupedal and wheeled platforms over 600--1000 m routes. Our method improves simulation success rate by over 10 percentage points over the strongest baseline and achieves a real-world success rate of 40%, compared to 17.5% for the strongest baseline, with substantially higher robustness during prolonged cue-free intervals.

</details>


### [311] [Simulation of collision avoidance behavior in crowd movement by data-driven approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31210)
*Xuanwen Liang,Eric Wai Ming Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CPGAN is a GAN-based crowd simulator that penalizes collisions via a new lateral-acceleration collision loss and uses Voronoi-derived features; it reduces opposite-direction collisions in bidirectional flow and reproduces lane formation and N-t curves.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven crowd models struggle with high collision rates in bidirectional/multidirectional flows. Incorporating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms into loss functions can improve realism, safety, and trajectory prediction in crowded environments.

Method: Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) architecture named CPGAN. Introduces a lateral-acceleration-based collision loss to penalize collisions and a Voronoi-based motion feature extraction approach to capture local spatial relations. The model integrates these into the training objective to produce collision-aware pedestrian trajectories.

Result: In bidirectional flow scenarios, CPGAN significantly reduces opposite-direction collision rates, achieving levels comparable to controlled experiments. It effectively simulates bidirectional flow, reproducing lane formation and N-t curves.

Conclusion: Integrating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms into loss functions for data-driven crowd simulation shows promise, potentially improving realism and safety. Future work could address generalization to more complex environments, scalability, and sensitivity to feature extraction methods.

Abstract: Crowd movement simulation is essential for pedestrian safety management and facility layout optimization. Data-driven models enhance trajectory prediction accuracy under Euclidean metrics, yet they suffer from excessively high collision rates, especially in bidirectional and multidirectional flows. In this paper, we establish a novel data-driven crowd simulation model that incorporates the pedestrian collision mechanism into the loss function to reduce collisions. A new lateral-acceleration-based collision loss function and a Voronoi-based motion feature extraction approach are proposed. The model is based on a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) architecture and is termed CPGAN (Collision-Penalized GAN). We evaluate CPGAN in bidirectional flow scenarios, which involve frequent collision avoidance behaviors. Results show that the proposed lateral-acceleration-based collision loss significantly reduces opposite-direction pedestrian collision rates to levels comparable with controlled experiments. CPGAN effectively simulates bidirectional flow, reproducing lane formation and N-t curves. The research outcomes can provide inspiration for integrating pedestrian dynamics mechanisms into loss functions in data-driven crowd simulation.

</details>


### [312] [HARP-VLA: Human-Robot Aligned Representation Learning for Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31234)
*Xiang Zhu,Puzhen Yuan,Yichen Liu,Jianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HARP introduces human-robot aligned representation learning to pretrain vision-language-action models from human videos by aligning robot visual encodings and latent actions with limited cross-embodiment demonstrations and abundant unpaired data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-embodiment gaps in visual observations and executable actions hinder generalization of VLA models trained on human data; misalignment leads to inconsistent policy inputs and domain-dependent latent actions, reducing effectiveness of co-training with human videos.

Method: Leverage limited paired human-robot demonstrations as cross-embodiment bridges and abundant unpaired human and robot videos as dynamics supervision. Train a robot-adapted visual encoder and a latent action model with manipulation-centric auxiliary cues and a source-relative pair-discriminative alignment loss to adapt robot representations toward human semantics while preserving pair-level discrimination.

Result: Learned aligned vision encoder and latent action model provide a unified vision-language-to-latent-action representation. Experiments show improved human-robot alignment and downstream policy performance, achieving 4.481 average length on CALVIN ABC→D and a 7.1% real-world success rate gain over the strongest baseline.
