Conclusion: HARP advances VLA pretraining by addressing cross-embodiment representation gaps, enabling more effective transfer from human videos to robot policies and improving real-world manipulation performance.

Abstract: Learning generalizable vision-language-action (VLA) models from large-scale human videos is promising but challenging due to cross-embodiment discrepancies in both visual observations and executable actions. While latent action models reduce the action execution gap by learning action abstractions, they still rely on visual features. Thus, misaligned human and robot visual representations can lead to inconsistencies in policy inputs and induce domain-dependent latent actions, hindering effective co-training with human videos. To address this, we propose HARP, a human-robot aligned representation learning framework for more effective VLA pretraining from human videos. Specifically, HARP uses limited paired human-robot demonstrations as cross-embodiment bridges and abundant unpaired human and robot videos as a scalable dynamics supervision data source. It trains a robot-adapted visual encoder and a latent action model with manipulation-centric auxiliary cues and a source-relative pair-discriminative alignment loss, which adapts robot representations toward human semantics while preserving pair-level discrimination. The learned aligned vision encoder and latent action model provide a unified vision and action representation for VLA-style policy learning, where human and robot videos provide vision-language-to-latent-action supervision and a lightweight robot action head grounds latent actions into executable commands. Experiments on feature visualization, simulation, and realworld manipulation show improved human-robot alignment and downstream policy performance, achieving 4.481 average length on CALVIN ABC$\rightarrow$D and a 7.1\% realworld success rate gain over the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [313] [Before Parc Fermé: RL-Time Pruning for Efficient Embodied LLMs in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31256)
*Luca Benfenati,Ali Azimi,Matteo Risso,Fabio Carapellese,Daniele Jahier Pagliari,Alessio Burrello*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A pruning-by-RL strategy (BPF) for embodied LLM controllers, with two variants (BPF-RL and BPF-SFT/RL), achieving better end-to-end performance per parameter and up to 27% throughput gain on deployment hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle real-time deployment challenges of embodied LLM controllers by pruning during training to account for task-specific supervision and closed-loop feedback, not just static post-training pruning.

Method: Introduce Before Parc Fermé (BPF) with iterative pruning during RL. Two variants: BPF-RL (prune during RL at predefined intervals) and BPF-SFT/RL (prune during SFT, then further prune during RL). Evaluation uses LLM-Pruner on RobotxR1, comparing against post-training pruning, RL recovery, SFT-stage pruning, and smaller dense models.

Result: BPF provides the best task-performance vs memory/throughput trade-off among strategies. BPF-SFT/RL achieves ~1.69x better size-end-to-end performance trade-off than selecting a smaller dense model. On Jetson AGX Orin, decode throughput improves up to 27%.

Conclusion: Pruning during RL (with optional SFT pre-pruning) leverages closed-loop feedback to optimize embodied LLM controllers efficiently, surpassing conventional post-training pruning and fixed smaller models in both end-to-end performance per parameter and deployment throughput.

Abstract: Embodied Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as reasoning modules in robotic control pipelines to improve human-robot interaction, but their memory and generation latency make real-time deployment difficult. Pruning can reduce these costs, but for controllers that undergo multiple pre- and post-training phases, the crucial question is not only how much to prune, but when pruning should occur.
  In this work, we propose Before Parc Fermé (BPF), a pruning strategy performed during RL that compresses embodied LLM controllers while they are still being optimized for closed-loop behavior. This allows pruning decisions to account for the task-specific supervision and closed-loop feedback that shape the final controller. We propose two variants: BPF-RL, which performs iterative pruning during RL by removing part of the model at predefined training intervals, and BPF-SFT/RL, which first prunes part of the model structure during SFT and then further compresses it during RL using the same iterative strategy as BPF-RL until the target pruning ratio is reached. We evaluate BPF on RobotxR1, an LLM-based autonomous-driving control pipeline, using an established LLM pruning framework (LLM-Pruner), and compare it against post-training pruning, post-training pruning with RL recovery, SFT-stage pruning, and smaller dense models from the same family.
  Our results show that BPF provides the best task-performance vs. memory and throughput trade-off among the considered pruning strategies. When compressing the larger RobotxR1 models, BPF-SFT/RL achieves a $1.69\times$ better size-end-to-end performance trade-off than directly selecting a smaller dense model from the same family, measured as removed parameters per lost percentage point of control adaptability. On the Jetson AGX Orin mounted on the target robotic platform, the compact models improve decode throughput by up to $27\%$.

</details>


### [314] [DeMaVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Foundation Model for Generalizable Deformable Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31286)
*Taiyi Su,Jian Zhu,Tianjian Wang,Youzhang He,Zitai Huang,Jianjun Zhang,Chong Ma,Hanyang Wang,Tianjiao Zhang,Munan Yin,Weihao Ding,Yi Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeMaVLA is a VLA foundation model for generalizable deformable manipulation, using a pruned action expert with flow-matching on a VLM backbone, trained on real-world data and DAgger; achieves competitive RoboTwin results and strong household-folding performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address across-object generalization in deformable manipulation; avoid task interference and category-specific policies in existing VLA systems.

Method: VLM backbone with an action expert pruned by skipping every other transformer layer while preserving layer alignment; continuous action generation via flow matching; pre-train on ~5k hours of real dual-arm demonstrations; post-train on mixed folding data using a human-in-the-loop DAgger pipeline; experiments on RoboTwin and a household folding benchmark.

Result: Competitive RoboTwin performance; strong real-world results on a household folding benchmark; demonstrates usefulness of scalable real-world data, efficient action generation, and corrective learning for generalizable VLA policies in deformable manipulation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable approach toward general-purpose VLA policies for deformable-object manipulation, combining data-scale, architecture efficiency, and corrective learning via DAgger.

Abstract: Real-world household robots require Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation models that can acquire reusable manipulation skills across diverse objects, task conditions, and household environments. Deformable-object folding is a representative challenge, requiring robots to handle clothing items from random initial states across varying categories, geometries, materials, and scenes. However, existing VLA systems commonly train separate policies for different object categories, while naively mixed multi-task training often suffers from task interference and degraded performance. To move beyond category-specific folding policies, we introduce DeMaVLA, a VLA foundation model for generalizable Deformable Manipulation. DeMaVLA adopts a VLM backbone with an action expert and formulates continuous action generation using flow matching. To improve efficiency, the action expert is constructed by pruning every other transformer layer while preserving layer-wise alignment with the VLM backbone, reducing training and inference cost. DeMaVLA is first pre-trained on approximately 5,000 hours of selected real-world dual-arm demonstrations to acquire general manipulation priors. It is then post-trained on mixed folding data that aggregates self-collected demonstrations and corrective trajectories from real-robot failures across multiple folding tasks through a human-in-the-loop Data Aggregation~(DAgger) pipeline. Experiments show that DeMaVLA achieves competitive performance on RoboTwin and strong real-world results on our household folding benchmark. These results highlight the value of scalable real-world data, efficient action generation, and corrective learning for general-purpose VLA policies in deformable-object manipulation.

</details>


### [315] [AR Forcing: Towards Long-Horizon Robot Navigation World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31314)
*Yifei Yang,Zehua Fan,Huan Li,Aoqi Wang,Lida Huang,Haibao Yu,Haiyan Liu,Xuanyao Mao,Jason Bao,Liang Xu,Bingchuan Sun,Yan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AR Forcing aligns training with inference by integrating diffusion loss into the autoregressive loop, mitigating distribution shift and improving long-horizon navigation robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based robot navigation models suffer from a training–inference distribution gap: models trained with teacher forcing on a diffusion process may fail when using their own predictions during long-horizon planning. This leads to unstable output and degraded trajectory accuracy.

Method: Incorporate the standard diffusion loss into an autoregressive training loop. At each step, the model uses its own previous predictions to update the context and optimize the single-step noise prediction objective. No extra discriminators or distribution-matching losses are added; the original diffusion framework and sampler are retained and enhanced for training.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multi-domain navigation datasets (RECON, SCAND, HuRoN, TartanDrive) shows that AR Forcing improves the consistency of generated images during long-horizon navigation and increases the accuracy of predicted trajectories, boosting robustness in both known and unknown environments compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: AR Forcing effectively exposes the model to inference state distributions during training, reducing distribution shift and enhancing long-horizon robustness without altering the base diffusion framework.

Abstract: The diffusion based robot navigation world models are typically trained using parallel supervision, while autoregressive inference is employed during path planning. This results in a distribution shift between training and inference, which destabilizes the performance over long-horizon prediction. We propose AR Forcing, an autoregressive training strategy, which integrates the standard diffusion loss into the autoregressive training loop. At each step, the model uses its own predictions to update the context and optimize the single step noise prediction objective, thereby explicitly exposing the model to the inference state distribution during training. Our method does not require additional discriminators or distribution-matching losses, retains the original diffusion framework and sampler, and is easy to integrate. Experiments on multi-domain navigation datasets (RECON, SCAND, HuRoN, TartanDrive) show that compared with strong baselines, AR Forcing improved the consistency of generated images during long-horizon navigation and the accuracy of predicted trajectories, enhancing robustness of the model in complex known and unknown environments. We will release the code soon.

</details>


### [316] [Surface Constraint Policy for Learning Surface-Constrained and Dynamically Feasible Robot Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31321)
*Shuai Ke,Jiexin Zhang,Huan Zhao,Zhiao Wei,Yikun Guo,Jie Pan,Han Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based imitation learning approach with a surface constraint policy (SCP) encodes free-form surface geometry constraints via a 2D weighted Gaussian kernel derived from demonstrations, uses diffusion-based policy to infer task intentions from multimodal inputs, and maps these intentions to surface-constrained dynamic movement primitives (DMPs) for smooth execution, achieving improved success and contact stability in surface manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of diffusion-based imitation learning in handling complex free-form surface constraints and dynamic feasibility, which lead to stochastic actions and unreliable surface alignment.

Method: Encode surface geometry constraints with a 2D weighted Gaussian kernel derived from demonstrations; use a diffusion-based policy to infer task-level intentions from multimodal sensory inputs (visual observations and robot state); transform intentions into surface-constrained DMPs via a similarity-based action mapping for compliant motion execution.

Result: Demonstrates superior task success rates and contact stability under surface constraints across multiple surface manipulation tasks when compared with existing methods.

Conclusion: SCP enables generation of structured surface geometric intent and dynamically admissible actions, validating the integration of explicit surface geometry constraints with diffusion-based planning and DMP-based execution for robust surface manipulation.

Abstract: Diffusion-based imitation learning methods have driven rapid progress in robot dexterous manipulation tasks. However, they have limitations when applied to tasks that involve complex free-form surface constraints because of their lack of explicit surface geometry constraint modeling and the dynamic feasibility issue, resulting in stochastic action generation that fails to achieve reliable surface alignment and maintain stable contact. To address these limitations, we propose a novel surface constraint policy (SCP) for generating robot actions that satisfy free-form surface constraints on the basis of human demonstrations and real-time visual observations. First, the surface geometry constraint is encoded using a two-dimensional weighted Gaussian kernel function that is derived from demonstrations. Building on the encoded surface geometry constraints, the diffusion-based policy is used to infer task-level action intentions from multimodal sensory inputs, including visual observations and robot state feedback. These intentions are further transformed into surface-constrained dynamic movement primitives (DMPs) through a similarity-based action mapping method, thereby enabling smooth and compliant motion execution. The SCP achieves generation of structured surface geometric intent and dynamically admissible actions. The proposed method is validated on multiple surface manipulation tasks and compared with existing techniques. The experimental results demonstrate superior task success rates and contact stability under surface constraints.

</details>


### [317] [Learning Terrain-Aware Whole-Body Control for Perceptive Legged Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31343)
*Sikai Guo,Yudong Zhong,Guoyang Zhao,Botao Dang,Zhihai Bi,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Terrain-aware whole-body control for legged manipulators (TA-WBC) using an RL-based unified policy, exteroceptive terrain encoding, foot-contact-plane-based end-effector sampling, and dual-policy distillation; validated in simulation and real-world with improved reachability, tracking, and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing whole-body controllers rely largely on proprioception and neglect exteroceptive terrain perception, limiting adaptability to varying terrains and navigation on complex surfaces.

Method: Introduce a hybrid exteroception encoder to extract terrain features; develop an RL-based unified policy for loco-manipulation across terrains; propose end-effector sampling based on the foot contact plane to decouple base motion from manipulation targets; implement a dual-policy distillation module to fuse broad motion capabilities with terrain adaptability while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate robustness, yielding a larger reachable space, reduced tracking error, and fewer unexpected stumbles.

Conclusion: TA-WBC with its unified policy effectively enables legged manipulators to perform loco-manipulation tasks across diverse terrains, expanding operational capabilities in human-centric environments.

Abstract: Legged manipulators integrate exceptional terrain adaptability along with mobile manipulation capabilities, which make them highly promising for deployment in human-centric environments. By coordinating the control of both legs and arms, a whole-body controller can significantly expand the operational workspace of legged manipulators. However, many existing whole-body controllers primarily depend on proprioception and do not incorporate the critical exteroception required for effective terrain topology perception. This limitation can hinder their ability to adapt to varying environmental conditions and navigate complex terrains effectively. In this paper, we introduce TA-WBC, a terrain-aware whole-body control framework for legged manipulators, which features a novel RL-based unified policy tailored to whole-body loco-manipulation tasks in various terrains. Specifically, we employ a hybrid exteroception encoder to extract terrain features, providing an essential basis for the robot to proactively adapt posture and footholds. Furthermore, to facilitate stable cross-terrain loco-manipulation, we propose a novel end-effector sampling method based on the foot contact plane, decoupling manipulation target from base fluctuations. Moreover, a dual-policy distillation module is introduced to integrate expansive whole-body motion with terrain adaptability without catastrophic forgetting. The simulation and real-world experiments validate the robustness of our proposed controller, which leads to a larger reachable space, less tracking error, and reduced unexpected stumbles. This unified policy highlights the promising capabilities of legged manipulators in performing loco-manipulation tasks across complex terrains.

</details>


### [318] [Haptic Sorter: A Unified Planning Framework for Online Shape Estimation and Real-Time Pose Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31352)
*Zhuoyi Lu,Lin Yang,Sri Harsha Turlapati,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified model-based framework for tactile-enabled manipulation that uses Bayesian Optimization to infer object shape, adaptive manipulation potentials to encode geometry, and an online ODE for real-time pose inference, demonstrated on a 2D sorting task with varied geometries in simulation and a real multi-arm setup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practice, exact object shape and pose are often unknown due to sensor uncertainties and occlusion. There is a need for an integrated, data-efficient perception and planning pipeline that can operate under partial geometric information.

Method: Key innovations: (i) Bayesian Optimization to guide haptic exploration for shape inference, with superellipses approximating the geometric boundary; (ii) adaptive manipulation potential encoding object geometry to facilitate quasi-static robot–object interaction; (iii) an online Ordinary Differential Equation that fuses model predictions with tactile feedback for real-time pose inference. Evaluation on a 2D robotic sorting task, testing robustness/generalization across varied geometries in both simulation and a real-world multi-arm setup.

Result: The framework achieves robust shape and pose inference under uncertainty, enabling effective manipulation planning despite partial geometry knowledge. Demonstrated robustness and generalizability across object geometries in both simulation and a real multi-arm system, indicating practical viability and real-time capability.

Conclusion: The work presents a cohesive, model-based tactile-perception–planning pipeline that leverages BO, adaptive potentials, and online pose inference to tackle geometry-uncertainty in manipulation. This approach enhances data efficiency and real-time operability, with potential extensions to 3D tasks and broader object classes.

Abstract: Robotics manipulation usually assumes that the shape and pose of the object are known to the robot prior to motion planning. However, precise geometric information is not always available in practice, and pose inference suffers from sensor uncertainties and view occlusion. In this work, we propose a unified model-based geometric framework integrating robotic haptic perception, modeling, and manipulation planning. Our novelties involve: \textit{i)} Introducing Bayesian Optimization (BO) to guide the haptic exploration for object shape inference, where superellipses are used to approximate geometric boundary; \textit{ii)} Adaptive formulation of manipulation potential encoding object geometry for quasi-static robot-object interaction; \textit{iii)} Proposing an online Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) for real-time pose inference based on model prediction and tactile feedback. We deploy our system on a 2D robotic sorting task, and vary object geometries to validate the robustness and generalizability of our framework in both simulation and a real-world multi-arm setup.

</details>


### [319] [LiftNav: Path Planning via Semantic Lifting in TSDF-Guided Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31376)
*Hannah Schieber,Dominik Frischmann,Victor Schaack,Angela P. Schoellig,Daniel Roth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LiftNav is a hybrid TSDF+GS navigation framework leveraging GSFusion's dual map, real-time YOLO detection, TSDF 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization with a hinge-loss collision penalty; evaluated in Replica with 100% feasibility and shorter trajectories compared to radiance-field baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The abstract addresses the need for simultaneous reliable collision avoidance and object-level understanding in unknown indoor environments. Classical TSDF offers safe planning but lacks semantics, while photorealistic Gaussian Splatting provides rich appearance but suffers from soft geometry, hindering precise obstacle avoidance. A fusion of these approaches with real-time perception aims to enable semantic navigation without dense 3D embeddings.

Method: The proposed LiftNav framework builds on GSFusion's TSDF+GS dual map and incorporates a real-time YOLO-based object detector, TSDF-based 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization. A hinge-loss-based collision penalty is introduced to improve trajectory smoothness and safety.

Result: In simulation on the Replica dataset, LiftNav achieves a 100% feasibility rate and shorter trajectories when compared to a state-of-the-art radiance field baseline.

Conclusion: LiftNav demonstrates that a hybrid TSDF+GS representation combined with real-time perception and optimization can deliver semantically aware navigation without dense 3D embeddings, yielding robust collision avoidance and more efficient paths.

Abstract: Autonomous robots in unknown indoor environments require both reliable collision avoidance and object-level understanding. Classical representations such as TSDF support safe planning but lack semantics, while photorealistic methods like Gaussian Splatting (GS) provide rich appearance yet suffer from soft geometry, limiting precise obstacle avoidance. We present LiftNav, a hybrid navigation framework built on GSFusion's TSDF+GS dual map, augmented with a real-time pipeline of YOLO-based detection, TSDF-based 3D lifting, and B-spline trajectory optimization. This design enables flexible semantic navigation without dense 3D embeddings. We further introduce a hinge-loss-based collision penalty that improves trajectory smoothness and safety. We evaluate our approach in a simulation using the Replica dataset. Compared against a state-of-the-art radiance field baseline we show a 100% feasibility rate and shorter trajectories.

</details>


### [320] [Adaptive Artificial Time-Delay Control with Barrier Lyapunov Constraints for Euler-Lagrange Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31405)
*Saksham Gupta,Rishabh Dev Yadav,Sarthak Mishra,Amitabh Sharma,Sourish Ganguly,Wei Pan,Spandan Roy,Simone Baldi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An adaptive control framework for Euler-Lagrange systems combines time-delay estimation with barrier Lyapunov functions to handle state-dependent uncertainties and time-varying state constraints; proven stable and validated experimentally on a 5-DOF manipulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics require simultaneous compensation of state-dependent uncertainties and enforcement of time-varying safety constraints; existing controllers inadequately address both in a model-free or uncertain setting.

Method: Integrates an artificial time-delay-based uncertainty estimation (time-delay estimation) with a barrier Lyapunov function-based controller. Formulates a state-dependent upper bound on the TDE error, proposes an online adaptive law for estimating its parameters, and enforces time-varying position and velocity bounds via BLF. Provides Lyapunov-based stability proof and validates experimentally on a 5-DOF manipulator.

Result: The framework achieves constraint satisfaction under dynamic uncertainties, demonstrates stability, and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in maintaining safety-critical constraints in experiments on a 5-DOF robotic arm.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture offers a model-free-adaptive, constraint-aware controller for Euler-Lagrange systems with state-dependent uncertainties, with proven stability and practical validation, indicating broad applicability in safety-critical robotic applications.

Abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of simultaneously compensating for state-dependent uncertainties and enforcing time-varying state constraints in Euler-Lagrange systems, a common requirement in robotics that remains underserved by existing control designs. A novel adaptive control framework is developed that combines an artificial time-delay-based uncertainty estimation strategy, also known as time-delay estimation, with a barrier Lyapunov function to enforce constraint-aware control design. Specifically, a state-dependent upper bound on the time-delay estimation approximation error is analytically formulated, and an adaptive law is constructed to estimate its parameters online, enabling real-time state-dependent uncertainty compensation without relying on prior model knowledge. To ensure constraint compliance, the barrier Lyapunov function-based controller enforces time-varying bounds on both position and velocity. The resulting architecture is provably stable via Lyapunov analysis. Experimental results on a five-degree-of-freedom robotic manipulator validate the framework's capability, compared with the state of the art, in maintaining strict adherence to safety-critical constraints under dynamic uncertainties.

</details>


### [321] [Shaft-integrated Force Sensing with Transformer-based Dynamics Compensation for Telesurgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31434)
*Shuyuan Yang,Grant Boone,Timo Markert,Sebastian Matich,Andreas Theissler,Martin Atzmueller,Zonghe Chua*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Integrates a six-axis force sensor into a distal end of a cable-driven RAMIS instrument, enabling end-effector force measurement while preserving original functionality; uses a transformer neural network to fuse force sensor data with robot state to estimate end-effector forces, compensating for internal cable forces; achieves <6% normalized error with good generalization, though saturation occurs at high cable forces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide accessible, reproducible methods to integrate force sensing into RAMIS tools to support force-informed performance assessment, tactile localization, and autonomy in minimally invasive surgery.

Method: Attach a six-axis commercial force sensor at the instrument tip; design emphasizes reproducibility and no specialized manufacturing tools; train a transformer neural network to fuse sensor data with robot state to estimate end-effector forces while compensating for actuation-induced cable forces.

Result: Normalized error below 6%; better generalization to unseen conditions than proximal-only data-driven sensing; saturation under high internal cable forces reduces observability along the tool's major axis and at higher loads.

Conclusion: The approach achieves a favorable balance between system integrability and performance, enabling RAMIS research on haptic feedback, skill assessment, and force-informed autonomy; open videos and code are available for replication.

Abstract: Robot-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery (RAMIS) enhances surgeon dexterity, with newer platforms leveraging haptic feedback to further improve performance. Such force information has broader potential to inform performance assessment, tactile localization, and surgical autonomy. This motivates the need for accessible approaches to integrating force sensing into RAMIS tools. This work presents a method for integrating a six-axis commercial force sensor into the distal end of a standard cable-driven surgical instrument, enabling end-effector force measurement while preserving the original mechanical functionality of the device. The proposed design emphasizes reproducibility and accessibility for research applications, requiring no specialized manufacturing tools. A transformer neural network integrates force sensor measurements with robot state information to aid estimation of applied forces at the end-effector, compensating for internal cable forces arising from actuation. Our proposed approach achieved normalized errors below 6%, and generalized to unseen conditions better than purely proximal data-driven sensing approaches. High internal cable forces caused sensor saturation and reduced axial force observability, which can degrade performance along the tool's major axis and under higher load conditions. Given current levels of performance, the balance of system integrability and performance enables applications and research into timely topics of haptic feedback, skill assessment, and force-informed autonomy in RAMIS. Videos and code are available at https://enhanced-telerobotics.github.io/shaft force sensing.

</details>


### [322] [Actuator-Aware Inverse Kinematics with Joint-Limit Admissibility for Torque-Controlled Redundant Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31436)
*Mohammad Dastranj,Mahdi Hejrati,Jouni Mattila*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An actuator-aware IK layer uses a convex QP to compute joint-level velocities for torque-controlled redundant robots, enforcing joint-limit admissibility with control-barrier-style bounds and using a penalized slack for task error; redundancy is resolved via a controller-compatibility objective that accounts for prior commands and actuator torque limits, and the approach is controller-agnostic as an intermediate IK layer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge IK with downstream torque-controlled actuation while respecting joint limits and actuator torque capacities; standard IK can push limits without improving realized motion; aim to align commanded velocities with actuator capabilities and prior commands to improve realized task performance.

Method: Formulate a convex quadratic program with the decision variable being the joint-level required velocity. Apply control barrier function–style bounds to enforce joint-limit admissibility. Use a penalized slack variable to handle task error. Resolve redundancy via a controller-compatibility objective that factors in previous command consistency and actuator torque-capacity weighting. The method is independent of the particular torque-level controller and can serve as an intermediate IK layer between an endpoint trajectory and a redundant robot controller.

Result: Compared to standard IK baselines and a constrained task-preserving QP baseline, the approach yields lower limit-pushing commands, bounded admissible required velocities, and improved realized task behavior in the tested trajectory, without requiring changes to the downstream controller.

Conclusion: The proposed actuator-aware IK layer provides a robust, controller-agnostic bridge between endpoint trajectories and torque-controlled redundant robots, improving safety and performance under joint-limit constraints without modifying the downstream controller.

Abstract: This paper proposes actuator-aware inverse kinematics for torque-controlled redundant robots under joint-limit constraints. In the considered architecture, the inverse-kinematic output is not merely a purely kinematic joint-velocity command; it is the required joint velocity supplied to a downstream torque-level controller. Therefore, a small commanded task residual may not necessarily improve realized motion. The proposed method formulates a convex quadratic programming problem whose decision variable is the joint-level required velocity. Control barrier function style bounds impose reference-level joint-limit admissibility, while the task equation is handled through a penalized slack variable. Redundancy is resolved using a controller-compatibility objective that accounts for previous-command consistency and actuator torque-capacity weighting. The method is independent of the particular torque-level controller and can serve as an intermediate IK layer between an endpoint trajectory and a redundant robot controller. Experiments on a virtual-decomposition-controlled seven-degree-of-freedom upper-limb exoskeleton compare the method with standard inverse-kinematic baselines and a constrained task-preserving quadratic programming baseline. The results indicate lower limit-pushing commands, bounded admissible required velocities, and improved realized task behavior in the tested trajectory, without modifying the downstream controller.

</details>


### [323] [On-Device Robotic Planning: Eliminating Inference Redundancy for Efficient Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31460)
*Joonhee Lee,Hyunseung Shin,Hyunmi Kim,Pei Zhang,Jeonggil Ko*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: REIS reduces reasoning latency in reasoning-based robotic policies by exploiting temporal redundancy in observations. It uses scene gating, KV-steered affordance routing, and deliberative reasoning to maintain semantic adaptability with lower overhead, achieving real-time capable performance on ALFRED and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language/vision-language model–driven robotic planning offers strong semantic reasoning but incurs high inference latency, hindering real-time deployment. The authors observe substantial temporal redundancy in robotic reasoning workloads, where consecutive observations yield identical actions/subgoals, motivating an architecture that minimizes unnecessary reasoning.

Method: Introduce REIS, a human-cognition-inspired decision-making framework. It combines lightweight scene gating to prune observations, KV-steered affordance routing to quickly map observations to actions via a key-value store of affordances, and deliberative reasoning to ensure semantic adaptability under embodied constraints.

Result: Empirical evaluation on the ALFRED benchmark and real-world robots shows REIS substantially reduces reasoning overhead while maintaining competitive task performance, demonstrating improved real-time capability without sacrificing semantic versatility.

Conclusion: REIS demonstrates that exploiting temporal redundancy in robotic reasoning can yield significant inference-time savings while preserving adaptability, enabling more practical deployment of reasoning-based robotic policies in embodied settings.

Abstract: Reasoning-based robotic policies using large language and vision-language models achieve strong semantic planning capabilities but mostly suffer from a high inference latency that limits practical real-time deployment. In this work, we observe that robotic reasoning workloads contain substantial temporal redundancy, where consecutive observations frequently produce identical actions and subgoals. Based on this insight, we present REIS, a human cognition inspired robotic decision-making framework that minimizes unnecessary reasoning while preserving semantic adaptability. REIS combines lightweight scene gating, KV-steered affordance routing, and deliberative reasoning to accelerate robotic control under embodied constraints. Experiments on ALFRED, and real-world robotic tasks demonstrate that REIS significantly suppresses reasoning overhead while maintaining competitive task performance.

</details>


### [324] [IDOL: Inverse-Dynamics-Guided Future Prediction for End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31476)
*Chenghao Zhang,Timin Li,Dongmei Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IDOL introduces inverse-dynamics-guided latent future prediction to bridge world-model forecasts and planning in end-to-end autonomous driving, achieving state-of-the-art on NAVSIM v1/v2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Future prediction in world-models is descriptively useful but often not tightly coupled to executable motion planning. Without decoding how state transitions translate into actionable trajectories, planning can be suboptimal.

Method: Predict multiple future latent BEV scene states with a BEV world model. Apply an inverse dynamics model to adjacent latent futures to decode transition-aware trajectory features and recover planning-relevant motion deltas that explain latent evolution. Use these signals to optimize the planned trajectory. Include a lightweight closed-loop refinement that reuses the optimized trajectory for another round of future-aware reasoning.

Result: Empirical evaluation on NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 demonstrates state-of-the-art performance among comparable methods.

Conclusion: Incorporating inverse dynamics into latent future reasoning tightens the coupling between world modeling and planning, enabling more actionable and consistent planning for long horizons.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving has emerged as a compelling paradigm for learning planning directly from sensor observations, while recent world-model-based approaches further enrich this paradigm by enabling explicit reasoning about how the scene may evolve in the future. Yet future prediction alone does not guarantee better planning unless the predicted evolution can be converted into planning-relevant trajectory updates. Many current methods still forecast future scene states without explicitly decoding the motion implications hidden in state transitions. As a result, future reasoning often remains descriptively useful but only weakly coupled to executable motion generation. To address this limitation, we propose \mathbf{IDOL}, an inverse-dynamics-guided future prediction framework for world-model-based end-to-end planning in latent BEV space, where inverse dynamics serves as the key bridge between future prediction and trajectory optimization. IDOL first predicts multiple future latent scene states with a BEV world model, then applies an inverse dynamics model to adjacent latent futures to decode transition-aware trajectory features and recover planning-relevant motion deltas that explain how the latent world evolves over time. These inverse-dynamics-derived signals are used to optimize the planned trajectory, turning future forecasting from passive scene anticipation into actionable planning guidance. A lightweight closed-loop refinement module further improves long-horizon consistency by reusing the optimized trajectory for another round of future-aware reasoning. By introducing inverse dynamics into latent future reasoning, IDOL tightens the coupling between world modeling and planning. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 benchmarks show that IDOL achieves state-of-the-art performance among comparable methods.

</details>


### [325] [Batched Differentiable Rigid Body Dynamics in PyTorch for GPU-Accelerated Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31481)
*Yue Wang,Yanran Xu,Wenbo Wu,Chuanhang Qiu,Zhaoxing Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PyTorch-based batched articulated rigid-body dynamics library (BARD) delivering GPU-accelerated, differentiable forward kinematics and Jacobians with substantial throughput gains over Pinocchio; validated on multiple robot models and integrated into a training pipeline; open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome CPU-bound bottlenecks from Pinocchio in large-scale, in-loop RL training with in-dynamics computation, enabling high-throughput GPU workflows and differentiable dynamics.

Method: Self-contained PyTorch implementation of Featherstone's rigid-body dynamics with three design pillars: (1) tiered lazy-evaluation cache to avoid redundant tree traversals, (2) matmul-free joint transforms via pre-computed Rodrigues constants, and (3) level-parallel propagation to convert sequential tree traversals into batched, depth-based steps; supports automatic differentiation for gradient-based learning.

Result: Matches Pinocchio numerically across five robot models (7–23 DOFs); up to 64x higher throughput for Forward Kinematics and 63x for Jacobians at batch size 4096 on NVIDIA H200; differentiable, enabling gradient-based system identification that recovered link masses with 1.24% mean error under 5% torque noise; integration into Isaac Lab AMP for an 11-DOF spined quadruped with 4096 environments, achieving 8.5x speedup over Pinocchio and 2.0x over ADAM for in-loop dynamics.

Conclusion: BARD delivers high-throughput, differentiable rigid-body dynamics on GPU, enabling scalable, data-efficient RL pipelines with in-loop dynamics; open-source at the provided URL.

Abstract: As robot control shifts toward large-scale reinforcement learning with in-loop dynamics computation, the community's reliance on CPU-bound libraries such as Pinocchio creates a throughput bottleneck in GPU-based training pipelines. We present BARD (Batched Articulated Rigid-body Dynamics), a self-contained PyTorch implementation of Featherstone's rigid-body dynamics algorithms, optimized for batched GPU evaluation and automatic differentiation. Three design choices make this efficient: a tiered lazy-evaluation cache that avoids redundant tree traversals, matmul-free joint transforms via pre-computed Rodrigues constants, and level-parallel propagation that reduces sequential operations to tree-depth batched steps. On five robot models (7-23 DOFs), BARD matches Pinocchio numerically while reaching up to 64x higher throughput for Forward Kinematics and 63x for Jacobians at batch size 4096 on an NVIDIA H200. We validate differentiability through gradient-based system identification on a 7-DOF manipulator, recovering link masses to 1.24% mean error under 5% torque noise, and integrate BARD into an Isaac Lab AMP training pipeline for an 11-DOF spined quadruped with 4096 parallel environments, where it is 8.5x faster than Pinocchio and 2.0x faster than ADAM for in-loop dynamics. BARD is open-sourced at: https://github.com/YueWang996/bard-pytorch-dynamics.

</details>


### [326] [Learning Controlled Separation of Small Objects Between Two Fingers with a Tactile Skin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31486)
*Ulf Kasolowsky,Berthold Bäuml*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A purely tactile, sim-to-real study of separating small objects held between two fingers of a robotic hand. The policy, trained by reinforcement learning with sparse reward, uses a spatially-resolved tactile skin to drop objects until a target count remains. High-resolution tactile sensing yields near-perfect performance; even a 4x4 taxel skin improves results over joint sensors. An auxiliary estimator predicts contact positions; successful transfer to a real DLR-Hand II with tactile skin is demonstrated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable manipulation in clutter using tactile feedback without relying on vision. Demonstrate that spatially-resolved tactile sensing can control finger-mediated separation tasks and quantify the benefits of tactile resolution for manipulation.

Method: Train a separation policy in simulation using reinforcement learning with a sparse reward that checks if the desired number of objects remains after dropping. Use a spatially-resolved tactile skin on a fingertip; compare high-resolution tactile sensing with a 4x4 taxel array; train an auxiliary estimator to predict ground-truth contact positions. Validate via sim-to-real transfer on the DLR-Hand II equipped with tactile skin.

Result: All-tactile policy can perform the task in simulation; a high-resolution tactile skin enables near-complete success, while a 4x4 taxel skin yields up to ~20% improvement over relying on joint sensors alone. The estimator accurately predicts contact positions and assists the policy. Real-world transfer to DLR-Hand II with tactile skin demonstrates feasibility of the approach.

Conclusion: Spatially-resolved tactile feedback substantially enhances manipulation by enabling pure-tactile control for separation tasks; even coarse tactile sensing provides meaningful gains over joint-sensor baselines. The work substantiates sim-to-real viability and highlights the value of contact-position estimation for tactile-guided policies.

Abstract: We introduce and solve the novel task of controlled separation of small objects with two fingers of a multi-purpose robotic hand: after grasping into a box of small objects, the task is to drop as many of them until a desired number remains between the fingers. The objects are small compared to the width of the fingers but also in absolute terms. In our case little pellets with a diameter of only 6mm are handled. We show that the task can be performed purely tactile (no vision) using a spatially-resolved tactile skin on a fingertip. The separation policy is trained in simulation via reinforcement learning using a straightforward sparse reward, which basically checks if the desired number of objects is reached. In simulation experiments, we provide an exhaustive analysis of the benefits of using spatially-resolved tactile feedback: while an ideal (high-resolution) tactile sensor allows solving the task almost perfectly, a sensor with lower spatial resolution (here 4x4 taxels) still leads to an improvement of up to 20% compared to using only the fingers' joint sensors. For this analysis, we further train an estimator alongside the policy that predicts the ground truth contact positions. Finally, we demonstrate the successful sim-to-real transfer for the DLR-Hand II equipped with a tactile skin.

</details>


### [327] [VLM-GLoc: Vision-Language Model Enhanced Monte Carlo Localization for Robust Semantic Global Localization in Cluttered Quasi-Static Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30506)
*Shivendra Agrawal,Bradley Hayes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) using open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models (VLMs) improves global localization in geometrically aliased indoor environments, outperforming geometry-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor environments exhibit strong geometric and semantic ambiguity due to repetitive layouts and clutter. Traditional geometry-based or domain-specific vision pipelines struggle with long-tail semantics and transient clutter; there is a need for robust, open-vocabulary semantic observations to improve localization.

Method: VLM-GLoc employs hierarchical semantic MCL with an open-vocabulary Vision-Language Model as the semantic observation front-end. Key components include an inverse semantic proposal mechanism that seeds particles via text-to-map retrieval and a three-fold benefit from VLMs: (1) discriminative text features, (2) implicit filtering of blurry/dynamic objects, (3) permanence reasoning for targeted data augmentation.

Result: Evaluated in two real-world settings (3,500 sq ft grocery store with a cellphone; 3,700 sq ft lab with a quadruped). Global localization success is 70% and 74% respectively, outperforming traditional geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

Conclusion: Open-vocabulary semantic observations enable robust global localization in geometrically aliased indoor environments, offering improved generalization over fixed-feature pipelines and reducing reliance on hand-crafted cues; results indicate strong potential for broad applicability across varied indoor spaces.

Abstract: Global localization in geometrically aliased, quasi-static environments such as grocery stores, offices, schools, and hospitals poses a significant challenge for mobile robots. Grocery stores with parallel aisles and a long tailed distribution of products, as well as offices and labs with repetitive furniture such as chairs, desks, monitors, and doors, exemplify common indoor environments that present geometric and even semantic ambiguity. Traditional approaches rely either on distinct geometric features or on domain-specific vision pipelines that struggle with long-tail semantic distributions and transient visual clutter. We present VLM-GLoc, a method for hierarchical semantic Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) that leverages open-vocabulary Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a unified semantic observation front-end. We hypothesize a three-fold benefit from VLMs: (1) extracting highly discriminative rich text features, (2) implicit quality filtering of blurry or dynamic objects, and (3) permanence reasoning for targeted data augmentation. We introduce an inverse semantic proposal mechanism that seeds particles via text-to-map retrieval. Evaluated across two real-world environments with different characteristics and two different platforms: a 3,500 sq. ft. grocery store with a cellphone and a 3,700 sq. ft. lab space with a quadruped, VLM-GLoc achieves 70% and 74% global localization success respectively, substantially outperforming traditional geometry-only and domain-specific baselines.

</details>


### [328] [ZAPS-DA: Zero-Phase Action Policy Smoothing with Decoupled Actor for Continuous Control in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30612)
*Faiq Shamass*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ZAPS-DA decouples a smooth, zero-lag policy from the RL actor by distilling, via imitation, zero-phase-filtered targets stored in replay buffers into a separate decoupled actor; deployed policy uses this smooth actor, achieving large action-jitter reductions with modest reward cost and no inference-time filtering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency action jitter in off-policy RL policies hinders real-world deployment; post-hoc filtering creates phase lag and smoothing in the RL loss entangles reward learning with smoothing. A zero-lag, smooth deployment requires decoupled supervision that does not affect training dynamics.

Method: Train a base RL actor with the standard loss. In parallel, train a separate decoupled actor via supervised imitation of targets produced by a zero-phase filter; targets are stored in the replay buffer. The deployed policy is the decoupled actor (no input history or inference-time filter). Use a magnitude-matched MSE loss to enable zero-hyperparameter portability across optimizers.

Result: In Soft Actor-Critic with a Savitzky–Golay filter, evaluated in two driving simulators (n=150 per protocol). On MetaDrive: steering jitter reduced 14–21x, throttle jitter 3–5x (all p<1e-4, Bonferroni-corrected); similar task performance with 6.3% reward cost. On a Webots adaptive cruise control environment: Pareto improvement—reward parity, 8–45x steering jitter reduction, total task-failure rate drop from 2.0% to 0.7%.

Conclusion: ZAPS-DA enables deployment-ready, smooth policies without post-processing or action-history input, delivering substantial jitter reductions with modest performance trade-offs; the magnitude-matched loss provides robustness across optimizers and maintains task performance close to the base RL policy.

Abstract: Continuous control policies trained with off-policy reinforcement learning frequently exhibit high-frequency action jitter, rendering direct deployment on physical actuators impractical. Post-hoc filtering attenuates jitter but introduces phase lag; embedding smoothness penalties in the actor's loss couples them with the RL gradient and conflates reward regression with over-aggressive smoothing. We present ZAPS-DA, a framework that reduces action jitter at deployment with negligible phase lag and no post-processing. ZAPS-DA pairs an unmodified main actor (trained by the base RL loss) with a separate decoupled actor trained via supervised imitation of zero-phase filtered targets stored in the replay buffer. The deployed policy is the decoupled actor: a feed-forward map from the current observation to a smooth action, with no inference-time filter and no action-history input -- a mechanism we term causal distillation of a non-causal filter. A magnitude-matched MSE loss provides zero-hyperparameter portability across optimizer classes. Validated with Soft Actor-Critic and a Savitzky--Golay filter in two driving simulators using paired n=150 evaluation protocols: on MetaDrive, ZAPS-DA reduces steering jitter by 14--21x and throttle jitter by 3--5x (all $p < 10^{-4}$, Bonferroni-corrected) while matching task-completion (p=0.28 success, p=0.31 crash) at a 6.3% reward cost; on a custom Webots adaptive cruise control environment, the same SG configuration produces a Pareto improvement -- reward parity (p=0.121), 8--45x steering jitter reduction, and total task-failure rate reduced from 2.0% to 0.7%.

</details>
